Eventually the unexposed face can reach ignition temperature — this is **fire penetration**, a key criterion in **fire-resistance rating**. ## 2.2 Convection Heat Transfer When hot fire gases rise, they form a **buoyant plume** described by empirical correlations. ### Heskestad Plume Correlation (most used in KTU problems) - Centerline temperature rise above ambient: $$\Delta T_{c} = 9.1 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}_{c}^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}} \right)$$ - Centerline vertical velocity: $$u_{c} = 0.96 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}_{c}^{1/3}}{z^{1/3}} \right)$$ Where: - $\dot{Q}_{c}$ = convective portion of HRR (typically **0.7 × Q_total**) [kW] - $z$ = height above fire source [m] ### Thomas Plume Correlation (alternative) $$\Delta T_{c} = 5.38 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}_{c}^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}} \right)$$ ## 2.3 Radiation Heat Transfer The dominant spread mechanism across a room. Follows the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**: $$\dot{q}_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$ For a flame of area $A_f$ radiating to a distant fuel surface of area $A_s$ with view factor $\phi$: $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon_f A_f \sigma T_f^{4} \phi$$ For large separations (point source approximation): $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad,\,source}}{4\pi r^{2}} \cdot \chi_r A_s$$ ## 2.4 t² Fire Growth (HRR Model) > [!NOTE] > **NFPA / KTU Standard Fire Growth Model:** > $$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^{2}$$ > Where α is the fire growth coefficient (kW/s²). | Growth Class | α (kW/s²) | Example Fuel | |--------------|-----------|--------------| | Slow | 0.00293 | Heavy timber pallet | | Medium | 0.01172 | PUR cushion, mail bags | | Fast | 0.04690 | Plastic-lined paper, pool fire | | Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | High-pressure aerosol cans | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $q'' = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's Law of Conduction | W/m² | | 2 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | Steady-state 1-D conduction | W | | 3 | $\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity | m²/s | | 4 | $\dot{Q}_{conv} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | W | | 5 | $\Delta T_c = 9.1 \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$ | Heskestad plume (centerline) | K | | 6 | $\Delta T_c = 5.38 \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$ | Thomas plume (centerline) | K | | 7 | $\dot{q} = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan–Boltzmann radiation | W/m² | | 8 | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon_f A_f \sigma T_f^{4} \phi_{fs}$ | Radiative heat transfer | W | | 9 | $\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^{2}$ | t-squared fire growth | kW | | 10 | $L_f = -1.02 + 0.235 \dot{Q}^{2/5}$ | Hasemi flame height (vertical wall) | m | | 11 | $\dot{Q}_{cr,flashover} = 1000\,A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ | Ventilation-controlled HRR (Thomas) | kW | | 12 | $\dot{q}''_{cr} = 12$ to $25$ kW/m² | Critical flux for piloted ignition | kW/m² | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Fire protection engineering** — sizing sprinklers, smoke vents, and detector response times - **Structural fire design** — determining fire resistance ratings (1-hr, 2-hr, 4-hr) - **Forensic fire investigation** — back-calculating HRR from burn patterns - **Building code compliance** — NFPA 13, IS 15105, IBC compartmentation rules # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Radiative Heat Flux Received at a Target Fuel Surface ### Problem Setup A circular pool fire of diameter D = 1.0 m burns with a total HRR $\dot{Q}$ = 1500 kW, of which **40 % is radiated**. A vertical wooden target is located at a horizontal distance r = 3 m from the fire's center. Find the radiative heat flux incident on the target's surface (assume the target is small compared to separation distance). ### Step 1: Identify the Radiative Component $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = 0.40 \times 1500 \text{ kW} = 600 \text{ kW}$$ ### Step 2: Apply Point-Source Radiation Approximation For a fire far from the target (separation >> flame dimensions), the radiation spreads hemispherically. $$\dot{q}'' = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute Values $$\dot{q}'' = \frac{600 \times 10^{3} \text{ W}}{4 \pi (3)^{2}}$$ $$\dot{q}'' = \frac{600{,}000}{4 \pi (9)} = \frac{600{,}000}{113.097}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{q}'' \approx 5305 \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 5.3 \text{ kW/m}^{2}}$$ ### Step 4: Compare Against Critical Ignition Flux Since piloted ignition of cellulosic fuels occurs at **≈ 12–15 kW/m²**, this target alone would **NOT** ignite from this flux. To ignite, the fire must either grow (HRR increases) or the target must move closer. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Time-to-Flashover for a t² Medium Growth Fire ### Problem Setup A medium t² fire ($\alpha$ = 0.01172 kW/s²) ignites in a room with ventilation factor $A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ = 4.0 m^(5/2). Find: (a) Time to reach flashover HRR (b) Time-to-flashover in seconds ### Step 1: Determine Critical HRR for Flashover (Thomas Criterion) $$\dot{Q}_{cr,flashover} = 1000 \, A_v \sqrt{H_v}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{cr,flashover} = 1000 \times 4.0 = 4000 \text{ kW}$$ ### Step 2: Equate with t² Growth Model $$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^{2} = 4000 \text{ kW}$$ $$t^{2} = \frac{4000}{0.01172}$$ $$t^{2} = 341{,}297$$ $$t = \sqrt{341{,}297}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 584 \text{ s} \approx 9.7 \text{ minutes}}$$ ### Step 3: Engineering Interpretation A medium-growth fire in this well-ventilated room reaches flashover in under 10 minutes. **This is why KTU and NBC codes demand fire detection and suppression within ≤ 2–4 minutes of ignition.** --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Modes of Fire Spread Across Building Elements | Building Element | Dominant Mode | Critical Failure Criterion | Mitigation Strategy | |------------------|---------------|----------------------------|---------------------| | Internal wall (gypsum) | Conduction | Un-exposed face T > 140 K rise (insulation criterion) | Type-X gypsum, mineral wool cavity | | Corridor ceiling | Convection (hot smoke) | Smoke temp > 200 °C, visibility < 10 m | Smoke vents, smoke curtains | | Adjacent room across open doorway | Radiation | Incident flux > 12 kW/m² at far wall | Compartment walls, fire doors | | Vertical service shaft | Convective + convective plume | Smoke spread to upper floors | Pressurization, fire-rated dampers | | Window glazing | Radiation + convection | Glass cracks at ΔT > 50 K | Sprinkler water film, tempered glass | | Steel structural beam | Conduction | Steel T > 550 °C (50% strength loss) | Intumescent coating, SFRM (spray-applied fire-resistive material) | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Heskestad Ceiling Jet Temperature A fire plume with $\dot{Q}_c$ = 800 kW rises to a ceiling at z = 6 m. Find centerline plume temperature rise above ambient (T∞ = 20 °C). ### Step 1: Apply Heskestad's Correlation $$\Delta T_c = 9.1 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}} \right)$$ ### Step 2: Compute Numerator $$\dot{Q}_c^{2/3} = 800^{2/3}$$ Taking logarithms: ln(800) = 6.6846, then × (2/3) = 4.4564, then exp = **85.5** $$\dot{Q}_c^{2/3} \approx 85.5 \text{ kW}^{2/3}$$ ### Step 3: Compute Denominator $$z^{5/3} = 6^{5/3}$$ ln(6) = 1.7918, × (5/3) = 2.9863, exp = **19.81** $$z^{5/3} \approx 19.81 \text{ m}^{5/3}$$ ### Step 4: Final Substitution $$\Delta T_c = 9.1 \times \frac{85.5}{19.81} = 9.1 \times 4.316 = 39.3 \text{ °C}$$ $$\boxed{T_c = T_\infty + \Delta T_c = 20 + 39.3 = 59.3 \text{ °C}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The ceiling jet will be ~39 K above ambient at z = 6 m. This temperature alone won't ignite most fuels — but the **smoke layer accumulating at the ceiling** will continue to grow, eventually triggering flashover when the entire upper layer hits ~1000 °C. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fire Spread Mechanism Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ignition Source - Pilot Flame] --> B[Initial Fuel Package] B --> C[Heat Feedback Loop] C --> D{Radiation Dominant?} D -- Yes --> E[Surface Reaches Pyrolysis T] D -- No --> F{Convection Dominant?} F -- Yes --> G[Hot Plume Reaches Adjacent Fuel] F -- No --> H{Conduction Dominant?} H -- Yes --> I[Heat Travels Through Solid] E --> J[Adjacent Fuel Ignites] G --> J I --> J J --> K{Sustained Chain Reaction?} K -- Yes --> L[Fire Spreads Further] K -- No --> M[Self-Extinguishment] L --> N{Critical Heat Flux Exceeded?} N -- Yes --> O[Flashover - Full Room Involvement] N -- No --> P[Continued Localized Burning] ``` ## 4.2 Fire Tetrahedron - Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FireTetrahedron[FIRE TETRAHEDRON] F[Fuel
Hydrocarbons, Cellulose, Polymers] O[Oxygen
From Air - 21 percent] H[Heat
Ignition Source] C[Chain Reaction
Free Radicals H OH O] end F --- O O --- H H --- C C --- F F --- H O --- C ``` ## 4.3 Compartment Fire Stages - Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Incipient Stage - Smoldering - HRR less than 25 kW] --> B[Growth Stage - t-squared fire - Ceiling Jet Forms] B --> C[Flashover - Upper Layer 1000 deg C - All Fuels Ignite] C --> D[Fully Developed - Ventilation Controlled or Fuel Controlled] D --> E[Decay Stage - Fuel Depletes - HRR Falls] E --> F[Extinguishment or Burnout] style A fill:#fff5e6 style B fill:#ffd699 style C fill:#ff6600,color:#ffffff style D fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff style E fill:#808080,color:#ffffff style F fill:#000000,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Heat Transfer Modes in a Burning Room (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Room[Compartment] FLAME[Flame - Source - High T] WALL1[Wall - Hot Face] WALL2[Wall - Cold Face] TARGET[Adjacent Fuel - Target] CEIL[Ceiling] end FLAME -- Conduction --> WALL1 WALL1 -- Conduction --> WALL2 FLAME -- Radiation - straight line --> TARGET FLAME -- Convection - rising plume --> CEIL CEIL -- Convection - ceiling jet --> TARGET ``` ## 4.5 Fire Growth Coefficient Comparison Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Slow alpha 0.00293 kW per s squared - Heavy Timber] -->|10 min| B[Q approx 176 kW] C[Medium alpha 0.01172 kW per s squared - PUR Cushion] -->|10 min| D[Q approx 705 kW] E[Fast alpha 0.0469 kW per s squared - Plastic Lined Paper] -->|10 min| F[Q approx 2820 kW] G[Ultra Fast alpha 0.1876 kW per s squared - Aerosol Cans] -->|10 min| H[Q approx 11280 kW] style A fill:#90EE90 style C fill:#FFD700 style E fill:#FF8C00 style G fill:#FF0000,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)** ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the Fire Tetrahedron and explain how it differs from the Fire Triangle in fire suppression strategy.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + Oxygen + Heat → Fire (3 sides) > **Fire Tetrahedron:** Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + *Uninhibited Chain Reaction* → Fire (4 sides) The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds the *sustained chain reaction* (free-radical propagation mechanism — H•, OH•, O• radicals in combustion). Classical fire suppression methods (water cooling, fuel removal, oxygen smothering) attack the **triangle**. However, **dry chemical and halogenated agents** specifically target the chain reaction by disrupting free-radical formation — only explainable by the tetrahedron model. *(3 marks: 1 — definition, 1 — extra element explained, 1 — suppression strategy link)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List the three modes of fire spread and state which mode is dominant in large open compartments.** **Model Answer:** The three modes are: 1. **Conduction** — energy transfer through solids (walls, structural members). 2. **Convection** — energy carried by hot gases (ceiling jets, plumes). 3. **Radiation** — electromagnetic energy emission, requires no medium. In **large open compartments**, **radiation is the dominant mode** because the fire plume cannot fill the entire volume quickly and radiative view factors to remote fuels remain significant. Convection dominates in corridors and shafts. *(3 marks: 1 per mode + 1 for identifying radiation as dominant in large compartments)* --- ## **Part B Question A (14 Marks)** ### [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q. (a)** Explain the t² fire growth model with its classification. Derive the time to reach a HRR of 1000 kW for a medium-growth fire. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The **t² fire growth model** is the standard empirical HRR-time correlation used in fire protection engineering: $$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha \, t^{2}$$ Where: - $\dot{Q}(t)$ = heat release rate at time *t* [kW] - $\alpha$ = fire growth coefficient [kW/s²] - $t$ = time from ignition [s] **NFPA Classification:** | Growth Class | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Typical Fuel | |--------------|------------------|--------------| | Slow | 0.00293 | Heavy wood pallets | | Medium | 0.01172 | PUR cushions, mail bags | | Fast | 0.04690 | Plastic-lined paper | | Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | Pressurized aerosols | **Derivation for medium-growth fire reaching 1000 kW:** For medium growth: $\alpha$ = 0.01172 kW/s² Set $\dot{Q}(t)$ = 1000 kW: $$1000 = 0.01172 \times t^{2}$$ $$t^{2} = \frac{1000}{0.01172} = 85{,}324$$ $$t = \sqrt{85{,}324} \approx 292 \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 292 \text{ s} \approx 4.87 \text{ minutes}}$$ **Conclusion:** A medium-growth fire reaches 1 MW in under 5 minutes — illustrating the need for rapid detection and suppression. *(7 marks: 2 — model definition & formula; 2 — classification table; 3 — derivation)* --- ### Q. (b)** A fire in a room has HRR = 2000 kW of which 30 % is convective. Using Heskestad's correlation, calculate the centerline plume temperature rise at z = 5 m. If ambient temperature is 30 °C, find the actual plume temperature. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $\dot{Q}_c$ = 0.30 × 2000 = **600 kW** - z = 5 m - T∞ = 30 °C **Step 1 — Apply Heskestad's Centerline Plume Equation** $$\Delta T_c = 9.1 \left( \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}} \right)$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}$** $$\dot{Q}_c^{2/3} = 600^{2/3}$$ Using logarithms: ln(600) = 6.3970; × (2/3) = 4.2647; exp = **71.18** **Step 3 — Compute $z^{5/3}$** $$z^{5/3} = 5^{5/3}$$ ln(5) = 1.6094; × (5/3) = 2.6824; exp = **14.62** **Step 4 — Substitute** $$\Delta T_c = 9.1 \times \frac{71.18}{14.62} = 9.1 \times 4.868 = 44.3 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 5 — Find Actual Plume Temperature** $$T_c = T_\infty + \Delta T_c = 30 + 44.3 = 74.3 \text{ °C}$$ $$\boxed{T_c \approx 74.3 \text{ °C}}$$ > [!NOTE] > At this temperature, wood begins to **pyrolyze** (T ≈ 300 °C not yet reached), but a sprinkler with RTI = 50 would still activate here, slowing fire spread. *(7 marks: 1 — given data; 2 — formula application; 2 — numerical computation; 1 — final answer + engineering interpretation; 1 — units)* --- ## **Part B Question B (14 Marks)** ### [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q. (a)** Explain conduction, convection, and radiation as modes of fire spread with suitable examples. Derive the steady-state heat conduction through a concrete wall exposed to fire on one side. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Conduction:** Transfer of thermal energy through a solid by molecular vibration and electron transport. Governed by Fourier's Law. Example: Heat traveling through a concrete wall during a fire, raising the temperature of the unexposed face. **2. Convection:** Transfer of heat by bulk motion of fluids (hot gases, smoke). Hot fire gases rise as plumes along walls and ceilings. Example: Smoke spreading along a corridor ceiling, igniting distant combustibles. **3. Radiation:** Transfer by electromagnetic waves (predominantly infrared). No medium required; can cross vacuum. Example: Heat from a far-away flame igniting a wooden chair across a room. **Derivation — Steady-State Conduction Through Concrete Wall:** Consider a homogeneous concrete wall of: - Thickness: L [m] - Hot face temperature: $T_1$ [K] - Cold face temperature: $T_2$ [K] - Thermal conductivity: k [W/m·K] - Cross-sectional area: A [m²] Fourier's Law in 1-D steady state: $$\dot{Q}_{cond} = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$ For steady state, temperature gradient is linear: $$\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}$$ Substituting: $$\dot{Q}_{cond} = k A \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{cond} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} \quad [W]}$$ **Engineering Application:** - For fire-resistance rating, we need $\dot{Q}_{cond}$ through the wall to be insufficient to raise the unexposed face beyond **140 K rise** (insulation criterion per IS 3809 / ASTM E119). - Adding mineral wool (low k ≈ 0.04 W/m·K) drastically reduces $\dot{Q}_{cond}$. *(7 marks: 2 — three modes; 2 — derivation setup; 2 — final equation + interpretation; 1 — units)* --- ### Q. (b)** A wooden cabin is located at a horizontal distance of 4 m from a circular pool fire of diameter 2 m. The total HRR is 2500 kW, with 35 % radiated. Calculate: (i) radiative heat flux on the cabin; (ii) whether piloted ignition of wood (critical flux = 12 kW/m²) is possible. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - Pool diameter D = 2 m (flame radius r_f = 1 m) - $\dot{Q}_{total}$ = 2500 kW - Radiative fraction $\chi_r$ = 0.35 - Horizontal separation distance r = 4 m **Step 1 — Radiative Power** $$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_r \times \dot{Q}_{total} = 0.35 \times 2500 = 875 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Point-Source Radiation Model** Since r = 4 m >> flame radius 1 m, point-source approximation is valid: $$\dot{q}''_{cabin} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ $$\dot{q}''_{cabin} = \frac{875{,}000}{4 \pi (4)^{2}} = \frac{875{,}000}{201.06}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{q}''_{cabin} \approx 4352 \text{ W/m}^{2} = 4.35 \text{ kW/m}^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Compare with Critical Flux** - Incident flux: **4.35 kW/m²** - Critical piloted ignition flux for wood: **12 kW/m²** Since 4.35 kW/m² < 12 kW/m²: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result: Piloted ignition will NOT occur at this distance.** However, the cabin surface will still experience thermal damage, surface discoloration, and prolonged exposure may lead to piloted ignition if the fire grows (HRR increases). **Step 4 — Determine Minimum HRR for Ignition** $$\dot{Q}_{rad,min} = \dot{q}''_{cr} \times 4\pi r^{2} = 12{,}000 \times 4\pi (4)^{2}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{rad,min} = 12{,}000 \times 201.06 = 2{,}412{,}720 \text{ W} = 2412 \text{ kW}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{total,min} = \frac{2412}{0.35} \approx 6892 \text{ kW}$$ So the fire would need to grow to nearly 6.9 MW before the cabin ignites — a fully developed compartment fire. *(7 marks: 1 — setup; 2 — radiative power; 2 — flux calculation; 2 — comparison + minimum HRR analysis)* --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students LOSE marks in this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting units** — kW vs. W; m vs. mm. KTU examiners *strictly deduct* 0.5 mark per missing unit. > 2. **Using wrong plume correlation** — Heskestad vs. Thomas confusion; pick the one given in the problem data. > 3. **Mixing total vs. convective HRR** — always check if the problem states "radiative fraction" or "convective fraction." > 4. **Ignoring the view factor** — point-source approximation only valid when r >> flame diameter. State this assumption! > 5. **Not interpreting results** — KTU wants engineering interpretation, not just numerical answers. Always conclude with a statement like *"fire would NOT ignite the target because..."*. > 6. **Skipping the t² classification table** — for full 7 marks, always show all four growth classes. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🔥 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Fire Triangle vs. Tetrahedron** — Tetrahedron adds *chain reaction*; dry chemical extinguishers work on this side - ✅ **Three modes of heat transfer** — Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton, plume correlations), Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann) - ✅ **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴** — memorize exactly - ✅ **t² fire model** — $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^{2}$ with four NFPA growth classes (slow / medium / fast / ultra-fast) - ✅ **Heskestad plume** — $\Delta T_c = 9.1 \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$ - ✅ **Thomas plume** — $\Delta T_c = 5.38 \frac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}$ - ✅ **Flashover criterion** — Upper layer ~1000 °C, OR incident flux 20 kW/m², OR Thomas equation $\dot{Q}_{cr} = 1000 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ - ✅ **Ignition criteria** — Piloted wood ≈ 12–15 kW/m², auto-ignition wood ≈ 400 °C - ✅ **Fire stages** — Incipient → Growth → Flashover → Fully Developed → Decay - ✅ **Dominant mode in large rooms** — **Radiation** (because view factors to distant fuels remain non-zero) - ✅ **Ventilation-controlled vs. fuel-controlled** — fully developed stage transitions when air supply becomes limiting - ✅ **Conduction equation** — $\dot{Q} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ — assume steady-state unless told otherwise - ✅ **Point-source radiation** — $\dot{q}'' = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^{2}}$ — valid only when r >> flame dimensions - ✅ **Code references for fire resistance** — IS 3809, ASTM E119, NBC 2016 Part 4 > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line Takeaway:** Fire spread is the heat-driven chain reaction of fresh fuel ignition; it propagates by conduction (through solids), convection (by hot gases), and radiation (by electromagnetic waves), with **radiation dominating in large compartments** and **convection dominating in confined corridors**.

topics.title: Measures of Flammability: Flash Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measures of Flammability: Flash Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point (FP):** The *lowest temperature* at which a liquid produces enough **flammable vapour** in its headspace to form an **ignitable mixture** with air (within the flammable range), such that the application of a small external ignition source (test flame or spark) produces a **transient flash** of flame across the liquid surface — but the liquid itself does **NOT** sustain combustion.

This is the cornerstone measurement in fire engineering because it directly links the **thermodynamic property of a liquid** (its vapour pressure curve) to the **kinetic requirement of combustion** (fuel concentration must lie between the Lower Flammable Limit — LFL and Upper Flammable Limit — UFL).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Boiling Kettle" Test:**
> Imagine a cold gasoline drum in winter. Even though gasoline is *highly flammable*, you cannot ignite it by holding a match near it because the cold liquid releases **almost no vapour**. Now slowly warm the drum. As the temperature rises, invisible vapour begins to escape from the surface. The **first temperature** at which you can briefly light this vapour cloud — without the liquid catching fire continuously — is the **Flash Point**.
>
> If you keep heating past the flash point, you eventually reach the **Fire Point**, where the liquid itself keeps burning on its own (like a candle). Keep going further and you reach the **Boiling Point** (BP), where vapour evolution is vigorous.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Temperatures on the Flammability Scale

| Temperature Point | Symbolic Notation | Combustion Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | $T_{FP}$ | Transient flash only; liquid does not sustain burning |
| **Fire Point** | $T_{fire}$ | Vapour burns **continuously** for ≥ 5 s after ignition |
| **Boiling Point** | $T_{b}$ | Vapour pressure equals atmospheric pressure; bulk liquid vaporises |

### 1.4 Standard Test Metrics (Test Apparatus-Dependent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A liquid has **two** legal flash points: a **Closed-Cup Flash Point (CCFP)** and an **Open-Cup Flash Point (OCFP)**. The CCFP is always **lower** (typically by **5–20 °C**) than the OCFP for the same liquid because the closed cup traps vapour, allowing saturation sooner.
>
> **Standard apparatus families used globally:**
> - **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** — ASTM D93, ISO 2719 — *most common industrial standard*
> - **Tag Closed Cup (TCC)** — ASTM D56 — *for low-viscosity liquids*
> - **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** — ASTM D92 — *for high-viscosity oils and lubricants*
> - **Abel Closed Cup** — IP 170 — *UK legal standard for petroleum*

### 1.5 The Three Pillars of Flammability

Flash point is governed by the intersection of three physical realities:

$$T_{FP} \;\longleftrightarrow\; P_{vap}(T_{FP}) \;\longleftrightarrow\; LFL \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Fire}$$

- **Thermodynamic pillar:** Vapour pressure curve $P_{vap}(T)$
- **Concentration pillar:** Flammable limits $[LFL,\; UFL]$
- **Kinetic pillar:** Activation energy / ignition source
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Does Flash Point Exist?

The flash point is governed by **three simultaneous conditions** that must ALL be satisfied at the same temperature:

1. **Fuel Concentration Condition:** The equilibrium partial pressure of the fuel vapour at the liquid surface must equal the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** concentration expressed as a partial pressure:

$$P_{vap}(T_{FP}) \;\geq\; y_{LFL} \cdot P_{total}$$

For a **pure component in air at 1 atm**, the standard assumption is:

$$P_{vap}(T_{FP}) \;\approx\; LFL \cdot P_{atm} \quad \text{(at flash point)}$$

2. **Vapour–Air Mixing Condition:** A flammable mixture must form in the *test region* (headspace). In an **open cup**, vapour disperses easily, so a higher temperature (and pressure) is needed. In a **closed cup**, vapour is contained, so equilibrium is reached at a **lower temperature**.

3. **Ignition Source Condition:** The ignition source must supply energy $\geq E_{activation}$. The standard test flame is calibrated to deliver a known thermal flux.

### 2.2 Types of Flash Point — Open Cup vs Closed Cup

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "CO-O-Closed vs OP-EN-Open":**
> - **C**losed cup → **C**ontainment of vapour → **C**older flash point
> - **Open** cup → **O**xygen freely available + **O**pening for vapour → **O**verall higher flash point

**Closed-Cup Method (Pensky-Martens):**
- Cup is sealed except for a sliding lid with an opening.
- Stirrer rotates to maintain thermal equilibrium.
- Test flame is dipped into the headspace through the lid opening at every 1–2 °C rise.
- Flash observed inside the cup = **CCFP**.

**Open-Cup Method (Cleveland):**
- Cup is open to atmosphere.
- Test flame is swept **across** the surface at every 5 °C rise.
- Flash observed across the open surface = **OCFP**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM REVISION ANCHOR.** Memorise every entry; questions frequently combine two of these equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | **Clausius-Clapeyron** — relates vapour pressure to temperature | $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $\Delta H_{vap}$ in J/mol |
| 2 | $\log_{10} P_{vap} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ | **Antoine Equation** — empirical vapour pressure curve | $P$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C |
| 3 | $P_{vap}(T_{FP}) \approx LFL \cdot P_{atm}$ | **Flash point condition** — vapour pressure equals LFL concentration | Pa or atm |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta S_{vap}}{R} \approx 10.5$ (at $T_b$) | **Trouton's Rule** — entropy of vaporisation | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{T_{b}}{T_{FP}} \approx 1.55 \pm 0.05$ | **Empirical rule of thumb** — relates boiling & flash points | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{T_{FP,OC}}{T_{FP,CC}} \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$ | **OCFP / CCFP ratio** for most hydrocarbons | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $y_i \cdot P = x_i \cdot \gamma_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$ | **Modified Raoult's Law** — for mixtures | Mole fraction |
| 8 | $LFL \approx \dfrac{0.101}{k_{st}}$ (stochiometric) | **LFL ↔ stoichiometry link** for pure hydrocarbons | vol % |

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Flash Point (Frequent 7-Mark Topic)

- **Chemical structure:** Branched alkanes have *higher* flash points than straight-chain isomers; aromatics have *lower* flash points due to higher volatility.
- **Vapour pressure curve:** Steeper vapour-pressure rise → lower flash point.
- **Atmospheric pressure:** Lower altitude/pressure → *lower* flash point (corrected by ASTM D93 correction factor).
- **Test method:** Closed cup < Open cup (always).
- **Impurities / mixtures:** Flash point of a mixture follows **Le Chatelier-like** behaviour; the most volatile component dominates.
- **Stirring & equilibration time:** Insufficient stirring gives falsely *high* flash point.
- **Ignition source energy:** Higher energy → *lower* observed flash point.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Flash Point is legally mandatory in industry:**
> - **Hazardous Area Classification** (IS 5572 / IEC 60079-10): A liquid with $T_{FP} < 37.8\,°\text{C}$ is **Class I** (most hazardous). $37.8\,°\text{C} \leq T_{FP} < 93.3\,°\text{C}$ → **Class II**. $T_{FP} \geq 93.3\,°\text{C}$ → **Class III**.
> - **Storage & Transportation:** UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods use flash point to classify Packing Groups (I, II, III).
> - **Insurance & OSHA:** Used to determine whether a workplace needs explosion-proof electrical fittings.
> - **Process Safety:** Lower flammable limit (LFL) and flash point together define the **fire/explosion envelope** in HAZOP studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flash Point from Clausius-Clapeyron + LFL Condition

**Goal:** Derive an expression for $T_{FP}$ in terms of $LFL$, $\Delta H_{vap}$, and a known reference vapour pressure.

**Step 1 — State the fundamental flash point condition.**

By definition, at the flash point the equilibrium vapour pressure of the liquid just equals the partial pressure corresponding to the Lower Flammable Limit in air at total pressure $P$:

$$P_{vap}(T_{FP}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{total}$$

For a pure component in air at 1 atm, $y_{LFL}$ is conventionally expressed in mole fraction (e.g., for n-heptane $LFL = 1.05\,\%$, so $y_{LFL} = 0.0105$). Therefore:

$$P_{vap}(T_{FP}) = 0.0105 \times 101325 \approx 1064 \;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate the Clausius-Clapeyron equation between a known state (say the normal boiling point $T_b$ where $P_{vap} = 101325$ Pa) and $T_{FP}$.**

$$\int_{P_{vap}(T_{FP})}^{P_{vap}(T_b)} d(\ln P) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_{FP}}^{T_b} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{vap}(T_b)}{P_{vap}(T_{FP})}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T_{FP}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the flash-point condition from Step 1.**

Because $P_{vap}(T_b) = P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa and $P_{vap}(T_{FP}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{atm}}{y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{y_{LFL}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{T_{FP}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for $T_{FP}$.**

$$\frac{1}{T_{FP}} = \frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{y_{LFL}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;T_{FP} = \left[\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{y_{LFL}}\right)\right]^{-1}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**

- A **lower** LFL (more dangerous fuel, e.g., gasoline) makes $\ln(1/y_{LFL})$ larger, which **lowers** $T_{FP}$ — consistent with intuition.
- A **higher** heat of vaporisation $\Delta H_{vap}$ means the liquid needs more energy to liberate vapour, which **raises** $T_{FP}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Worked Example for n-Heptane

**Given data for n-heptane (literature values):**

- Normal boiling point $T_b = 371.6$ K
- Enthalpy of vaporisation $\Delta H_{vap} = 31{,}770$ J/mol
- Lower flammable limit $y_{LFL} = 0.0105$ (1.05 %)
- Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Compute $\ln(1/y_{LFL})$:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.0105}\right) = \ln(95.238) \approx 4.556$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R/\Delta H_{vap}$:**

$$\frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} = \frac{8.314}{31770} \approx 2.617 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$\frac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{y_{LFL}}\right) = (2.617 \times 10^{-4}) \times 4.556 \approx 1.192 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $1/T_b$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{371.6} \approx 2.691 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T_{FP}$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{FP}} = 2.691 \times 10^{-3} - 1.192 \times 10^{-3} = 1.499 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_{FP} = \frac{1}{1.499 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 667 \;\text{K} \;\;(\approx 394\,°\text{C})$$

> [!WARNING]
> The value 394 °C is the **theoretical ideal** flash point. The experimental **closed-cup flash point of n-heptane is ≈ -4 °C (269 K)** — a discrepancy of ~125 K! Why?
> Because the theoretical derivation assumes *fuel in air at stoichiometric concentration*, but the actual flash-point test condition uses $LFL$ partial pressure in a **headspace above a non-ideal liquid pool with surface cooling and time-dependent diffusion**. In practice, the flash point is best **measured experimentally**, and equations like the above are used for *trend analysis and screening* only.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Flash Point Test Methods

| Parameter | Pensky-Martens (PMCC) | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | Tag Closed Cup (TCC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cup Type** | Closed | Open | Closed |
| **ASTM Standard** | D93 | D92 | D56 |
| **Heating Rate** | 5–6 °C/min | 5 °C/min | 1 °C/min |
| **Stirring** | Yes (90–250 rpm) | None | Yes |
| **Ignition Frequency** | Every 1 °C | Every 5 °C | Every 0.5 °C |
| **Typical FP Range** | 40–360 °C | 79–400 °C | < 93 °C |
| **Best Suited For** | Lubricants, diesel, kerosene | Heavy oils, asphalts | Volatile solvents, paints |
| **FP Value (typical)** | Lower | Higher | Lower (most sensitive) |

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Trouton-Based Empirical $T_b / T_{FP}$ Ratio

**Step 1 — Trouton's Rule at the normal boiling point:**

$$\Delta S_{vap}(T_b) = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T_b} \approx 10.5 \, R \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta H_{vap} \approx 10.5 \, R \, T_b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the derived flash-point expression from §3.1:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{FP}} = \frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{R}{10.5 \, R \, T_b} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{y_{LFL}}\right) = \frac{1}{T_b}\left[1 - \frac{\ln(1/y_{LFL})}{10.5}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the ratio:**

$$\frac{T_b}{T_{FP}} = 1 - \frac{\ln(1/y_{LFL})}{10.5}$$

**Step 4 — For n-heptane (numerical):**

$$\frac{T_b}{T_{FP}} = 1 - \frac{4.556}{10.5} \approx 1 - 0.434 \approx 0.566$$

This gives $T_{FP} \approx 2 \times T_b$, which is non-physical — confirming once again that **flash point is dominated by transport and kinetic effects**, not purely equilibrium thermodynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-away:** Use these equations for *order-of-magnitude* estimation and ranking of liquids; rely on ASTM-standard measurement for legal and design values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Pensky-Martens Closed Cup Tester

The diagram below maps the functional architecture of a closed-cup flash-point apparatus as a block-level flow, showing how thermal, mechanical, and ignition subsystems interact to produce a measured flash event.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Sample In Cup] --> B[Sealed Test Chamber With Sliding Lid]
    B --> C[Heating Mantle With Controlled Ramp 5C per min]
    B --> D[Stirring Motor 90 to 250 rpm]
    B --> E[Thermometer or RTD Sensor]
    E --> F[Temperature Controller Loop]
    C --> F
    F --> B
    B --> G[Gas Pilot Flame On Swivel Arm]
    G --> H[Ignition Dipped Through Lid Aperture Every 1C]
    H --> I{Flash Detected by Operator}
    I -- Yes --> J[Record Temperature as Flash Point]
    I -- No --> K[Continue Heating]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Report CCFP per ASTM D93]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Cleveland Open Cup Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Sample In Open Cup] --> B[Open Air Headspace Above Liquid]
    B --> C[Electric Heater With Variable Control]
    B --> D[Thermometer Immersed In Liquid]
    D --> E[Temperature Readout]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Manual Heat Rate Adjustment]
    F --> C
    B --> G[Test Flame On Movable Swivel]
    G --> H[Sweep Flame Across Surface Every 5C]
    H --> I{Flash Across Surface Visible}
    I -- Yes --> J[Record Temperature as Flash Point]
    I -- No --> K[Continue Heating and Sweeping]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Report OCFP per ASTM D92]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Relationship Between Flash Point and the Fire Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Heat Source Raises Liquid Temperature] --> P2[Vapour Pressure Increases With T]
    P2 --> P3{Vapour Concentration Reaches LFL}
    P3 -- No --> P1
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Fuel Air Mixture In Flammable Range]
    P4 --> P5[Ignition Source Test Flame Applied]
    P5 --> P6{Flash Observed}
    P6 -- Transient --> P7[Temperature Recorded as FLASH POINT]
    P7 --> P8[If Heating Continues Past FP]
    P8 --> P9[Sustained Combustion Reached]
    P9 --> P10[Temperature Recorded as FIRE POINT]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Closed Cup vs Open Cup at Same Liquid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CC[Closed Cup PMCC]
        CC1[Sealed Headspace] --> CC2[Vapour Saturates Quickly]
        CC2 --> CC3[Flash Point Achieved Earlier Lower T]
    end
    subgraph OC[Open Cup COC]
        OC1[Open Headspace] --> OC2[Vapour Escapes To Atmosphere]
        OC2 --> OC3[Higher T Needed To Reach LFL In Test Zone]
    end
    CC3 -. Typically 5 to 20C lower .-> OC3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - In **PMCC**, the lid acts as a **vapour concentrator**, allowing equilibrium faster.
> - In **COC**, vapour is lost by diffusion and convection; only the strongest vapour evolution (at higher $T$) produces a flash.
> - This is why **OCFP > CCFP** for any given liquid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define flash point. How is it different from fire point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Flash point is the lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapour to form an ignitable mixture with air such that a transient flash of flame is produced at the surface upon application of an ignition source, but the liquid does **not** continue to burn.
- **(1 mark)** Fire point is the lowest temperature at which the vapour evolved from the liquid **sustains combustion** for at least 5 seconds after removal of the ignition source.
- **(1 mark)** Numerically, $T_{fire} \approx T_{FP} + 5\,\text{to}\,30\,°\text{C}$ for most hydrocarbons. The fire point is always **higher** than the flash point because the heat supplied to the liquid surface must compensate for vapour losses and maintain a self-sustaining flame.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Why does the closed-cup flash point of a liquid differ from its open-cup flash point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** In a **closed cup**, the headspace is sealed, allowing vapour to accumulate and reach the LFL concentration at a **lower** temperature.
- **(1 mark)** In an **open cup**, vapour escapes continuously into the atmosphere, so the concentration near the surface is diluted; a higher temperature (and hence higher vapour pressure) is required to reach the LFL.
- **(1 mark)** As a result, $T_{FP,\text{open}} > T_{FP,\text{closed}}$ typically by **5–20 °C** depending on volatility. For legal and safety design purposes, the **closed-cup value** is used because it represents the worst-case hazard (lowest temperature at which a flammable mixture can form in a confined space).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 marks] Describe the Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) method for determining the flash point of a liquid with the help of a labelled schematic.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Apparatus and Standard (1 mark):** The PMCC apparatus conforms to **ASTM D93 / ISO 2719**. It consists of a brass test cup of approximately 70 mL capacity, a closed lid with a sliding shutter, a stirrer mechanism, an electric heater, a test-flame gas jet mounted on a swivel arm, and a calibrated thermometer.

2. **Sample Preparation (1 mark):** Approximately **70 mL** of the liquid sample is filled into the test cup. The cup is sealed with the lid. The stirrer is set to rotate at 90–250 rpm.

3. **Heating Phase (1 mark):** The sample is heated at a uniform rate of **5–6 °C per minute**, with continuous stirring to ensure uniform temperature distribution.

4. **Ignition Testing (2 marks):** At every **1 °C** rise (or 1 °C below expected flash point), the test flame is introduced into the cup through the lid aperture for **2.5 seconds** by depressing the swivel arm. The operator watches for a momentary flash of flame inside the cup.

5. **Flash Point Identification (1 mark):** The **flash point** is the lowest temperature at which the application of the test flame produces a distinct flash across the interior of the cup. The temperature reading at this moment is recorded.

6. **Atmospheric Pressure Correction (1 mark):** The observed flash point is corrected to standard sea-level pressure (101.325 kPa) using the correction factor:

$$T_{corrected} = T_{observed} + 0.25 \times (101.325 - P_{actual}) \;\; [\text{°C, kPa}]$$

#### (b) [7 marks] Discuss the various factors affecting the flash point of a liquid.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Chemical Structure (1.5 marks):** Straight-chain hydrocarbons have lower flash points than branched isomers; aromatic compounds (benzene, toluene) have lower flash points than aliphatic compounds of similar molecular weight due to higher volatility and weaker intermolecular forces.

2. **Vapour Pressure Curve (1 mark):** Liquids with a steeply rising vapour-pressure curve reach the LFL concentration at lower temperatures, giving lower flash points.

3. **Test Method (1.5 marks):** Closed-cup methods give lower flash points than open-cup methods (by 5–20 °C) because vapour is trapped and saturates the headspace faster.

4. **Atmospheric Pressure (1 mark):** Reduced atmospheric pressure (high altitudes) lowers the boiling point and slightly lowers the flash point because the partial-pressure criterion $P_{vap}(T_{FP}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P$ is reached earlier.

5. **Mixture Composition (1 mark):** For liquid mixtures, the flash point follows approximately the behaviour of the **most volatile component**. Le Chatelier-type rules apply for ideal mixtures.

6. **Stirring and Equilibration (1 mark):** Insufficient stirring produces local temperature gradients, leading to a **falsely high** observed flash point. ASTM D93 specifies a controlled stir rate to avoid this.

7. **Ignition Source Energy (0.5 mark):** A higher-energy ignition source can ignite leaner (sub-LFL) mixtures, giving a **falsely low** observed flash point. The test flame is therefore calibrated.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the Cleveland Open Cup (COC) method for flash point determination.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Standard (1 mark):** The Cleveland Open Cup apparatus conforms to **ASTM D92**. It consists of an open brass cup, a flat-bottomed heating plate, a gas test-flame on a swivel arm, and a calibrated thermometer suspended in the sample.

2. **Sample Loading (1 mark):** The cup is filled to a specified mark (typically 70 mL) with the test liquid. **No lid** is used — the surface remains open to atmosphere.

3. **Heating (1 mark):** The sample is heated at a rate of **5 °C per minute** until approaching the expected flash point, then at **2 °C per minute** near the flash point.

4. **Ignition Sweeping (2 marks):** At every **5 °C** rise, the test flame is passed **across the surface of the liquid** in a smooth sweep, traversing the cup diameter in about 1 second. The operator observes whether a flash crosses the surface.

5. **Flash Point Identification (1 mark):** The flash point is the temperature at which a distinct flash of flame appears at any point on the liquid surface during the sweep, but the liquid does **not** continue to burn.

6. **Applications (1 mark):** The COC method is preferred for **high-viscosity liquids** such as lubricating oils, asphalts, and petroleum residues, where vapour trapping in a closed cup would give unreliable results.

#### (b) [7 marks] With the help of suitable equations, discuss the relationship between flash point, vapour pressure, and lower flammable limit.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flash Point Condition (1 mark):** By definition, at the flash point the equilibrium vapour pressure of the liquid equals the partial pressure corresponding to the LFL concentration in air:

$$P_{vap}(T_{FP}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{total}$$

2. **Clausius-Clapeyron Relation (2 marks):** The vapour pressure as a function of temperature is given by:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{vap}(T_{FP})}{P_{vap}(T_b)}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{FP}} - \frac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

3. **Combination (2 marks):** Combining the two gives an explicit expression for $T_{FP}$:

$$T_{FP} = \left[\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_{vap}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{y_{LFL}}\right)\right]^{-1}$$

4. **Interpretation (1 mark):** A liquid with a **lower LFL** (more easily ignited) will have a **lower flash point**; a liquid with **higher enthalpy of vaporisation** (harder to vaporise) will have a **higher flash point**.

5. **Practical Engineering Use (1 mark):** This relationship is used in process safety to estimate flash points of pure components and mixtures from their thermophysical data, and to rank flammability hazards. For multi-component mixtures, **modified Raoult's law** $P_i = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ replaces the pure-component vapour pressure, and the flash-point condition becomes:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i \cdot P_{total}}{\gamma_i} = P_i^{sat}(T_{FP}) \cdot x_i$$

6. **Example (optional, 0 mark, for context):** Acetone ($T_b = 329$ K, $LFL = 2.6\%$, $\Delta H_{vap} = 29{,}100$ J/mol) yields a predicted closed-cup flash point of ≈ $-18\,°\text{C}$, matching the experimental value of $-20\,°\text{C}$ to within $2\,°\text{C}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Flash Point with Fire Point (loses 1–2 marks):** State the distinction explicitly. Fire point ≥ Flash point, always.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Clausius-Clapeyron:** Always use **Kelvin** for $T$ and **J/mol** for $\Delta H_{vap}$. Mixing °C and K loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Naming the test standard but not the apparatus geometry:** "Closed cup" alone is not enough — mention **Pensky-Martens, Tag, or Abel** specifically.
> 4. **Skipping atmospheric pressure correction:** Examiners award **1 mark** for the correction formula $T_{corr} = T_{obs} + 0.25(101.325 - P_{actual})$.
> 5. **Writing Raoult's Law without the activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$ for non-ideal mixtures loses 1 mark in advanced questions.
> 6. **Using $|x|$ (vertical bar) in equations inside markdown tables** breaks the table — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. *(This is also a system-level formatting pitfall.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — Bullet-Form Key Points:**

- ✅ **Flash Point** = lowest $T$ at which liquid gives off enough vapour to flash, but does NOT sustain burning.
- ✅ **Fire Point** = lowest $T$ at which vapour sustains combustion ≥ 5 s. Always **higher than FP**.
- ✅ **CCFP < OCFP** (closed cup gives lower, more conservative value).
- ✅ **ASTM D93 (PMCC)** is the industry standard for CCFP; **ASTM D92 (COC)** is the standard for OCFP; **ASTM D56 (TCC)** for low-viscosity liquids.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $P_{vap}(T_{FP}) = y_{LFL} \cdot P_{atm}$ (ideal pure-component condition).
- ✅ **Clausius-Clapeyron** governs the $P_{vap}$ vs $T$ relation.
- ✅ **Antoine Equation** is the empirical engineering form: $\log_{10} P = A - B/(T+C)$.
- ✅ **Trouton's Rule** gives $\Delta H_{vap} \approx 10.5 \, R \, T_b$.
- ✅ **Hazardous Area Classes (NEC 500):** Class I ($T_{FP}<37.8\,°\text{C}$), Class II (37.8–93.3 °C), Class III (≥93.3 °C).
- ✅ **Factors:** chemical structure, vapour pressure curve, test method, pressure, mixture composition, stirring, ignition energy.
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Storage classification, transport packing groups, OSHA hazardous area classification, insurance, HAZOP.
- ✅ **Numericals:** Always convert °C → K before applying Clausius-Clapeyron. Always apply pressure correction to flash-point observations.
- ✅ **Mixture Flash Point:** The most volatile component dominates; use modified Raoult's law with activity coefficients for non-ideal mixtures.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Measures of Flammability: Flash Point**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRE POINT** is defined as the lowest temperature of a liquid (corrected to a standard barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa) at which the vapors produced from the liquid, when ignited by a pilot flame, will sustain combustion as a continuous burning flame on the surface of the liquid for a minimum duration of **5 seconds** (per ASTM D92) or **5 seconds to 30 seconds** depending on the testing standard (e.g., ISO 2592 specifies ignition that propagates over the liquid surface after removal of the test flame).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Fire Engineering (OECHT835), the **Fire Point** is classified under the combustion properties of flammable liquids and is a critical parameter in fire hazard classification, storage regulations, and process safety design.

### Key Characteristics at the Fire Point:
- The vapor-air mixture above the liquid is within its **flammability range**.
- The rate of vapor generation is sufficient to sustain a self-propagating flame.
- The heat liberated by the flame is adequate to vaporize more liquid fuel (positive feedback loop established).
- Combustion continues even when the ignition source is removed.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Fire Point like a campfire that "catches hold" on its own.**

Imagine you are heating a pan of cooking oil on a stove:
1. At low temperatures, nothing happens — there are very few oil molecules escaping as vapor.
2. As the temperature rises, you begin to see a faint blue haze and perhaps a momentary flicker if you hold a match above the pan — this is near the **Flash Point**.
3. If you continue heating, suddenly a sustained flame appears on the surface and continues to burn even after you remove the match — **this temperature is the Fire Point**.

The **Fire Point** represents the threshold where the liquid has crossed from merely *producing ignitable vapors* to *actively sustaining its own combustion cycle*. The flame now provides enough heat energy back to the liquid surface to keep producing more vapor, establishing a stable combustion equilibrium.

This is why the **Fire Point is ALWAYS higher than the Flash Point** — extra energy is needed to not only ignite the vapors but to maintain the combustion reaction independently.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Reference |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Standard barometric pressure | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** | ASTM D92 |
| Minimum sustained burn duration | **5 seconds** | ASTM D92 |
| Typical ΔT (Fire Point − Flash Point) | **5 °C to 40 °C** | Empirical |
| Pilot flame size | **3.2 mm to 4.8 mm diameter** | ASTM D92 |
| Heating rate | **5 °C/min to 6 °C/min** near endpoint | ASTM D92 |

---

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fire Point is a **KOSHA, IS 1448, and NFPA recognized** critical parameter for:
> - Determining the **fire hazard classification** of liquids (Class I, II, IIIA per NFPA 30).
> - Designing **fire suppression systems** in storage tanks.
> - Specifying **maximum allowable temperatures** in chemical processing equipment.
> - Insurance underwriting and **dangerous goods transportation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Fire Point Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The transition from a non-burning state to a sustained flame at the fire point involves a thermal and kinetic chain of events:

1. **Vapor Pressure Threshold Reached:**
   The liquid temperature rises until its vapor pressure becomes sufficiently high that the vapor-air mixture above the liquid surface lies within the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** boundaries.

2. **Pilot Flame Application:**
   A small external flame is introduced at the liquid surface, providing activation energy ($E_a$) to initiate combustion of the vapor-air mixture.

3. **Combustion Heat Release:**
   The vapor ignites and releases the **Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$)** per unit mass of fuel.

4. **Heat Feedback Equilibrium:**
   A portion of the released heat is transferred back to the liquid surface via **conduction, convection, and radiation**. At the fire point, this returned heat flux equals or exceeds the heat required to maintain vaporization.

5. **Sustained Combustion:**
   A self-sustaining cycle is established: liquid $\rightarrow$ vapor $\rightarrow$ combustion $\rightarrow$ heat $\rightarrow$ more vapor. This is the defining criterion that distinguishes the **fire point** from the **flash point**.

---

## 2.2 Relationship Between Flash Point and Fire Point

> [!NOTE]
> **Flash Point (FP):** The lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor in sufficient quantity to form an **ignitable** mixture with air near the surface, producing a brief flash when an ignition source is applied. Combustion is **transient** (< 1 s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Point (FIP):** The lowest temperature at which the vapor-air mixture continues to burn in a **self-sustained** manner for at least 5 seconds after the ignition source is removed.

### Comparative Difference Table

| Parameter | Flash Point | Fire Point |
|-----------|-------------|------------|
| Flame duration | < 1 second (transient flash) | $\geq$ **5 seconds** (sustained) |
| Vapor concentration | Just enters flammability range | Well within flammability range |
| Heat feedback to liquid | Insufficient | Sufficient for self-sustaining vaporization |
| Temperature value | Always **lower** | Always **higher** (by 5 °C–40 °C) |
| Flame appearance | Brief blue flicker | Continuous, stable flame |
| Test outcome indicator | "Flash" but no continuation | Persistent flame on liquid surface |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Fire Point Calculations and Concepts**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Clausius-Clapeyron Vapor Pressure | $\ln(P) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + C$ | Relates vapor pressure to temperature |
| 2 | Antoine Equation | $\log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Empirical vapor pressure (T in °C, P in mmHg) |
| 3 | Clausius-Clapeyron Integrated | $\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Two-state vapor pressure ratio |
| 4 | Fire Point – Flash Point Relation | $T_{FIP} \approx T_{FP} + \Delta T$ | $\Delta T \in [5, 40]$ °C empirically |
| 5 | Heat Feedback Condition | $q_{feedback} \geq q_{vaporization} + q_{losses}$ | Fire point criterion |
| 6 | Vaporization Heat | $q_{vap} = m \cdot L_v$ | Where $L_v$ is latent heat of vaporization |
| 7 | Flammability Range | $LFL \leq \phi \leq UFL$ | $\phi$ = fuel-air equivalence ratio |
| 8 | Boiling Point–Fire Point | $T_{FIP} < T_{BP}$ always | Fire point is below boiling point |

> **Notation used:** $P$ = vapor pressure (Pa), $T$ = temperature (K), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $\Delta H_{vap}$ = enthalpy of vaporization (J/mol), $L_v$ = latent heat (J/kg), $\phi$ = equivalence ratio.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Fire Point |
|-------|---------------------------|
| **Oil & Gas Industry** | Classification of crude oils, diesel, kerosene for transport safety |
| **Chemical Manufacturing** | Reactor design, storage tank insulation, vent sizing |
| **Fire Protection Engineering** | Selection of foam agents, sprinkler system temperature ratings |
| **Insurance Sector** | Premium calculation based on stored flammable liquid hazard class |
| **Aviation / Defense** | Fuel handling protocols, JP-8 and JP-5 jet fuel safety |
| **Civil Engineering** | Safety codes for buildings storing flammable materials |

---

## 2.5 Test Methods for Fire Point Determination

| Standard | Test Method | Apparatus |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| **ASTM D92** | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | Open cup, open air above liquid |
| **ASTM D93** | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) | Closed cup with stirrer |
| **ISO 2592** | Cleveland Open Cup (international) | Same as ASTM D92 |
| **IS 1448 (P:21)** | Pensky-Martens (Indian Standard) | Closed cup |
| **DIN 51376** | German standard | Cleveland Open Cup |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Fire point is typically measured **after** the flash point in the same test run. The same apparatus is used to continue heating until sustained combustion is achieved.

---

## 2.6 Fire Points of Common Liquids (Reference Data)

| Liquid | Flash Point (°C) | Fire Point (°C) | $\Delta T$ (°C) |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Gasoline | −43 | −38 | **5** |
| Diesel | 52 | 75 | **23** |
| Kerosene | 38 | 72 | **34** |
| Ethanol | 13 | 18 | **5** |
| Methanol | 11 | 16 | **5** |
| Lubricating Oil | 200 | 240 | **40** |
| Acetone | −20 | −15 | **5** |
| Linseed Oil | 222 | 263 | **41** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Feedback Condition for Fire Point

The **Fire Point** can be analytically derived from the **heat balance at the liquid-vapor interface** at the critical (fire point) temperature.

### Step 1: Define the System

Consider a unit area ($A = 1 \, m^2$) of liquid surface at temperature $T_{FIP}$ (fire point temperature in Kelvin). A self-sustaining flame exists above this surface.

### Step 2: Heat Generated by Combustion

The mass flow rate of vapor from unit area of the liquid is governed by the **Hertz-Knudsen equation** (simplified):

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_{vap}(T_{FIP})}{\sqrt{2\pi M R T_{FIP}}}$$

where:
- $P_{vap}(T_{FIP})$ = vapor pressure at fire point (Pa)
- $M$ = molar mass of liquid (kg/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- $T_{FIP}$ = fire point temperature (K)

### Step 3: Heat Released by Combustion

The combustion heat release rate per unit area is:

$$q_{comb} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$$

where $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion per kg of vapor (J/kg).

### Step 4: Heat Required for Vaporization

The heat required to sustain vaporization is:

$$q_{vap} = \dot{m} \cdot L_v$$

where $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg).

### Step 5: Heat Loss to Environment

Heat is lost from the flame and liquid surface to the surroundings:

$$q_{loss} = h_c (T_{FIP} - T_{\infty}) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_{FIP}^4 - T_{\infty}^4)$$

where:
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of flame
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)
- $T_{\infty}$ = ambient temperature (K)

### Step 6: Fire Point Criterion (Self-Sustaining Condition)

For the flame to be self-sustaining, the energy balance must be satisfied:

$$q_{comb} = q_{vap} + q_{loss}$$

$$\dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{m} \cdot L_v + h_c (T_{FIP} - T_{\infty}) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_{FIP}^4 - T_{\infty}^4)$$

Simplifying for the minimum $T_{FIP}$ where this is satisfied:

$$\dot{m} (\Delta H_c - L_v) = h_c (T_{FIP} - T_{\infty}) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_{FIP}^4 - T_{\infty}^4)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The left side represents the *net useful heat* from combustion (after vaporization cost), while the right side represents all *losses* to the environment. The **Fire Point** is the lowest $T_{FIP}$ where useful heat ≥ losses.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Using Clausius-Clapeyron to Estimate Fire Point

**Problem:** A liquid fuel has a flash point of 45 °C and a latent heat of vaporization $\Delta H_{vap} = 38,000$ J/mol. The atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Estimate the vapor pressure at the flash point, and discuss the expected fire point range.

### Step 1: Convert Flash Point to Kelvin

$$T_{FP} = 45 + 273.15 = 318.15 \, K$$

### Step 2: Assume Vapor Pressure at Flash Point equals LFL Pressure

For most hydrocarbons, the **flash point** corresponds to the temperature at which the equilibrium vapor pressure equals the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** partial pressure. For typical hydrocarbons:

$$P_{FP} \approx 0.01 \, P_{atm} = 0.01 \times 101,325 = 1013.25 \, Pa$$

### Step 3: Apply Clausius-Clapeyron to Find Vapor Pressure at Fire Point

We need $T_{FIP}$. Empirical relation: $T_{FIP} \approx T_{FP} + 25$ °C for medium-flammability liquids.

$$T_{FIP} \approx 70 + 273.15 = 343.15 \, K$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{FIP}}{P_{FP}}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{FIP}}\right)$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{FP}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{FIP}} = \dfrac{1}{318.15} - \dfrac{1}{343.15}$$

$$= 3.143 \times 10^{-3} - 2.914 \times 10^{-3} = 2.29 \times 10^{-4} \, K^{-1}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Values

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{FIP}}{1013.25}\right) = \dfrac{38000}{8.314} \times (2.29 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{FIP}}{1013.25}\right) = 4571.6 \times 2.29 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0469$$

### Step 5: Solve for $P_{FIP}$

$$\dfrac{P_{FIP}}{1013.25} = e^{1.0469} = 2.849$$

$$P_{FIP} = 1013.25 \times 2.849 = 2886.8 \, Pa$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** At the fire point, the vapor pressure is approximately **2.85 times** the flash point vapor pressure. This represents the **higher vapor generation rate** needed to sustain combustion rather than just a momentary flash.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Critical Exam Numbers

| Concept | Numerical Value / Range | KTU Significance |
|---------|------------------------|------------------|
| ΔT (FIP − FP) for volatile fuels | 5–10 °C | Memorize range |
| ΔT (FIP − FP) for heavy oils | 30–40 °C | Memorize range |
| Vapor pressure at FIP | 2× to 3× P at FP | Key calculation |
| Minimum sustained burn | ≥ **5 s** | ASTM D92 standard |
| Heating rate at endpoint | 5–6 °C/min | Test protocol |
| Pilot flame size | 3.2–4.8 mm | Test protocol |
