## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Zabetakis Temperature Correlation — Logic Walkthrough

The Zabetakis correlation is **empirical** (fitted to data from Bulletin 627, U.S. Bureau of Mines), but it has a clear physical basis. Let us derive its form from first principles of flame propagation, then show the empirical fit.

---

#### **Step 1: Adiabatic Flame Temperature as the Governing Parameter**

For a flame to propagate, the temperature of the burned gas ($T_b$) must be high enough to ignite the unburned gas. The minimum fuel concentration (LFL) corresponds to a situation where the heat released by the small amount of fuel is just sufficient to raise the unburnt gas to its **quenching temperature** ($T_q$).

**Energy balance at the LFL flame front:**

$$m_{fuel} \cdot (-\Delta H_{c}) \;=\; m_{unburnt} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{q} - T_{u})$$

where $T_u$ is the **unburnt gas temperature** (initial temperature). At LFL, the ratio $m_{fuel}/m_{unburnt}$ is small.

---

#### **Step 2: Express $m_{fuel}/m_{unburnt}$ in terms of LFL**

For an ideal-gas mixture of fuel in air:

$$\frac{m_{fuel}}{m_{unburnt}} \;=\; \frac{y_{fuel} \cdot M_{fuel}}{M_{mix}} \;\approx\; y_{fuel} \cdot \frac{M_{fuel}}{M_{air}}$$

where $y_{fuel}$ is the mole fraction of fuel (≈ LFL/100).

Substituting:

$$LFL \cdot \frac{M_{fuel}}{100 \cdot M_{air}} \cdot (-\Delta H_{c}) \;=\; c_{p} \cdot (T_{q} - T_{u})$$

---

#### **Step 3: Solve for LFL as a function of $T_u$**

$$LFL \;=\; \frac{100 \cdot M_{air} \cdot c_{p}}{M_{fuel} \cdot (-\Delta H_{c})} \cdot (T_{q} - T_{u})$$

This is the **fundamental linear relationship** between LFL and $T_u$. As $T_u$ increases, the term $(T_q - T_u)$ decreases, so LFL decreases linearly.

---

#### **Step 4: Evaluate at the Standard Reference Condition (25 °C)**

$$LFL_{25} \;=\; \frac{100 \cdot M_{air} \cdot c_{p}}{M_{fuel} \cdot (-\Delta H_{c})} \cdot (T_{q} - 25)$$

---

#### **Step 5: Form the Ratio $LFL_T / LFL_{25}$**

$$\frac{LFL_{T}}{LFL_{25}} \;=\; \frac{T_{q} - T}{T_{q} - 25}$$

---

#### **Step 6: Linearize about $T = 25$ °C**

Use the first-order Taylor expansion: $\frac{T_q - T}{T_q - 25} = 1 - \frac{T - 25}{T_q - 25}$.

Empirically, $T_q - 25 \approx \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{\Delta H_c}{1}$ (in proper units), so:

$$\frac{T - 25}{T_q - 25} \;\approx\; 0.75 \cdot \frac{T - 25}{\Delta H_{c}}$$

This recovers the Zabetakis form:

$$\boxed{\,LFL_{T} \;=\; LFL_{25} \cdot \left[\, 1 \;-\; 0.75 \, \frac{T - 25}{\Delta H_{c}} \,\right]\,}$$

**UFL follows the mirror logic** — more fuel is needed at higher T, but the slope is opposite:

$$\boxed{\,UFL_{T} \;=\; UFL_{25} \cdot \left[\, 1 \;+\; 0.75 \, \frac{T - 25}{\Delta H_{c}} \,\right]\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) has the following properties at 25 °C, 1 atm:
> $LFL_{25} = 5.0\%$, $UFL_{25} = 15.0\%$, heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 191.8\;\text{kcal/mol}$.
> A natural gas pipeline operates at $T = 200\;°\text{C}$ and $P = 5\;\text{atm}$.
> Find the corrected LFL and UFL at these conditions.

---

#### **Part (a) — Temperature Correction**

Apply the Zabetakis formula:

$$LFL_{T} \;=\; 5.0 \cdot \left[\, 1 \;-\; 0.75 \cdot \frac{200 - 25}{191.8} \,\right]$$

Compute the temperature-correction term:

$$\frac{T - 25}{\Delta H_{c}} \;=\; \frac{200 - 25}{191.8} \;=\; \frac{175}{191.8} \;\approx\; 0.9125$$

Multiply by 0.75:

$$0.75 \cdot 0.9125 \;\approx\; 0.6844$$

Apply:

$$LFL_{T} \;=\; 5.0 \cdot (1 - 0.6844) \;=\; 5.0 \cdot 0.3156 \;\approx\; 1.58\%$$

$$UFL_{T} \;=\; 15.0 \cdot (1 + 0.6844) \;=\; 15.0 \cdot 1.6844 \;\approx\; 25.27\%$$

> **Interpretation:** The flammable range at 200 °C has *widened* from 10% to ≈23.7%. This dramatically increases explosion hazard.

---

#### **Part (b) — Pressure Correction**

Apply the Zabetakis pressure correction. $P - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4\;\text{atm}$:

$$LFL_{P} \;=\; LFL_{1\,atm} \cdot (1 - 0.35 \cdot 4) \;=\; 5.0 \cdot (1 - 1.4) \;=\; -2.0\%$$

A negative value is **physically meaningless** — it signals that the linear correlation has broken down. The empirical Zabetakis pressure correction is only valid for $|P - 1| \lesssim 3\;\text{atm}$. For $P = 5\;\text{atm}$, we should use the more detailed correlation (e.g., by G.F.P. Harris or data from NFPA 68 tables) or accept that LFL is approximately **stable** (≈ 4.0–5.0%).

For UFL:

$$UFL_{P} \;=\; 15.0 \cdot (1 + 0.65 \cdot 4) \;=\; 15.0 \cdot 3.6 \;=\; 54.0\%$$

This is again outside the linear range; actual UFL of methane at 5 atm is reported as ≈ 35–40%. The trend (UFL rising sharply) is correct, but the linear correlation overestimates.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Linear Correlation Validity:** Students often blindly apply Zabetakis pressure correlations at very high or very low pressures. Always state the **validity range** of the correlation. For $|P - 1| > 3\;\text{atm}$, prefer chart-based data (NFPA 68) or higher-order correlations.

---

### 3.3 Combined T & P Effects (Multiplicative Approach)

In practice, T and P effects are **multiplicatively superimposed** for moderate deviations:

$$LFL_{T,P} \;\approx\; LFL_{25,\,1\,atm} \cdot f_T(T) \cdot f_P(P)$$

$$UFL_{T,P} \;\approx\; UFL_{25,\,1\,atm} \cdot g_T(T) \cdot g_P(P)$$

For the methane example at $T = 200\;\text{°C}$, $P = 5\;\text{atm}$:

$$LFL \;\approx\; 5.0 \cdot 0.3156 \cdot 0.80 \;\approx\; 1.26\%$$

$$UFL \;\approx\; 15.0 \cdot 1.6844 \cdot 1.40 \;\approx\; 35.37\%$$

(The 0.80 and 1.40 are conservative $f_P$ and $g_P$ factors for $P = 5\;\text{atm}$ from NFPA 68 charts.)

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flammability Envelope Shift with Temperature (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Standard Conditions: T = 25 C, P = 1 atm"] --> B["LFL_ref and UFL_ref known from tables"]
    B --> C["Process Conditions: T higher? P higher?"]
    C --> D{"Temperature Increased?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["LFL decreases, UFL increases"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Temperature unchanged"]
    C --> G{"Pressure Increased?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["UFL increases sharply, LFL nearly constant"]
    G -- "No" --> I["Pressure unchanged"]
    E --> J["Apply Zabetakis T Correction"]
    H --> K["Apply Zabetakis P Correction"]
    F --> J
    I --> K
    J --> L["Combine using multiplicative superposition"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["Final Corrected Flammability Envelope"]
    M --> N["Use for NFPA 68 vent sizing and NFPA 69 inerting design"]
```

### 4.2 Flammability Diagram — Effect of Temperature (Schematic Plot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Lean Region below LFL"] -->|Increasing Fuel %| B["Flammable Region"]
    B -->|Increasing Fuel %| C["Rich Region above UFL"]
    subgraph "LFL boundary shifts"
    L1["LFL at 25 C = 5.0 %"] -.- L2["LFL at 200 C = 1.58 %"]
    end
    subgraph "UFL boundary shifts"
    U1["UFL at 25 C = 15.0 %"] -.- U2["UFL at 200 C = 25.27 %"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid graphs above provide a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of how the temperature and pressure shifts occur in flammability envelopes. As visualised, on heating, the flammable region widens (LFL moves left, UFL moves right). On pressurising, primarily UFL moves right (richer mixtures become flammable), while LFL is nearly stationary for most hydrocarbons.

### 4.3 Effect of Reduced Pressure — Limiting Oxygen / Limiting Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sea Level P = 1 atm"] --> B["Flammable Range = Normal"]
    C["High Altitude P = 0.6 atm"] --> D["Narrower Range, LFL rises"]
    E["Very Low P less than 0.3 atm"] --> F["No propagation - below minimum P"]
    G["Very High P greater than 10 atm"] --> H["UFL rises sharply - hazard increases"]
```

> **Key Engineering Insight:** In aerospace and high-altitude applications, *depressurisation* can be a **safety mechanism** (limits narrow and vanish). Conversely, in deep-sea oil and gas operations, *high pressure* dramatically widens the flammable envelope, especially on the UFL side.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, OECHT835]**
*Define Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) and Upper Flammability Limit (UFL). How does an increase in temperature affect the flammability range of a hydrocarbon fuel?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **LFL (1 mark):** The minimum concentration of fuel vapour in air, below which propagation of flame cannot occur upon ignition. Also called Lower Explosive Limit (LEL). Conventionally measured at 25 °C and 1 atm.
- **UFL (1 mark):** The maximum concentration of fuel vapour in air, above which propagation of flame cannot occur. Also called Upper Explosive Limit (UEL).
- **Effect of temperature (1 mark):** As temperature increases, the LFL decreases and the UFL increases. The flammability range ($UFL - LFL$) widens. This is because the initial gas temperature is closer to the auto-ignition temperature, requiring less additional heat for flame propagation. The Zabetakis correlation $LFL_T = LFL_{25} \cdot [1 - 0.75(T-25)/\Delta H_c]$ quantifies this.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 – OECHT835]**
*State the effect of pressure on the flammability limits of a typical hydrocarbon. What is meant by the "lower pressure limit" of flammability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Effect on LFL (1 mark):** The LFL is relatively insensitive to pressure for most hydrocarbons, decreasing only slightly as pressure increases.
- **Effect on UFL (1 mark):** The UFL increases significantly with increasing pressure, as the higher molecular density promotes flame propagation in richer mixtures.
- **Lower pressure limit (1 mark):** The minimum pressure below which flame propagation cannot occur for *any* fuel-air composition. For most hydrocarbons, this lies between 0.2 and 0.4 atm. At these low pressures, the mean free path is too large and collisions too infrequent to sustain the chain-branching reactions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Temperature Dependence Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, OECHT835, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how temperature affects the LFL and UFL of a flammable gas. Derive the Zabetakis empirical correlation for temperature correction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Methane has $LFL_{25} = 5.0\%$, $UFL_{25} = 15.0\%$, and $\Delta H_c = 191.8\;\text{kcal/mol}$. A storage tank contains methane at $T = 150\;\text{°C}$. Calculate the corrected LFL and UFL. Comment on the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Sketch and qualitative description (2 marks):**
   Draw a flammability envelope with fuel concentration (x-axis) vs. temperature (y-axis). Show the LFL curve sloping downward and the UFL curve sloping upward as $T$ increases. Mark the standard reference point (25 °C) and a higher temperature ($T_2$). Indicate that the envelope widens.

2. **Physical reasoning (2 marks):**
   - At higher initial temperature, the unburnt gas is closer to the quenching/auto-ignition temperature.
   - Smaller fuel concentration suffices to release enough heat to sustain propagation → **LFL drops**.
   - In rich mixtures, higher initial temperature compensates for the heat-sink effect of excess unburnt fuel → **UFL rises**.

3. **Derivation of Zabetakis correlation (3 marks):**
   Starting from the energy balance at the flame front:
   $$\text{Fuel fraction} \times (-\Delta H_c) = c_p \cdot (T_q - T_u)$$
   leading to
   $$LFL = K \cdot (T_q - T_u)$$
   Forming the ratio $LFL_T/LFL_{25}$ and linearising gives:
   $$\frac{LFL_T}{LFL_{25}} = 1 - 0.75 \cdot \frac{T - 25}{\Delta H_c}$$

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Apply the Zabetakis formula (4 marks):**

$$\frac{T - 25}{\Delta H_c} = \frac{150 - 25}{191.8} = \frac{125}{191.8} \approx 0.6517$$

$$LFL_{150} = 5.0 \cdot (1 - 0.75 \cdot 0.6517) = 5.0 \cdot (1 - 0.4888) = 5.0 \cdot 0.5112 \approx 2.56\%$$

$$UFL_{150} = 15.0 \cdot (1 + 0.75 \cdot 0.6517) = 15.0 \cdot 1.4888 \approx 22.33\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate the new flammability range (1 mark):**
$$\Delta F = 22.33 - 2.56 = 19.77\% \quad \text{(vs. 10\% at 25 °C)}$$

**Step 3: Safety implications (2 marks):**
- The flammable range has nearly *doubled* (from 10% to 19.77%).
- A leak of methane at 150 °C has a far higher probability of forming an explosive atmosphere in air.
- Vent sizing per NFPA 68 must be based on these corrected values; using 25 °C limits would under-size vents.
- Recommend continuous flammable-gas detection, inert gas blanketing, and explosion-proof electrical fittings (flameproof enclosures, intrinsic safety) for the storage tank.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **No units shown in calculation (–1 mark):** Always state $T$ in °C and $\Delta H_c$ in kcal/mol *before* substituting. Writing $\frac{125}{191.8}$ without context will lose a mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the correction to *both* LFL and UFL (–1 mark):** Many students compute only the LFL. UFL is equally important for safety.
> 3. **Vague safety commentary (–1 mark):** "It is dangerous" is not enough. Mention NFPA 68, NFPA 69, detection, and inerting specifically.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Pressure Dependence Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, OECHT835, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the effect of pressure on the flammability limits of a gaseous fuel. How does the UFL change with pressure, and what is the physical reason? Also define the "lower pressure limit." **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A propane storage sphere is rated for 8 atm working pressure at 30 °C. Propane has $LFL_{1\,atm} = 2.1\%$, $UFL_{1\,atm} = 9.5\%$, and $\Delta H_c = 530.6\;\text{kcal/mol}$. Estimate the *worst-case* (most hazardous) flammability envelope inside the sphere assuming a leak to ambient. Also comment on the validity of the linear Zabetakis correlation at $P = 8\;\text{atm}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Effect on LFL (2 marks):** The LFL is *relatively insensitive* to pressure for most hydrocarbons. As pressure increases, LFL decreases only slightly. Empirically:
   $$LFL_P = LFL_{1\,atm} \cdot [1 - 0.35(P-1)] \quad \text{(valid } P \in [0.5, 5]\;\text{atm)}$$

2. **Effect on UFL (3 marks):** The UFL increases *significantly* with pressure. Empirically:
   $$UFL_P = UFL_{1\,atm} \cdot [1 + 0.65(P-1)]$$
   **Physical reason:** Higher pressure → higher molecular density → more frequent bimolecular collisions → faster chain-branching reactions → even rich mixtures (which at 1 atm would be fuel-saturated and self-quenching) can now propagate. The fuel-rich limit is set by oxygen availability; at higher P, more oxygen is dissolved per unit volume, lifting UFL.

3. **Lower pressure limit (2 marks):** Below a critical pressure (typically 0.2–0.4 atm for hydrocarbons, ≈ 0.05 atm for hydrogen), the molecular mean free path is too long and collision frequency too low to sustain chain-branching. No fuel-air composition will propagate flame. This is exploited in some aerospace applications using reduced-pressure chambers to suppress fire.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Temperature correction at 30 °C (2 marks):**
$$\frac{30 - 25}{\Delta H_c} = \frac{5}{530.6} \approx 0.00943$$
$$LFL_{30} \approx 2.1 \cdot (1 - 0.75 \cdot 0.00943) \approx 2.1 \cdot 0.9929 \approx 2.085\% \approx 2.1\%$$
$$UFL_{30} \approx 9.5 \cdot (1 + 0.75 \cdot 0.00943) \approx 9.5 \cdot 1.00707 \approx 9.57\%$$

So temperature correction at 30 °C is negligible.

**Step 2: Pressure correction attempt (3 marks):**
At $P = 8\;\text{atm}$:
$$LFL_{8\,atm} = 2.1 \cdot [1 - 0.35(8-1)] = 2.1 \cdot [1 - 2.45] = 2.1 \cdot (-1.45) = -3.05\%$$

A negative LFL is physically impossible. This is the **first signal of correlation breakdown**.

For UFL:
$$UFL_{8\,atm} = 9.5 \cdot [1 + 0.65(8-1)] = 9.5 \cdot [1 + 4.55] = 9.5 \cdot 5.55 = 52.7\%$$

This is *much higher* than the actual UFL of propane at 8 atm (reported as ≈ 28–32% in NFPA 68 tables). The linear Zabetakis correlation **over-estimates** UFL at high pressure.

**Step 3: Validity comment (2 marks):**
The Zabetakis pressure correction is only valid for $P \in [0.5, 5]\;\text{atm}$. For $P = 8\;\text{atm}$, the correlation diverges from real data. The correct approach is to:
- Use **NFPA 68 Annex G** or **Coward-Jones** chart-based values.
- Or apply a **logarithmic** correction: $\ln(UFL) = a + b \cdot \ln(P)$.

**Worst-case envelope (with chart-based values for propane at 8 atm, 30 °C):**
$$LFL \approx 2.0\% \quad ; \quad UFL \approx 30\%$$
$$\Delta F \approx 28\% \quad \text{(vs. 7.4\% at 1 atm — a } 4\times \text{widening)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Blindly applying Zabetakis at 8 atm without flagging its invalidity (–2 marks):** The examiner *deliberately* chose 8 atm to test whether students recognise correlation limits. Always state the validity range of any empirical correlation.
> 2. **No "worst-case" framing (–1 mark):** A safety-oriented question expects a clear statement of *which* limit is the binding concern. For propane, the LFL is the dangerous one (it is low and remains low even at 8 atm).
> 3. **Missing lower pressure limit discussion (–1 mark):** Part (a) explicitly asked for it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Facts for KTU Board Exams:**

- [x] **LFL/UFL** are conventionally defined at **25 °C, 1 atm, upward propagation in a 5 cm tube**.
- [x] **Temperature up → LFL down, UFL up → range widens.** Linear Zabetakis: $LFL_T = LFL_{25} \cdot [1 - 0.75(T-25)/\Delta H_c]$.
- [x] **Pressure up → UFL up sharply, LFL nearly constant.** Zabetakis: $UFL_P = UFL_{1\,atm} \cdot [1 + 0.65(P-1)]$.
- [x] **Validity of Zabetakis pressure correction:** $P \in [0.5, 5]\;\text{atm}$. Outside this range, use chart data (NFPA 68 Annex G).
- [x] **Lower Pressure Limit** for most hydrocarbons: **0.2 – 0.4 atm**. No flame propagation below this.
- [x] **Hydrogen** is a special case: very wide flammable range (4–75% in air at 1 atm) and *minimum ignition energy* of 0.02 mJ (vs. 0.25 mJ for methane).
- [x] **Stoichiometric concentration** $C_{st}$ usually lies closer to LFL than UFL for hydrocarbons.
- [x] **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)** is used in inerting design; typically 5–12% O₂ depending on fuel.
- [x] **Le Chatelier's Rule** is for *mixtures of fuels*; it does *not* correct for T or P.
- [x] **Units consistency:** $T$ in °C, $\Delta H_c$ in kcal/mol, $P$ in atm for Zabetakis. Convert before substituting.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** NFPA 68 vent sizing, NFPA 69 inerting, gas detection thresholds, hazardous area classification (IEC 60079).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mantra for the KTU Exam:** *"Heat widens the envelope; pressure lifts the ceiling."* — i.e., Temperature broadens both LFL and UFL outwards; Pressure mostly stretches UFL upwards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Flammability Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating Flammability Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Fire Engineering and Combustion Science, **Flammability Limits** are defined as the boundary concentrations of a combustible substance (fuel) in an oxidizer (typically air) beyond which self-propagation of a flame cannot occur under specified test conditions. These limits define the *envelope of ignitability* for any fuel-oxidizer system.

The two fundamental limits are:

- **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** — also called the *Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)*: the **minimum** volumetric concentration of fuel in air below which the mixture is too *lean* to sustain combustion. Below LFL, the energy released by the limited fuel cannot heat the unburnt gases to the flame temperature required for propagation.

- **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** — also called the *Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)*: the **maximum** volumetric concentration of fuel in air above which the mixture is too *rich* to sustain combustion. Above UFL, insufficient oxygen is available to complete the oxidation chain.

The region **between** LFL and UFL is the **Flammable Range** (or Explosive Range).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The LFL and UFL values are *experimentally determined* in standard test apparatus (e.g., the **ASTM E681** tube, **CEN 14034** tube, or **NFPA 68/69** referenced methods), and are reported at standard conditions of **25 °C** and **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **campfire**: it will not ignite if the wood is too sparse (too little fuel, like being *below* the LFL) and it will smother and go out if you pile on too much green wood without enough air (too much fuel, like being *above* the UFL). The fire only sustains itself when the proportion of wood-to-air lies in a sweet middle zone.

Another analogy: think of a **popcorn maker**. The popcorn (fuel) needs both the heating element (oxidizer) and the kernels. Too few kernels → nothing happens (sub-LFL). Too many kernels → they jam and burn unevenly (super-UFL). Just the right amount → perfect popping (within flammable range).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Role of Pressure and Temperature:** Increasing **pressure** typically *widens* the flammable range (LFL decreases slightly, UFL increases significantly). Increasing **temperature** also widens the range, lowering LFL appreciably. These are key considerations in KTU problems involving *non-standard conditions*.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics

The following constants and metrics are central to the topic:

- **Stoichiometric Concentration (C_st)**: the fuel–air ratio at which all fuel and oxidizer are consumed in the ideal chemical reaction. Expressed as **vol % of fuel in air**.

- **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)**: the minimum oxygen concentration (typically in mol % or vol %) below which combustion of any fuel-air mixture is impossible, regardless of fuel concentration. For most hydrocarbons, LOC lies around **8–12 vol % O₂**.

- **Burning Velocity (S_u)**: the laminar flame speed (m/s) at which the flame front propagates through a quiescent fuel–air mixture. S_u peaks near stoichiometric composition.

- **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (T_ad)**: the maximum theoretical temperature reached assuming no heat loss.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Stoichiometric Combustion of a Hydrocarbon

For a general **C–H–O** compound of empirical formula **C_x H_y O_z**, complete combustion in air proceeds as:

$$C_x H_y O_z + \nu_{O_2} \left( O_2 + 3.76\, N_2 \right) \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2O + 3.76\,\nu_{O_2}\, N_2$$

The stoichiometric oxygen requirement per mole of fuel is:

$$\nu_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$

The total moles of combustion products + inerts per mole of fuel is:

$$n_{total} = x + \frac{y}{2} + 3.76\left( x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} \right)$$

The **stoichiometric fuel concentration** (vol % of fuel in air) is therefore:

$$C_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + 3.76\,\nu_{O_2}} \times 100 = \frac{100}{1 + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for Flammability?** The LFL is *always* below C_st (lean side), and the UFL is *always* above C_st (rich side). For most hydrocarbons, the **LFL is roughly ½ of C_st**, and the **UFL is roughly 2–3× of C_st**. This empirical relationship (Bureau of Mines correlation) is highly testable in KTU exams.

### 2.1.2 Le Chatelier's Rule (Mixture Flammability)

For a mixture of multiple flammable gases, the **Lower Flammability Limit of the mixture (LFL_mix)** can be estimated by **Le Chatelier's Rule (1891)**:

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

where:
- $y_i$ = mole fraction (or vol %) of component *i* in the fuel mixture
- $LFL_i$ = lower flammability limit of pure component *i* (in vol %)
- $n$ = number of flammable components

**This formula assumes the components are thermally independent and additively contributing** — a reasonable assumption for paraffinic and similar non-interacting species. It is widely used in industry for natural gas, refinery gas, and solvent-vapor mixtures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Le Chatelier's Rule is valid only for the **LFL** of the mixture. While an analogous UFL formula exists, the UFL prediction is *less reliable* due to complex rich-side chemistry (soot formation, preferential diffusion).

### 2.1.3 Method for Estimating LFL of Pure Hydrocarbons

For a single-component C_x H_y O_z compound where experimental LFL is unavailable, a widely used empirical correlation (from Crowl & Louvar, "Chemical Process Safety") is:

$$LFL = \frac{100 \, \cdot \, M}{N_{O_2} \times 32 + 32 - 12\,x - 4\,y + 16\,z}$$

where:
- $M$ = molecular weight of the fuel
- $N_{O_2}$ = moles of O₂ required for complete combustion = $x + y/4 - z/2$
- $x, y, z$ = number of C, H, O atoms in the molecule

A simpler empirical shortcut: **LFL ≈ 55 / Heat of Combustion (in MJ/kg)**.

### 2.1.4 Estimation at Non-Standard Temperature (KTU favourite!)

The LFL decreases as temperature rises, approximated by:

$$LFL(T) = LFL(25^\circ C) \left[ 1 - \frac{T - 25}{T_{ad} - 25} \right]$$

or via the widely used Zabetakis correlation:

$$LFL(T) = LFL(25^\circ C) \cdot \frac{298}{T}$$

(with T in Kelvin) for modest temperature ranges.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

Estimating flammability limits is critical for:
- **Process Safety Design** — sizing of ventilation in chemical plants.
- **Inerting Calculations** — determining nitrogen blanketing requirements to stay below LOC.
- **Explosion Protection** — area classification (Class I Div 1/Div 2 in NFPA 30, IEC 60079-10).
- **Fire Investigation** — determining if a vapor cloud could ignite at given concentrations.
- **Refrigerant Safety** — many new refrigerants are mildly flammable; their LFLs must be known.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{st} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 3.76\nu_{O_2}}$ | Stoichiometric fuel concentration in air | vol % of fuel |
| $\nu_{O_2} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}$ | O₂ requirement for C_xH_yO_z combustion | mol O₂ per mol fuel |
| $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum y_i / LFL_i}$ | Le Chatelier's rule for mixtures | vol %; LFL_i in vol % |
| $LFL \approx 0.55 \cdot C_{st}$ | Empirical LFL–C_st correlation (Bureau of Mines) | vol % |
| $UFL \approx (2 \text{ to } 3) \cdot C_{st}$ | Empirical UFL–C_st correlation | vol % |
| $LOC \approx 0.6 \cdot C_{st, oxygen}$ | Limiting Oxygen Concentration | vol % O₂ |
| $LFL(T) = LFL(25) \cdot \dfrac{298}{T}$ | Temperature correction for LFL | T in K |
| $n_{air,fuel} = 1 + 3.76\,\nu_{O_2}$ | Total moles per mol fuel in stoich. air | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert LFL/UFL from vol % to mole fraction by **dividing by 100** before plugging into Le Chatelier's formula, and convert back at the end. Students commonly lose marks for unit inconsistency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of C_st for a General Hydrocarbon C_xH_yO_z

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion reaction with air:**

$$C_x H_y O_z + \nu_{O_2}\, O_2 + 3.76\,\nu_{O_2}\, N_2 \rightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2O + 3.76\,\nu_{O_2}\, N_2$$

> *Logic:* Air is modelled as 1 mole O₂ + 3.76 moles N₂ (since N₂/O₂ molar ratio in air is 79/21 ≈ 3.76). The N₂ is inert and passes through unchanged.

**Step 2 — Atom balance for oxygen:**

The oxygen on the right-hand side must equal that on the left:

$$2x + \frac{y}{2} = 2\,\nu_{O_2} + z$$

**Step 3 — Solve for ν_O₂:**

$$2\,\nu_{O_2} = 2x + \frac{y}{2} - z$$

$$\nu_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$

> *Logic:* This is the number of moles of pure O₂ needed to combust 1 mole of fuel. The '−z/2' term accounts for the oxygen already present in the fuel molecule.

**Step 4 — Total moles in the reactant stream per mole of fuel:**

$$n_{react} = \underbrace{1}_{\text{fuel}} + \underbrace{\nu_{O_2}}_{O_2} + \underbrace{3.76\,\nu_{O_2}}_{N_2} = 1 + 4.76\,\nu_{O_2}$$

> *Logic:* We add the moles of fuel (1) plus moles of O₂ plus moles of accompanying N₂.

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric fuel concentration (vol %):**

$$C_{st} = \frac{\text{moles of fuel}}{\text{total moles}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{1 + 4.76\,\nu_{O_2}} \times 100$$

> *Logic:* For an ideal gas mixture, volume fraction = mole fraction. Hence, the vol % of fuel = (moles of fuel / total moles) × 100.

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{C_{st} = \frac{100}{1 + 4.76\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

### Example 3.2.1 — Estimating LFL of Methane (CH₄)

**Given:** CH₄ in air, standard conditions. Experimental LFL of CH₄ = **5.0 vol %**.

**(a) Compute the stoichiometric concentration C_st.**

For CH₄: x = 1, y = 4, z = 0.

$$\nu_{O_2} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - 0 = 2$$

$$C_{st} = \frac{100}{1 + 4.76 \times 2} = \frac{100}{1 + 9.52} = \frac{100}{10.52}$$

$$C_{st} \approx 9.51 \text{ vol \%}$$

**(b) Verify empirical LFL ≈ 0.55 × C_st:**

$$LFL_{est} = 0.55 \times 9.51 = 5.23 \text{ vol \%}$$

This matches the experimental LFL (5.0 vol %) within ~5% — confirming the Bureau of Mines correlation.

### Example 3.2.2 — Le Chatelier's Rule on a Natural Gas Substitute

A fuel mixture contains **80 vol % CH₄ (LFL = 5.0%)** and **20 vol % C₃H₈ (LFL = 2.1%)**. Estimate the LFL of the mixture.

**Step 1 — Convert vol % to mole fractions (y_i):**

$$y_{CH_4} = 0.80, \quad y_{C_3H_8} = 0.20$$

**Step 2 — Apply Le Chatelier's rule:**

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{y_{CH_4}}{LFL_{CH_4}} + \dfrac{y_{C_3H_8}}{LFL_{C_3H_8}}}$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{0.80}{5.0} + \dfrac{0.20}{2.1}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator terms:**

$$\frac{0.80}{5.0} = 0.160$$

$$\frac{0.20}{2.1} = 0.0952$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.160 + 0.0952 = 0.2552$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{0.2552} \approx 391.8 \text{ ????}$$

> ⚠️ **Wait — that gives an absurdly high value, indicating a unit error!**

**Step 5 — Correct the calculation (this is the most common student mistake):**

Le Chatelier's rule requires $y_i$ as **vol % values** (not fractions) and $LFL_i$ in **vol %** as well — and the result is also in **vol %**.

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{80}{5.0} + \dfrac{20}{2.1}}$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{16 + 9.524} = \frac{100}{25.524}$$

$$\boxed{LFL_{mix} \approx 3.92 \text{ vol \%}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Lesson:** The **form of Le Chatelier's equation depends on input units**. If $y_i$ is in vol % AND $LFL_i$ is in vol %, the formula $100 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$ is correct. If both are in fractions, the formula reduces to $1 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$. The units must be *consistent*.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Temperature Correction for LFL

**Given:** LFL of acetone at 25 °C = **2.6 vol %**. Estimate LFL at 100 °C (T = 373 K).

**Step 1 — Apply Zabetakis correlation:**

$$LFL(100^\circ C) = LFL(25^\circ C) \times \frac{298}{T}$$

$$LFL(100^\circ C) = 2.6 \times \frac{298}{373}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{298}{373} = 0.7989$$

**Step 3 — Final LFL:**

$$\boxed{LFL(100^\circ C) \approx 2.6 \times 0.7989 \approx 2.08 \text{ vol \%}}$$

**Interpretation:** The LFL decreases as temperature increases, widening the flammable range. This is why hot process vessels are *more* hazardous.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Different Factors Influence Limits

| **Factor** | **Effect on LFL** | **Effect on UFL** | **Effect on Range Width** | **Physical Reason** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | Decreases | Increases | Widens | More thermal energy available; less O₂ needed |
| ↑ Pressure | Decreases slightly | Increases significantly | Widens | Higher molecule density, longer mean free path effect |
| ↑ Inert (N₂) content | Increases | Decreases | Narrows | Inert dilutes both fuel and oxidant effectiveness |
| ↑ Oxygen concentration | Decreases | Increases | Widens greatly | Replaces inert N₂ with reactive O₂ |
| Smaller molecule size | Generally higher LFL | — | Narrows | Smaller alkanes tend to be more flammable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Flammability Limit Estimation Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the systematic procedure a fire engineer follows when estimating flammability limits of a fuel (or fuel mixture) under given conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Fuel and Oxidizer] --> B{Is fuel single component or mixture?}
    B -- Single Component --> C[Determine Molecular Formula CxHyOz]
    B -- Mixture --> D[Determine Composition yi of each component]
    C --> E[Compute nu_O2 = x + y/4 - z/2]
    E --> F[Compute C_st = 100 / 1 + 4.76 x nu_O2]
    F --> G[Apply Empirical Correlations]
    G --> G1[LFL est = 0.55 x C_st]
    G --> G2[UFL est = 2.5 x C_st]
    G1 --> H{Apply temperature or pressure correction?}
    G2 --> H
    D --> I[Apply Le Chateliers Rule]
    I --> I1[LFLmix = 100 / sum yi / LFLi]
    I1 --> J[Compare with experimental NFPA / IEC data if available]
    H -- Yes --> K[Zabetakis: LFL T = LFL25 x 298 / T]
    H -- No --> J
    K --> J
    J --> L{Result acceptable for design?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Output Final LFL and UFL for Design]
    L -- No --> N[Refine with experimental test or more rigorous model]
    N --> M
```

## 4.2 Schematic — The Flammability Envelope (Concentration vs. Burning Velocity)

This Mermaid block visualizes the *flammability envelope* concept that KTU students must be able to draw in exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph zone0[Below LFL - Too Lean]
        Z0[Mixture cannot propagate flame]
    end
    subgraph zone1[Flammable Range - LFL to UFL]
        Z1[Flame propagates - Su max near Cst]
    end
    subgraph zone2[Above UFL - Too Rich]
        Z2[Insufficient O2 - Flame cannot propagate]
    end
    A[0 percent fuel] --> Z0
    Z0 -- LFL boundary --> Z1
    Z1 -- UFL boundary --> Z2
    Z2 --> B[100 percent fuel]
    style Z0 fill:#a8d5ba
    style Z1 fill:#ffcc66
    style Z2 fill:#a8d5ba
    style Z1 stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "sketch the flammability curve," label the axes (*Vol % Fuel* on x-axis, *Burning Velocity Su* on y-axis), mark the LFL, UFL, and the peak of the curve (which occurs at a concentration slightly above C_st due to diffusion effects, approximately at fuel equivalence ratio φ ≈ 1.05–1.1).

## 4.3 Process Flow: From Stoichiometry to LOC Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Combustion equation] --> P2[Atom balance for O2]
    P2 --> P3[nu O2 calculation]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Cst]
    P4 --> P5[Compute LOC: ~ 0.6 x Cst oxygen]
    P5 --> P6[Inerting: hold O2 < LOC for safety]
    P6 --> P7[Output required N2 or CO2 blanketing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) and Upper Flammability Limit (UFL). State the standard conditions under which they are measured.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** is the minimum volumetric concentration of a flammable vapor in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. The mixture is said to be *too lean*.

The **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** is the maximum volumetric concentration of a flammable vapor in air above which propagation of a flame does not occur; the mixture is *too rich*.

**Standard conditions:** Temperature **25 °C**, Pressure **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**, upward flame propagation in a standard test vessel (e.g., ASTM E681 tube of 5 cm diameter, 1.5 m height).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Le Chatelier's Rule for estimating the LFL of a fuel mixture. What is its principal limitation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Le Chatelier's Rule (1891):**

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

where $y_i$ is the vol % of component *i* in the fuel mixture and $LFL_i$ is the LFL of pure component *i*.

**Principal Limitation:** The rule is empirically derived and assumes **independent (non-interacting) thermal behavior of components**. It is **reasonably accurate for LFL** but **poorly reliable for UFL**, since rich-side combustion involves complex chemistry (soot, preferential diffusion). It also fails for highly reactive mixtures (e.g., acetylene with hydrogen) where chemical synergy occurs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the stoichiometric concentration (vol % of fuel in air) of a hydrocarbon C_x H_y O_z. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas has the composition: **CH₄ = 85 vol %, C₂H₆ = 10 vol %, C₃H₈ = 5 vol %**. The LFLs of the pure components are: CH₄ = 5.0%, C₂H₆ = 3.0%, C₃H₈ = 2.1%. Using Le Chatelier's Rule, estimate the LFL of the mixture. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation already covered in **Section 3.1** above. The student should present the atom balance, derive $\nu_{O_2} = x + y/4 - z/2$, and arrive at:

$$C_{st} = \frac{100}{1 + 4.76\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)} \quad \text{(vol \%)}$$

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

- Component fractions: $y_{CH_4} = 85$, $y_{C_2H_6} = 10$, $y_{C_3H_8} = 5$ (in vol %).
- LFLs: $LFL_{CH_4} = 5.0$, $LFL_{C_2H_6} = 3.0$, $LFL_{C_3H_8} = 2.1$ (in vol %).
- Apply Le Chatelier's rule:

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{85}{5.0} + \dfrac{10}{3.0} + \dfrac{5}{2.1}}$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{17 + 3.333 + 2.381} = \frac{100}{22.714}$$

$$\boxed{LFL_{mix} \approx 4.40 \text{ vol \%}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (most common, ~3 marks lost):** Students plug in mole fractions (0.85, 0.10, 0.05) but keep LFLs as vol %, leading to a denominator of 17 + 0.33 + 0.24 = 17.57, and a *wrong* LFL ≈ 5.7%. Always keep units consistent.
> 2. **Confusing LFL with UFL:** The formula works best for LFL; using it for UFL without comment loses marks.
> 3. **Skipping the standard conditions statement** in part (a) costs 1 mark.
> 4. **No units in the final answer** — KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 – Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the empirical correlation between the Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) and the Stoichiometric Concentration (C_st). Why is the LFL always *less* than C_st? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The LFL of acetone (C₃H₆O) at 25 °C is **2.6 vol %**. Estimate the LFL at **150 °C** using the Zabetakis correlation. Comment on the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a)** The Bureau of Mines empirical correlation states:

$$LFL \approx 0.55 \cdot C_{st}$$

**Why is LFL < C_st?** The lower limit corresponds to the *lean* mixture boundary, where the energy release per unit volume of gas (fuel mass per unit volume) is just enough to heat the *unburnt* gas (consisting mostly of inert N₂ and excess O₂) to the auto-ignition temperature. At exactly C_st, every molecule of fuel has a stoichiometric partner of O₂, releasing the maximum possible heat. Going to *leaner* mixtures reduces fuel density, so the heat released per unit volume drops. The LFL corresponds to the threshold where heat losses (mainly by radiation to vessel walls) become equal to chemical heat release — so flame propagation just barely fails.

**(b) Solution:**

- $LFL_{25} = 2.6$ vol %; $T_1 = 298$ K; $T_2 = 150 + 273 = 423$ K.
- Zabetakis:

$$LFL(150^\circ C) = 2.6 \times \frac{298}{423} = 2.6 \times 0.7045$$

$$\boxed{LFL(150^\circ C) \approx 1.83 \text{ vol \%}}$$

**Engineering implications:** In hot process equipment (e.g., distillation columns, hot oil lines, dryer exhausts), the LFL drops significantly. A vapor mixture considered *non-flammable* at ambient temperature may enter the flammable range when the equipment is hot. This is critical for **area classification** under IEC 60079-10, where hot surfaces warrant tighter zone boundaries and may require **purge protection** or **explosion-proof enclosures**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert °C to K — costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. Using the Zabetakis correlation for *UFL* without modification — it applies *only* to LFL.
> 3. Stating the engineering implication generically (e.g., "it is dangerous") without quantifying the temperature effect or referring to area classification — costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Estimating Flammability Limits**

- 🔑 **LFL** = minimum fuel concentration for flame propagation (mixture is *lean*).
- 🔑 **UFL** = maximum fuel concentration for flame propagation (mixture is *rich*).
- 🔑 **Flammable Range** = region between LFL and UFL.
- 🔑 **Stoichiometric concentration (C_st)** divides the range: LFL is on the lean side, UFL on the rich side.
- 🔑 Empirical rule: **LFL ≈ 0.55 × C_st** and **UFL ≈ 2.5 × C_st** (Bureau of Mines).
- 🔑 **Le Chatelier's Rule** for LFL of mixtures: $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$ — keep all $y_i$ and $LFL_i$ in **vol %**.
- 🔑 For C_xH_yO_z: $\nu_{O_2} = x + y/4 - z/2$, and $C_{st} = 100 / (1 + 4.76\,\nu_{O_2})$.
- 🔑 **Air composition** for stoichiometric calculations: 1 O₂ + 3.76 N₂.
- 🔑 **Zabetakis temperature correction:** $LFL(T) = LFL(25°C) \times 298 / T$ (T in Kelvin).
- 🔑 **Increasing T or P** widens flammable range. **Increasing inerts** narrows it.
- 🔑 **LOC** (Limiting Oxygen Concentration) is the key parameter for **inerting** design.
- 🔑 Le Chatelier's Rule is **reliable for LFL** of paraffinic mixtures, **unreliable for UFL** and for highly reactive fuels.
- 🔑 Always state **standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm)** when reporting LFL/UFL values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flammability Diagram: Construction and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flammability Diagram: Construction and Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Flammability Diagram** (also called a **Coward-Jones Triangular Diagram** or **Flammability Limit Diagram**) is a graphical representation in the form of an equilateral triangle used in fire engineering to delineate the regions in which a combustible mixture of fuel, oxygen, and an inert gas (typically nitrogen) will support combustion. Each vertex of the triangle represents the pure component at **100%** concentration: **Fuel**, **Oxygen**, and **Inert Gas (N₂)**.

The diagram plots experimental data points of fuel concentration, oxygen concentration, and inert gas concentration, drawing critical boundary lines that separate **flammable** and **non-flammable** regions under specified conditions of temperature and pressure (usually ambient: 25°C and 1 atm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic forms the foundational basis for all subsequent fire prevention and inerting calculations in **Module 1**, and directly feeds into the design of explosion-proof equipment, fire suppression systems (especially inert gas blanketing), and risk assessment procedures required in process safety.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Pre-Requisite Vocabulary)

| Term | Abbreviation | Definition |
|------|--------------|------------|
| Lower Flammability Limit | **LFL** (or LEL) | Minimum fuel concentration in air below which combustion cannot occur (mixture too lean). |
| Upper Flammability Limit | **UFL** (or UEL) | Maximum fuel concentration in air above which combustion cannot occur (mixture too rich). |
| Limiting Oxygen Concentration | **LOC** (or MOC) | Minimum oxygen concentration required to support combustion of a fuel in the presence of any fuel concentration. |
| Stoichiometric Concentration | C_st | Fuel concentration at which exact (complete) combustion occurs with no excess fuel or oxygen. |
| Flammable Range | — | The range of fuel concentration between LFL and UFL within which combustion is possible. |
| Flash Point | — | Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture near the surface. |
| Fire Point | — | Lowest temperature at which the vapor burns continuously for at least 5 seconds after ignition. |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **AIT** | Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously without an external source. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Fire Triangle on Steroids"**
>
> Think of the flammability diagram as a **"Weather Map for Fire"**. Just as a meteorologist plots temperature, pressure, and humidity on a chart to predict where rain or storms will form, a fire engineer plots fuel %, oxygen %, and inert gas % to predict **"where fire can ignite."**
>
> - The **three corners** of the triangle are like the three legs of the fire triangle (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat source) but in a quantitative, engineering-ready format.
> - The **central "Flammable Region"** is like a "fire zone" – a danger area that engineers must keep their process mixtures OUT of.
> - Drawing a horizontal line at 21% O₂ (the percentage in normal air) tells the engineer the exact LFL and UFL of a fuel in air – this is the simplest and most commonly used application.

The diagram is constructed by **experimentally igniting** thousands of fuel-oxygen-nitrogen mixtures and recording the boundary points where combustion just barely propagates. These boundary points are then connected to form a closed curve (envelope) defining the flammable region.

### 1.4 Historical Context

The diagram was developed by **H.F. Coward and G.W. Jones** in **1928** at the U.S. Bureau of Mines, based on extensive experimental work. The same conceptual framework is sometimes referred to as the **Le Chatelier diagram** due to the use of Le Chatelier's empirical rule for calculating mixed gas limits.

### 1.5 Importance in Modern Fire Engineering

The flammability diagram is used to:
- Design **inert gas blanketing systems** for storage tanks containing flammable liquids.
- Determine the **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)** for safety in confined spaces.
- Calculate safe operating envelopes for **chemical reactors** and **distillation columns**.
- Establish **gas detection** set-points (alarm and trip levels) in process plants.
- Comply with **NFPA 69** (Standard on Explosion Prevention Systems) requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Triangular Coordinate System

An equilateral triangle of side length 100 units (representing 100%) is used. Any point inside the triangle represents a unique mixture of fuel, oxygen, and inert gas such that:

$$C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{\text{O}_2} + C_{\text{inert}} = 100\%$$

The three sides of the triangle are special cases:
- **Bottom side (Fuel–Oxygen axis):** Inert = 0% → represents fuel-air mixtures
- **Left side (Fuel–Inert axis):** Oxygen = 0% → represents fuel-inert mixtures
- **Right side (Oxygen–Inert axis):** Fuel = 0% → represents air or oxygen-enriched air

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any horizontal line drawn on the diagram, the percentage of **inert gas is constant**. This is a critical reading convention used throughout fire engineering practice.

### 2.2 Construction Logic – Step-by-Step

The diagram is constructed through the following structured logic:

1. **Data Acquisition:** Experimentally determine flammability limits for various fuel-oxygen-inert mixtures. The standard apparatus is a **vertical tube (or sphere)** of defined volume where a spark or pilot flame is introduced.

2. **Plotting Boundary Points:** Each data point is plotted as a triple (C_fuel, C_O₂, C_inert) on the triangular diagram. Repeat for many compositions.

3. **Drawing Boundary Envelope:** Connect the boundary points with smooth curves to form the **flammability envelope** – a closed shape with vertices typically touching the fuel-inert axis (at LFL_pure) and the oxygen-inert axis (LOC value).

4. **Marking Key Lines:**
   - **LFL Line (Lower):** The lower boundary of the envelope
   - **UFL Line (Upper):** The upper boundary of the envelope
   - **Stoichiometric Line:** A straight line connecting the stoich-fuel-inert point to the stoich-oxygen-inert point
   - **Air Line:** A line representing air composition (21% O₂, 79% N₂)

5. **Identifying Regions:** Three distinct regions emerge from the diagram (see Section 4).

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention for Markdown Tables:** All absolute value expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` (not the bare pipe `|`) to preserve table formatting. Formulas are grouped by application for rapid revision.

#### **Table 2.3.1: Stoichiometric Combustion Formulas for Hydrocarbons**

| Fuel Type | General Formula | O₂ Required per mole of fuel | Stoichiometric O₂ in Air (%) | Stoichiometric Fuel in Air (%) |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Paraffin (Alkane) $C_mH_n$ | Combustion → $m\,CO_2 + \frac{n}{2}\,H_2O$ | $\left(m + \frac{n}{4}\right)$ moles | $21 \cdot \dfrac{m + n/4}{1 + 4.76(m + n/4)}$ | $\dfrac{100}{1 + 4.76(m + n/4)}$ |
| Methane ($CH_4$) | $CH_4 + 2O_2 → CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | 2 | 19.0 | 9.5 |
| Ethane ($C_2H_6$) | $C_2H_6 + 3.5O_2 → 2CO_2 + 3H_2O$ | 3.5 | 17.4 | 5.7 |
| Propane ($C_3H_8$) | $C_3H_8 + 5O_2 → 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$ | 5.0 | 15.9 | 4.0 |
| Butane ($C_4H_{10}$) | $C_4H_{10} + 6.5O_2 → 4CO_2 + 5H_2O$ | 6.5 | 14.8 | 3.1 |
| Ethylene ($C_2H_4$) | $C_2H_4 + 3O_2 → 2CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | 3.0 | 17.9 | 6.5 |
| Acetylene ($C_2H_2$) | $C_2H_2 + 2.5O_2 → 2CO_2 + H_2O$ | 2.5 | 18.7 | 7.7 |
| Hydrogen ($H_2$) | $H_2 + 0.5O_2 → H_2O$ | 0.5 | 5.9 | 29.6 |

> **Universal Air Composition Used:** $N_2/O_2 = 79/21 = 3.76$; Total air = $1 + 3.76 = 4.76$ moles per mole of O₂.

#### **Table 2.3.2: Le Chatelier's Rule for Gas Mixtures**

| Application | Formula | Notes |
|-------------|---------|-------|
| **LFL of Mixture** | $LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | Where $y_i$ is the volume (mole) % of fuel $i$ in the fuel mixture, and $LFL_i$ is the LFL of pure fuel $i$ in air. **Valid only when $y_i / LFL_i$ terms are summed over all fuel components.** |
| **UFL of Mixture** | $UFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$ | Same form as LFL formula. Less accurate than LFL prediction; experimentally tends to deviate ± 10–15%. |

> [!WARNING]
> Le Chatelier's rule is **empirical** and was developed for hydrocarbon fuel mixtures. It is **not reliable** for mixtures containing **hydrogen + hydrocarbons** (errors can exceed 25%) and **non-hydrocarbon inerts** other than nitrogen. For KTU examinations, ensure you clearly state the assumptions: (a) fuel-air system, (b) ideal behavior, (c) heat capacities constant.

#### **Table 2.3.3: LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration) Calculation**

| Method | Formula / Approach | Best For |
|--------|-------------------|----------|
| **Graphical (from diagram)** | Locate the nose of the flammable envelope on the O₂-inert axis | Most accurate; used in design |
| **Rule of Thumb (Zabetakis)** | $LOC \approx 0.6 \cdot C_{\text{O}_2, \text{stoich}}$ when fuel is in stoichiometric ratio with air | Quick estimation |
| **Empirical Correlation** | $LOC \approx \dfrac{LFL}{LFL + UFL} \cdot 21$ (very rough) | Order-of-magnitude only |
| **Direct Experimental** | ASTM E918, ISO 10156 standard test | Authoritative result |

#### **Table 2.3.4: Adiabatic Flame Temperature Criterion**

A mixture is **flammable** only if the theoretical adiabatic flame temperature exceeds the **critical flame temperature** of the fuel (typically the auto-ignition temperature minus 100–200°C).

$$\Delta H_{\text{combustion}} = \sum_i n_i \int_{T_0}^{T_{\text{ad}}} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT$$

Where:
- $\Delta H_{\text{combustion}}$ = Heat of combustion (J/mol)
- $T_{\text{ad}}$ = Adiabatic flame temperature (K)
- $T_0$ = Initial temperature (K)
- $C_{p,i}(T)$ = Temperature-dependent specific heat of product $i$
- $n_i$ = Moles of product $i$ per mole of fuel

### 2.4 The Three Critical Regions of the Diagram

The flammability envelope divides the triangular plane into **three non-flammable** regions and **one flammable** region:

| Region | Identifier | Why Non-Flammable? |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Below the envelope, near fuel-inert side** | Fuel-Lean (Inert-rich) | Insufficient fuel to propagate flame; heat loss to excess inert > heat release |
| **Above the envelope, near fuel corner** | Fuel-Rich (Oxygen-deficient) | Insufficient oxygen; all O₂ consumed in thin reaction zone |
| **Left of envelope, near inert corner** | Inert-Dominated | Heat capacity of excess inert cools the flame below critical T |
| **Inside the envelope** | **FLAMMABLE** | Sufficient fuel + oxygen; adiabatic flame temperature > critical |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Inerting Calculations in Storage Tanks:** A typical diesel fuel tank must be inerted with nitrogen to ensure the oxygen concentration is below the LOC. The diagram tells the operator exactly how much N₂ to introduce to a fuel vapor space.

2. **Confined Space Entry (OSHA 1910.146):** The diagram helps determine the maximum allowable oxygen concentration for safe entry (typically 19.5% — below most LOCs for common hydrocarbons).

3. **Process Safety Management (PSM):** Plant operating procedures specify safe operating windows defined by flammable envelopes. The diagram is the basis for the **"Operating Envelope"** documented in P&IDs and operating manuals.

4. **Fire & Explosion Protection (NFPA 69):** The LOC is the **key design parameter** for explosion prevention by oxygen reduction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Concentration in Air

**Problem Statement:** For a general paraffin hydrocarbon $C_mH_n$, derive the volume percent of oxygen in a stoichiometric fuel-air mixture.

**Step 1 – Write the balanced combustion equation**

For complete combustion:

$$C_mH_n + \left(m + \dfrac{n}{4}\right)O_2 \longrightarrow m\,CO_2 + \dfrac{n}{2}\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 – Account for the nitrogen in air**

Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ on a molar basis. Therefore, for every 1 mole of O₂, we have $79/21 = 3.76$ moles of N₂ (this is the **air-to-oxygen ratio** for combustion stoichiometry).

The total air required per mole of fuel is:

$$n_{\text{air}} = \left(m + \dfrac{n}{4}\right) \times 4.76$$

where $4.76 = 1 + 3.76$ is the total moles of "air" per mole of O₂.

**Step 3 – Express the mole fractions in the stoich fuel-air mixture**

| Component | Moles | Mole Fraction in Mixture |
|-----------|-------|--------------------------|
| Fuel ($C_mH_n$) | 1 | $1 / (1 + 4.76(m + n/4))$ |
| Oxygen ($O_2$) | $m + n/4$ | $(m + n/4) / (1 + 4.76(m + n/4))$ |
| Nitrogen ($N_2$) | $3.76(m + n/4)$ | $3.76(m + n/4) / (1 + 4.76(m + n/4))$ |

**Step 4 – Express percentages**

$$\boxed{\%O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{100 \cdot (m + n/4)}{1 + 4.76(m + n/4)}}$$

$$\boxed{\%\text{Fuel}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{100}{1 + 4.76(m + n/4)}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Methane ($CH_4$) Stoichiometry

**Given:** $m = 1$, $n = 4$ for methane. The combustion reaction:

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

**Step 1 – Identify moles of O₂ required**

$$n_{O_2} = m + \dfrac{n}{4} = 1 + \dfrac{4}{4} = 2 \text{ moles}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate total moles in stoich fuel-air mixture**

$$n_{\text{total}} = 1 \text{ (fuel)} + 2 \text{ (O}_2\text{)} + 2 \times 3.76 \text{ (N}_2\text{)} = 1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52 \text{ moles}$$

**Step 3 – Calculate percentage oxygen**

$$\%O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{2}{10.52} \times 100 = 19.0\%$$

**Step 4 – Calculate percentage methane**

$$\%\text{CH}_{4,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1}{10.52} \times 100 = 9.5\%$$

**Step 5 – Verify the sum**

$$9.5\% + 19.0\% + 71.5\% = 100\% \checkmark$$

The nitrogen carries 71.5% of the mixture — this is a critical insight: **most of the "air" in any combustion system is useless nitrogen that must be heated, which reduces flame temperature.**

### 3.3 Derivation: Le Chatelier's Rule (Empirical Foundation)

**Le Chatelier (1891) proposed that the lower limit of flammability of a fuel mixture corresponds to a constant value of the sum of the "partial contributions" to flammability.**

Define for each fuel $i$ in a mixture, a quantity:

$$P_i = \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}$$

where $y_i$ is the volume % of fuel $i$ in the fuel blend, and $LFL_i$ is the LFL of pure fuel $i$ in air.

The mixture LFL is reached when:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = 1$$

Solving for $LFL_{\text{mix}}$ (where $LFL_{\text{mix}}$ is the total fuel % in the fuel-air mixture):

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 100 \times \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

**Why does this work?** At the LFL, the energy density per unit volume of gas just barely reaches the minimum required for flame propagation. Each fuel contributes proportionally to this energy density. The same logic applies inversely to the UFL where the limit is reached when excess fuel above stoichiometric causes the oxygen depletion to exceed the threshold.

### 3.4 Worked Example: LFL of Methane-Propane (50:50) Mixture

**Given:**
- $LFL_{CH_4} = 5.0\%$
- $LFL_{C_3H_8} = 2.1\%$
- $y_{CH_4} = 50\%$, $y_{C_3H_8} = 50\%$

**Step 1 – Apply Le Chatelier's rule**

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{y_{CH_4}}{LFL_{CH_4}} + \dfrac{y_{C_3H_8}}{LFL_{C_3H_8}}}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values**

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{50}{5.0} + \dfrac{50}{2.1}}$$

**Step 3 – Compute the partial contributions**

$$\dfrac{50}{5.0} = 10.0 \quad ; \quad \dfrac{50}{2.1} = 23.81$$

**Step 4 – Sum and invert**

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{10.0 + 23.81} = \dfrac{100}{33.81} = 2.96\%$$

**Step 5 – Compare with experimental value**

The experimental LFL of 50:50 $CH_4/C_3H_8$ in air is approximately **2.9–3.0%**, confirming excellent agreement with Le Chatelier's rule. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reading the Flammability Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Diagram – Universal Conventions:**
>
> 1. **Reading % Fuel:** The line parallel to the **O₂–Inert side** (opposite the Fuel vertex) gives the fuel %.
> 2. **Reading % O₂:** The line parallel to the **Fuel–Inert side** (opposite the O₂ vertex) gives the oxygen %.
> 3. **Reading % Inert:** The line parallel to the **Fuel–O₂ side** (opposite the Inert vertex) gives the inert %.
> 4. **Air line:** Always passes through the point (21% O₂, 79% N₂, 0% Fuel) — the bottom-left area of the triangle.
> 5. **Pure oxygen line:** The right edge of the triangle (Fuel = 0% line).

### 3.6 Determination of LOC from the Diagram

**Step 1:** Locate the **nose** (lowest oxygen point) of the flammable envelope. This point is the **standoff point** where further reduction in oxygen extinguishes the flame regardless of fuel concentration.

**Step 2:** Drop a perpendicular from this point to the O₂ axis (parallel to the Fuel–Inert side). The reading on the O₂ axis is the **LOC**.

**Step 3:** For methane, the experimentally determined LOC in N₂ is approximately **12%** (varies with temperature and pressure). This means: if the oxygen in a methane-containing space drops below 12%, **no concentration of methane will burn**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Design Insight:** Storage tanks containing gasoline, diesel, or methane should be inerted with nitrogen until the oxygen concentration falls **below the LOC**. A common safety factor of 5–10% is added, leading to a design target of **2% to 5% below the LOC** for "oxygen-deficient" environments.

### 3.7 Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Limits (Higher-Order Analysis)

| Condition | Effect on LFL | Effect on UFL | Effect on LOC |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Increased Temperature** | Decreases (wider range) | Increases (wider range) | Decreases (easier to burn) |
| **Increased Pressure** | Slight decrease | Marked increase (wider range) | Decreases |
| **Increased Inert (N₂)** | Slight effect | Decreases sharply | Marked decrease |
| **Oxygen-Enriched Air** | Decreases | Increases dramatically | Increases |

**Empirical correlation (Zabetakis, 1965):**

$$LFL_T \approx LFL_{25°C} \left[1 - \dfrac{T - 25}{1600}\right]$$

$$UFL_T \approx UFL_{25°C} \left[1 + \dfrac{T - 25}{600}\right]$$

where $T$ is in °C. These are valid for hydrocarbon fuels within a moderate temperature range (25°C to 400°C).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Coward-Jones Triangular Flammability Diagram (Mermaid Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used here to render a **conceptual block-and-flow architecture** of the flammability diagram because the physical triangular plot is best understood as a functional map of fire zones. The following diagram shows the relationships among the **three vertices (pure components)**, the **three critical boundary lines**, and the **four classified regions**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["VERTEX F1: PURE FUEL 100 percent"] -->|Decreasing Fuel| B["LFL LINE: Lower Flammability Boundary"]
    A -->|Increasing Inert| C["VERTEX I1: PURE INERT 100 percent N2"]
    A -->|Increasing Oxygen with Inert| D["VERTEX O1: PURE OXYGEN 100 percent"]

    B --> E["REGION R1: FUEL LEAN NON FLAMMABLE"]
    B --> F["REGION R2: CENTRAL FLAMMABLE ZONE"]
    B --> G["REGION R3: FUEL RICH NON FLAMMABLE"]

    D --> H["UFL LINE: Upper Flammability Boundary"]
    H --> F
    H --> I["REGION R4: INERT DOMINATED NON FLAMMABLE"]

    F --> J["STOICHIOMETRIC LINE: Divides lean and rich flammability"]
    J --> F

    C --> K["AIR LINE: Connects air composition 21 O2 79 N2 to fuel corner"]
    K --> F

    L["LOC POINT: Nose of envelope on O2 axis"] --> F
    M["LFL AIR POINT: 5 percent for methane"] --> K
    N["UFL AIR POINT: 15 percent for methane"] --> K

    style A fill:#FFB300,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#42A5F5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#EF5350,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style E fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#8D6E63,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style H fill:#8D6E63,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style J fill:#FFA726,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style K fill:#AB47BC,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style L fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style M fill:#7B1FA2,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style N fill:#7B1FA2,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Application Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT STAGE: Fuel identification and ambient conditions T and P"] --> B["DATA STAGE: Retrieve LFL and UFL from NFPA tables or experiment"]
    B --> C["CONSTRUCTION STAGE: Plot boundary points on triangular coordinates"]
    C --> D["ANALYSIS STAGE: Draw envelope and mark LOC, stoich line, air line"]
    D --> E["INTERPRETATION STAGE: Classify operating point as flammable or safe"]
    E --> F["DECISION STAGE: Apply mitigation inert blanketing or ventilation"]
    F --> G["OUTPUT STAGE: Documented safe operating envelope for plant"]

    A2["STEP 1: Identify fuel type"] --> A
    A3["STEP 2: Specify T and P"] --> A
    B2["STEP 3: Look up limits"] --> B
    C2["STEP 4: Plot 10 to 20 data points"] --> C
    D2["STEP 5: Connect boundary curve"] --> D
    E2["STEP 6: Locate operating composition on diagram"] --> E
    F2["STEP 7: Inert to below LOC if needed"] --> F
    G2["STEP 8: Update P and ID and SOPs"] --> G

    style A fill:#42A5F5,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style B fill:#42A5F5,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style C fill:#FFA726,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFA726,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#EF5350,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style F fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#66BB6A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Locating the Operating Point – Analytical Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Begin: Given fuel and inert gas concentrations"] --> Q1{"Is mixture composition inside the envelope?"}
    Q1 -->|YES| A1["FLAMMABLE - Action Required"]
    Q1 -->|NO| A2["SAFE - Continue normal operation"]
    Q1 -->|ON BOUNDARY| A3["MARGINAL - Critical condition, act with caution"]

    A1 --> A1A["Option 1: Increase inert until O2 below LOC"]
    A1 --> A1B["Option 2: Increase ventilation to dilute fuel below LFL"]
    A1 --> A1C["Option 3: Eliminate ignition sources"]
    A1 --> A1D["Option 4: Add explosion suppression system"]

    A2 --> A2A["Monitor continuously with gas detectors"]
    A2 --> A2B["Maintain safe operating envelope"]

    A3 --> A3A["Immediately increase safety margin"]
    A3 --> A3B["Investigate cause of boundary approach"]

    style START fill:#42A5F5,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style Q1 fill:#FFA726,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#EF5350,stroke:#000,color:#FFF

    style A1B fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
