```mermaid
    style A3B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic: The Triangular Diagram (for quick sketching in exams)

```
                  FUEL (100%)  •  A
                                  /\
                                 /  \
                                /    \
                               /  FL  \      <-- FLAMMABLE REGION
                              /  (red) \
                             /__________\
                            /            \
                           /  R1   R4  R3 \  <-- Three Non-flammable
                          /                \
                         /__________________\
              OXYGEN (100%) •  C        INERT (100%) •  B

  Where:
    R1 = Fuel-lean region (below LFL line)
    R2 = Central flammable zone (inside envelope)
    R3 = Fuel-rich region (above UFL line)
    R4 = Inert-dominated region (left of envelope)

  Key points to mark:
    L: LFL of fuel in air (e.g., 5% for methane) - on air line, lower
    U: UFL of fuel in air (e.g., 15% for methane) - on air line, upper
    S: Stoichiometric point on air line (e.g., 9.5% for methane)
    N: Nose of envelope - defines LOC (~12% for methane)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the terms **(a)** Lower Flammability Limit, **(b)** Upper Flammability Limit, and **(c)** Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC). State their physical significance in fire engineering.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Lower Flammability Limit (LFL):** The minimum concentration of a flammable vapor in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. Physically, below the LFL, the mixture is too **lean** (insufficient fuel) to release enough energy to heat the unburned gases to the ignition temperature.

**(b) Upper Flammability Limit (UFL):** The maximum concentration of a flammable vapor in air above which propagation of a flame does not occur. Physically, above the UFL, the mixture is too **rich** (insufficient oxygen) to sustain combustion — the available oxygen is consumed in a thin reaction zone near the igniter, and the heat release is insufficient to propagate.

**(c) Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC):** The minimum concentration of oxygen in a fuel-inert-gas mixture that will support combustion. Physically, it is the **nose** of the flammability envelope on the O₂–inert axis. Below this concentration, no amount of fuel will burn because heat losses to the inert gas exceed the heat of combustion.

> **(3 marks: 1 mark each definition + significance)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2.** What is a **flammability diagram**? Sketch and label its key features.

**Model Answer:**

A **flammability diagram** (or Coward-Jones diagram) is a triangular graph used to represent the flammability characteristics of a fuel-oxygen-inert gas system.

**Sketch and Labels (essential features):**

```
              FUEL
                *
               / \
              /   \
             / FL  \         FL = Flammable Region
            /       \        LOC = Limiting Oxygen Concentration
           /_________\       LFL = Lower Flammability Limit
          /  |    |   \      UFL = Upper Flammability Limit
         /   |    |    \     S = Stoichiometric Point
        /    |    |     \    A = Air Line (21% O₂, 79% N₂)
       /_____|____|______\
   OXYGEN              INERT (N₂)
```

**Key features (must be labelled for full marks):**
1. Three vertices: Fuel, Oxygen, Inert
2. Flammability envelope (closed curve)
3. LFL and UFL lines forming the envelope
4. Stoichiometric line
5. Air line (21% O₂, 79% N₂)
6. LOC point (nose of envelope on O₂ axis)

> **(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark sketch + 1 mark labels)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction procedure** of a flammability diagram for a fuel–oxygen–nitrogen system. Discuss any **two applications** of the diagram in fire safety engineering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A storage tank contains **hexane vapor** at 25°C. Using the data below, determine:
  - (i) The stoichiometric concentration of hexane in air. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The stoichiometric oxygen concentration. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The LFL of a mixture of **75% hexane and 25% toluene** (by volume) in air, using **Le Chatelier's rule**. **(3 marks)**

  **Data given:** $LFL_{\text{hexane}} = 1.1\%$, $LFL_{\text{toluene}} = 1.2\%$, $C_6H_{14} + 9.5O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 7H_2O$, Air composition: 21% O₂, 79% N₂.

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction Procedure (7 marks):**

The flammability diagram is constructed as follows:

**Step 1 – Selection of Apparatus:** A standardized vertical glass tube (or sphere) apparatus of known volume is used, with an igniter (spark or pilot flame) at the bottom and a viewing window.

**Step 2 – Preparation of Mixtures:** Known volumes of fuel, oxygen, and nitrogen are mixed in the test vessel at controlled temperature (typically 25°C) and pressure (1 atm).

**Step 3 – Ignition Tests:** The mixture is ignited. Observations are recorded:
- **Flame propagates upward** → mixture is **flammable**.
- **Flame does not propagate** → mixture is **non-flammable**.

**Step 4 – Iterative Boundary Determination:** For each fuel-inert ratio, the oxygen concentration is varied in small steps to find the precise boundary between flammable and non-flammable behavior. The boundary point is recorded as a triple ($C_{\text{fuel}}$, $C_{O_2}$, $C_{N_2}$).

**Step 5 – Plotting on Triangular Coordinates:** The data points are plotted on the equilateral triangle. The boundary points are connected to form a smooth closed curve (the **flammability envelope**).

**Step 6 – Marking Critical Lines:**
- The lower part of the envelope is the **LFL line**.
- The upper part is the **UFL line**.
- The minimum O₂ point on the envelope is the **LOC**.
- The line connecting stoich-fuel-N₂ to stoich-O₂-N₂ is the **stoichiometric line**.

**Step 7 – Verification with Air Line:** The air line (21% O₂, 79% N₂) is drawn. The LFL and UFL in air are read directly from where the air line intersects the envelope.

**Two Applications (4 marks – 2 marks each):**

1. **Inerting of Storage Tanks:** The diagram is used to determine the minimum nitrogen concentration required to render a fuel tank non-flammable. By finding the LOC, operators know the safe oxygen level (typically LOC minus 5–10% safety margin). This is mandated by **NFPA 69** for explosion prevention.

2. **Design of Confined Space Entry Procedures:** OSHA regulations require oxygen levels between 19.5% and 23.5% for human entry. The flammability diagram ensures that the chosen oxygen level is also below the LOC of any anticipated fuel, preventing explosion hazards during entry, hot work, or maintenance.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric Concentration of Hexane in Air (2 marks):**

The balanced equation: $C_6H_{14} + 9.5O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 7H_2O$

Per mole of hexane, we need 9.5 moles of O₂. With air, 9.5 moles of O₂ come with $9.5 \times 3.76 = 35.72$ moles of N₂.

Total moles in stoich fuel-air mixture:
$$n_{\text{total}} = 1 + 9.5 + 35.72 = 46.22 \text{ moles}$$

$$\%\text{Hexane}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1}{46.22} \times 100 = 2.16\%$$

**(ii) Stoichiometric Oxygen Concentration (2 marks):**

$$\%O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{9.5}{46.22} \times 100 = 20.55\%$$

**Verification:** $\%N_2 = 35.72 / 46.22 \times 100 = 77.29\%$
Sum: $2.16 + 20.55 + 77.29 = 100.00\%$ ✓

**(iii) LFL of 75% Hexane + 25% Toluene Mixture (3 marks):**

Apply Le Chatelier's rule with $y_{\text{hexane}} = 75\%$, $y_{\text{toluene}} = 25\%$, $LFL_{\text{hexane}} = 1.1\%$, $LFL_{\text{toluene}} = 1.2\%$:

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{y_{\text{hexane}}}{LFL_{\text{hexane}}} + \dfrac{y_{\text{toluene}}}{LFL_{\text{toluene}}}}$$

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{75}{1.1} + \dfrac{25}{1.2}}$$

$$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{68.18 + 20.83} = \dfrac{100}{89.01}$$

$$\boxed{LFL_{\text{mix}} = 1.12\%}$$

This is reasonable: the mixture's LFL lies between the LFLs of the two pure fuels (closer to hexane's 1.1% since hexane dominates 75% of the fuel blend).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** with a suitable diagram. Discuss its **importance in the design of inert gas blanketing systems** for storage tanks containing flammable liquids. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **LOC of methane in nitrogen** using the following approach:
  - (i) Apply the **rule of thumb** (Zabetakis approximation): $LOC \approx 0.6 \times C_{O_2, \text{stoich}}$ **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) Apply the **empirical correlation**: $LOC \approx \dfrac{LFL}{LFL + UFL} \times 21$ **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Compare both values with the **experimental LOC of 12%** for methane and comment on accuracy. **(3 marks)**

  **Data given:** $LFL_{CH_4} = 5.0\%$, $UFL_{CH_4} = 15.0\%$, Stoichiometric O₂ for methane = 19.0%.

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Concept of LOC and Inert Gas Blanketing (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** is the minimum oxygen concentration in a fuel-oxidant-inert mixture that will just barely support combustion of any fuel concentration. On the flammability diagram, it is located at the **nose (vertex)** of the flammable envelope, where the LFL and UFL boundaries converge on the O₂–inert axis.

**Diagram Description:**

```
                 FUEL
                   *
                  /|\
                 / | \
                /  |  \      <-- Flammable Envelope
               /  _N_  \     N = Nose Point = LOC
              /  / | \  \
             /__/__|__\__\
         OXYGEN   |    INERT
                   |
                  ↓
              LOC Reading on O₂ axis
```

**Importance in Inert Gas Blanketing (5 marks):**

1. **Prevention of Vapor Space Explosion:** Flammable liquids stored in fixed-roof or floating-roof tanks release vapors that form explosive mixtures with air in the ullage (vapor space). Introducing nitrogen to reduce the O₂ below the LOC prevents flame propagation.

2. **Compliance with Standards:** **NFPA 69** mandates that oxygen be reduced to a level no more than 60% of the LOC (i.e., design O₂ ≤ 0.6 × LOC) for explosion prevention. For methane (LOC = 12%), the design oxygen would be ≤ 7.2%.

3. **Design Procedure:**
   - Determine the fuel's LOC experimentally or from literature.
   - Apply safety factor (typically 5–10% absolute O₂ reduction).
   - Calculate nitrogen flow rate: $\dot{V}_{N_2} = \dot{V}_{\text{ullage}} \times \ln\dfrac{C_{O_2, \text{initial}}}{C_{O_2, \text{target}}}$
   - Install oxygen analyzer with alarm at 50% of LOC and trip at 75% of LOC.

4. **Cost-Benefit Consideration:** Blanketing with N₂ is more expensive than venting, but is justified for high-value or highly flammable fluids (e.g., gasoline, hexane, MTBE) where vapor cloud explosion could cause catastrophic loss.

5. **Alternative Inerts:** While N₂ is most common, **CO₂** and **flue gas** can also be used. Each has a different LOC value due to differing heat capacities. The diagram must be re-drawn for each inert gas.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem on LOC of Methane (7 marks):**

**(i) Zabetakis Rule of Thumb (2 marks):**

$$LOC \approx 0.6 \times C_{O_2, \text{stoich}} = 0.6 \times 19.0\% = 11.4\%$$

**(ii) Empirical Correlation (2 marks):**

$$LOC \approx \dfrac{LFL}{LFL + UFL} \times 21 = \dfrac{5.0}{5.0 + 15.0} \times 21$$

$$LOC \approx \dfrac{5.0}{20.0} \times 21 = 0.25 \times 21 = 5.25\%$$

**(iii) Comparison and Comment (3 marks):**

| Method | Calculated LOC | Experimental LOC | % Error |
|--------|---------------|------------------|---------|
| Zabetakis Rule | 11.4% | 12.0% | **5.0%** (very good) |
| Empirical Correlation | 5.25% | 12.0% | **56.3%** (very poor) |

**Comment:** The Zabetakis rule of thumb ($LOC \approx 0.6 \times C_{O_2, \text{stoich}}$) gives excellent agreement with experimental data (within 5%) for methane, and is generally applicable to most hydrocarbon fuels. It is the **preferred quick-estimation method** in KTU-style problems.

The empirical correlation $LOC \approx \dfrac{LFL}{LFL + UFL} \times 21$ is **highly inaccurate** for methane (and most hydrocarbons), giving a value less than half the experimental value. It is **not recommended** for engineering design and should be flagged as a "rough order-of-magnitude" only.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Part B – 14 Mark Questions):**
>
> 1. **Diagram Labelling:** When asked to draw the flammability diagram, students often forget to label the **three vertices (Fuel, Oxygen, Inert)**, the **LOC point**, and the **stoichiometric line**. Missing labels can cost 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Le Chatelier's Rule Misapplication:** A common error is plugging in $y_i$ as a fraction (0.5) instead of as a percentage (50). Remember: **the numerator is 100, and the $y_i$ values are in %**.
>
> 3. **Air Composition Confusion:** Students sometimes use $N_2/O_2 = 4$ (i.e., 80/20) instead of the more accurate 79/21 = 3.76. For most KTU problems, the textbook approximation of 4 is acceptable, but the precise value 3.76 is preferred and may earn partial credit distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Sum Check:** Always verify that $C_{\text{fuel}} + C_{O_2} + C_{\text{inert}} = 100\%$. A small calculation error here can cascade through the entire problem.
>
> 5. **Stoichiometry of Hydrocarbons:** Be careful with the oxygen coefficient: for $C_mH_n$, it is $(m + n/4)$, not $(m + n/2)$. This is a high-frequency mistake.
>
> 6. **Definition vs. Explanation:** In Part A, writing the definition alone (without physical significance) may earn only 1 mark out of 3. Always include the **"why"** behind the limit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Flammability Diagram (Coward-Jones):** A triangular plot with three vertices: **Fuel, Oxygen, Inert (typically N₂)**. Used to identify flammable regions in a fuel-oxidant-inert system.

- [ ] **Three Critical Limits:** LFL (lean limit), UFL (rich limit), LOC (oxygen limit). Always remember: LFL < Stoich < UFL.

- [ ] **Stoichiometric Oxygen (Hydrocarbon $C_mH_n$):**
  $$\%O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{21 \cdot (m + n/4)}{1 + 4.76(m + n/4)}$$

- [ ] **Air Constant:** Air has the molar ratio $N_2/O_2 = 3.76$ (precise) or 4 (approximate). Total air per O₂ = 4.76 moles.

- [ ] **Le Chatelier's Rule (LFL):**
  $$LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i (y_i / LFL_i)}$$
  Most accurate for hydrocarbon mixtures; least accurate for H₂-containing systems.

- [ ] **Le Chatelier's Rule (UFL):** Same form as LFL, but typically 10–15% less accurate.

- [ ] **LOC Determination Methods (in order of preference):**
  1. Direct experimental (ASTM E918 / ISO 10156)
  2. From flammability diagram (nose of envelope)
  3. Zabetakis rule: $LOC \approx 0.6 \times \%O_{2,\text{stoich}}$
  4. Avoid: $\dfrac{LFL}{LFL+UFL} \times 21$ (very inaccurate)

- [ ] **The Four Regions of the Diagram:** Three non-flammable (fuel-lean, fuel-rich, inert-dominated) and one central **flammable** envelope.

- [ ] **Effect of Temperature:** Higher T → wider flammable range (LFL decreases, UFL increases, LOC decreases).

- [ ] **Effect of Pressure:** Higher P → UFL increases markedly; LFL nearly unchanged. Pressure has minor effect on LOC.

- [ ] **Effect of Oxygen Enrichment:** O₂ > 21% → UFL rises sharply, LFL falls; flammable range widens dramatically. Critical for medical, diving, and space applications.

- [ ] **Inerting Design Rule (NFPA 69):** Reduce O₂ to **at most 60% of LOC** (i.e., design O₂ ≤ 0.6 × LOC). For methane (LOC = 12%), design O₂ ≤ 7.2%.

- [ ] **Confined Space Entry Rule (OSHA 1910.146):** O₂ between **19.5% and 23.5%**; no entry above 23.5% (oxygen-enriched) or below 19.5% (oxygen-deficient).

- [ ] **Safety Factor of 5–10% absolute:** Always add a safety margin below LOC for industrial inerting (target: LOC minus 5% to 10% in volume % O₂).

- [ ] **Critical Adiabatic Flame Temperature Criterion:** A mixture is flammable only if the theoretical adiabatic flame temperature exceeds the fuel's critical flame temperature (typically 1000–1500 K for hydrocarbons).

- [ ] **Key Industrial Applications:**
  1. Storage tank inerting (NFPA 69)
  2. Confined space entry
  3. Process plant operating envelope design
  4. Gas detection set-point selection
  5. Explosion suppression system design

- [ ] **Common Exam Pitfalls:** (a) Wrong air constant (3.76 vs. 4), (b) units confusion in Le Chatelier, (c) missing labels in sketch, (d) forgetting to verify sum = 100%, (e) using empirical correlation for LOC when rule of thumb is available.

- [ ] **High-Value Memorization Items for KTU:**
  - LFL/UFL of common fuels: Methane (5/15), Propane (2.1/9.5), Hexane (1.1/7.5), Hydrogen (4/75)
  - LOC of methane = 12%, propane = 11.5%, gasoline ≈ 11%
  - Stoichiometric O₂: Methane = 19%, Propane = 15.9%, Hexane = 20.5%
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Products of Combustion: Flame
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Products of Combustion: Flame

# 🟥 Products of Combustion: Flame

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Combustion** is defined as a rapid, self-sustaining, exothermic oxidation chemical reaction between a substance (the **fuel**) and an oxidizing agent (typically atmospheric **oxygen**, O₂), accompanied by the evolution of **heat**, **light**, and a series of chemically transformed **products of combustion (POC)**.

A **flame** is the visible, luminous (or sometimes non-luminous) gaseous envelope of a combustion reaction, representing the zone in which the fuel and oxidizer are actively reacting, releasing thermal energy and emitting electromagnetic radiation (primarily in the visible and infrared spectra). It is essentially the luminous manifestation of the **reaction zone** where the chemical energy of the fuel is converted into thermal and radiant energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1 (Fire Engineering)**
> Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, the study of "Products of Combustion & Flame" is foundational to:
> - **CO1:** Describe the chemistry and physics of fire (Bloom Level: Understand / Remember)
> - **CO2:** Apply principles of combustion to fire growth and smoke movement (Bloom Level: Apply)
> Mastery of stoichiometry, flame types, and combustion chemistry is **mandatory** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine lighting a candle. The wick draws molten wax upward through **capillary action**; this liquid wax vaporizes and mixes with surrounding air. Where this vaporized wax meets sufficient oxygen and heat, a **combustion reaction** occurs — and we see the result as a **flame**, a teardrop-shaped luminous envelope. The flame is, in essence, a "**window**" into the reaction zone, where the unseen chemical transformation (fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + heat) becomes visible to us as light and heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Gas Stove vs. the Candle**
> - A **Bunsen burner** (air premixed before combustion) produces a **blue, almost invisible flame** — efficient, complete combustion.
> - A **candle** (fuel vapor diffuses into surrounding air) produces a **yellow, luminous, sooty flame** — incomplete combustion, yielding carbon (soot) that glows yellow when heated to incandescence.
> This intuitive contrast maps directly onto the formal classification of **premixed** vs. **diffusion flames** in fire engineering.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following metrics are used throughout KTU 2024 Fire Engineering problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric Oxygen | O₂ (theo) | **Volume basis**: 21% of air |
| Molar mass of air | M_air | **28.97 g/mol** |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature | T_std | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard Pressure | P_std | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** |
| Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Methane in air) | T_ad | **~2223 K (≈1950 °C)** |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature of Methane | AIT | **~537 °C (810 K)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **adiabatic flame temperature** is the theoretical maximum temperature attained when combustion occurs with no heat loss to the surroundings — a critical upper-bound parameter in fire dynamics modeling.

### 1.4 Products of Combustion (POC) — Overview

When hydrocarbon fuels (CₓHᵧ) combust, the products of combustion are classified into the following categories:

- **Complete Combustion Products:** CO₂, H₂O, N₂, O₂ (excess)
- **Incomplete Combustion Products:** CO (carbon monoxide), unburnt hydrocarbons (HC), soot (C), H₂
- **Air-borne Contaminants:** SO₂, NO, NO₂ (NOx), HCN (from nitrogen-containing fuels), HCl (from chlorinated polymers)
- **Particulate Matter:** Smoke, aerosols, carbonaceous soot
- **Heat & Radiation:** IR, visible, UV radiation

The general balanced reaction for a hydrocarbon fuel is:

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \text{heat}$$

When air (rather than pure O₂) is the oxidizer, the nitrogen in air passes through as a thermal ballast:

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)N_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)N_2$$

The factor **3.76** arises because air is approximately **21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume**, so for every mole of O₂ there are **3.76 moles of N₂**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flames

A systematic understanding of flame types is essential to KTU Module 1 outcomes. Flames are classified on **two orthogonal axes**:

### 2.1.1 By Mixing Configuration

**A. Premixed Flames (Pre-mixed Combustion)**

In a premixed flame, the fuel and oxidizer are **mixed prior to the reaction zone**. Combustion occurs as the mixture flows into a region where temperature/ignition conditions are met. Examples: **Bunsen burner**, **spark-ignition engine**, **gas stove flame (blue)**.

Key characteristics:
- Reaction zone is **thin** (often sub-millimeter) and well-defined.
- Flame speed is a **property of the mixture** (laminar burning velocity, S_L).
- Combustion efficiency is high; little soot forms (provided the mixture is not too rich).
- Flame can **flash back** (propagate upstream into the unburnt mixture) if flow velocity is too low.

**B. Diffusion Flames (Non-premixed Combustion)**

In a diffusion flame, fuel and oxidizer are **initially separate** and mix by molecular or turbulent diffusion at the reaction zone. Examples: **candle flame**, **pool fire**, **wood fire**, **diesel engine flame**.

Key characteristics:
- Reaction zone is **broad** and diffusion-limited.
- Flame color is typically **yellow/orange** (luminous) due to soot incandescence.
- Combustion is often **incomplete**, producing CO, soot, and unburnt HC.
- Cannot flash back (mixing is incomplete upstream).

### 2.1.2 By Flow Regime

| Regime | Reynolds Number (Re) | Physical Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar Flame** | Re < ~2300 | Smooth, layered streamlines; thin, smooth reaction sheet |
| **Turbulent Flame** | Re > ~4000 | Chaotic eddies; thickened, wrinkled reaction zone; enhanced mixing |

The **Reynolds number** characterizing flame flow is given by:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, u \, L}{\mu}$$

where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $u$ = flow velocity (m/s), $L$ = characteristic length (m), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (kg/m·s).

### 2.2 Structure of a Laminar Diffusion Flame (e.g., Candle)

A canonical laminar diffusion flame consists of **three concentric zones**:

1. **Inner Zone (Fuel-Rich Core):** Unburnt fuel vapors flow upward. Temperature is **below the reaction threshold**. Often appears dark.
2. **Reaction Zone (Flame Sheet):** A thin annular region where fuel and O₂ meet and react. The temperature is at its **peak** (~1400 °C for a candle). This is the visible luminous envelope. Soot particles form in the fuel-rich side and oxidize on the O₂-rich side.
3. **Outer Zone (Oxidizer-Rich Plume):** Hot combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) mix with ambient air and rise by buoyancy. Temperature decreases radially outward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — The "Luminous Zone"**
> The yellow glow in a candle flame is **not** the flame itself but rather **incandescent soot particles** heated to ~1400 °C. In a clean, soot-free diffusion flame (e.g., hydrogen), the flame is **non-luminous and nearly invisible** — yet equally hot.

### 2.3 Laminar Burning Velocity (Premixed Flames)

The **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$ is the speed at which a planar, one-dimensional, adiabatic premixed flame propagates into a quiescent unburnt mixture. It is a fundamental property of the fuel-oxidizer mixture and is critical to flame stability analysis.

**Theoretical framework (Zel'dovich, von Kármán):**

$$S_L = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\tau_{ign}}}$$

where $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s), $\tau_{ign}$ = characteristic ignition delay time (s).

For methane-air at stoichiometric conditions:
$$S_L \approx 0.40 \text{ m/s (laminar)}$$

Turbulent burning velocity $S_T$ scales empirically as:
$$S_T \approx S_L \cdot (1 + C \cdot Re^{0.5})$$

where $C$ is a constant (~0.1–0.2 for jet flames).

### 2.4 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR_st)

The **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** is the minimum mass of air required to completely combust a unit mass of fuel (with no excess O₂). It is derived by balancing the combustion equation.

For a generic hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ:

$$\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{\text{Mass of stoichiometric air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} = \frac{\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times 4.76 \times M_{air}}{x \cdot M_C + y \cdot M_H}$$

where $4.76 = (1 + 3.76)$ accounts for O₂ plus accompanying N₂ per mole of O₂, $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol, $M_C = 12.01$ g/mol, $M_H = 1.008$ g/mol.

**Quick-reference values for common fuels:**

| Fuel | Formula | AFR_st (mass basis) | Heat of Combustion (HHV, MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH₄ | **17.2** | 55.5 |
| Propane | C₃H₈ | **15.7** | 50.3 |
| Octane (gasoline) | C₈H₁₈ | **15.1** | 47.9 |
| Methanol | CH₃OH | **6.4** | 22.7 |
| Hydrogen | H₂ | **34.3** | 142.0 |

### 2.5 Equivalence Ratio & Combustion Regimes

The **equivalence ratio** $\phi$ quantifies departure from stoichiometry:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is the **excess air ratio**. Three regimes are defined:

- $\phi = 1$ (or $\lambda = 1$): **Stoichiometric** (theoretical air, complete combustion)
- $\phi < 1$ (or $\lambda > 1$): **Lean** (excess air, lower flame temperature)
- $\phi > 1$ (or $\lambda < 1$): **Rich** (insufficient air, incomplete combustion, CO and soot)

### 2.6 Adiabatic Flame Temperature

The **adiabatic flame temperature** $T_{ad}$ is computed from the First Law for a constant-pressure, steady-flow, adiabatic combustion process:

$$\sum_i n_i \cdot \bar{h}_{f,i}^{\circ} \text{(reactants at } T_1) = \sum_j n_j \cdot \bar{h}_{f,j}^{\circ} \text{(products at } T_{ad})$$

Iterating over $T_{ad}$:

$$\sum_j n_j \int_{T_1}^{T_{ad}} \bar{c}_{p,j}(T)\, dT = -\sum_j n_j \bar{h}_{f,j}^{\circ} + \sum_i n_i \bar{h}_{f,i}^{\circ}$$

For methane stoichiometric combustion in air (simple estimate using constant $\bar{c}_p$):

$$T_{ad} \approx T_1 + \frac{\Delta H_c^°}{\sum_j n_j \bar{c}_{p,j}}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD KTU FORMULA TABLE — Products of Combustion & Flame**

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand for CₓHᵧ | $n_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4}$ | mol O₂ / mol fuel | Combustion stoichiometry |
| 2 | Stoichiometric air | $n_{air} = 4.76 \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$ | mol air / mol fuel | Air supply design |
| 3 | AFR (stoichiometric) | $\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{n_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{x M_C + y M_H}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Engine tuning, fire load |
| 4 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = (F/A)_{act} / (F/A)_{st}$ | dimensionless | Combustion regime classification |
| 5 | Excess air ratio | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | dimensionless | Boiler/combustor design |
| 6 | Laminar burning velocity | $S_L = \sqrt{\alpha / \tau_{ign}}$ | m/s | Flame stability, flashback |
| 7 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho u L / \mu$ | dimensionless | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| 8 | Adiabatic flame temperature | $T_{ad} = T_1 + \frac{\Delta H_c^°}{\sum n_j \bar{c}_{p,j}}$ | K | Fire severity, material selection |
| 9 | Mass flow rate of fuel for given HRR | $\dot{m}_f = \dot{Q} / \Delta H_c$ | kg/s | Fire growth modeling |
| 10 | Smoke yield (mass) | $y_s = \dot{m}_{smoke} / \dot{m}_{fuel}$ | kg/kg | Smoke hazard assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World Fire Engineering):**
> - In **compartment fire modeling** (e.g., NIST's FDS — Fire Dynamics Simulator), $\phi$ and $T_{ad}$ govern **heat release rate (HRR) per unit area**, which is the primary driver of flashover.
> - In **smoke control design** (NFPA 92), $y_s$ and the **yield of CO** determine the make-up air and exhaust capacities.
> - In **explosion venting** (NFPA 68), $S_L$ governs deflagration pressure rise rates.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Combustion of Methane (CH₄) and AFR_st

### Step 1 — Write the Unbalanced Reaction

We begin with the combustion of methane in pure oxygen:

$$CH_4 + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O$$

### Step 2 — Balance Carbon

1 C atom on the left → 1 C atom required on the right as CO₂:

$$CH_4 + O_2 \rightarrow 1\,CO_2 + H_2O$$

### Step 3 — Balance Hydrogen

4 H atoms on the left → 2 H₂O on the right:

$$CH_4 + O_2 \rightarrow 1\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2O$$

### Step 4 — Balance Oxygen

Right side has $1 \times 2 + 2 \times 1 = 4$ O atoms → requires 2 O₂ on the left:

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \rightarrow 1\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2O$$

This is the **stoichiometric equation in pure oxygen**. The stoichiometric O₂ coefficient is $x + y/4 = 1 + 4/4 = 2$, confirming the derivation.

### Step 5 — Convert to Air as Oxidizer (Include N₂)

For every mole of O₂, air contains 3.76 moles of N₂. Total O₂ = 2 mol, so N₂ = 2 × 3.76 = 7.52 mol:

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 7.52\,N_2 \rightarrow 1\,CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

### Step 6 — Compute Molecular Weights

$M_{CH_4} = 12.01 + 4(1.008) = 16.04$ g/mol

$M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol (given)

### Step 7 — Compute Mass-Based Air-Fuel Ratio

$$\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{n_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{M_{CH_4}} = \frac{9.52 \times 28.97}{16.04} = \frac{275.79}{16.04} \approx 17.19 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\text{AFR}_{st} \approx \mathbf{17.2}$ kg air/kg fuel for methane — matches the literature value within rounding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio from Volumetric Combustion Analysis

### Given

A furnace is fired with propane (C₃H₈) using 30% excess air.

### Step 1 — Write the Stoichiometric Equation in Air

For C₃H₈: $n_{O_2,st} = 3 + 8/4 = 5$ mol O₂. With air: $5 \times 4.76 = 23.8$ mol air per mol fuel.

$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 + 18.8\,N_2 \rightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 18.8\,N_2$$

### Step 2 — Apply 30% Excess Air

Excess air fraction = 0.30, so actual air = $1.30 \times$ stoichiometric air:

$$n_{O_2,act} = 5 \times 1.30 = 6.5 \text{ mol O}_2$$

$$n_{N_2,act} = 6.5 \times 3.76 = 24.44 \text{ mol N}_2$$

### Step 3 — Write the Actual Combustion Equation

$$C_3H_8 + 6.5\,O_2 + 24.44\,N_2 \rightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 1.5\,O_2 + 24.44\,N_2$$

### Step 4 — Compute Equivalence Ratio

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{act}}{(A/F)_{st}} = 1.30$$

$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.30} \approx 0.769$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\phi = 0.769 < 1$ → **Lean combustion regime**. The 1.5 mol of O₂ appears in the products, confirming excess.

## 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Flame Temperature Estimate for Methane

### Given

- Fuel: CH₄ at 298 K
- Oxidizer: Air at 298 K
- Stoichiometric mixture
- Higher Heating Value (HHV): $\Delta H_c^° = -890.4$ kJ/mol (combustion releases 890.4 kJ/mol)
- Assume products at $T_{ad}$ with constant average $\bar{c}_p$

### Step 1 — Energy Balance (Constant Pressure, Adiabatic)

$$\sum_i n_i h_i(T_{in}) = \sum_j n_j h_j(T_{ad})$$

$$\sum_i n_i \left[\bar{h}_{f,i}^° + \bar{c}_{p,i}(T_{in} - 298)\right] = \sum_j n_j \left[\bar{h}_{f,j}^° + \bar{c}_{p,j}(T_{ad} - 298)\right]$$

### Step 2 — Standard Heats of Formation (kJ/mol)

$\bar{h}_{f,CH_4}^° = -74.8$, $\bar{h}_{f,O_2}^° = 0$, $\bar{h}_{f,N_2}^° = 0$, $\bar{h}_{f,CO_2}^° = -393.5$, $\bar{h}_{f,H_2O(g)}^° = -241.8$

### Step 3 — Heat Released at 298 K

$$Q = \sum_j n_j |\bar{h}_{f,j}^°| - \sum_i n_i |\bar{h}_{f,i}^°| = (1)(393.5) + (2)(241.8) - (1)(74.8) - 0 = 393.5 + 483.6 - 74.8 = 802.3 \text{ kJ/mol CH}_4$$

> Note: This is the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** since water leaves as vapor. HHV would include condensation of H₂O (additional 2 × 44 kJ = 88 kJ), giving 890.3 kJ — consistent with the tabulated HHV.

### Step 4 — Sum of Molar Heat Capacities of Products

Using approximate $\bar{c}_p$ values (kJ/mol·K): CO₂ ≈ 0.044, H₂O(g) ≈ 0.040, N₂ ≈ 0.030

$$\sum n_j \bar{c}_{p,j} = 1(0.044) + 2(0.040) + 7.52(0.030) = 0.044 + 0.080 + 0.2256 = 0.3496 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)}$$

### Step 5 — Compute T_ad

Assuming all reactants enter at 298 K:

$$T_{ad} \approx 298 + \frac{802.3}{0.3496} \approx 298 + 2295 = 2593 \text{ K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The simplified estimate **2593 K** is somewhat higher than the more rigorous value (~2223 K) because we used constant $\bar{c}_p$ and ignored dissociation of CO₂ and H₂O at high temperatures (which absorbs energy and reduces $T_{ad}$). In KTU problems, dissociation is typically neglected unless explicitly stated.

### Step 6 — Cross-Check with Energy Balance Identity

$$\sum_i n_i \bar{h}_{f,i}^° - \sum_j n_j \bar{h}_{f,j}^° = \sum_j n_j \bar{c}_{p,j}(T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$802.3 \text{ kJ/mol} = 0.3496 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)} \times (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$T_{ad} - 298 = 2295 \text{ K} \Rightarrow T_{ad} \approx 2593 \text{ K} \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Complete vs. Incomplete Combustion Decision Tree

| Diagnostic Observation | Likely Cause | Combustion Regime | $\phi$ Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blue, non-luminous flame | Sufficient O₂, complete combustion | Lean / Stoichiometric | $\phi \leq 1$ |
| Yellow/orange luminous flame | Soot formation, incomplete combustion | Rich diffusion flame | $\phi > 1$ |
| Dark smoke from flame | Severely fuel-rich, soot exhausted unburned | Very rich | $\phi \gg 1$ |
| No visible flame, only hot gases | Flameless oxidation, very lean | Strongly lean | $\phi \ll 1$ |
| CO > 0.1% in flue gas | Incomplete combustion | Slightly rich or mixing-limited | Variable |
| Flame lift-off from burner port | Flow velocity > $S_L$ | Velocity-limited, blow-off risk | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Fire Engineering Note:** Post-flashover compartment fires often exhibit **ventilation-controlled burning** — i.e., $\phi \gg 1$ because air inflow through openings is rate-limiting. This is the regime in which **CO toxicity** and **smoke yield** peak, making it the most lethal phase of a structure fire.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Laminar Diffusion Flame — Three-Zone Architecture (Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates the canonical **three-zone structure** of a laminar diffusion flame (e.g., a candle), with the temperature profile and species gradients.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Ambient Air: O2 21 percent, N2 79 percent"] -->|"O2 inwards"| R
    F["Fuel Vapor: CxHy"] -->|"Fuel outwards"| R
    R["REACTION ZONE Flame Sheet"]
    R --> P["PRODUCTS: CO2, H2O, N2"]
    R --> S["SOOT: Carbonaceous particles"]
    S -->|"Incandesces at 1400 C"| G["Yellow Visible Glow"]
    P -->|"Buoyant rise"| H["Hot Plume Rises"]

    classDef zone fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef flow fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef hazard fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000

    class R,S zone
    class A,F flow
    class P,H product
    class G hazard
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**

- **Fuel vapor** issues from the wick (or nozzle) and flows **outward and upward**.
- **Ambient air** diffuses **inward** to meet the fuel.
- At the **reaction zone**, the two streams meet; the temperature peaks (~1400 °C for candle).
- **Soot** forms on the fuel-rich side of the reaction zone and is **oxidized** on the O₂-rich side; any soot escaping the reaction zone **incandesces**, producing the yellow glow.
- **Combustion products** and **hot gases** rise buoyantly as a plume.

## 4.2 Combustion Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["INPUTS"]
        I1["Fuel Storage Hydrocarbon Liquid Gas Solid"]
        I2["Oxidizer Air 21 percent O2 79 percent N2"]
        I3["Ignition Source Spark Pilot Hot Surface"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["COMBUSTION PROCESS"]
        P1["1. Fuel Vaporization or Pyrolysis"]
        P2["2. Mixing Diffusion or Premixed"]
        P3["3. Chemical Reaction Oxidation"]
        P4["4. Heat Release Exothermic"]
        P5["5. Flame Formation Visible Reaction Zone"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUTS Products of Combustion POC"]
        O1["CO2 Carbon Dioxide"]
        O2["H2O Water Vapor"]
        O3["N2 Nitrogen"]
        O4["CO Carbon Monoxide Toxic"]
        O5["Soot C Particulate"]
        O6["NOx SO2 Air Pollutants"]
        O7["Heat Radiation Convection"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    P5 --> O4
    P5 --> O5
    P5 --> O6
    P5 --> O7
```

**Functional interpretation:**

The combustion process is a **five-stage pipeline**:
1. **Vaporization/Pyrolysis** — Solid/liquid fuels must first vaporize or thermally decompose.
2. **Mixing** — Fuel vapors mix with oxidizer (premixed or diffusion).
3. **Chemical Reaction** — Oxidation reactions occur at the molecular level.
4. **Heat Release** — Chemical energy converts to thermal energy.
5. **Flame Formation** — The visible luminous zone marks the reaction zone.

The outputs (**POC**) include both desired products (CO₂, H₂O, heat) and hazardous byproducts (CO, soot, NOx, SO₂) that determine the **fire hazard profile**.

## 4.3 Premixed vs. Diffusion Flame Comparison — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRE["PREMIXED FLAME e.g. Bunsen Burner"]
        P_F["Fuel"] --> M["MIXER"]
        P_A["Air"] --> M
        M -->|"Homogeneous Mixture"| RZ1["Reaction Zone"]
        RZ1 --> P1["Hot Products"]
        RZ1 -->|". Blue Non-luminous"| N1["High Efficiency, Low Soot"]
    end

    subgraph DIF["DIFFUSION FLAME e.g. Candle"]
        D_F["Fuel"] --> RZ2["Reaction Zone"]
        D_A["Air"] -->|"Diffuses Inwards"| RZ2
        RZ2 --> D1["Hot Products"]
        RZ2 -->|"Yellow Luminous"| N2["Soot Incandesces"]
    end

    classDef premix fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef diff fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#993300,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fffacd,stroke:#999900,color:#000

    class PRE,M,RZ1,N1 premix
    class DIF,RZ2,N2 diff
```

**Engineering takeaway:**

The **fundamental distinction** is the **location of mixing** relative to the reaction zone:
- **Premixed:** Mixing is complete **before** reaction → uniform, predictable, often blue.
- **Diffusion:** Mixing occurs **at** the reaction zone by diffusion → broad reaction zone, often yellow/luminous.

This distinction governs **fire behavior, smoke production, and hazard** in real-world fires (most accidental fires are diffusion-dominated, hence the prevalence of CO and soot hazards).
