# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define a "flame" and distinguish clearly between a premixed flame and a diffusion flame. Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **flame** is the visible, luminous gaseous envelope of a combustion reaction, representing the zone in which the fuel and oxidizer are actively reacting and releasing thermal and radiant energy.

| Feature | Premixed Flame | Diffusion Flame |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing of fuel and oxidizer | **Before** the reaction zone | **At** the reaction zone by molecular/turbulent diffusion |
| Reaction zone thickness | Thin, well-defined sheet | Broad, diffusion-limited |
| Typical color | Blue, non-luminous | Yellow/orange, luminous (soot) |
| Combustion efficiency | High (complete combustion) | Lower (often incomplete) |
| Flashback risk | Yes (if flow < $S_L$) | No |

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Premixed flame:** Bunsen burner; gas stove (blue inner cone); spark-ignition (SI) engine combustion chamber.
- **Diffusion flame:** Candle flame; pool fire; wood/log fire; diesel engine combustion chamber.

*(Word count: ~120. Marks awarded for: definition 1, distinction table 1, examples 1.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the major products of complete and incomplete combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel. Why is carbon monoxide (CO) of particular concern in fire safety engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

**Products of Complete Combustion:**
- Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
- Water vapor (H₂O)
- Nitrogen (N₂) — from air
- Excess oxygen (O₂) — when $\phi < 1$

**Products of Incomplete Combustion:**
- Carbon monoxide (CO)
- Unburnt hydrocarbons (HC)
- Soot (solid carbon particles)
- Hydrogen (H₂)
- Polynuclear aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs)

**Why CO is of particular concern in fire safety:**

1. **Toxicity:** CO binds to hemoglobin with an affinity ~240× greater than O₂, forming **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**. Blood COHb levels of 50% cause loss of consciousness; >60% is frequently fatal.
2. **Colorless and Odorless:** CO is undetectable by human senses — victims are incapacitated before realizing exposure.
3. **Produced in large quantities in ventilation-controlled fires** (post-flashover compartment fires, $\phi \gg 1$).
4. **Leading cause of fire fatalities:** NFPA data attributes 60–70% of fire deaths in structure fires to CO poisoning (often combined with HCN from synthetic materials).

*(Marks awarded: products listing 1.5, CO concern justification 1.5.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal choice between two full questions. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** For the combustion of propane (C₃H₈) with **theoretical air** (21% O₂, 79% N₂ by volume), write the balanced stoichiometric equation and compute the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (AFR_st) on a mass basis**. Show all steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the propane is burned with **50% excess air**, determine the **equivalence ratio (φ)**, the **air-fuel ratio by mass**, and the **molar composition of the wet flue gas products** (per mole of fuel).

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Stoichiometric Combustion of C₃H₈

**Step 1: Stoichiometric oxygen requirement**

For CₓHᵧ: $n_{O_2,st} = x + y/4 = 3 + 8/4 = 3 + 2 = 5$ mol O₂ per mol C₃H₈.

**Step 2: Stoichiometric air requirement**

$$n_{air,st} = 5 \times (1 + 3.76) = 5 \times 4.76 = 23.8 \text{ mol air per mol fuel}$$

**Step 3: Balanced equation with air**

$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 + 18.8\,N_2 \rightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 18.8\,N_2$$

**Step 4: Compute molecular weights**

$M_{C_3H_8} = 3(12.01) + 8(1.008) = 36.03 + 8.064 = 44.094$ g/mol

$M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol

**Step 5: Compute AFR_st (mass basis)**

$$\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{n_{air,st} \times M_{air}}{M_{C_3H_8}} = \frac{23.8 \times 28.97}{44.094}$$

$$\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{689.486}{44.094} \approx 15.64 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $\text{AFR}_{st} \approx \mathbf{15.64}$ kg air/kg fuel — consistent with the literature value of 15.7.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 50% Excess Air Case

**Step 1: Determine actual air supplied**

Excess air fraction = 0.50, so actual O₂ supplied = $5 \times 1.50 = 7.5$ mol O₂

Actual N₂ supplied = $7.5 \times 3.76 = 28.2$ mol N₂

**Step 2: Compute equivalence ratio**

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{act}}{(F/A)_{st}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.50} \approx 0.667$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $\phi \approx \mathbf{0.667}$ → **Lean combustion regime** ($\phi < 1$).

**Step 3: Compute actual AFR by mass**

$$\text{AFR}_{act} = 1.50 \times \text{AFR}_{st} = 1.50 \times 15.64 = 23.46 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

> ✅ **Answer:** $\text{AFR}_{act} \approx \mathbf{23.46}$ kg air/kg fuel.

**Step 4: Compute product composition**

Combustion equation with 50% excess air:

$$C_3H_8 + 7.5\,O_2 + 28.2\,N_2 \rightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + 2.5\,O_2 + 28.2\,N_2$$

**Step 5: Total moles of products**

$$n_{total} = 3 + 4 + 2.5 + 28.2 = 37.7 \text{ mol products per mol fuel}$$

**Step 6: Mole fractions (wet basis)**

| Species | Moles | Mole Fraction (%) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 3.0 | 7.96% |
| H₂O | 4.0 | 10.61% |
| O₂ | 2.5 | 6.63% |
| N₂ | 28.2 | 74.80% |
| **Total** | **37.7** | **100.00%** |

> ✅ **Answer:** Wet flue gas composition: **74.80% N₂, 10.61% H₂O, 7.96% CO₂, 6.63% O₂** (by volume, since for ideal gases mole % = volume %).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **structure of a laminar diffusion flame** (e.g., candle flame). Identify the **three distinct zones** and describe the temperature profile and dominant species in each zone.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$ and explain its significance in:
  (i) **Flashback** phenomenon,
  (ii) **Blow-off** phenomenon,
  (iii) **Flame stability** in a Bunsen burner.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Laminar Diffusion Flame Structure

A laminar diffusion flame is a non-premixed flame in which fuel and oxidizer are initially separate and mix by molecular diffusion. The classic example is a candle flame.

**Three zones** (from inner core to outer plume):

**Zone 1 — Inner Dark Zone (Fuel-Rich Core):**
- **Location:** Innermost region, surrounding the wick.
- **Temperature:** Low (well below ignition temperature, typically 300–600 °C).
- **Species:** Unburnt fuel vapor (wax pyrolysis products: alkanes, alkenes, etc.) with **no oxygen** present.
- **Visual:** Dark or faintly luminous.

**Zone 2 — Reaction Zone (Luminous Flame Sheet):**
- **Location:** Annular sheath surrounding Zone 1.
- **Temperature:** Peak temperature (~1400 °C for candle; up to ~1900 °C in large diffusion flames).
- **Species:** Fuel and O₂ meet here; both are **consumed**. Products CO₂, H₂O, and intermediate CO, H₂, and **soot** are present.
- **Visual:** Bright yellow/orange (luminous due to **incandescent soot particles** heated to incandescence).

**Zone 3 — Outer Non-Luminous Zone (Oxidizer-Rich Plume):**
- **Location:** Outermost, surrounding Zone 2.
- **Temperature:** Decreases radially outward (from ~1400 °C to ambient).
- **Species:** Hot combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) mixing with ambient air; excess O₂ diffuses inward; soot oxidizes and disappears.
- **Visual:** Faintly bluish, often invisible.

**Temperature profile:** Increases from Zone 1 (low) to Zone 2 (peak) and decreases through Zone 3 (monotonic decay).

**Sketch reference:** See the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Laminar Burning Velocity and Flame Stability

**Definition:**
The **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$ is the speed at which a one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic premixed flame propagates into a quiescent unburnt gas mixture, normal to the flame surface. It is a thermodynamic-kinetic property of the fuel-oxidizer mixture (e.g., for methane-air at $\phi = 1$, $S_L \approx 0.40$ m/s).

**Significance in flame stability:**

**(i) Flashback:**
- **Phenomenon:** The flame propagates **upstream** into the unburnt premixture (back into the burner port or supply line).
- **Condition:** When the gas flow velocity $u$ at the burner port **decreases below** $S_L$ (i.e., $u < S_L$), the flame can travel upstream.
- **Engineering prevention:** Maintain $u > S_L$ at the burner exit; use **quenching** at the port (e.g., small port diameter less than the **quenching distance**, $\delta_q$).

**(ii) Blow-off (or Blow-out):**
- **Phenomenon:** The flame is **lifted off** the burner port and extinguished.
- **Condition:** When $u$ **exceeds** a critical velocity (related to the **blow-off velocity** $u_b$, which scales roughly as $u_b \propto S_L$), the flame can no longer anchor to the port.
- **Engineering prevention:** Use **pilot flames** or **flame holders** (e.g., perforated plates, gauze) to stabilize the flame at higher $u$.

**(iii) Flame Stability in a Bunsen Burner:**
- The **stability diagram** (often plotted as fuel flow rate vs. air flow rate) shows the operating envelope between flashback and blow-off.
- The flame cone height adjusts such that the **gas velocity at the cone surface equals $S_L$**.
- The **blue inner cone** of a Bunsen burner is a **premixed flame**; the **faint outer mantle** (if visible) is a **diffusion flame** where excess fuel escaping the inner cone burns with ambient air.

**Summary diagram (textual):**

$$u < S_L \Rightarrow \text{Flashback} \quad | \quad u = S_L \Rightarrow \text{Stable anchoring} \quad | \quad u > u_b \Rightarrow \text{Blow-off}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the N₂ in air:** Students frequently write combustion equations with O₂ alone, omitting the 3.76 N₂ per O₂. This causes **incorrect mole fractions** of flue gas products (N₂ dominates at ~75%!). Always include N₂ explicitly in the equation.
> 2. **Confusing $\phi$ and $\lambda$:** $\phi = (F/A)_{act}/(F/A)_{st}$, while $\lambda = (A/F)_{act}/(A/F)_{st}$. They are **reciprocals** ($\phi \lambda = 1$). Marking schemes deduct 1–2 marks for inversion.
> 3. **Using wet vs. dry flue gas inconsistently:** In combustion calculations, "wet" products include H₂O vapor; "dry" products exclude it. State your basis **explicitly** at the start of the solution.
> 4. **Neglecting dissociation at high $T_{ad}$:** Above ~2000 K, CO₂ and H₂O partially dissociate (CO₂ ⇌ CO + ½ O₂, H₂O ⇌ H₂ + ½ O₂), reducing $T_{ad}$. For KTU problems, dissociation is usually **ignored** unless explicitly mentioned.
> 5. **Confusing "luminous" with "hotter":** A yellow luminous flame (candle) is **not** hotter than a blue non-luminous flame (Bunsen). The yellow color is due to soot **incandescence**, not higher temperature. In fact, a stoichiometric premixed flame is typically **hotter** than a diffusion flame.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Products of Combustion: Flame**

- ✅ **Combustion equation** for CₓHᵧ: requires $x + y/4$ mol O₂ per mol fuel.
- ✅ **Air (not pure O₂)**: factor **4.76** (1 O₂ + 3.76 N₂) per mol O₂ in stoichiometric problems.
- ✅ **AFR_st (mass basis)** formula: $\text{AFR}_{st} = \frac{(x + y/4) \times 4.76 \times 28.97}{12.01x + 1.008y}$
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio** $\phi$: $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi = 1$ stoich, $\phi > 1$ rich.
- ✅ **Excess air ratio** $\lambda = 1/\phi$.
- ✅ **Premixed flame** (Bunsen): thin reaction zone, blue, $S_L$ governs stability.
- ✅ **Diffusion flame** (candle): broad reaction zone, yellow (soot), mixing-limited.
- ✅ **Laminar burning velocity** $S_L$: typical CH₄-air = 0.40 m/s; H₂-air = 2.80 m/s.
- ✅ **Flashback:** $u < S_L$; **Blow-off:** $u > u_b$ (critical).
- ✅ **Three zones of diffusion flame:** fuel-rich core (dark) → reaction zone (luminous, ~1400 °C peak) → oxidizer-rich plume (fades).
- ✅ **Adiabatic flame temperature** for CH₄-air stoich: ~2223 K (with dissociation) / ~2593 K (without).
- ✅ **Products of complete combustion:** CO₂, H₂O, N₂ (excess O₂ if lean).
- ✅ **Products of incomplete combustion:** CO, soot, H₂, unburnt HC, PAHs.
- ✅ **CO toxicity:** binds Hb 240× over O₂; primary killer in fire fatalities.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** classifies flow: Re < 2300 laminar, Re > 4000 turbulent.
- ✅ **Yellow color** = soot incandescence, **not** higher temperature.
- ✅ **NFPA 92** (smoke control), **NFPA 68** (explosion venting), **FDS** (NIST) — all use $\phi$, $S_L$, $T_{ad}$ as inputs.

> **End of Notes — Module 1, OECHT835 Fire Engineering, KTU 2024 Scheme**
> *These notes are mapped to Course Outcomes CO1 and CO2 of the KTU 2024 B.Tech (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus and follow the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, and Analyze.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Heat # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Heat (As per Fire Engineering Context) **Heat** is a form of energy in transit that flows spontaneously from a body at higher temperature to a body at lower temperature due to a temperature gradient. In the framework of Fire Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme), heat is the **driving potential** that initiates, sustains, and propagates combustion. It is measured in **Joules (J)** or **Calories (cal)** in the SI and CGS systems respectively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "Heat is the energy transferred between systems or bodies due to a temperature difference. In fire engineering, heat is the energy that causes ignition, sustains combustion, and is responsible for fire spread." The thermodynamic distinction is critical: - **Heat (Q)** → Energy *in transit* (Path function) - **Temperature (T)** → *State property* indicating the degree of hotness - **Internal Energy (U)** → Total microscopic kinetic + potential energy of molecules - **Thermal Energy** → The kinetic energy component associated with molecular motion ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine **water flowing downhill**: - **Water** represents **Heat** (the energy moving) - **Height of the hill** represents **Temperature** (the driving potential) - **Slope of the hill** represents the **Temperature Gradient (dT/dx)** - **The pipe/pipe diameter** represents **Thermal Conductivity (k)** Just as water never flows *uphill* on its own, heat never flows from a cold body to a hot body *spontaneously* (this is the Second Law of Thermodynamics). The hill (temperature) must be higher at the source. In a **fire scenario**: think of a burning matchstick. The flame generates heat, the surrounding air gets hot (temperature rises), and that hot air rises via convection, transferring heat to the ceiling — which is exactly the **fire spread mechanism** we study in fire engineering. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ | | Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | $8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$ | | Standard Temperature | $T_0$ | $25^{\circ}\text{C} = 298 \text{ K}$ | | Calorific Value of Methane | $HCV$ | $55.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | | Calorific Value of Wood (typical) | $HCV$ | $17 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | | Calorific Value of Gasoline | $HCV$ | $46 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | | Calorific Value of Paper | $HCV$ | $17 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | > [!NOTE] > In Fire Engineering, the **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** is typically expressed in **kilowatts (kW)** or **megawatts (MW)**, NOT in Joules. A typical small wood crib fire releases ~50 kW, while a fully developed room fire releases 1–10 MW. ## 1.4 Heat in the Fire Triangle / Tetrahedron The **Fire Tetrahedron** consists of four elements: 1. **Fuel** (combustible material) 2. **Oxidizer** (typically atmospheric $O_2$) 3. **Heat (or Activation Energy)** — *our focus* 4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat plays a dual role in fire**: (1) It is the *initiator* (activation energy for ignition), and (2) It is the *product* of the exothermic combustion reaction. Removing heat (cooling) is one of the three classical fire suppression methods. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Modes of Heat Transfer — The Three Pillars of Fire Spread Heat is transferred by three distinct mechanisms. Understanding each is **critical** for KTU examination purposes as this is a frequently tested area (typically 7–14 marks in Part B). ### A. Conduction (Solid/Stationary Media) Heat transfer through a material by **molecular vibration and free electron transfer** without bulk motion of the medium. **Governing Equation (Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction):** $$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$ Where: - $q$ = Heat flux (W/m²) - $k$ = Thermal conductivity of the material (W/m·K) - $dT/dx$ = Temperature gradient (K/m) - Negative sign indicates heat flows from hot to cold **For a plane wall of thickness $L$:** $$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$ **Thermal Resistance Analogy (Very Important for KTU):** $$R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad \text{(like electrical resistance } R = \rho L/A\text{)}$$ ### B. Convection (Fluids in Motion) Heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas). In fires, **hot smoke rising to the ceiling is a convective process**. **Governing Equation (Newton's Law of Cooling):** $$Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$$ Where: - $h$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $T_s$ = Surface temperature - $T_\infty$ = Bulk fluid temperature Typical values of $h$: - Natural convection in air: $h \approx 5-25 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ - Forced convection in air: $h \approx 10-200 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ - Boiling water: $h \approx 2500-75000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ ### C. Radiation (Electromagnetic Waves) Heat transfer via **electromagnetic waves** — does NOT require a medium (can occur in vacuum). This is the dominant mode in large fires and flame-to-target heat transfer. **Governing Equation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law):** $$Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$$ Where: - $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity (0 ≤ ε ≤ 1) - $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ - $A$ = Surface area - $T$ = Absolute temperature (Kelvin) **Net radiation exchange between two surfaces (view factor $F_{12}$):** $$Q_{1 \to 2} = \varepsilon \sigma A_1 F_{12} (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they form 80% of numerical problems in KTU ESE.** | # | Concept | Formula | SI Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ | W | | 2 | Thermal Resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | K/W | | 3 | Thermal Conductivity | $k = \dfrac{QL}{A\Delta T}$ | W/m·K | | 4 | Heat Flux | $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | W/m² | | 5 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | | 6 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | W | | 7 | Heat of Combustion (Lower) | $LCV = HCV - h_fg \times 9H/100$ | kJ/kg | | 8 | Calorimetric Heat Release | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | kW | | 9 | Ignition Temperature | $T_{ig}$ (material property) | °C or K | | 10 | Fire Load | $q = \dfrac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_{floor}}$ | MJ/m² | | 11 | Heat Transfer by Convection (mass flow) | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | W | | 12 | Biot Number (Internal vs Surface) | $Bi = \dfrac{hL_c}{k}$ | Dimensionless | | 13 | Fourier Number (Transient) | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L^2}$ | Dimensionless | | 14 | Emissivity Power | $q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | | 15 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | m²/s | ## 2.3 Why These Matter in Engineering Practice - **Fire Safety Design**: Engineers use the conduction equation to design **fire-resistant walls and insulation**. - **Sprinkler System Design**: Convection and radiation heat transfer determine **sprinkler activation time** and **drop size**. - **Smoke Management**: Convective heat in smoke plumes drives HVAC design for stairwells. - **Heat Detection**: Smoke and heat detectors rely on the convective/radiative heat flux thresholds. - **Structural Fire Engineering**: Steel loses strength above 550°C — heat transfer determines **time-to-collapse**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis**: Questions on **fire load calculation**, **calorific value**, and **heat release rate** are almost guaranteed in the ESE (End Semester Examination). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Conduction Through a Composite Wall **Problem Context:** A furnace wall is constructed of three layers — fire brick (inside), insulating brick (middle), and red brick (outside). Determine the steady-state heat transfer rate per unit area. **Given:** - Fire brick: $k_1 = 1.7 \text{ W/m·K}$, $L_1 = 0.115 \text{ m}$ - Insulating brick: $k_2 = 0.28 \text{ W/m·K}$, $L_2 = 0.230 \text{ m}$ - Red brick: $k_3 = 0.7 \text{ W/m·K}$, $L_3 = 0.115 \text{ m}$ - Inside surface temp: $T_1 = 1500 \text{ K}$ - Outside surface temp: $T_4 = 350 \text{ K}$ **Step 1: Apply Fourier's Law to Each Layer** For steady-state 1-D conduction (heat flux is constant through all layers): $$q = k_1 \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} = k_2 \frac{(T_2 - T_3)}{L_2} = k_3 \frac{(T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}$$ **Step 2: Use Thermal Resistance Network (Series)** Total thermal resistance per unit area: $$R_{total} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3}$$ **Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values** $$\frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.115}{1.7} = 0.0676 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$\frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.230}{0.28} = 0.8214 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$\frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.115}{0.7} = 0.1643 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$R_{total} = 0.0676 + 0.8214 + 0.1643 = 1.0533 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ **Step 4: Compute Heat Flux** $$q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{1500 - 350}{1.0533}$$ $$q = \frac{1150}{1.0533} = 1091.8 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Compute Interface Temperatures (Verification for KTU)** Find $T_2$ (between fire brick and insulating brick): $$T_2 = T_1 - q \cdot \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 1500 - 1091.8 \times 0.0676$$ $$T_2 = 1500 - 73.8 = 1426.2 \text{ K}$$ Find $T_3$ (between insulating brick and red brick): $$T_3 = T_2 - q \cdot \frac{L_2}{k_2} = 1426.2 - 1091.8 \times 0.8214$$ $$T_3 = 1426.2 - 896.8 = 529.4 \text{ K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** $T_4 = T_3 - q \cdot (L_3/k_3) = 529.4 - 1091.8 \times 0.1643 = 529.4 - 179.4 = 350 \text{ K}$ ✓ (matches the given value) ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat of Combustion and Heat Release Rate **Definition (KTU Standard):** The **Heat of Combustion ($\Delta H_c$)** is the heat released when 1 kg (or 1 kmol) of fuel is completely burned with oxygen at constant pressure (or volume). **Higher Calorific Value (HCV / Gross Calorific Value):** Heat released assuming water vapor in products is condensed to liquid (latent heat recovered). **Lower Calorific Value (LCV / Net Calorific Value):** Heat released when water leaves as vapor (typical in fires). **Conversion Formula:** $$LCV = HCV - 9 \times H \times h_{fg} \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Where: - $H$ = mass % of hydrogen in the fuel - $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water ≈ $2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $9$ comes from the stoichiometry: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O$, where 2 kg of $H$ produces 18 kg of $H_2O$ **Example Calculation:** - A fuel has $HCV = 45000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and contains $H = 6\%$ by mass. - Find $LCV$. $$LCV = 45000 - 9 \times 6 \times 2260 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$LCV = 45000 - 122.04 = 44877.96 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fire Load Calculation (Mandatory KTU Question Type) **Fire Load (q)** is defined as the total calorific energy of all combustible materials in a compartment per unit floor area: $$q = \frac{1}{A_{floor}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \cdot H_i$$ Where: - $M_i$ = mass of the $i^{th}$ combustible material (kg) - $H_i$ = calorific value of the $i^{th}$ material (kJ/kg) - $A_{floor}$ = floor area (m²) - $q$ has units of MJ/m² (or kJ/m²) **Numerical Example (KTU Style):** A room of floor area $5 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} = 20 \text{ m}^2$ contains: - 200 kg of wood furniture ($H_{wood} = 17000 \text{ kJ/kg}$) - 50 kg of paper ($H_{paper} = 17000 \text{ kJ/kg}$) - 30 kg of plastic (PVC, $H_{PVC} = 17000 \text{ kJ/kg}$) **Step 1: Total Calorific Content** $$Q_{total} = (200 \times 17000) + (50 \times 17000) + (30 \times 17000)$$ $$Q_{total} = 3400000 + 850000 + 510000 = 4760000 \text{ kJ} = 4760 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 2: Fire Load** $$q = \frac{4760 \text{ MJ}}{20 \text{ m}^2} = 238 \text{ MJ/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Classification (as per Indian Standard IS 875 / NBC 2016)** | Fire Load (MJ/m²) | Occupancy Hazard | |---|---| | < 250 | Light Hazard | | 250 – 500 | Moderate Hazard (Ordinary) | | 500 – 1000 | High Hazard | | > 1000 | Very High Hazard | **Conclusion:** This room is classified as **Light Hazard** (low fire load). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show the **unit conversion** (kg × kJ/kg = kJ) and the **final unit conversion to MJ/m²** for full marks. The classification table is a favorite 7-mark question. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Heat Transfer Modes | Property | Conduction | Convection | Radiation | |---|---|---|---| | Medium Required | Yes (Solid preferred) | Yes (Fluid only) | No (Vacuum OK) | | Mechanism | Molecular vibration | Bulk fluid motion | EM waves | | Governing Law | Fourier's Law | Newton's Law of Cooling | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | | Key Property | $k$ (W/m·K) | $h$ (W/m²·K) | $\varepsilon$ (dimensionless) | | T-Dependence | Linear in $T$ | Linear in $T$ | $T^4$ (highly nonlinear) | | Speed | Slow | Moderate | Fast (speed of light) | | Dominant in | Wall, floor, steel | Smoke, plume, fire gases | Flame, hot surfaces | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Heat Transfer Modes in a Compartment Fire (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ignition Source
Match or Spark] --> B[Flame Zone
High Temperature] B --> C1[Conduction
Through Wall] B --> C2[Convection
Hot Smoke Rises] B --> C3[Radiation
EM Waves from Flame] C1 --> D1[Wall Material
Steel Wood Concrete] C2 --> D2[Ceiling Smoke Layer
Hot Gases] C3 --> D3[Adjacent Fuel
Painted Wall Furniture] D1 --> E[Heat Accumulation
in Structure] D2 --> F[Fire Spread
via Ceiling Jet] D3 --> G[Secondary Ignition
Flashover Risk] E --> H[Structural Failure
Time to Collapse] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Full Room Fire
Engulfment] ``` ## 4.2 Fire Tetrahedron with Heat Highlighted (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR F[Fuel
Wood Gasoline Paper] -->|provides mass| R[Chain Reaction] OX[Oxygen
21 percent Air] -->|supports oxidation| R HT[Heat
Activation Energy] -->|initiates and sustains| R R[Uninhibited
Chain Reaction] -->|produces| HT R -->|produces| FR[Flame Fire] style HT fill:#ff6b35,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style FR fill:#d62828,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Heat Flow Through Composite Wall (Mermaid Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[T1 Hot Side
1500 K] --> L1[Layer 1 Fire Brick
k1 1.7 L1 0.115] L1 --> T2[Interface
T2 1426 K] T2 --> L2[Layer 2 Insulating Brick
k2 0.28 L2 0.230] L2 --> T3[Interface
T3 529 K] T3 --> L3[Layer 3 Red Brick
k3 0.7 L3 0.115] L3 --> T4[T4 Cold Side
350 K] style T1 fill:#ff0000,color:#fff style T4 fill:#0000ff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Fire Load Classification Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Combustible Mass in Room] --> B[Sum Mi times Hi
kJ] B --> C[Divide by Floor Area
MJ per m2] C --> D{Compare with IS 875} D -->|Below 250| E1[Light Hazard] D -->|250 to 500| E2[Moderate Hazard] D -->|500 to 1000| E3[High Hazard] D -->|Above 1000| E4[Very High Hazard] E1 --> F1[Design Sprinklers
Light Density] E2 --> F2[Ordinary Hazard
Sprinkler System] E3 --> F3[High Hazard
Sprinkler System] E4 --> F4[Extra High Hazard
with Hydrants] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Heat" and distinguish it from "Temperature".** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Heat** is a form of energy in transit that flows from a body at higher temperature to a body at lower temperature due to a temperature gradient. It is measured in **Joules (J)**. > > **Temperature** is a thermodynamic state property that indicates the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It is measured in **Kelvin (K)** or **°C**. > > | Heat | Temperature | > |---|---| > | Path function | Point function | > | Measured in J | Measured in K | > | Causes temperature change | Causes heat transfer | > | Exists only in transit | Exists as a property | ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **State the Stefan-Boltzmann Law and explain the significance of emissivity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a black body is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**: > > $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$ > > Where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. > > **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** is the ratio of the radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a black body at the same temperature ($0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$). A polished surface has low emissivity (~0.05), while a sooty surface has high emissivity (~0.95). This is significant in fire engineering because **flames and soot are highly emissive** and dominate radiative heat transfer. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### Q.A. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three modes of heat transfer with neat sketches. Derive the expression for heat transfer through a composite wall. **(b) [7 Marks]** A composite wall of an industrial furnace is made of 0.10 m thick fire brick ($k = 1.4 \text{ W/m·K}$), 0.20 m thick insulating brick ($k = 0.21 \text{ W/m·K}$), and 0.15 m thick red brick ($k = 0.7 \text{ W/m·K}$). The inside and outside surface temperatures are 1400 K and 340 K respectively. Calculate: - (i) The heat loss per unit area. - (ii) The interface temperatures. - (iii) The percentage heat saved compared to a wall of 0.45 m thick fire brick only. ### Model Solution for Q.A: **(a) Three modes of heat transfer [7 marks]:** **1. Conduction** [2 marks]: It is the transfer of heat through a material by molecular vibration and free electron motion without the bulk movement of the medium. Governed by **Fourier's Law**: $$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$ **2. Convection** [2 marks]: It is the transfer of heat between a solid surface and a fluid in motion. In a fire, hot smoke rises and transfers heat to the ceiling. Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $$Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$$ **3. Radiation** [2 marks]: It is the transfer of heat by electromagnetic waves that requires no medium. Governed by **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $$Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$$ **Derivation for composite wall** [1 mark]: For steady-state conduction through layers in series, the heat flux is constant, and the total resistance is the sum of individual resistances: $$q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:** **Step 1: Calculate individual thermal resistances per unit area:** $$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.4} = 0.0714 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.20}{0.21} = 0.9524 \text{ m}^2\text/K\text{W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.15}{0.7} = 0.2143 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$R_{total} = 0.0714 + 0.9524 + 0.2143 = 1.2381 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2: Heat loss per unit area:** $$q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{1400 - 340}{1.2381} = \frac{1060}{1.2381}$$ $$\boxed{q = 856.1 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **Step 3: Interface temperatures:** $$T_2 = T_1 - q R_1 = 1400 - 856.1 \times 0.0714 = 1400 - 61.1 = 1338.9 \text{ K}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 - q R_2 = 1338.9 - 856.1 \times 0.9524 = 1338.9 - 815.4 = 523.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4: Heat loss with fire brick only (0.45 m):** $$R_{only} = \frac{0.45}{1.4} = 0.3214 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$q_{only} = \frac{1060}{0.3214} = 3298.0 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 5: Percentage heat saved:** $$\% \text{ saved} = \frac{q_{only} - q_{composite}}{q_{only}} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ saved} = \frac{3298.0 - 856.1}{3298.0} \times 100 = \frac{2441.9}{3298.0} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ heat saved} = 74.04\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget to include the **negative sign** interpretation of Fourier's Law. The law itself has a negative sign indicating direction, but when computing magnitude of heat flux, we drop the sign. Also, ensure the units are **W/m²** (heat flux) and NOT W (heat rate) unless area is given. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice ### Q.B. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Define: - (i) Heat of Combustion (HCV and LCV) - (ii) Fire Load - (iii) Heat Release Rate (HRR) Explain with examples how fire load classification determines the sprinkler system design as per IS 875 / NFPA standards. **(b) [7 Marks]** A fire safety audit of a 6 m × 5 m office room is conducted. The following combustible materials are present: | Material | Mass (kg) | Calorific Value (kJ/kg) | |---|---|---| | Wooden furniture | 350 | 17,000 | | Paper (files, books) | 80 | 17,000 | | Cotton curtains | 25 | 18,400 | | Plastic insulation (PVC) | 15 | 17,000 | Calculate: - (i) Total fire load in MJ/m² - (ii) Classify the occupancy hazard - (iii) Estimate the equivalent heat release rate if 50% of the load burns in 600 seconds ### Model Solution for Q.B: **(a) Definitions [7 marks]:** **(i) Heat of Combustion [2 marks]:** - **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** — Total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely burned and the products are cooled such that water vapor condenses to liquid (latent heat recovered). - **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** — Heat released when water leaves as vapor (typical in fires). $$LCV = HCV - 9H \cdot h_{fg} \times 10^{-3}$$ **(ii) Fire Load [2 marks]:** Total calorific energy of all combustible materials per unit floor area: $$q = \frac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_{floor}} \quad \text{[MJ/m}^2\text{]}$$ **(iii) Heat Release Rate (HRR) [1 mark]:** Rate of heat generation by the fire, typically measured in kW or MW. It is the most important variable in characterizing fire severity. **Sprinkler design based on fire load [2 marks]:** | Fire Load (MJ/m²) | Hazard Class | Sprinkler Density (mm/min) | |---|---|---| | < 250 | Light | 2.5 | | 250 – 500 | Ordinary (Group 1-3) | 5.0 – 8.0 | | 500 – 1000 | High (Extra Hazard Group 1) | 12.5 | | > 1000 | Very High (Extra Hazard Group 2) | 25+ | **(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:** **Floor area:** $A = 6 \times 5 = 30 \text{ m}^2$ **Step 1: Total Calorific Energy** $$E_{wood} = 350 \times 17000 = 5,950,000 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{paper} = 80 \times 17000 = 1,360,000 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{cotton} = 25 \times 18400 = 460,000 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{PVC} = 15 \times 17000 = 255,000 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{total} = 5,950,000 + 1,360,000 + 460,000 + 255,000 = 8,025,000 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{total} = 8025 \text{ MJ}$$ **Step 2: Fire Load** $$q = \frac{8025 \text{ MJ}}{30 \text{ m}^2} = 267.5 \text{ MJ/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Occupancy Classification** Since $250 < 267.5 < 500$, this falls under **Moderate / Ordinary Hazard (Group 2)**. **Step 4: Equivalent HRR** Energy released from 50% of total load: $$E_{released} = 0.5 \times 8025 = 4012.5 \text{ MJ}$$ Convert to kJ: $4,012,500 \text{ kJ}$ Time = $600 \text{ s}$ $$\dot{Q} = \frac{4,012,500 \text{ kJ}}{600 \text{ s}} = 6687.5 \text{ kW} \approx 6.69 \text{ MW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In HRR calculations, students commonly forget the **time component** or use **wrong units**. Ensure energy is in **kJ** and time in **seconds** to get HRR in **kW**. Watch out: 1 MJ = 1000 kJ. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This:** ✅ **Heat** = Energy in transit; **Temperature** = State property (degree of hotness) ✅ **Three modes of heat transfer** = Conduction + Convection + Radiation ✅ **Fourier's Law**: $Q = kA\Delta T / L$ — conduction dominant in solids (walls, steel) ✅ **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ — convection in smoke plumes ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ — radiation dominant in flame ✅ **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** = 0 (polished) to 1 (black body); soot has $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$ ✅ **HCV vs LCV**: LCV = HCV - 9 × H% × $h_{fg}$ × $10^{-3}$ ✅ **Fire Load** ($q$) = Total energy / Floor area, in **MJ/m²** ✅ **Fire Load classification**: <250 Light | 250–500 Moderate | 500–1000 High | >1000 Very High ✅ **HRR units**: kW or MW (typical room fire 1–10 MW) ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron**: Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Chain Reaction ✅ **Thermal resistance analogy**: $R_{th} = L/kA$ (series resistances add up) ✅ **Biot number** $Bi = hL_c/k$: tells if internal conduction or surface convection dominates ✅ **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$: how fast heat diffuses through a material ✅ **SI Units for Heat**: Joules (J); **Power**: Watt (W) = J/s ✅ **Fire spread direction**: Always from hot to cold (spontaneous, Second Law of Thermodynamics) ✅ **Time-temperature curve for fire**: Use the standard ISO 834 or ASTM E119 curve for structural design ✅ **In KTU exam**: Always show units, show all algebraic steps, draw neat diagrams, and state the final answer in a **box** for clarity. --- > **End of Module 1 - Heat | OECHT835 Fire Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Smoke
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoke

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Smoke

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of *Fire Engineering* lays the foundation by distinguishing the gaseous, liquid, and solid products of a fire. Within this module, **Smoke** is treated as the *primary killer* in fire statistics — the toxic, obscuring, and heat-carrying airborne effluent released during thermal decomposition and combustion.

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Smoke** is the airborne suspension of **solid particulates (soot/carbonaceous aerosols)**, **condensed liquid droplets (tars, moisture)**, and **toxic/irritant gases** that is evolved when a material undergoes **pyrolysis, thermal degradation, or incomplete combustion**.

Mathematically, smoke is conventionally characterized as the *buoyant, contaminated gas-particulate mixture* leaving the combustion zone, and its engineering property of interest is quantified by the **optical density** and the **mass smoke yield** ($m_s$ in **kg of smoke per kg of fuel burned**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (use verbatim in 2-mark answers):**
> *"Smoke is a heterogeneous mixture of heated gases, vapors, suspended solid particulates, and condensed liquid aerosols produced during the thermal decomposition or incomplete combustion of a material, and it is characterized by its optical density, toxicity, temperature, and mass yield."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen pressure cooker releasing steam** through its safety valve. The steam is invisible at the nozzle, but as it hits cooler air it forms a white, billowing cloud. Now imagine the same cooker, but with **burning oil instead of water**:
- The **white cloud** becomes **black/grey smoke** (soot particulates).
- The **steam** is replaced by **hot toxic gases** (CO, CO₂, HCN, HCl).
- The cloud **rises rapidly to the ceiling** because it is *buoyancy-driven* (hotter than air).
- It then **spreads horizontally** along the ceiling, forming a hot upper layer that *descends* as more smoke is added.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Treat smoke as a **"hot, dirty, poisonous balloon"** that *always wants to rise, spread, and fill a room from the top down*. This simple mental model correctly predicts almost every smoke movement phenomenon (stack effect, ceiling jets, layer descent, and backlayering).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally adopted in fire engineering and codified in **ISO 5659 (smoke chamber)** and **ASTM E662**:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ambient temperature | $T_\infty$ | **293 K (20 °C)** | Reference state for plume calculations |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Drives buoyancy |
| Specific heat of air | $c_p$ | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** | Used in plume equations |
| Specific extinction area | $\sigma_s$ | **m²/kg** (typ. 100–1500 for plastics) | Soot production per kg fuel |
| Optical density | $D_L$ | dimensionless (per m) | Log-attenuation of light |
| Specific optical density | $D_s$ | dimensionless | Normalized obscuration index |
| Smoke yield | $m_s$ | **kg smoke/kg fuel** | Mass conversion factor |

> [!NOTE]
> The **specific extinction area ($\sigma_s$)** is the single most important soot-property — it is *measured in a cone calorimeter* and used directly in CFD/Foam smoke calculations. Memorize its range: **~50 m²/kg** for well-ventilated wood fires, up to **~1500 m²/kg** for flaming polyurethane.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of Smoke — Tri-Phase Mixture

Smoke is **NOT a single substance**. It is a four-component mixture:

1. **Gaseous phase (≈ 70–90 % by volume):** CO, CO₂, H₂O, HCl, HCN, SO₂, NOₓ.
2. **Solid phase (soot particulates, ≈ 1–10 % by mass):** Carbonaceous agglomerates 0.01–5 µm in diameter.
3. **Liquid phase (condensed aerosols):** Tars, water droplets, unburned hydrocarbons.
4. **Vapor phase:** High-molecular-weight pyrolysis products (PAHs, phenols).

## 2.2 Generation Mechanisms (Why Smoke is Produced)

The pedagogical "why" of smoke production proceeds in four logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Pyrolysis / Thermal Decomposition:** When a solid fuel is heated above ~300 °C, long-chain polymers break into smaller volatile fragments. *Why?* The bond energies of C–C, C–H, and C–O linkages are exceeded.
- **Step 2 — Vaporization & Mixing:** These volatile fragments mix with entrained air. *Why?* Vapors follow the buoyancy-driven flow upward.
- **Step 3 — Incomplete Combustion (Soot Nucleation):** In fuel-rich zones, hydrocarbons crack to form polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) that nucleate into soot particles. *Why?* Insufficient oxygen (under-ventilated) leads to fuel-rich pockets.
- **Step 4 — Aggregation & Optical Effects:** Soot particles agglomerate; light scatters/absorbs on them. *Why?* Particle diameters (0.1–1 µm) are comparable to visible-light wavelengths → **maximum optical obscuration**.

## 2.3 Key Engineering Properties of Smoke

| Property | Physical Meaning | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical density ($D_L$)** | Light extinction per unit path | Reduces visibility → hampers escape |
| **Specific optical density ($D_s$)** | Chamber-normalized obscuration | Material ranking in smoke tests |
| **Toxicity** | LC₅₀ of combustion products | Determines tenability / FED |
| **Temperature ($T_s$)** | Smoke gas temperature | Drives buoyancy & layer spread |
| **Yield ($m_s$)** | kg of smoke per kg of fuel | Mass balance input for filling time |
| **Visibility ($V$)** | Distance at which a sign is legible | Egress design criterion |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **board-exam essentials**. Memorize them as they appear — substitution into a numerical problem is a guaranteed 7–14 marker.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_L = \dfrac{1}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Optical density per unit length, $L$ in metres |
| 2 | $D_s = \dfrac{V}{\alpha\,L}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Specific optical density (ASTM E662), $V$=chamber volume, $\alpha$=exposed area |
| 3 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{D_s}{\rho_s\,L}$ | Specific extinction area, m²/kg |
| 4 | $V_{vis} = \dfrac{C}{D_s}$ | Visibility (m); $C \approx 3$ for illuminated signs, $\approx 8$ for reflective |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_p = 0.071\,\dot{Q}_c^{1/3}\,z^{5/3}$ | **Zukoski plume mass flow**, $\dot{m}_p$ kg/s, $\dot{Q}_c$ in kW, $z$ in m |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_p = 0.0058\,\dot{Q}_c\,z$ | **Heskestad plume** (alternative form) |
| 7 | $\Delta T_p = 9.1\left(\dfrac{\dot{Q}_c^{2/3}}{z^{5/3}}\right)$ | Plume centerline temperature rise (°C) |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_c = 0.7\,\dot{Q}$ | Convective fraction of total HRR (typical $\chi_c = 0.7$) |
| 9 | $t^2$ **fire**: $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ | Growing fire, $\alpha$ in kW/s² |
| 10 | $t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{2\,A_{\text{floor}}\,(H-z_{\text{crit}})}{0.071\,\dot{Q}_c^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}$ | Smoke filling time (s) |
| 11 | $FED = \sum_i \dfrac{C_i\,t_{eff,i}}{LC_{50,i}}$ | Fractional Effective Dose for toxicity |
| 12 | $\Delta p = \rho_0\,g\,H\,\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_0}$ | Stack-effect pressure (Pa), $H$=height |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol trap:** In the formulas above, *never* write absolute values as `|x|` inside a markdown table. Use `\vert x \vert` or $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX mode. This is a known markdown-table rendering bug.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Egress design (NFPA 101, IS 1644):** Visibility ≤ 10 m triggers evacuation alarm.
- **Smoke control in atria:** Plume equations feed into atrium smoke exhaust sizing.
- **Railway tunnels (NFPA 502):** FED model determines tenable evacuation time.
- **Warehouse storage:** $\sigma_s$ data used for smoke venting calculations.
- **Aircraft cabin certification:** $D_s \le 200$ at 4 minutes is the FAR 25.853 criterion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Optical Density from Beer–Lambert Law

**Given:** A beam of monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ traverses a smoke-filled chamber of path length $L$, emerging with intensity $I$.

**Step 1:** Apply **Beer–Lambert's law** for extinction by suspended particulates:

$$I = I_0\,e^{-\sigma_s\,M}$$

where $M = \rho_s\,L$ is the *smoke mass per unit area* (kg/m²) and $\sigma_s$ is the mass extinction coefficient.

**Step 2:** Convert natural log to base-10 (since obscuration measurements use base-10):

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \dfrac{\sigma_s\,M}{2.303} = \dfrac{\sigma_s\,\rho_s\,L}{2.303}$$

**Step 3:** Define the **transmittance-based optical density per unit length**:

$$D_L = \dfrac{1}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right) = \dfrac{\sigma_s\,\rho_s}{2.303}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that in the ASTM E662 chamber, $M = (\rho_s \cdot V)/A$. Define the **specific optical density**:

$$D_s = \dfrac{V}{\alpha\,L}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $M = \rho_s L$ and $\alpha = A$ (exposed specimen area):

$$D_s = \dfrac{V}{\alpha\,L}\cdot D_L \cdot L = \dfrac{V}{\alpha}\,D_L = \dfrac{V\,\sigma_s\,\rho_s}{2.303\,\alpha}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The factor $V/\alpha$ normalizes the optical measurement to the *chamber geometry* so that results from different labs are directly comparable. Numerically, $V/\alpha \approx 814$ for the standard E662 chamber (47.6 cm × 91.4 cm × 0.91 m × 0.6 m / 0.0046 m²).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Smoke Filling Time for a $t^2$ Growing Fire

**Given:** A compartment of floor area $A_{\text{floor}}$ and height $H$, with a growing $t^2$ fire of growth coefficient $\alpha$.

**Step 1:** Total heat release rate (HRR) at time $t$:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha\,t^2$$

**Step 2:** Convective HRR (only the convective fraction generates buoyant smoke plume):

$$\dot{Q}_c = \chi_c \cdot \dot{Q}(t) = 0.7\,\alpha\,t^2$$

**Step 3:** Smoke mass flow rate at ceiling using **Zukoski's plume correlation**:

$$\dot{m}_s(t) = 0.071\,(\dot{Q}_c)^{1/3}\,H^{5/3} = 0.071\,(0.7\,\alpha\,t^2)^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using $(0.7)^{1/3} \approx 0.888$:

$$\dot{m}_s(t) = 0.071 \times 0.888 \times \alpha^{1/3}\,t^{2/3}\,H^{5/3} = 0.0631\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}\,t^{2/3}$$

**Step 5:** Mass balance on smoke layer of thickness $H - z$ (where $z$ is interface height). Smoke fills from top; layer descends at rate:

$$A_{\text{floor}}\,\rho_s\,\dfrac{dz}{dt} = -\dot{m}_s(t)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\rho_s \approx \rho_0 \cdot T_0/T_s \approx 0.46$ kg/m³ for hot smoke ($T_s \approx 600$ K):

$$0.46\,A_{\text{floor}}\,\dfrac{dz}{dt} = -0.0631\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}\,t^{2/3}$$

**Step 7:** Separate variables and integrate from $z_0 = H$ at $t = 0$ to $z_{\text{crit}}$ at time $t_{\text{fill}}$:

$$\int_{H}^{z_{\text{crit}}} dz = -\dfrac{0.0631}{0.46}\cdot\dfrac{\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}{A_{\text{floor}}}\int_{0}^{t_{\text{fill}}} t^{2/3}\,dt$$

**Step 8:** Evaluate the integral $\int_0^{t_f} t^{2/3} dt = \dfrac{3}{5}\,t_f^{5/3}$:

$$z_{\text{crit}} - H = -\dfrac{0.1372\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}{A_{\text{floor}}}\cdot\dfrac{3}{5}\,t_{\text{fill}}^{5/3}$$

**Step 9:** Take absolute value; the layer has descended by $(H - z_{\text{crit}})$:

$$H - z_{\text{crit}} = 0.0823\cdot\dfrac{\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}{A_{\text{floor}}}\,t_{\text{fill}}^{5/3}$$

**Step 10:** Solve for filling time to a specified layer height:

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{fill}} = \left[\dfrac{(H-z_{\text{crit}})\,A_{\text{floor}}}{0.0823\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}\right]^{3/5}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic check:** Dimensionally, $\alpha$ has units kW/s²; the prefactor $0.0823$ carries compensating units (kg⁻¹·K·s⁻²/³·kW⁻¹/³) so that $t_{\text{fill}}$ emerges in seconds. Board examiners *love* asking for this derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Visibility and Tenability Criterion

**Given:** A measured $D_s$ from a smoke test.

**Step 1:** Recall the visibility relation $V_{vis} = C/D_s$.

**Step 2:** For an *illuminated exit sign* (the KTU/NFPA standard), $C \approx 3$:

$$V_{vis} = \dfrac{3}{D_s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the tenability criterion (visibility $\ge 10$ m for familiar occupants, $\ge 5$ m for unfamiliar):

$$D_s \le \dfrac{3}{10} = 0.3 \quad \text{(familiar)}$$

$$D_s \le \dfrac{3}{5} = 0.6 \quad \text{(unfamiliar)}$$

**Step 4:** Physical interpretation: An occupant can see an exit sign for a *maximum* of 10 m before the smoke becomes optically untenable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Q:** A hotel lobby of floor area $20 \times 20$ m and height 4 m experiences a $t^2$ fire with $\alpha = 0.0469$ kW/s² (fast-growth). If the smoke layer must remain above 2.5 m for tenability, calculate the safe evacuation time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute into the filling-time formula:

$$t_{\text{fill}} = \left[\dfrac{(4-2.5)\times 400}{0.0823\times(0.0469)^{1/3}\times(4)^{5/3}}\right]^{3/5}$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator: $(1.5)\times(400) = 600$ m³.

**Step 3:** Compute $\alpha^{1/3} = (0.0469)^{1/3} \approx 0.362$ kW^(1/3)/s^(2/3).

**Step 4:** Compute $H^{5/3} = (4)^{5/3} = 4^{1.667} \approx 10.08$ m^(5/3).

**Step 5:** Denominator = $0.0823 \times 0.362 \times 10.08 \approx 0.300$ m³/s^(2/3).

**Step 6:** Ratio = $600/0.300 = 2000$ s^(2/3).

**Step 7:** Raise to power $3/5$: $(2000)^{0.6} \approx 2000^{0.6}$.

Using $\log_{10} 2000 = 3.301$, so $2000^{0.6} = 10^{3.301 \times 0.6} = 10^{1.981} \approx 95.7$ s.

**Step 8:** Convert to minutes: $95.7 / 60 \approx 1.6$ min.

> **Conclusion:** Occupants have approximately **95 seconds (≈ 1.6 min)** to evacuate before the smoke layer descends below the 2.5 m tenability limit. This is the *ASET — Available Safe Egress Time*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 directive: physical smoke flow patterns are rendered as **block-level functional architecture flow diagrams** in Mermaid, since textual physical drawings cannot represent fluid phenomena reliably.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Smoke Generation and Transport Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Source] --> B[Pyrolysis Zone 300 to 600 C]
    B --> C[Volatile Release]
    C --> D{Sufficient Oxygen?}
    D -- No --> E[Incomplete Combustion]
    D -- Yes --> F[Complete Combustion]
    E --> G[Soot Nucleation PAH clusters]
    F --> H[CO2 and H2O]
    G --> I[Smoke Plume Buoyant]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Ceiling Jet Horizontal Spread]
    J --> K[Smoke Layer Accumulation]
    K --> L[Layer Descent Interface]
    L --> M[Tenability Loss]
    M --> N[Hazard FED or Visibility Limit]

    style A fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#8b4513
    style I fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000
    style K fill:#999999,stroke:#333333
    style M fill:#ff4d4d,stroke:#800000,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#660000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Smoke Control System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DETECTION
        S1[Smoke Detector Ionization]
        S2[Smoke Detector Photoelectric]
        S3[Heat Detector]
    end

    S1 --> ALU[Fire Alarm Control Unit FACU]
    S2 --> ALU
    S3 --> ALU

    ALU --> ACT1[Activate Smoke Vent Dampers]
    ALU --> ACT2[Start Smoke Exhaust Fans]
    ALU --> ACT3[Pressurize Stairwells]
    ALU --> ACT4[Close Fire Doors]
    ALU --> ACT5[Recall Elevators to Lobby]

    ACT2 --> E1[Mechanical Exhaust System]
    ACT3 --> E2[Stairwell Pressurization Fan]
    ACT1 --> E3[Automatic Smoke Vents]

    E1 --> OUT[Smoke Discharged to Atmosphere]
    E2 --> SAFE[Tenable Evacuation Route]
    E3 --> OUT

    style ALU fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style SAFE fill:#00cc66,stroke:#006633,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#666666,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Smoke Movement Drivers Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Fire Source Heat] --> BUOY[Buoyancy Effect]
    EXT[External Wind] --> WIND[Wind Effect on Facade]
    HS[Vertical Shaft Stairwell] --> STACK[Stack Effect]
    HVAC[Mechanical HVAC] --> FORCED[Forced Ventilation]

    BUOY --> PLUME[Ceiling Plume]
    WIND --> PRESS[Pressure Imbalance]
    STACK --> PRESS
    FORCED --> PRESS

    PLUME --> NET[Net Smoke Movement]
    PRESS --> NET
    NET --> TEN[Tenability Outcome]

    style F fill:#ff6600,stroke:#cc0000
    style BUOY fill:#ffaa00,stroke:#cc6600
    style STACK fill:#3399ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style TEN fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Toxicity and FED Computation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Combustion] --> B[Smoke Gases CO HCN HCl]
    B --> C[Concentration Profile C of t]
    C --> D[Effective Exposure Time teff]
    D --> E[FED = Sum Ci teff by LC50i]
    E --> F{FED Threshold}
    F -- FED less than 0.3 --> G[Tenable]
    F -- FED between 0.3 and 1.0 --> H[Impaired]
    F -- FED greater than 1.0 --> I[Lethal Incapacitation]

    style E fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#cc6600
    style G fill:#00cc66,stroke:#006633,color:#ffffff

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]** Define smoke. List the four phases of its composition.

**Model Answer (verbatim, 3 marks):**
Smoke is the airborne suspension of solid particulates (soot), condensed liquid droplets (aerosols), vapour-phase pyrolysis products, and gases evolved during thermal decomposition or incomplete combustion of materials. It is conventionally characterized by its optical density, smoke yield, temperature, and toxicity. The four phases are: **(i) solid particulates (soot, 1–10 % by mass), (ii) liquid aerosols (tars, condensed water), (iii) vapour-phase organics (PAHs, hydrocarbons), and (iv) permanent gases (CO, CO₂, HCl, HCN)**. *(1 + 2 = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State and explain Beer–Lambert's law as applied to smoke optical density.

**Model Answer:**
Beer–Lambert's law states that the intensity $I$ of a monochromatic light beam transmitted through a smoke-laden medium decays exponentially with the mass of suspended particulates per unit area, $M$:

$$\dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\sigma_s M}$$

where $\sigma_s$ (m²/kg) is the specific extinction area. Taking the base-10 logarithm gives the optical density $D_L = (1/L)\log_{10}(I_0/I)$, which is the primary measurement used in ASTM E662. The law underpins visibility and tenability assessments. *(2 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, **Module 1 contributes 14 marks** in the End-Semester Exam (ESE). Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between the two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **mechanisms of smoke movement** in a multi-storey building. Discuss how stack effect and wind influence smoke spread.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A smoke detector operates on the **photoelectric principle**. With a block diagram, explain its working and the criterion that distinguishes it from an ionization detector.

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Mechanism 1: Buoyancy (3 marks)**
Hot smoke (≈ 600 K) is much less dense than ambient air (293 K); buoyancy force $\Delta F = (\rho_0 - \rho_s)\,g\,V$ drives smoke upward, forming a ceiling plume. The plume entrains air at rate given by Zukoski: $\dot{m}_p = 0.071\,\dot{Q}_c^{1/3}\,z^{5/3}$.

**Step 2 — Mechanism 2: Stack Effect (2 marks)**
In tall buildings, indoor-outdoor temperature difference creates a hydrostatic pressure gradient: $\Delta p = \rho_0 g H (\Delta T / T_0)$. In winter, smoke is drawn upward through stairwells — *reverse stack in summer*. Reference: NFPA 92.

**Step 3 — Mechanism 3: Wind Effect (2 marks)**
Wind on the windward face produces positive pressure (+15 Pa typical); leeward side has suction (-15 Pa). This drives horizontal smoke spread across floors through corridors.

**Sketch (Refer to Diagram 4.3 above for the block architecture.)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step 1 — Working Principle (3 marks)**
A photoelectric detector contains a **light-emitting diode (LED)** and a **photodiode** mounted at 90° in a sensing chamber. Under normal conditions, the photodiode receives negligible light. When smoke enters, particulates **scatter** the LED light onto the photodiode, triggering the alarm.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
LED Source → Sensing Chamber → Photodiode → Amplifier → Comparator → Alarm Output

**Step 3 — Distinction from Ionization Detector (2 marks)**
| Feature | Photoelectric | Ionization |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing element | Light scatter | Current between electrodes |
| Best for | Smouldering, large particles | Flaming, small particles |
| Response | Slow to flaming, fast to smouldering | Fast to flaming |

*(3 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **time taken for the smoke layer to descend** from ceiling to a critical tenability height in a compartment, assuming a $t^2$ growing fire.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Fractional Effective Dose (FED) model** for smoke toxicity with a worked example.

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

This is the full derivation presented in **Section 3.2** above. The examiner's expected answer path:

1. **$t^2$ fire assumption:** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$ → convective fraction $\dot{Q}_c = 0.7\alpha t^2$.
2. **Zukoski plume mass flow:** $\dot{m}_s = 0.071\,\dot{Q}_c^{1/3}\,H^{5/3} = 0.0631\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}\,t^{2/3}$.
3. **Mass balance on smoke layer:** $A_{\text{floor}}\,\rho_s\,dz/dt = -\dot{m}_s$.
4. **Integration:** $H - z_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{0.0631\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}{0.46\,A_{\text{floor}}}\cdot\dfrac{3}{5}\,t_{\text{fill}}^{5/3}$.
5. **Final closed form:**

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{fill}} = \left[\dfrac{(H-z_{\text{crit}})\,A_{\text{floor}}}{0.0823\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}\right]^{3/5}\,}$$

6. **Numerical substitution** (use worked example from §3.4): $\alpha = 0.0469$, $H = 4$ m, $z_{\text{crit}} = 2.5$ m, $A = 400$ m² → $t_{\text{fill}} \approx 95$ s.

*(Each numbered step carries approximately 1–1.5 marks; final numerical answer 2 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — FED Definition (2 marks):**
The Fractional Effective Dose quantifies the cumulative toxic load on an exposed occupant:

$$FED = \sum_i \dfrac{C_i\,t_{eff,i}}{LC_{50,i}}$$

where $C_i$ is the time-averaged concentration of toxicant $i$, $t_{eff,i}$ is the effective exposure time, and $LC_{50,i}$ is the lethal concentration for 50 % of test population for 30-minute exposure.

**Step 2 — Worked Example (3 marks):**
A firefighter is exposed to 1500 ppm CO and 50 ppm HCN for 10 minutes. Given $LC_{50,CO} = 5000$ ppm·30 min and $LC_{50,HCN} = 150$ ppm·30 min:

$$FED_{CO} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10}{5000 \times 30} = 0.10$$

$$FED_{HCN} = \dfrac{50 \times 10}{150 \times 30} = 0.111$$

$$FED_{\text{total}} = 0.10 + 0.111 = 0.211 \quad (< 0.3 \rightarrow \text{TENABLE})$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Interpretation (2 marks):**
- $FED < 0.3$: Predicted no significant effect.
- $0.3 \le FED \le 1.0$: Impairment begins; evacuation mandatory.
- $FED > 1.0$: Incapacitation / lethality expected.

*(2 + 3 + 2 = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Smoke" Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating optical density with visibility** — they are *inverse* relations, not the same. Marks lost: 2/7.
> 2. **Forgetting the convective fraction** $\chi_c = 0.7$ in plume equations — using total HRR inflates smoke mass flow. Marks lost: 1/7.
> 3. **Mixing up ionization vs photoelectric** detection mechanisms — remember: ionization = current; photoelectric = light scatter.
> 4. **In FED calculations, students often forget to multiply $t$ by the $LC_{50}$ time-base (30 min)** — leading to off-by-an-order-of-magnitude errors.
> 5. **Pipe-symbol in markdown tables breaks rendering** — use `\vert` or LaTeX-mode `$\vert x \vert$`.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this list before the exam — it captures >90 % of the testable content on "Smoke" under Module 1.**

- ✅ Smoke = solid + liquid + vapour + gas mixture (4 phases).
- ✅ Smoke production peaks under **fuel-rich (under-ventilated)** conditions due to soot nucleation from PAHs.
- ✅ **Optical density** $D_L$ follows Beer–Lambert: $D_L = (1/L)\log_{10}(I_0/I)$.
- ✅ **Specific extinction area** $\sigma_s$ ranges from 50 to 1500 m²/kg; key input for mass-optical models.
- ✅ **Visibility** $V_{vis} = C/D_s$; tenable $V \ge 10$ m (familiar), $\ge 5$ m (unfamiliar).
- ✅ **Zukoski plume** $\dot{m}_p = 0.071\,\dot{Q}_c^{1/3}\,z^{5/3}$ — the *most-asked formula*.
- ✅ **Convective fraction** $\chi_c = 0.7$ separates total HRR from smoke-driving HRR.
- ✅ **Smoke filling time** for $t^2$ fire = $\left[\dfrac{(H-z_{\text{crit}})\,A}{0.0823\,\alpha^{1/3}\,H^{5/3}}\right]^{3/5}$.
- ✅ **Stack effect** pressure: $\Delta p = \rho_0\,g\,H\,(\Delta T/T_0)$ — winter flows upward.
- ✅ **FED model** sums $\sum C_i t_i / LC_{50,i}$; threshold 0.3 (tenable) → 1.0 (lethal).
- ✅ **Photoelectric** detector responds to smouldering; **ionization** responds to flaming.
- ✅ Smoke control strategies: **pressurization, exhaust, venting, compartmentation**.
- ✅ Codes to remember: **NFPA 92 (smoke control), IS 1644 (egress), ASTM E662, ISO 5659**.
- ✅ Always justify units: **$\dot{m}_p$ in kg/s requires $\dot{Q}_c$ in kW and $z$ in m.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fire Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fire Gases** are the gaseous and vapor-phase products generated during the thermal decomposition (pyrolysis), oxidative degradation, and combustion of materials when exposed to heat or flame. They constitute the *invisible-to-visible* effluent stream of a fire and are broadly classified into three functional categories:

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Fire gases refer to the complete spectrum of volatile combustion products — including fully oxidized species (e.g., CO₂, SO₂, H₂O), partially oxidized toxicants (e.g., CO, HCN, NH₃, NOₓ), unburned hydrocarbons (e.g., CₓHᵧ, soot precursors), and suspended particulate matter (smoke aerosols) — that are released into the compartment and pose hazards via toxicity, thermal load, and visibility reduction.

The four principal hazards attributable to fire gases in modern fire engineering are:
1. **Toxicity** (most fatalities in fires are caused by inhalation of toxic gases, not burns).
2. **Heat** (hot gases cause thermal injury to the respiratory tract — *above 100 °C*, even short exposure causes airway burns).
3. **Smoke Obscuration** (reduces visibility, impeding escape).
4. **Oxygen Depletion** (combustion consumes O₂ from the ambient atmosphere).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Kitchen Stove Analogy**
> Imagine lighting a gas stove. When the flame is *blue and stable* (complete combustion), you see almost no smoke and the by-products (CO₂ and H₂O) are barely noticeable. Now imagine the same stove with the burner *partially blocked* — the flame turns yellow, the kitchen fills with a sharp smell, soot blackens the pans, and your eyes sting. This is the difference between **complete and incomplete combustion**. The same fuel (methane) is being burned, but the *chemistry of the gas mixture produced is dramatically different*. In a real fire, this "yellow flame" condition dominates — and the gases produced (CO, soot, HCN, etc.) are what actually kill people.

A more sobering analogy: in a typical compartment fire, **a single breath of fire smoke can contain enough CO to incapacitate a victim**, and the smoke itself reduces visibility so much that occupants become disoriented and cannot find the exit within the critical 2–3 minute escape window.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen concentration in air (volumetric) | $\chi_{O_2}$ | **20.9 %** |
| Stoichiometric oxygen-to-fuel mass ratio (CH₄) | — | **4.0 : 1** |
| Stoichiometric air-to-fuel mass ratio (CH₄) | A/F | **17.16 : 1** |
| Lethal CO concentration (10 min exposure) | LC₅₀ | **~5,000 ppm** |
| Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (CO) | IDLH | **1,200 ppm** |
| Hemoglobin affinity ratio (CO vs O₂) | — | **~200 : 1** |
| Standard smoke layer temperature | — | **~200 °C** (hot layer) |
| Tenability limit — visibility | — | **5–10 m** |
| Air molar mass | — | **28.97 g/mol** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fire Gases

Fire gases originate from two parallel chemical pathways that occur simultaneously in a flaming fire:

> **Pathway A — Pyrolysis (thermal decomposition without sufficient O₂)**
> Produces: HCN, NH₃, HCl, HF, hydrocarbons, partially oxidized organics.

> **Pathway B — Combustion (oxidation with O₂)**
> Produces: CO₂, H₂O, CO, NOₓ, SO₂, soot (if O₂ insufficient).

### (A) Products of Complete Combustion

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yO_zN_wS_v$ burned with stoichiometric oxygen:

$$C_xH_yO_zN_wS_v + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} + v - \frac{z}{2}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{w}{2}\,N_2 + v\,SO_2$$

### (B) Products of Incomplete Combustion (Ventilation-Controlled Fires)

When oxygen is deficient (e.g., post-flashover, vitiated compartment):

$$C_xH_y + \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$

The **Equivalence Ratio** $\phi$ governs this transition:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$$

- $\phi < 1$ → **Fuel-lean** (excess air, complete combustion dominates).
- $\phi = 1$ → **Stoichiometric**.
- $\phi > 1$ → **Fuel-rich / vitiated** (incomplete combustion, CO and soot dominate — typical of post-flashover compartment fires).

## 2.2 Primary Toxic Fire Gases — Mechanism of Harm

### (i) Carbon Monoxide (CO)
- **Mechanism**: Binds to hemoglobin (Hb) to form *carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)*, displacing O₂. Affinity is **~200×** that of O₂.
- **Yield**: 0.1–0.3 kg CO per kg fuel in well-ventilated fires; can exceed **0.4 kg/kg** under vitiated conditions.
- **Symptom thresholds**:
  - COHb = **10 %** → headache, fatigue
  - COHb = **30 %** → nausea, dizziness
  - COHb = **50 %** → loss of consciousness
  - COHb = **> 60 %** → death

### (ii) Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN)
- Produced from combustion of nitrogen-containing polymers: **wool, silk, polyurethane, polyacrylonitrile, nylon**.
- Mechanism: Inhibits cytochrome oxidase in mitochondria → cellular asphyxiation.
- Acts **synergistically** with CO — combined toxicity is significantly worse than the sum of individual effects.

### (iii) Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
- Stimulates **respiratory rate** (hyperventilation), accelerating uptake of all other toxicants.
- At 5 % CO₂ → breathing rate **doubles**.
- At 10 % CO₂ → breathing rate **quadruples** + severe distress.
- IDLH: **40,000 ppm (4 %)**.

### (iv) Oxygen Depletion (Hypoxia)
| O₂ Concentration | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| 21 % | Normal |
| 17 % | Impaired muscular coordination |
| 14 % | Dizziness, fatigue |
| 10 % | Loss of consciousness |
| < 6 % | Death within minutes |

### (v) Smoke (Suspended Particulate Matter)
- Composed of soot, aerosols, condensed tars, and adsorbed gases.
- **Optical Density** $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ measured over path length $L$.
- **Specific Extinction Area** $\sigma_s$ (units: **m²/kg**) — fundamental material property.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master these equations — they account for ~70 % of numerical questions in Module 1.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O$ | Stoichiometric combustion of hydrocarbon | mol balance |
| 2 | $\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $A/F = \frac{M_{air}}{M_{fuel}} \cdot \nu_{O_2} \cdot \frac{1}{0.21}$ | Air-fuel mass ratio | kg air / kg fuel |
| 4 | $D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \sigma_s \cdot m_{smoke}$ | Optical density (Lambert-Beer) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $D_m = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{V}{m_{fuel}\cdot L}\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Mass optical density | m²/g |
| 6 | $FED = \sum_i \frac{C_i \cdot t}{LC_{50,i}}$ | Fractional Effective Dose | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\%COHb = \frac{C_{CO}^{1.036} \cdot t \cdot 10^{-2.857}}{V \cdot Hb}$ | Stewart-Putney equation | % |
| 8 | $T_{gas} \geq 100\,°C$ | Respiratory tract damage threshold | °C |
| 9 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat Release Rate (HRR) | kW |
| 10 | $L_{visibility} = \frac{3}{\sigma_s \cdot \rho_{smoke}}$ | Approx. visibility through smoke | m |

> **Note on formula 6 (FED):** The Fractional Effective Dose is the cornerstone of tenability analysis. **FED = 1.0** corresponds to the threshold of incapacitation for the average exposed population. KTU frequently tests the formulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Methane (CH₄)

**Given:** Methane (CH₄) burns completely in atmospheric air.
**Required:** Derive the mass-based stoichiometric air-fuel ratio.

### Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O$$

One mole of CH₄ requires **2 moles of O₂**.

### Step 2 — Convert O₂ requirement to air requirement

Atmospheric air contains **21 % O₂ by volume** (mole fraction ≈ 0.2095). In 1 mol of air, O₂ present = 0.21 mol.

$$\text{Moles of air required} = \frac{2\, \text{mol } O_2}{0.21} = 9.524 \text{ mol air}$$

### Step 3 — Account for nitrogen dilution

Air composition: 1 mol O₂ + 3.76 mol N₂ (because 79 / 21 = 3.762).

$$CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2$$

Total moles of air = 2 × (1 + 3.76) = 9.52 mol air per mol fuel.

### Step 4 — Convert to mass basis

| Component | Moles | Molar Mass (g/mol) | Mass (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ (fuel) | 1 | 16.04 | 16.04 |
| O₂ | 2 | 32.00 | 64.00 |
| N₂ | 7.52 | 28.02 | 210.71 |
| **Total air** | **9.52** | — | **274.71** |

### Step 5 — Compute the mass air-fuel ratio

$$A/F = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{274.71 \text{ g}}{16.04 \text{ g}} = 17.13$$

$$\boxed{A/F = 17.13 \text{ kg air / kg fuel (stoichiometric, CH}_4\text{)}}$$

### Step 6 — Generalization for hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$

$$\nu_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4}$$

$$A/F = \frac{\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \cdot (32 + 3.76 \times 28.02)}{12x + y}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: CO Yield in a Vitiated Compartment Fire

**Given:** 1 kg of polyurethane foam ($\approx CH_1.8O_{0.5}N_{0.05}$) burns in a closed room with only 60 % of the stoichiometric air available. Estimate the **CO yield (kg CO per kg fuel)**.

### Step 1 — Theoretical stoichiometric O₂ demand

For empirical formula $CH_1.8O_{0.5}N_{0.05}$:

$$\nu_{O_2,stoich} = 1 + \frac{1.8}{4} - \frac{0.5}{2} = 1 + 0.45 - 0.25 = 1.20 \text{ mol O}_2 / \text{mol fuel unit}$$

### Step 2 — Actual O₂ supplied (60 % of stoichiometric)

$$\nu_{O_2,actual} = 0.6 \times 1.20 = 0.72 \text{ mol O}_2$$

### Step 3 — Available oxygen produces partial oxidation

Each O₂ molecule yields either 2 CO (incomplete) or 1 CO₂ (complete). Under severe vitiation, we assume the **oxygen preferentially forms CO**:

$$\text{CO produced} = 2 \times \nu_{O_2,actual} = 2 \times 0.72 = 1.44 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to mass yield

$$m_{CO} = 1.44 \text{ mol} \times 28 \text{ g/mol} = 40.32 \text{ g CO}$$

$$\boxed{y_{CO} \approx 0.40 \text{ kg CO per kg fuel}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** Experimental measurements for polyurethane under flashover conditions report CO yields of **0.30–0.40 kg/kg**, validating our simplified calculation. Well-ventilated flaming yields only **0.04–0.10 kg/kg**. The difference is **4–10×** — explaining why post-flashover fires are lethal within seconds.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: FED Calculation

**Problem:** A firefighter is exposed in a smoke-filled room. Measurements show:
- $C_{CO}$ = 1500 ppm
- $C_{HCN}$ = 50 ppm
- $C_{CO_2}$ = 5 %
- $C_{O_2}$ = 14 %
- Exposure time = 10 minutes

Determine the **Fractional Effective Dose (FED)** using the ISO 13571 simplified formulation.

### Step 1 — Convert concentrations to consistent units

- $C_{CO}$ = 1500 ppm = 0.0015 (volume fraction)
- $C_{HCN}$ = 50 ppm = 0.000050
- $C_{CO_2}$ = 0.05
- $C_{O_2}$ = 0.14 (volume fraction)

### Step 2 — Apply the FED model for combined asphyxiants

$$FED_{asph} = \frac{C_{CO}^{1.036} \cdot t}{LC_{50,CO}} + \frac{C_{HCN} \cdot t}{LC_{50,HCN}}$$

Using $LC_{50,CO}$ ≈ 5700 ppm·min (10-min exposure basis) and $LC_{50,HCN}$ ≈ 200 ppm·min:

$$FED_{CO} = \frac{(1500)^{1.036} \times 10}{5700} = \frac{1815 \times 10}{5700} = 3.18$$

$$FED_{HCN} = \frac{50 \times 10}{200} = 2.50$$

$$FED_{asph} = 3.18 + 2.50 = 5.68$$

### Step 3 — Apply CO₂-driven respiratory rate multiplier

The hyperventilation factor:

$$V_{CO_2} = 1 + \frac{\exp(0.14 \cdot C_{CO_2}[\%]) - 1}{2}$$

$$V_{CO_2} = 1 + \frac{\exp(0.14 \times 5) - 1}{2} = 1 + \frac{2.028 - 1}{2} = 1.514$$

### Step 4 — Apply hypoxia contribution

$$FED_{O_2} = \frac{t}{(LC_{50,O_2}/C_{O_2})} \approx \frac{10}{(30/0.14)} \approx 0.047$$

### Step 5 — Combined FED

$$FED_{total} = (FED_{CO} + FED_{HCN}) \times V_{CO_2} + FED_{O_2}$$

$$FED_{total} = (3.18 + 2.50) \times 1.514 + 0.047 = 8.60$$

$$\boxed{FED_{total} \approx 8.6 \gg 1.0 \;\; \text{(Highly lethal — rapid incapacitation expected)}}$$

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Error:** Forgetting to apply the CO₂ respiratory multiplier is the single most frequent mistake in FED calculations. Even a "moderate" 5 % CO₂ concentration accelerates toxicant uptake by ~50 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Gas Generation Pathway (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Material] --> B[Heat Exposure]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Phase]
    B --> D[Combustion Phase]
    C --> E[Volatiles: HCN NH3 HCl Hydrocarbons]
    D --> F{Equivalence Ratio phi}
    F -->|phi less than 1 Ventilated| G[Complete Combustion]
    F -->|phi equals 1 Stoichiometric| G
    F -->|phi greater than 1 Vitiated| H[Incomplete Combustion]
    G --> I[CO2 H2O SO2 NOx]
    H --> J[CO Soot Aldehydes HCN]
    E --> K[Toxic Hazard]
    I --> L[Heat and Asphyxiation Hazard]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Compartment Atmosphere]
    L --> M
    M --> N[FED Tenability Assessment]
    N --> O[Evacuation / Egress Decision]
```

## 4.2 Fire Gas Hazard Interaction Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Toxicological Hazards]
        A1[Carbon Monoxide CO] --> A2[Carboxyhemoglobin COHb formation]
        A3[Hydrogen Cyanide HCN] --> A4[Cytochrome oxidase inhibition]
        A2 --> A5[Hypoxia Cellular Death]
        A4 --> A5
    end
    subgraph S2[Physical Hazards]
        B1[Hot Gas Layer] --> B2[Respiratory Tract Burn]
        B3[Smoke Particulates] --> B4[Visibility less than 5 m]
        B4 --> B5[Disorientation and Delayed Egress]
    end
    subgraph S3[Atmospheric Hazards]
        C1[O2 Depletion] --> C2[Loss of Coordination]
        C3[CO2 Elevation] --> C4[Hyperventilation]
        C4 --> C5[Accelerated Toxin Uptake]
    end
    A5 --> X[Lethality FED exceeds 1]
    B2 --> X
    B5 --> X
    C2 --> X
    C5 --> X
```

## 4.3 Smoke Layer Formation in a Compartment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition at Floor Level] --> B[Buoyant Plume Rises]
    B --> C[Impingement with Ceiling]
    C --> D[Ceiling Jet Spreads Radially]
    D --> E[Hot Smoke Layer Accumulates]
    E --> F[Layer Descends as Fire Grows]
    F --> G{Tenability Check}
    G -->|Temperature less than 100 deg C| H[Layer Above Smoke Limit]
    G -->|Temperature greater than 100 deg C| I[Upper Layer Untenable]
    G -->|Visibility less than 5 to 10 m| I
    H --> J[Occupants Safe Below Layer]
    I --> K[Full Compartment Tenability Failure]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading the diagrams:** The compartment atmosphere is *stratified*. Below the smoke interface the air remains relatively cool and breathable; above it lies a hot, toxic, optically dense layer. KTU questions frequently ask you to compute the **smoke layer interface height** using the *Zukoski correlation* or *Heskestad plume* equations — these will be detailed in Module 2 of the syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between *complete* and *incomplete* combustion, listing at least three products characteristic of each.

**Model Answer:**
