*Complete combustion* occurs when a fuel reacts with **sufficient** oxygen (equivalence ratio $\phi \leq 1$). The principal products are: **(i)** carbon dioxide (CO₂), **(ii)** water vapour (H₂O), **(iii)** sulfur dioxide (SO₂), and **(iv)** nitrogen (N₂) from the air.

*Incomplete combustion* occurs under **oxygen-deficient** (vitiated) conditions ($\phi > 1$), typical of post-flashover compartment fires or smouldering. Characteristic products: **(i)** carbon monoxide (CO) — the dominant toxicant; **(ii)** soot (carbonaceous particulate matter); **(iii)** hydrogen cyanide (HCN) from nitrogen-containing fuels; **(iv)** unburned hydrocarbons; **(v)** aldehydes (e.g., acrolein).

The hazard contrast is significant: well-ventilated fires produce CO yields of ~0.05 kg/kg, whereas vitiated fires can reach **0.30–0.40 kg/kg** — a 6–8× increase in toxic potency.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is **carbon monoxide (CO)** considered the most lethal single toxicant in structural fires? Cite physiological evidence.

**Model Answer:**
CO is the predominant toxicant in fires because of three converging factors:

**(i) High yield:** Every fuel containing carbon produces CO under oxygen-deficient conditions; yields of **0.10–0.40 kg CO per kg fuel** are routinely measured in post-flashover fires.

**(ii) Extreme hemoglobin affinity:** CO binds to hemoglobin with an affinity **~200 times greater** than that of O₂, forming *carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)*. Even low inhaled CO concentrations rapidly saturate blood oxygen-carrying capacity.

**(iii) Concentration in fire atmospheres:** CO levels in enclosed fire smoke frequently exceed **1,000–5,000 ppm**, whereas the IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) value is only **1,200 ppm**. At 5,000 ppm, unconsciousness can occur in **5–10 minutes**.

Empirical evidence from fire fatalities shows that **~60–80 %** of fire deaths exhibit blood COHb levels > 50 %, confirming CO as the primary lethal agent in fire atmospheres.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**
> (a) [7 Marks] Derive the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** by mass for the complete combustion of **propane (C₃H₈)**. Assume air contains 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume.
> (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the formation mechanism of **HCN** in fire atmospheres. Why is HCN particularly dangerous in fires involving natural polymers (wool, silk) and synthetic polymers (polyurethane, polyacrylonitrile)?

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Stoichiometric A/F for C₃H₈:**

**Step 1 — Balanced equation:**
$$C_3H_8 + 5\,O_2 \longrightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O$$

**Step 2 — Moles of air needed** (1 mol O₂ + 3.76 mol N₂):
$$\text{Air} = 5 \times (1 + 3.76) = 5 \times 4.76 = 23.81 \text{ mol air per mol fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Mass computation:**

| Species | Moles | Molar Mass | Mass (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| C₃H₈ | 1 | 44.10 | 44.10 |
| O₂ | 5 | 32.00 | 160.00 |
| N₂ | 18.81 | 28.02 | 527.06 |
| **Total Air** | — | — | **687.06** |

**Step 4 — A/F ratio:**
$$A/F = \frac{687.06}{44.10} = 15.58 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich,\,C_3H_8} \approx 15.58}$$

**(b) HCN Formation Mechanism:**

HCN forms via two main pathways in fires:

**Pathway 1 — Fuel-nitrogen route (most significant):**
Nitrogen-containing polymers (wool contains ~16 % N by mass; polyurethane has ~6 % N; polyacrylonitrile ~26 % N) undergo thermal decomposition, releasing nitrogen as **amine radicals** (·NH, ·NH₂). These radicals react with hydrocarbon fragments in the fuel-rich pyrolysis zone:

$$R-CH_2-NH_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} R-CH=NH \longrightarrow R-CN \xrightarrow{H\text{-abstraction}} HCN$$

**Pathway 2 — Reverse Zeldovich route:**
In the *flame zone* of nitrogen-containing fuels, atomic nitrogen reacts with CH radicals:
$$N_2 + CH \longrightarrow HCN + N$$

HCN is particularly dangerous because:
- It acts **30–50× faster** than CO on cellular respiration.
- **Synergistic toxicity:** Even sub-lethal CO concentrations become lethal when combined with HCN — the combined FED exceeds the additive sum by **1.5–2.0×**.
- HCN yields from polyurethane can reach **0.015 kg/kg** — small in mass but lethal in minute quantities (LC₅₀ ≈ 200 ppm for 10-min exposure).

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> (a) [7 Marks] Define the **Fractional Effective Dose (FED)** as used in fire tenability analysis. Calculate the FED for a victim exposed to **C_CO = 800 ppm**, **C_HCN = 30 ppm**, and **C_CO₂ = 4 %** for **8 minutes** in a vitiated fire atmosphere.
> (b) [7 Marks] Describe the **Stewart-Putney equation** for carboxyhemoglobin formation. Using it, estimate the %COHb in a victim exposed to **200 ppm CO for 90 minutes**, assuming resting ventilation rate.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) FED Calculation:**

**Definition:** The Fractional Effective Dose (FED), per ISO 13571, is a dimensionless index that quantifies the cumulative toxic load on an exposed occupant. It is computed as the ratio of the *actual dose received* to the *dose expected to cause incapacitation*:

$$FED = \sum_{i} \frac{C_i \cdot t}{LC_{50,i}}$$

When FED = 1.0, the exposed population reaches the threshold of incapacitation.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $C_{CO} = 800$ ppm, $C_{HCN} = 30$ ppm, $C_{CO_2} = 4\%$, $t = 8$ min.

**Step 1 — Asphyxiant FED:**
$$FED_{CO} = \frac{(800)^{1.036} \times 8}{5700} = \frac{912 \times 8}{5700} = 1.28$$

$$FED_{HCN} = \frac{30 \times 8}{200} = 1.20$$

$$FED_{asph} = 1.28 + 1.20 = 2.48$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ respiratory multiplier:**
$$V_{CO_2} = 1 + \frac{\exp(0.14 \times 4) - 1}{2} = 1 + \frac{1.749 - 1}{2} = 1.375$$

**Step 3 — Total FED:**
$$\boxed{FED_{total} = 2.48 \times 1.375 \approx 3.41}$$

Since FED ≫ 1, **incapacitation is certain** within 8 minutes.

**(b) Stewart-Putney Equation:**

The Stewart-Putney model predicts blood carboxyhemoglobin concentration under continuous CO exposure:

$$\%COHb = \frac{C_{CO}^{1.036} \cdot t \cdot 10^{-2.857}}{V \cdot Hb}$$

Where:
- $C_{CO}$ = CO concentration (ppm)
- $t$ = exposure time (minutes)
- $V$ = alveolar ventilation rate (L/min)
- $Hb$ = total hemoglobin content (g)

**Numerical Application:**

Given: $C_{CO} = 200$ ppm, $t = 90$ min. For a resting adult: $V = 6$ L/min, $Hb = 750$ g (15 g/dL × 5 L blood).

$$\%COHb = \frac{(200)^{1.036} \times 90 \times 10^{-2.857}}{6 \times 750}$$

$$(200)^{1.036} = 200 \times 200^{0.036} = 200 \times 1.305 = 261$$

$$\%COHb = \frac{261 \times 90 \times 0.001388}{4500} = \frac{32.6}{4500} = 0.00725$$

$$\boxed{\%COHb \approx 0.73\%}$$

This sub-clinical level (below 10 %) suggests the victim would experience mild headache and fatigue but **not** incapacitation — illustrating that well-ventilated smouldering fires are far less immediately lethal than vitiated flaming fires at equivalent CO yield.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the N₂ contribution** to stoichiometric air (each mol of O₂ brings 3.76 mol of N₂ — worth **2 marks** in any derivation).
> 2. **Confusing mass and mole air-fuel ratios** — these differ by ~the molar mass of air (~29 g/mol); always read the question carefully.
> 3. **Neglecting the CO₂ hyperventilation factor** in FED calculations — examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Using %COHb vs ppm CO interchangeably** — COHb is a **physiological blood concentration**; CO concentration in air is a **gas concentration**. They are related via the Stewart-Putney equation, *not* equivalent.
> 5. **Mixing up toxicity units** — ppm, mg/m³, % and volume fractions all appear in different sections of the KTU syllabus. Master the conversion:
> $$C [mg/m^3] = \frac{C [ppm] \times M_{gas}}{24.45}$$
> where 24.45 is the molar volume of air at 25 °C, 1 atm.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CO is the dominant fire killer** — affinity 200× that of O₂ for hemoglobin; COHb > 50 % typically indicates lethal exposure.
- ✅ **Incomplete combustion** (vitiated, $\phi > 1$) yields 4–10× more CO and soot than complete combustion.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F for CH₄ ≈ 17.13**, for C₃H₈ ≈ 15.58, for C₈H₁₈ ≈ 15.13.
- ✅ **HCN** originates from nitrogen-containing fuels (wool, polyurethane, PAN) — synergistic with CO.
- ✅ **CO₂** at 5 % doubles breathing rate; always multiply FED by the hyperventilation factor $V_{CO_2}$.
- ✅ **O₂ < 14 %** causes physiological distress; < 10 % → unconsciousness; < 6 % → death.
- ✅ **Smoke visibility < 5–10 m** triggers tenability failure for occupant egress.
- ✅ **FED > 1.0** → incapacitation threshold reached.
- ✅ **Hot gas layer > 100 °C** at breathing height → respiratory tract damage imminent.
- ✅ The **Stewart-Putney equation** links atmospheric CO to blood COHb — be ready to derive and apply it.
- ✅ Air composition standard: **21 % O₂, 79 % N₂** by volume; **N₂:O₂ = 3.76:1**.
- ✅ Smoke is **particulates + adsorbed gases** — toxicity and obscuration are coupled.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 1: Fire Gases**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Course OECHT835, NEP 2020 OBE framework, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2–L5.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of Fire: Incipient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development of Fire: Incipient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
The **Incipient Stage** (also called the *pre-ignition* or *incipient phase*) is the **first and earliest stage** in the development of a fire, beginning immediately after the application of sufficient ignition energy to a combustible material and continuing until the establishment of sustained flaming combustion. During this stage, the fuel undergoes *thermal decomposition* (pyrolysis) and *oxidative pyrolysis*, producing **smoke particulates, aerosols, and invisible combustion gases**, but **sustained visible flames are absent or only just emerging**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The incipient stage is characterized by **smoldering combustion**, **low temperature rise (typically 0 °C – 50 °C above ambient)**, and the **generation of toxic gases (CO, HCN)** — it is the critical detection window for early warning fire suppression systems.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you place a **loose bundle of dry twigs** (fuel) over a freshly extinguished matchstick that still has a glowing ember (ignition source). Before the twigs burst into visible flames:

- You will first **smell a faint acrid odor** (combustion gases escaping).
- You may notice a thin **wispy grayish stream** rising from the bundle (smoke particulates — unburnt hydrocarbons and aerosols).
- If you place your hand above the twigs, you will feel **mild warmth** but no scorching heat.
- No bright, dancing flames yet — just hidden, invisible chemistry at work.

This "quiet, sneaky, pre-flame chemistry" is precisely the **Incipient Stage** of a fire. The fire is *alive* but not yet *visible* — and crucially, this is the **safest and most cost-effective moment to intervene**.

---

### Standard Quantitative Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Metric | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage Temperature** | **Ambient to ~50 °C above ambient** | Below the ignition threshold |
| **Heat Release Rate (HRR)** | **0 – 20 kW** | Minimal radiant output |
| **Smoke Density** | High (visible) | Key detection parameter |
| **CO Concentration** | Rises from **0 to ~100 ppm** | Toxic hazard begins |
| **Stage Duration** | **Minutes to hours** (fuel dependent) | Detection window |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant to Memorize:**
> The standard reference temperature for incipient fire modeling is **T₀ = 20 °C (293 K)** with HRR ≤ **20 kW** per NFPA 92B conventions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Phases of Fire Development — Where Incipient Fits

Fire development is canonically divided into **four sequential stages** in fire protection engineering:

1. **Incipient Stage** ← *(Current Topic)*
2. Growth Stage
3. Fully Developed Stage (Flashover → Post-Flashover)
4. Decay Stage

The incipient stage acts as the **gateway phase**: if suppression occurs here, fire damage is negligible. If ignored, the fire transitions to the growth stage with exponential HRR rise.

---

### 2.2 The Chemistry of the Incipient Stage — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1: Heat Application
An external ignition source (match flame, electrical spark, hot surface) delivers energy to the fuel. The fuel's surface temperature begins rising:

$$T_{surface}(t) = T_{ambient} + \frac{q'' \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p}$$

Where:
- $q''$ = incident heat flux (kW/m²)
- $t$ = exposure time (s)
- $m$ = mass of heated material (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·K)

#### Step 2: Pyrolysis Begins
Once the fuel reaches its **decomposition temperature (T_d)**, long-chain hydrocarbons crack into smaller, volatile combustible molecules:

$$C_xH_y_{(s)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} C_xH_y_{(g)} + \text{Chars}_{(s)} + \text{Tars}$$

#### Step 3: Oxidative Pyrolysis & Smoke Generation
The volatiles mix with surrounding oxygen in the **reaction zone**, producing **incomplete combustion products**:

$$C_xH_y + O_2 \longrightarrow CO + CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Soot}_{(s)} + \text{Unburnt HC}$$

This is why the incipient stage is dominated by **smoke and gas** rather than flame.

#### Step 4: Pre-Flame Conditions (Critical Threshold)
When **fuel vapor concentration** reaches the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and local temperature reaches the **Fire Point**, a **transient flame kernel** appears — marking the **end of the incipient stage** and the **start of the growth stage**.

---

### 2.3 The Fire Tetrahedron — Incipient Stage Perspective

The classical **fire tetrahedron** consists of four required elements:

1. **Fuel** — Present (source material)
2. **Oxygen** — Present (atmospheric ~21%)
3. **Heat** — Present (from ignition source)
4. **Sustaining Chemical Reaction** — **NOT YET SELF-SUSTAINING** ← *Key differentiator*

> [!NOTE]
> During the incipient stage, the **chemical reaction** is **not yet chain-branching or self-sustaining**. Once the reaction becomes self-sustaining, the fire transitions to the Growth Stage. This is the foundation of all fire suppression theory.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface temperature rise | $T_s = T_a + \dfrac{q'' t}{m c_p}$ | K or °C | Pre-ignition heating |
| 2 | Pyrolysis mass loss rate | $\dot{m}_{pyro} = A \, k_0 \, e^{-E_a/RT}$ | kg/s | Fuel decomposition |
| 3 | Arrhenius rate constant | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Chemical kinetics |
| 4 | Lower Flammability Limit | $\text{LFL}_{fuel}$ | % vol | Fire initiation threshold |
| 5 | Smoke obscuration | $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | dimensionless | Detector activation |
| 6 | CO yield | $Y_{CO} = \dfrac{m_{CO}}{m_{fuel, consumed}}$ | kg/kg | Toxicity assessment |
| 7 | Incipient HRR ceiling | $\dot{Q}_{incipient} \leq 20$ | kW | NFPA reference |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Smoke Detector Design:** Photoelectric and ionization smoke detectors are engineered specifically for the incipient stage, exploiting high smoke density before flames appear.
- **VESDA (Very Early Smoke Detection Apparatus):** Detects incipient-stage aerosols in data centers and museums.
- **Fire Modeling Software (FDS, PyroSim):** Incipient stage boundary conditions are input parameters for CFD simulations.
- **Building Egress Design:** Detection in the incipient stage gives occupants 5–10 minutes of egress time versus < 30 seconds after flashover.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time Required to Reach Pyrolysis Temperature

**Problem Setup:** A wooden plank (mass $m = 0.5$ kg, $c_p = 1.7$ kJ/kg·K) is exposed to a radiant heat flux $q'' = 5$ kW/m² over an absorbing area $A = 0.05$ m². Ambient temperature $T_a = 293$ K. Wood decomposition temperature $T_d = 573$ K (300 °C). Find the time $t_d$ required to reach pyrolysis.

---

#### Step 1: Write the Energy Balance

The net energy absorbed by the wood equals the sensible heat required to raise its temperature from ambient to decomposition temperature:

$$q'' \cdot A \cdot t_d = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_d - T_a)$$

*Conversion logic:* Heat flux times area gives total power (W); multiplied by time gives total energy (J). This must equal the thermal energy stored in the mass (mass × specific heat × temperature rise).

#### Step 2: Substitute Known Values

$$5 \times 10^3 \, \text{W/m}^2 \times 0.05 \, \text{m}^2 \times t_d = 0.5 \, \text{kg} \times 1700 \, \text{J/kg·K} \times (573 - 293) \, \text{K}$$

$$250 \, \text{W} \times t_d = 0.5 \times 1700 \times 280$$

$$250 \, t_d = 238{,}000$$

#### Step 3: Solve for $t_d$

$$t_d = \frac{238{,}000}{250} = 952 \, \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_d \approx 952 \, \text{seconds} \approx 15.87 \, \text{minutes}}$$

---

#### Step 4: Interpretation

This means under these conditions, the incipient stage for the wooden plank lasts approximately **16 minutes** before active pyrolysis begins in earnest. This duration is the **critical detection window** during which intervention can prevent flashover.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Fire Stages (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Incipient | Growth | Fully Developed | Decay |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | < 50 °C above ambient | 50 °C – 600 °C | 600 °C – 1100 °C | Falling |
| **HRR** | 0 – 20 kW | 20 – 1000 kW | 1000 – 8000+ kW | Decreasing |
| **Flames** | None / intermittent | Visible, growing | Fully developed | Smoldering |
| **Smoke** | Abundant, light-colored | Thick, darkening | Dark, dense | Light |
| **CO (ppm)** | 0 – 100 | 100 – 5000 | 5000+ | Falling |
| **Detection Method** | Smoke/heat detector | Smoke + flame detector | Multiple | All sensors |
| **O₂ Level** | ~21% | 21% – 15% | < 15% | Restoring |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fire Development Stage Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition Source Applied] --> B[Incipient Stage]
    B --> C{Sufficient Fuel Vapor and Heat?}
    C -->|No - Suppression Here| D[Fire Extinguished]
    C -->|Yes - Unchecked| E[Growth Stage]
    E --> F{Flashover Reached?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Fully Developed Stage]
    F -->|No - Suppression Here| D
    G --> H{Fuel Depleting?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Decay Stage]
    H -->|No| G
    I --> J[Smoldering to Extinction]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Incipient Stage Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Heat["HEAT INPUT DOMAIN"]
        H1[External Ignition Source]
        H2[Conductive and Radiative Flux]
    end

    subgraph Fuel["FUEL DOMAIN"]
        F1[Solid Combustible Material]
        F2[Pyrolysis Reaction Zone]
        F3[Volatile Hydrocarbon Release]
    end

    subgraph Atm["ATMOSPHERIC DOMAIN"]
        A1[Ambient Oxygen 21 percent]
        A2[Incomplete Combustion Mix]
    end

    subgraph Output["OUTPUT SIGNATURE DOMAIN"]
        O1[Smoke Aerosols]
        O2[Carbon Monoxide Gas]
        O3[Low Level Heat Rise]
        O4[No Sustained Flame]
    end

    subgraph Detection["DETECTION DOMAIN"]
        D1[Photoelectric Smoke Sensor]
        D2[Ionization Smoke Sensor]
        D3[VESDA Aspirating System]
    end

    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> O1
    A2 --> O2
    A2 --> O3
    A2 --> O4

    O1 --> D1
    O1 --> D3
    O2 --> D2
    O3 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Fire Tetrahedron — Incipient Stage State

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL: Solid Hydrocarbon Source] --> D[INCIPIENT FIRE]
    B[OXYGEN: Atmospheric 21 percent] --> D
    C[HEAT: Ignition Energy Present] --> D
    E[SUSTAINING REACTION: NOT YET ESTABLISHED] -.-> D
    D --> F[Visible Output: Smoke and Gas Only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed line** from element E indicates a *missing/incomplete* reaction chain — the defining characteristic that separates the incipient stage from the growth stage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the "incipient stage" of fire development. List any two characteristic features that distinguish it from the growth stage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The incipient stage is the **initial phase of fire development** that begins with the application of an ignition source to a combustible material and continues until the establishment of sustained flaming combustion. During this stage, the fuel undergoes thermal decomposition (pyrolysis) producing **smoke, aerosols, and toxic gases** without visible flames.

**Distinguishing Features (1 mark — any two):**
1. Temperature rise is minimal (< 50 °C above ambient); growth stage shows rapid temperature escalation.
2. No sustained flame kernel — only intermittent flaming if any.
3. HRR remains below 20 kW; growth stage shows exponential HRR rise.
4. Abundant light-colored smoke (pre-flaming pyrolysis products).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**
*With the help of the fire tetrahedron, explain why the incipient stage is considered a "suppression-friendly" stage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The fire tetrahedron comprises **fuel, oxygen, heat, and a self-sustaining chemical chain reaction**. In the incipient stage:

- **Fuel** is present (1 mark)
- **Oxygen** is present (atmospheric ~21%)
- **Heat** is present from the ignition source
- **The self-sustaining chemical reaction is NOT YET ESTABLISHED** (1 mark)

Because the chain reaction is incomplete, **removing any single element** (especially the small ignition heat) extinguishes the fire. This makes the incipient stage highly amenable to suppression (1 mark) using portable extinguishers, smoke detection-triggered sprinklers, or manual intervention — before the fire grows into a self-sustaining combustion process.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **four stages of fire development** in a compartment fire. Clearly indicate the position and boundaries of the incipient stage. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A polyurethane foam sample (mass 0.3 kg, specific heat 1.5 kJ/kg·K) is exposed to a heat flux of 8 kW/m² over an area of 0.04 m². If the ambient temperature is 30 °C and the pyrolysis temperature is 280 °C, calculate the **time required to reach the pyrolysis threshold**. Comment on whether this duration is favorable for incipient-stage detection. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution for Question A(a):

The four stages of a compartment fire are:

**1. Incipient Stage (0–t₁):**
Begins with ignition. Fuel heats up, pyrolysis begins. Only **smoke and gases** are produced. No sustained flame. Temperature is near ambient (within 50 °C). This stage ends when a **sustained flame kernel** appears. *(2 marks)*

**2. Growth Stage (t₁–t₂):**
Sustained flaming combustion spreads across the fuel surface. HRR rises exponentially. Temperature climbs rapidly. Plume rises to ceiling, forming a **hot gas layer**. *(2 marks)*

**3. Fully Developed Stage (t₂–t₃):**
Occurs after **flashover** — when all combustible surfaces ignite simultaneously. HRR reaches maximum (potentially MW scale). Temperature peaks at 800–1100 °C. *(1.5 marks)*

**4. Decay Stage (t₃–t₄):**
Fuel is consumed; HRR and temperature decline. Fire may continue as smoldering until extinction. *(1.5 marks)*

*Sketch description (compulsory for full marks):* A standard HRR-vs-time t-squared curve showing all four stages labeled, with incipient stage highlighted at the leftmost portion near zero HRR.

---

##### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:**
- $m = 0.3$ kg
- $c_p = 1500$ J/kg·K
- $q'' = 8 \times 10^3$ W/m²
- $A = 0.04$ m²
- $T_a = 303$ K (30 °C)
- $T_d = 553$ K (280 °C)

**Step 1: Energy balance equation**

$$q'' \cdot A \cdot t_d = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_d - T_a)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$8000 \times 0.04 \times t_d = 0.3 \times 1500 \times (553 - 303)$$

$$320 \, t_d = 450 \times 250$$

$$320 \, t_d = 112{,}500$$

**Step 3: Solve**

$$t_d = \frac{112{,}500}{320} = 351.56 \, \text{seconds}$$

$$\boxed{t_d \approx 352 \, \text{s} \approx 5.87 \, \text{minutes}}$$

**Step 4: Comment (2 marks)**

A duration of ~5.9 minutes is **favorable for incipient-stage detection** because:
- It exceeds the **30-second to 2-minute response time** of most modern smoke detection systems.
- It allows ample opportunity for **automatic sprinkler actuation** (typically activates within 1–3 minutes).
- It provides **safe egress time** for building occupants.

However, polyurethane foam is highly hazardous — even a brief incipient stage produces significant **HCN and CO**, so detection must be paired with ventilation analysis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **fire tetrahedron** in detail. Discuss which element is *missing or incomplete* during the incipient stage and how this informs fire suppression strategy. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A smoke detector of the **photoelectric type** operates on the principle of light scattering by smoke particles. If the obscuration threshold is $D = 0.05$ per meter (light intensity drops to 90%) at a smoke source rate of $\dot{m}_s = 0.02$ kg/s in a room of volume $V = 50$ m³, estimate the **time to detector activation**. Assume the smoke mass concentration grows linearly at rate $k = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m³·s. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**The Fire Tetrahedron (4 marks):**

The fire tetrahedron is a 3-D model showing the four elements required simultaneously for sustained combustion:

1. **Fuel** — Any combustible material (solid, liquid, gas)
2. **Oxygen** — Typically from air (~21% by volume)
3. **Heat** — Energy to reach and maintain ignition temperature
4. **Sustaining Chemical Chain Reaction** — Free-radical chain branching (H•, O•, OH•) propagating combustion

**Missing Element in Incipient Stage (2 marks):**

The **sustaining chemical chain reaction** is **not yet self-propagating**. The fuel has begun pyrolyzing, and some oxidation is occurring, but the chain-branching radical reactions have **not yet reached steady state**. The fire is essentially a *fuel-limited incipient process* dependent on the original ignition source.

**Suppression Strategy Implication (1 mark):**

Because only the chemical chain reaction is incomplete, **disrupting the heat source** (cooling with water), **smothering** (cutting oxygen), or **chemical interruption** (clean agent extinguishers breaking chain reactions) can fully extinguish the fire with minimal energy — making this stage the **most cost-effective suppression window**.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Obscuration threshold: $D = 0.05$ m⁻¹
- Linear concentration growth rate: $k = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m³·s
- Source rate: $\dot{m}_s = 0.02$ kg/s (used for verification — not directly needed for time calculation)
- Room volume: $V = 50$ m³

**Step 1: Estimate critical smoke mass concentration**

A standard empirical correlation between optical density and mass concentration for typical smokes is:

$$D \approx K_m \cdot C_s$$

Where $K_m \approx 1 \, \text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ for typical combustion aerosols. Thus:

$$C_s^{critical} = \frac{D}{K_m} = \frac{0.05}{1} = 0.05 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Apply linear growth assumption**

$$C_s(t) = k \cdot t$$

Setting $C_s(t) = C_s^{critical}$:

$$k \cdot t_{act} = 0.05$$

$$t_{act} = \frac{0.05}{4 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$t_{act} = 1250 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to minutes**

$$\boxed{t_{act} \approx 20.83 \, \text{minutes}}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation (1 mark)**

A detector activation time of ~21 minutes is **longer than typical code requirements** (smoke detectors should activate within 1–3 minutes per NFPA 72). This indicates either:
- The room is too large for the given fire size, OR
- Additional ventilation or mechanical smoke control is needed, OR
- A more sensitive detection technology (e.g., VESDA) should be deployed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse incipient stage with growth stage** — many students incorrectly assign the same temperature range (~50 °C) to both. Remember: incipient = **NO flame**; growth = **visible flame present**.
> 2. **Always state the units** in numerical answers — losing 0.5 marks for missing units is the most common KTU deduction.
> 3. **Sketch must be neat and labeled** in Q-type questions — unmarked diagrams lead to partial marking.
> 4. In fire tetrahedron questions, students frequently **omit the chemical reaction element**; remember it's the 4th vertex of the tetrahedron (not triangle).
> 5. For derivation problems, **show every algebraic step** — partial steps receive partial credit per KTU 2024 valuation key norms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Incipient Stage** = First fire stage = **No sustained flame**, only smoke + gas + low heat.
- ✅ **Temperature** remains within ~50 °C of ambient.
- ✅ **HRR ≤ 20 kW** during incipient phase.
- ✅ **Smoke detectors** (photoelectric + ionization) and **VESDA** are designed for this stage.
- ✅ **Pyrolysis** is the key chemical process — solid fuel → volatile combustible gases.
- ✅ **Fire tetrahedron's 4th element** (sustaining reaction) is **not yet self-propagating**.
- ✅ Detection window: **minutes to hours** — critical for early suppression.
- ✅ Key toxic products: **CO, HCN, soot particulates**.
- ✅ Transition to Growth Stage occurs when **flame kernel** becomes sustained and HRR crosses ~20 kW.
- ✅ Energy balance equation: $q'' A t = m c_p \Delta T$ is the **go-to formula** for KTU derivations on incipient stage heating.
- ✅ Arrhenius expression $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ governs pyrolysis kinetics.
- ✅ Memorize **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit)** concept — it marks the upper boundary of the incipient stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoldering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoldering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition
> **Smoldering** is a slow, low-temperature, **flameless** form of combustion that is sustained by the heterogeneous reaction of a solid fuel with an oxidizer (usually atmospheric **oxygen at 21% by volume**) and the heat evolved during this reaction. It propagates through the porous solid fuel by **thermal conduction, natural convection, and radiation** at typical temperatures ranging from **450 °C to 700 °C**, far below the typical flaming combustion temperatures of **800 °C to 1200 °C**.

In formal fire engineering nomenclature (per **ISO 13943**), smoldering is classified as a *non-flaming combustion* phenomenon in which oxidation of the condensed-phase fuel occurs on the **surface of the solid** rather than in the gas phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE] The "Charcoal in Your Backyard" Analogy
> Imagine you have just finished a barbecue. The visible flames are gone, but the charcoal briquettes continue to glow red, with a thin wisp of white smoke drifting upward. If you blow gently on the briquettes, they glow brighter but still do **not** produce a flame. That glowing, flameless, slow-burning process is **smoldering**.
>
> Now imagine you bury that burning ember in a pile of dry leaves — the heat slowly creeps into the unburnt leaves (a process called **propagation**) and eventually, after hours, the whole pile may either remain smoldering or suddenly transition into a flaming fire. This dual possibility — *steady smoldering* vs. *transition to flaming* — is one of the most critical concepts in fire safety.

## 1.3 Key Physical Characteristics

| Parameter | Smoldering | Flaming Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Range | **450 – 700 °C** | **800 – 1200 °C** |
| Flame | **Absent** | Present (visible flame) |
| Reaction Phase | Heterogeneous (solid + gas) | Homogeneous (gas phase) |
| Propagation Speed | **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³ m/s** | **10⁻³ to 10⁻¹ m/s** |
| Light Emission | Glowing red/orange | Bright yellow/white |
| Smoke | Dense, white/grey, slow | Black, fast-rising |

## 1.4 Common Smoldering Materials (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT] Engineering-Relevant Smoldering Fuels
> - **Cellulosic materials**: Cotton, jute, paper, sawdust, wood dust, agricultural residue (hay, grain dust).
> - **Carbonaceous solids**: Charcoal, coal (especially low-rank lignite), peat, coke.
> - **Polymeric foams**: Flexible **polyurethane (PU) foam** — extremely hazardous in upholstered furniture.
> - **Tobacco products**: Cigarettes and cigars (the original ignition source in ~25 % of structure fires globally).
> - **Dust accumulations**: Industrial dusts (flour, sugar, aluminium, magnesium) can transition from smoldering to dust explosions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Zone Architecture of a Smoldering Front

A propagating smoldering front is conceptually divided into **three thermal zones**, each with a distinct dominant mechanism:

> [!NOTE] Three Zones of a Smoldering Wave
>
> **Zone A — Preheating (Drying) Zone:**
> Ahead of the reaction zone, the unburnt solid fuel is heated by conduction from the smoldering front. Moisture evaporates, and the fuel temperature rises from ambient to ~**100–150 °C**.
>
> **Zone B — Pyrolysis (Devolatilization) Zone:**
> The fuel is heated further (**150–400 °C**). Volatile matter (tars, combustible gases) is released by thermal decomposition. This zone is the **supply source of fuel gases** that may ignite externally and cause the **smoldering-to-flaming transition**.
>
> **Zone C — Char Oxidation (Reaction) Zone:**
> The residual **char** reacts heterogeneously with infiltrated oxygen at temperatures of **450–700 °C**. This is the **heat-generating zone** that sustains the entire process. Behind it lies inert **ash**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Sustained Smoldering

For smoldering to be self-sustaining, a **thermal feedback loop** must be closed:

1. **Oxygen Supply** → Air is drawn into the porous solid by natural convection (chimney effect) or forced ventilation.
2. **Surface Reaction** → O₂ reacts exothermically with the exposed char surface:
   $$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
   $$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
3. **Heat Generation** → The exothermic surface reaction releases heat at the reaction front.
4. **Heat Transfer** → Heat flows **forward** into the unreacted solid by conduction, convection (through the porous matrix), and to a lesser extent radiation.
5. **Thermal Feedback** → Preheating of the next layer of fuel to its **ignition temperature for char** (~**400 °C**) ensures the wave propagates indefinitely as long as fuel and oxygen remain.

> [!IMPORTANT] The "Why" Behind Smoldering
> Unlike flaming combustion (which depends on the gas-phase mixture reaching the flammability limit), smoldering depends only on **surface kinetics**. That is why it can persist for **hours or even days** in deep-seated materials such as coal seams, peat bogs, and landfill dumps — a phenomenon known as the **"Holdover Fire"** in fire investigation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] All High-Yield Equations for Smoldering

| Symbol | Equation / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_s$ | Smoldering propagation velocity | Speed of the reaction front | m/s |
| $v_s \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{eff}}{\rho_s \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | Classical analytical expression (Ohlemiller-style) | Conduction-limited smolder velocity | m/s |
| $Da$ | $\dfrac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}}$ | Damköhler number — flow time vs. reaction time | dimensionless |
| $Da \gg 1$ | Reaction-limited regime | Chemistry is the bottleneck | — |
| $Da \ll 1$ | Flow-limited regime | Oxygen supply is the bottleneck | — |
| $\dot{q}''_{crit}$ | Critical heat flux for smoldering-to-flaming transition | Threshold for pyrolysis gas ignition | kW/m² |
| $\rho_s$ | Bulk density of the porous solid | Mass per unit volume of fuel | kg/m³ |
| $c_p$ | Specific heat capacity of the solid | Energy to raise 1 kg by 1 K | J/(kg·K) |
| $\lambda_{eff}$ | Effective thermal conductivity (solid + gas) | Heat conduction through porous medium | W/(m·K) |
| $\Delta H_{ox}$ | Heat of char oxidation | Energy released per kg of carbon | kJ/kg |
| $Y_{O_2, crit}$ | Critical oxygen mole fraction | Below this, smoldering extinguishes | typically **~0.10 – 0.16** |
| $T_{ig}$ | Char ignition temperature | Minimum temperature for sustained surface reaction | **~400 °C** |
| $L_m$ | Characteristic smolder depth | Distance from surface to reaction zone | m |

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE] Why This Topic Matters in Industry
> - **Fire Forensics:** Cigarette-induced smoldering is the leading cause of upholstered-furniture and mattress fires. Detection of "trailing cigarette" smoldering vs. flaming ignition is a routine forensic task.
> - **Building Design:** Smoldering in concealed wall cavities can persist undetected for **days** before transitioning to flaming — a phenomenon known as the **"Long-Duration Latent Fire"**.
> - **Industrial Safety:** Self-heating in coal silos, oil-soaked lagging, and agricultural dust piles can lead to spontaneous smoldering, culminating in catastrophic fires or dust explosions.
> - **Detection Engineering:** Smoldering produces minimal heat and almost no flame — making it notoriously difficult to detect with **point-type smoke detectors (especially ionization types)**, which is why **photoelectric smoke detectors and aspirating smoke detection (ASD)** systems are mandated in critical facilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Smoldering Propagation Velocity (Conduction-Limited Model)

We consider a one-dimensional, planar, steady smoldering front propagating through a porous solid in the **+x direction** at velocity $v_s$.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance at the Reaction Front
At steady state, the heat generated by surface oxidation must equal the heat conducted forward into the unburnt solid plus the convective heat loss to the flowing oxidizer:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2} \, v_s &= \lambda_{eff} \, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = 0^+} + \rho_g \, c_{p,g} \, v_g \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- **LHS**: Volumetric heat release rate per unit area at the reaction front.
- **First term on RHS**: Conductive heat flux into the unreacted solid (the dominant forward-propagating term).
- **Second term on RHS**: Convective heat carried away by the incoming oxidizer gas.

### Step 2 — Assume Conduction Dominance (Low Flow Regime)
If the airflow is small (low $Da$ regime ignored — conduction-dominant), the convective term is negligible. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2} \, v_s &\approx \lambda_{eff} \, \dfrac{\Delta T}{\delta}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\delta$ is the thermal penetration depth ahead of the front, and $\Delta T = T_{ig} - T_\infty$.

### Step 3 — Steady Conduction Solution Ahead of the Front
In the moving frame of the front, the temperature profile ahead of the reaction zone decays exponentially:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(x) - T_\infty &= (T_{ig} - T_\infty) \, e^{-v_s x \, / \, \alpha_{eff}}
\end{aligned}$$

where the effective thermal diffusivity of the porous medium is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{eff} &= \dfrac{\lambda_{eff}}{\rho_s \, c_p}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Combine and Solve for $v_s$
Taking the temperature gradient at $x = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = 0} &= -\dfrac{v_s}{\alpha_{eff}} \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back into the energy balance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2} \, v_s &= \lambda_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{v_s}{\alpha_{eff}} \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\alpha_{eff} = \lambda_{eff} / (\rho_s c_p)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2} \, v_s &= \rho_s \, c_p \, v_s \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — The Conduction-Limited Smolder Velocity (Final Result)

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,v_s \;\approx\; \dfrac{\lambda_{eff} \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}{\rho_s \, c_p \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2}} \,}
\end{aligned}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $v_s$ is **directly proportional** to the effective thermal conductivity $\lambda_{eff}$ — better conductors (e.g., metallized foams) smolder faster.
- $v_s$ is **inversely proportional** to the volumetric heat capacity $\rho_s c_p$ — denser fuels smolder slower.
- $v_s$ is **inversely proportional** to the heat of oxidation $\Delta H_{ox}$ — fuels with high char yield (high $\Delta H_{ox}$) propagate faster.

> [!IMPORTANT] Numerical Estimate for Cellulosic Solids
> For cellulose paper ($\rho_s \approx 600$ kg/m³, $c_p \approx 1500$ J/(kg·K), $\lambda_{eff} \approx 0.1$ W/(m·K), $T_{ig} = 400$ °C, $\Delta H_{ox} = 32$ MJ/kg, $Y_{O_2} = 0.21$):
> $$v_s \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.1 \times 400}{600 \times 1500 \times 32\times 10^6 \times 0.21} \;\approx\; 6.6\times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s} \;\approx\; 0.24 \text{ mm/hr}$$
> This is the order of magnitude of **forward smolder propagation** in horizontal cellulosic insulation — slow enough to be undetectable for many hours.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Damköhler Number for Smoldering

### Step 1 — Define Characteristic Times

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{chem}} &= \dfrac{1}{A \, e^{-E_a / R T_s}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** $\tau_{\text{chem}}$ is the characteristic time for surface oxidation, governed by the **Arrhenius law**. $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is the activation energy of char oxidation (typically **120–180 kJ/mol**), $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T_s$ is the surface temperature.

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{flow}} &= \dfrac{L_m}{u_{\text{eff}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** $\tau_{\text{flow}}$ is the time for the oxidizer gas to traverse the characteristic smolder depth $L_m$ at the effective Darcy velocity $u_{\text{eff}}$ through the porous medium.

### Step 2 — Form the Damköhler Number

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,Da \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} \;=\; \dfrac{L_m \, A \, e^{-E_a / R T_s}}{u_{\text{eff}}}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Regime Identification

$$\begin{aligned}
Da \gg 1 &\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Reaction-limited (heat-limited)} \\
Da \ll 1 &\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Flow-limited (oxygen-limited)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Practical meaning:**
- **Reaction-limited** ($Da \gg 1$): Air supply is plentiful; the smolder speed is dictated by how fast char can oxidize. Increasing ventilation has **little effect**.
- **Flow-limited** ($Da \ll 1$): Air supply is the bottleneck; increasing ventilation accelerates smoldering, while reducing oxygen can **extinguish** it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Oxygen Concentration for Smolder Extinction

### Step 1 — Energy Balance at Extinction Threshold
At the extinction point, the heat generated exactly equals the heat losses:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}''_{\text{gen}} &= \dot{q}''_{\text{loss}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Heat Generation Term
The volumetric heat generation per unit area is proportional to the oxygen mass fraction reaching the reaction zone:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{q}''_{\text{gen}} &= \rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2, \text{crit}} \, k_{\text{Arr}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $k_{\text{Arr}}$ is the effective Arrhenius rate constant at the incipient reaction temperature.

### Step 3 — Solve for the Critical Oxygen Mass Fraction

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,Y_{O_2, \text{crit}} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{q}''_{\text{loss}}}{\rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, k_{\text{Arr}}}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

**Physical meaning:**
- If the local oxygen concentration drops below $Y_{O_2, \text{crit}}$, the reaction cannot sustain itself and smoldering **self-extinguishes**.
- Experimentally, for most cellulosic fuels, **$Y_{O_2, \text{crit}} \approx 0.10$–$0.16$** (i.e., **10–16 % O₂ by volume**), which is why **oxygen reduction fire prevention systems** (maintaining O₂ below ~15 %) are used in archives, vaults, and server rooms.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Smoldering vs. Flaming Combustion

| Property | Smoldering | Flaming Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| Dominant Heat Transfer | **Conduction through solid** | **Convection + radiation in gas** |
| Reaction Phase | Heterogeneous (surface) | Homogeneous (gas phase) |
| Critical Oxygen | **~10–16 %** | **~14–18 %** (depends on fuel) |
| Critical Temperature | **~400 °C** | **~300 °C (flash point of pyrolysate)** |
| Activation Energy $E_a$ | **High (120–180 kJ/mol)** | Moderate (80–120 kJ/mol) |
| Smoke Yield | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Detection Difficulty | **Extreme** (low heat, no flame) | Moderate (flame detectors work) |
| Transition Possibility | **Yes → flaming fire** | Cannot revert to smoldering |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Zone Smoldering Front (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a propagating smoldering wave, identifying the dominant physical processes and energy flows within each zone.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[AMBIENT AIR 21 PERCENT O2] --> B[FORCED OR BUOYANCY DRIVEN FLOW]
    B --> C[CHAR OXIDATION ZONE 450 TO 700 DEG C]
    C --> D[HEAT GENERATION SURFACE REACTION]
    D --> E[CONDUCTION FORWARD HEAT FLUX]
    E --> F[PYROLYSIS ZONE 150 TO 400 DEG C]
    F --> G[VOLATILE FUEL GASES TAR CO H2 CH4]
    G --> H{PYROLYSATE IGNITABLE YES OR NO}
    H -- YES --> I[FLAMING TRANSITION]
    H -- NO --> J[CONTINUED SMOULDERING]
    F --> K[PREHEATING ZONE 25 TO 150 DEG C]
    K --> L[MOISTURE EVAPORATION]
    L --> M[UNBURNT FUEL BED]
    C --> N[ASH RESIDUE]
    D --> O[CONVECTIVE HEAT LOSS TO GAS]
    D --> P[RADIATIVE HEAT LOSS]

    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    style F fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style I fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    style J fill:#228B22,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Char Oxidation Zone** (red, centre) is the heat-generating core.
- Heat flows leftward through **Conduction Forward Heat Flux** into the **Pyrolysis Zone** (orange), which in turn feeds the **Preheating Zone** (gold) and the unburnt fuel bed.
- The decision diamond **"PYROLYSATE IGNITABLE YES OR NO"** is the **smoldering-to-flaming transition gate** — the most critical branch in fire safety analysis.

---

## 4.2 Damköhler Regime Map (Reaction vs. Flow Limitation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[SMOULDERING PROCESS] --> Q{AIRFLOW RATE}
    Q -- HIGH FLOW --> R[Da LESS THAN 1]
    Q -- LOW FLOW --> T[Da GREATER THAN 1]
    R --> U[REACTION LIMITED REGIME]
    T --> V[FLOW LIMITED OXYGEN LIMITED REGIME]
    U --> X[SPEED GOVENED BY CHEMICAL KINETICS]
    V --> Y[SPEED GOVENED BY OXYGEN SUPPLY]
    X --> Z[INCREASING VENTILATION HAS NO EFFECT]
    Y --> W[INCREASING VENTILATION ACCELERATES SMOULDER]
    W --> AA[REDUCING O2 CAN EXTINGUISH FIRE]
    Z --> AB[FIRE EXTINCTION REQUIRES COOLING OR SMOTHERING]

    style S fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    style R fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style T fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style U fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style V fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style AA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

---

## 4.3 Smoldering-to-Flaming Transition Pathway

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[STAGE 1 IGNITION SOURCE] --> P2[STAGE 2 SMOULDER ESTABLISHED]
    P2 --> P3[STAGE 3 PYROLYSIS GASES ACCUMULATE]
    P3 --> P4[STAGE 4 MIXTURE WITH AIR IN FLAMMABLE RANGE]
    P4 --> P5[STAGE 5 EXTERNAL PILOT IGNITION]
    P5 --> P6[STAGE 6 FLAMING COMBUSTION ESTABLISHED]
    P3 --> P7[ALTERNATIVE SMOULDER PERSISTS]
    P7 --> P8[STAGE 7a CHAR DEPLETION]
    P8 --> P9[STAGE 8a SELF EXTINCTION]

    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    style P6 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    style P9 fill:#228B22,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Engineering takeaway:** Stages 3–5 are the **"Critical Decision Window"**. Any active fire-protection intervention (smoke detection, sprinkler cooling, oxygen reduction) during Stages 2–4 can suppress the fire **before** it transitions to flaming.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
> **Define smoldering combustion. List four characteristic features that distinguish it from flaming combustion.**

**Model Answer:**
Smoldering combustion is a slow, flameless, heterogeneous form of combustion in which a porous solid fuel reacts directly with oxygen on its surface, propagating at temperatures between **450–700 °C**.

Four distinguishing features:

1. **No visible flame** — heat is released by surface (heterogeneous) reaction, not by gas-phase oxidation.
2. **Lower temperature** — typically 450–700 °C compared to 800–1200 °C for flaming.
3. **Slow propagation velocity** — of the order of **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³ m/s**.
4. **Dense smoke production** — large volumes of white/grey smoke due to incomplete oxidation of volatiles and char.

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
> **Explain the role of polyurethane (PU) foam in smoldering fire hazards in upholstered furniture.**

**Model Answer:**
Flexible polyurethane foam is one of the most hazardous smoldering fuels due to the following reasons:
- **Open-cell porous structure** allows deep oxygen infiltration and internal smoldering propagation.
- **High carbon yield** upon thermal decomposition, providing abundant char for sustained surface oxidation.
- **High specific surface area** accelerates heterogeneous reaction rates.
- **Low thermal conductivity** traps heat within the foam, raising internal temperatures.
- **Emission of flammable volatiles** (isocyanates, hydrocarbons) during pyrolysis — these can ignite externally and cause **smoldering-to-flaming transition**.

Because of this hazard, **cigarette-ignition-resistance (CFR)** standards (e.g., **NFPA 260, NFPA 261, BS 5852**) require barriers or fire-resistant interliners in residential upholstered furniture.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**
>
> **(a)** With the aid of a labelled schematic, describe the **three thermal zones** of a propagating smoldering front in a porous solid. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **conduction-limited expression for smoldering propagation velocity** starting from a one-dimensional steady-state energy balance at the reaction front. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Three Thermal Zones (7 marks)

The smoldering front in a porous solid can be partitioned into **three thermal zones** moving in the direction of propagation:

| Zone | Temperature Range | Dominant Process | Physical Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preheating Zone** | 25 → 150 °C | Conductive heat inflow, moisture evaporation | Drying of solid |
| **Pyrolysis Zone** | 150 → 400 °C | Thermal decomposition of solid | Release of volatiles (tars, CO, CH₄) |
| **Char Oxidation Zone** | 450 → 700 °C | Heterogeneous surface reaction | Char + O₂ → CO/CO₂ + Heat |

A schematic block diagram should show:
- Unburnt solid on the left → Preheating → Pyrolysis → Char Oxidation → Ash on the right.
- Incoming oxidizer entering from the right (or above) into the char oxidation zone.
- Heat feedback from char zone to pyrolysis zone by **conduction** through the solid matrix.
- Volatile gases escaping from the pyrolysis zone upward.

> **Mark allocation:** Zone identification (3 marks), temperature ranges (2 marks), heat/mass flow arrows (2 marks).

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Conduction-Limited $v_s$ (7 marks)

The full derivation has already been provided in **Section 3.1**. The student should present the following sequence:

**Step 1.** Write the **steady-state energy balance** at the reaction front:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_s \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2} \, v_s \;=\; \lambda_{eff} \, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = 0^+}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** State the **exponential temperature profile** ahead of the front in the moving frame:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(x) - T_\infty \;=\; (T_{ig} - T_\infty) \, e^{-v_s x / \alpha_{eff}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate and evaluate the gradient at $x = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = 0} \;=\; -\dfrac{v_s}{\alpha_{eff}} \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute back and simplify using $\alpha_{eff} = \lambda_{eff}/(\rho_s c_p)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_s \;\approx\; \dfrac{\lambda_{eff} \, (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}{\rho_s \, c_p \, \Delta H_{ox} \, Y_{O_2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5.** Discuss the physical dependence: $v_s \propto \lambda_{eff}$ and $v_s \propto (T_{ig} - T_\infty)$, but $v_s \propto 1/(\rho_s c_p)$ and $v_s \propto 1/\Delta H_{ox}$.

> **Mark allocation:** Energy balance (2), temperature profile (2), gradient evaluation (1), final expression with physical discussion (2).

---

> **Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**
>
> **(a)** Define the **Damköhler number** in the context of smoldering combustion. Distinguish between **reaction-limited** and **flow-limited** regimes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of **smoldering-to-flaming transition** in upholstered furniture fires. What fire-protection measures can interrupt this transition? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Damköhler Number (7 marks)

**Definition:**
The Damköhler number ($Da$) is a dimensionless ratio comparing the **characteristic flow time** to the **characteristic chemical reaction time** in a smoldering system:

$$\begin{aligned}
Da \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{chem}} &= \dfrac{1}{A \, e^{-E_a / R T_s}}, \qquad \tau_{\text{flow}} = \dfrac{L_m}{u_{\text{eff}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Regime distinction:**

- **Reaction-limited regime** ($Da \gg 1$): Chemical kinetics is slower than oxygen supply. The smoldering rate is governed by the surface reaction rate; ventilation has minimal effect. Extinguishment requires **cooling** or **chemical inhibition** (e.g., water mist, dry chemical).
- **Flow-limited regime** ($Da \ll 1$): Oxygen supply is slower than the chemical reaction. Smoldering rate is governed by the Darcy airflow through the porous solid; increasing ventilation accelerates the smolder. Extinguishment can be achieved by **smothering** (reducing O₂ below ~10–16 %).

> **Mark allocation:** Definition (2), each regime (2 each), practical implication (1).

#### Part (b) — Smoldering-to-Flaming Transition (7 marks)

**Mechanism:**
In upholstered furniture fires, a smoldering source (typically a lit cigarette) initiates **deep-seated smoldering** within the flexible PU foam cushion. As the smolder front propagates inward and downward, the **Pyrolysis Zone** generates large volumes of flammable volatiles (isocyanates, aldehydes, hydrocarbons, CO). These gases escape through the fabric cover and **accumulate above the cushion**, where they mix with ambient air.

When the **fuel-air mixture in this boundary layer reaches its lower flammability limit** and encounters a sufficient **pilot ignition source** (e.g., a stray spark, hot ember, or open flame from another source), the gases ignite. This **pilot ignition of accumulated pyrolysates** marks the smoldering-to-flaming transition. Once flaming begins, the entire foam can be engulfed in seconds.

**Fire-protection measures to interrupt the transition:**

1. **Smoke Detection (Photoelectric or ASD):** Detects the high smoke yield of the pre-transition smolder phase, allowing evacuation before flaming ignition.
2. **Water Mist / Fine Water Spray:** Cools the pyrolysis zone below the pyrolysis temperature, stopping volatile gas generation.
3. **Cigarette-Ignition-Resistant (CIR) Interliners:** Physical barriers that prevent the cigarette from reaching the PU foam.
4. **Smothering Blankets:** Cut off oxygen supply to the smoldering foam (effective in flow-limited regime).
5. **Oxygen Reduction Systems:** Maintain ambient O₂ below 16 % in protected compartments (e.g., data centres, archives).
6. **Flame-Resistant (FR) Fabric Coverings:** Self-extinguishing fabrics reduce the likelihood of external pilot ignition of pyrolysates.

> **Mark allocation:** Transition mechanism (3 marks), five protection measures (1 mark each = 5 marks), but capped at 7 — pick the most relevant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)
>
> 1. **Mistaking smoldering for flaming combustion**: Students often write "smoldering is a slow type of fire" without specifying it is **flameless and heterogeneous**. This immediately costs **2 marks** in any Part A definition.
> 2. **Confusing smolder velocity $v_s$ with flame spread velocity**: These are **physically different**. Smolder spread is conduction-dominated through the solid matrix; flame spread is radiation/convection-dominated over the gas-phase boundary layer.
> 3. **Wrong temperature ranges**: Smoldering temperature is **450–700 °C**, NOT 1000 °C+. Writing the flaming range will be flagged.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw arrows** in the schematic for heat feedback and oxidizer flow in the three-zone diagram. KTU examiners explicitly award **2 marks** for arrows and labels in diagrams.
> 5. **Skipping steps in derivation**: Even if the final boxed equation is correct, **partial credit is given for intermediate steps**. Always show the substitution of $\alpha_{eff} = \lambda_{eff}/(\rho_s c_p)$ explicitly.
> 6. **Damköhler regime confusion**: Writing "$Da \gg 1$ = flow-limited" is the **most common single error** in the entire fire engineering syllabus. Memorize correctly: **$Da \gg 1$ → reaction-limited; $Da \ll 1$ → flow-limited**.
> 7. **Not naming real-world examples**: KTU examiners reward **application-based answers**. Always cite PU foam, cigarette-induced fires, or coal self-heating wherever relevant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist — Smoldering

- **Definition:** Slow, flameless, **heterogeneous** combustion of porous solids at **450–700 °C**.
- **Mechanism:** Surface oxidation of **char** by atmospheric O₂; heat propagates by **conduction** through the solid matrix.
- **Three Zones:** **Preheating (25–150 °C)** → **Pyrolysis (150–400 °C)** → **Char Oxidation (450–700 °C)**.
- **Smolder Velocity Formula:** $v_s \approx \dfrac{\lambda_{eff} (T_{ig} - T_\infty)}{\rho_s c_p \Delta H_{ox} Y_{O_2}}$ — **conduction-limited**.
- **Damköhler Number:** $Da = \tau_{\text{flow}} / \tau_{\text{chem}}$ — **$Da \gg 1$ = reaction-limited, $Da \ll 1$ = flow-limited**.
- **Critical Oxygen:** ~**10–16 % by volume** — basis of oxygen-reduction fire prevention systems.
- **Common Fuels:** PU foam, cellulosic materials (cotton, paper, hay), coal, charcoal, sawdust, cigarette tobacco.
- **Hazards:** **Smoldering-to-flaming transition** via ignition of accumulated pyrolysates; **deep-seated, long-duration latent fires**; extreme detection difficulty.
- **Detection:** **Photoelectric smoke detectors and ASD systems** are preferred over ionization detectors.
- **Key Distinction from Flaming:** Smoldering = **no flame, low temperature, heterogeneous**; Flaming = **flame, higher temperature, gas-phase**.
- **Forensic Marker:** Smoldering fires often show **cone-shaped char patterns** with the **point of origin at the base**, unlike V-shaped or U-shaped patterns of flaming fires.
- **Industry Standards:** NFPA 260, NFPA 261, BS 5852, ISO 13943, NFPA 909 (cleanrooms), NFPA 75 (data centres).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame and Heat Stages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flame and Heat Stages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Flame?

A **flame** is the visible, luminous zone of a combustion reaction in which a fuel and an oxidizer undergo rapid, self-sustaining exothermic oxidation, releasing **heat**, **light**, and **combustion products**. In fire engineering, a flame is treated as a *non-equilibrium reacting flow* where chemical energy of the fuel is converted into thermal energy at temperatures typically ranging from **600 K to 2000 K** depending on the fuel and ventilation conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OECHT835 – Module 1):**
> A flame is a reaction-diffusion zone where fuel vapor and oxidizer mix, ignite, and liberate energy. The study of flame structure is foundational to fire growth, compartment fire dynamics, and suppression engineering.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Candle in a Dark Room"

Imagine lighting a candle in a dark room. The tiny blue region near the wick is the **reaction zone** (where chemistry happens). The glowing yellow zone above it is the **luminous flame** (where soot particles radiate light). The heat you feel on your hand from a distance is **radiative and convective heat transfer**, not the flame itself. Just like the candle, every fire has three regions: a fuel source, an oxidizer supply, and a reaction zone — remove any one and the flame extinguishes. This is the **Fire Triangle** in its simplest form.

## 1.3 What are "Heat Stages" in a Fire?

The term **"heat stages"** refers to the time-resolved evolution of thermal energy liberation during the life-cycle of a fire, typically broken into four classic phases:

| Stage | Duration Range | Temperature | Key Characteristic |
|-------|---------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Incipient (Ignition) Stage** | 0 – 5 min | Ambient – 100 °C | Smouldering, pre-flashover |
| **Growth Stage** | 5 – 10 min | 100 °C – 600 °C | Flame spread, HRR rises |
| **Fully Developed Stage** | 10 – 25 min | 600 °C – 1100 °C | Flashover, peak HRR |
| **Decay (Cooling) Stage** | > 25 min | Falling curve | Fuel depletion, smoulder out |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners love testing the boundaries between stages (e.g., *"At what temperature does flashover occur?"*) and the corresponding **heat release rate (HRR)** at each stage. Memorize the table above.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Temperature

The **ASTM E119 Standard Fire Curve** (also called the *Cellulosic Fire Curve*) is the most commonly cited time-temperature relationship in fire engineering and is used for structural fire resistance testing worldwide.

$$T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

where:
- $T(t)$ = average furnace temperature in °C
- $t$ = time in minutes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **hydrocarbon fire curve** is far more severe and is used for petrochemical facilities:
> $$T(t) = 20 + 1080 \left(1 - 0.325 e^{-0.167 t} - 0.675 e^{-2.5 t}\right)$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flames

Flames are classified along **two independent axes**:

### A. Based on Mixing of Fuel and Air
1. **Premixed Flame** — Fuel and oxidizer are mixed *before* reaching the reaction zone (e.g., Bunsen burner with air inlet open). Combustion is fast and uniform.
2. **Non-Premixed (Diffusion) Flame** — Fuel and oxidizer mix *by diffusion* at the reaction zone (e.g., candle, pool fire). Combustion rate is controlled by molecular mixing.

### B. Based on Flow Character
1. **Laminar Flame** — Smooth, layered flow; low Reynolds number. Found in small candles, matchsticks.
2. **Turbulent Flame** — Chaotic, fluctuating flow; high Reynolds number. Found in compartment fires, industrial pool fires.

## 2.2 Structure of a Laminar Diffusion Flame (Candle Model)

A canonical candle flame has three concentric zones:

- **Inner Zone (Dark Cone):** Unburnt fuel vapor rising from the wick; no oxygen; temperature ~ **600 K**.
- **Reaction Zone (Blue Luminous Shell):** Combustion occurs; stoichiometric mixing; peak temperature ~ **1500–1800 K**; emits blue–violet light due to $\text{CH}^*$ and $\text{C}_2^*$ radicals.
- **Outer Zone (Yellow/Glowing):** Soot particles formed in fuel-rich regions are heated and **incandesce**, giving the yellow glow; temperature drops to ~ **1200 K**.

> [!NOTE]
> The yellow luminosity of a candle is **not** due to combustion, but due to **blackbody radiation from incandescent soot particles**. In a Bunsen burner with sufficient air, the flame appears blue because soot is fully oxidized.

## 2.3 Heat Stages — Detailed Phase Analysis

### Stage 1: Incipient (Ignition) Stage
- **Trigger:** External heat flux raises the fuel to its **piloted ignition temperature** $T_{ig}$.
- **Chemistry:** Pyrolysis of solid fuel releases combustible vapors.
- **Heat Transfer:** Dominated by **conduction** into the solid fuel and **convection** away from the fuel surface.
- **HRR:** Negligible ($\dot{Q} < 25$ kW).
- **Detection:** Smoke detectors activate; heat detectors do *not*.

### Stage 2: Growth Stage
- **Phenomenon:** Flames spread across the fuel surface or to adjacent fuels via **radiative** and **convective** feedback.
- **Critical Event — Flashover** is approached when the upper gas layer temperature reaches **600 °C** and the radiant flux to the floor is **~20 kW/m²**.
- **HRR Growth:** Often modeled using the famous **$t^2$ fire growth model** (see Section 3).

### Stage 3: Fully Developed Stage
- **Phenomenon:** All combustible contents within the compartment are involved. The fire is **ventilation-controlled**.
- **Peak HRR:** Determined by the ventilation factor $A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ (see Section 2.4).
- **Temperatures:** Hot gas layer reaches **800 °C – 1100 °C**.
- **Structural Threat:** Steel loses ~50% of its strength at **550 °C**; concrete spalls at **300 °C+**.

### Stage 4: Decay Stage
- **Cause:** Fuel depletion or ventilation limitation.
- **Behavior:** HRR decays either as a **linear decrease** or **exponential decay** depending on the fire load.
- **Residual Risk:** Smouldering can persist for hours; dangerous for forensic investigators and firefighters during overhaul.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat release rate from mass loss | kW |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{t^2} = \alpha t^2$ | $t^2$ fire growth model (NFPA) | kW, s |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{vent} = 1500 \, A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ | Ventilation-controlled HRR (post-flashover) | kW |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}'' = \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c$ | HRR per unit area (HRRPUA) | kW/m² |
| 5 | $T_{ig}$ (Piloted) | Piloted ignition temperature | K or °C |
| 6 | $T_{fl} = T_\infty + \frac{\dot{Q}''_{cr}}{h_c}$ | Flame temperature with critical flux | °C |
| 7 | $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ | ASTM E119 standard fire | °C, min |
| 8 | $\text{Re} = \frac{\rho u L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for flame regime | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dot{q}''_r = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Radiative heat flux (Stefan–Boltzmann) | kW/m² |
| 10 | $\dot{q}''_c = h_c (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat flux (Newton's law) | kW/m² |
| 11 | $\dot{q}''_k = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$ | Conductive heat flux (Fourier's law) | kW/m² |
| 12 | $A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ | Ventilation factor | m$^{5/2}$ |
| 13 | $\alpha$ (slow/medium/fast/ultra-fast) | 0.0029 / 0.0117 / 0.0469 / 0.1876 | kW/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The ventilation-controlled HRR formula $\dot{Q}_{vent} = 1500 \, A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ is valid *only* in **post-flashover** ventilation-controlled fires. The constant 1500 has units of $\text{kW/m}^{5/2}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Structural Design** | ASTM E119 / ISO 834 used to rate fire resistance of beams, columns, walls |
| **Fire Detection** | $t^2$ model predicts time-to-detect; used in detector placement codes (NFPA 72) |
| **Suppression Design** | Sprinkler activation time estimated using ceiling-jet temperature vs. RTI |
| **Evacuation Modeling** | RSET calculated from fire growth (ASET vs. RSET analysis) |
| **Forensic Engineering** | Burn patterns + HRR curves help reconstruct fire origin & cause |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the $t^2$ Fire Growth Model

The $t^2$ model assumes that, for a free-burning fire in the growth stage, the **heat release rate grows as the square of time**, originating from the observation that the **flame area (or fire perimeter) grows linearly with time**, and HRR is proportional to flame area.

### Step 1 — Geometric Assumption

Assume the fire spreads radially outward in a circular pattern with constant velocity $v$ (m/s). At time $t$, the fire radius is:

$$r(t) = v \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Burning Area

The burning area of a circular pool/area fire is:

$$A_f(t) = \pi r^2(t) = \pi v^2 t^2$$

### Step 3 — HRR per Unit Area

The HRR per unit area is the material property $\dot{Q}''$ (kW/m²), assumed constant for the given fuel:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \dot{Q}'' \cdot A_f(t) = \dot{Q}'' \cdot \pi v^2 t^2$$

### Step 4 — Compact Form

Define the fire growth coefficient $\alpha = \dot{Q}'' \pi v^2$ (units: kW/s²). Then:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2}$$

where $\alpha$ takes the NFPA 72 standard values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{\text{slow}} &= 0.00293 \; \text{kW/s}^2 \\
\alpha_{\text{medium}} &= 0.01172 \; \text{kW/s}^2 \\
\alpha_{\text{fast}} &= 0.0469 \; \text{kW/s}^2 \\
\alpha_{\text{ultra-fast}} &= 0.1876 \; \text{kW/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Ventilation-Controlled HRR

In a post-flashover, ventilation-controlled compartment fire, the air supply through openings is the rate-limiting step. The mass flow rate of air entering through a vertical opening of area $A_v$ and height $H_v$ is given by the orifice equation:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = C_d A_v \sqrt{2 \rho_\infty g H_v} \cdot \left(\frac{T_\infty}{T_{gas}}\right)^{0.5}$$

### Step 1 — Simplification

For a fully developed fire, $T_{gas} / T_\infty \approx 4$, so the correction factor is approximately $\sqrt{1/4} = 0.5$. Combining constants $C_d$, $\rho_\infty$, and $g$ yields the empirical Kawagoe constant $1500$ in SI units.

### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Conversion

Each kg of air consumes approximately $1/\phi$ kg of fuel, releasing $\Delta H_c / r$ kJ per kg of air. With a stoichiometric air-to-fuel mass ratio of about **5.7** for typical combustibles, the empirical result is:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{vent} = 1500 \, A_v \sqrt{H_v}}$$

where:
- $A_v$ = vertical opening area (m²)
- $H_v$ = opening height (m)
- $\dot{Q}_{vent}$ = peak HRR in kW

### Step 3 — Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A compartment fire has a single window of width 2 m and height 1.5 m. Estimate the peak HRR if the fire becomes ventilation-controlled.

**Solution:**

$$A_v = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$\dot{Q}_{vent} = 1500 \times 3 \times \sqrt{1.5} = 1500 \times 3 \times 1.2247$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{vent} \approx 5511 \; \text{kW} \approx 5.5 \; \text{MW}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Critical Heat Flux for Ignition

The piloted ignition of a solid occurs when the **net heat flux** into the surface equals the **critical heat flux for ignition** $\dot{q}''_{cr}$. The surface energy balance is:

$$\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{q}''_e - \dot{q}''_{re} - \dot{q}''_{loss}$$

### Step 1 — At the Threshold of Ignition

At ignition, $\dot{q}''_{net} = \dot{q}''_{cr}$, hence:

$$\dot{q}''_{cr} = \dot{q}''_{e} - \dot{q}''_{re} - h_c (T_{ig} - T_\infty) - \varepsilon \sigma T_{ig}^4$$

### Step 2 — Solving for Ignition Temperature

Rearranging and solving numerically (or via Babrauskas correlation):

$$\boxed{\dot{q}''_{cr} = \sigma \left(T_{ig}^4 - T_\infty^4\right) \cdot \text{(view factor correction)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values of $\dot{q}''_{cr}$:**
> - Wood (piloted): 10 – 13 kW/m²
> - PMMA: 11 – 15 kW/m²
> - Cotton fabric: 15 – 20 kW/m²
> - Paper: 10 – 12 kW/m²

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stage-by-Stage Energy Equation

| Stage | Governing Equation | Dominant Mode |
|-------|--------------------|----------------|
| Incipient | $\dot{q}''_{cr} = \sigma(T_{ig}^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Conduction |
| Growth | $\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$ | Convection + Radiation |
| Fully Developed | $\dot{Q} = 1500 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ | Radiation (dom.) |
| Decay | $\dot{Q}(t) = \dot{Q}_{peak} e^{-k(t - t_p)}$ | Mixed, declining |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗂️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Fire Development Stages with Heat Indicators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition Source] --> B[Incipient Stage]
    B --> C[Growth Stage]
    C --> D[Flashover Threshold]
    D --> E[Fully Developed Stage]
    E --> F[Decay Stage]
    F --> G[Extinguishment]

    B1[Ambient T, smouldering, low HRR] -.-> B
    C1[Flame spread, HRR = alpha times t squared] -.-> C
    D1[600 C upper layer, 20 kW per sqm floor flux] -.-> D
    E1[Ventilation controlled, peak HRR via A_v sqrt H_v] -.-> E
    F1[Fuel depletion, exponential HRR decay] -.-> F
    G1[Smoulder out, overhaul begins] -.-> G

    B --- B1
    C --- C1
    D --- D1
    E --- E1
    F --- F1
    G --- G1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat Transfer Modes in a Compartment Fire

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fuel[FUEL PACKAGE]
        F1[Combustible Material]
    end

    subgraph ZoneA[REACTION ZONE]
        R1[Flame Plume]
        R2[Hot Gas Layer]
    end

    subgraph ZoneB[COMPARTMENT BOUNDARIES]
        C1[Ceiling]
        C2[Walls]
        C3[Floor]
    end

    subgraph ZoneC[EXTERIOR / TARGETS]
        T1[Adjacent Fuel]
        T2[Detector]
        T3[Structural Member]
    end

    F1 -->|Pyrolysis vapors| R1
    R1 -->|Convection| R2
    R2 -->|Radiation| C1
    R2 -->|Radiation| C2
    C1 -->|Conduction| C2
    C2 -->|Conduction| C3
    R2 -->|Convection + Radiation| T1
    R2 -->|Ceiling jet convection| T2
    C1 -->|Conduction + Radiation| T3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Flame Regime Classification

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Premixed
    [*] --> Diffusion

    Premixed --> Laminar_Premix : Low Re
    Premixed --> Turbulent_Premix : High Re
    Diffusion --> Laminar_Diff : Low Re
    Diffusion --> Turbulent_Diff : High Re

    Laminar_Premix --> Bunsen_Flame : Example
    Turbulent_Premix --> Industrial_Burner : Example
    Laminar_Diff --> Candle : Example
    Turbulent_Diff --> Pool_Fire : Example
```

## 4.4 Schematic of ASTM E119 Standard Fire Curve (Tabular Form)

| Time $t$ (min) | Temperature $T$ (°C) |
|----------------|---------------------|
| 5 | 576 |
| 10 | 678 |
| 30 | 842 |
| 60 | 925 |
| 120 | 1029 |
| 240 | 1133 |
| 480 | 1242 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard fire curve **does not** decay — it is monotonically increasing and used for design conservatism. The natural fire curve (e.g., Eurocode parametric) does decay.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "flame" as studied in fire engineering and distinguish between a premixed flame and a diffusion flame.**

**Model Answer:**

A **flame** is the visible luminous zone of a combustion reaction where fuel and oxidizer react in a self-sustaining exothermic process, releasing heat and light.

- **Premixed Flame:** Fuel and oxidizer are mixed *before* reaching the reaction zone. Combustion is rapid, uniform, and the flame is typically short and blue (e.g., Bunsen burner with air inlet open). Combustion rate is controlled by **chemistry**.

- **Diffusion Flame:** Fuel and oxidizer mix *by diffusion* at the reaction zone. The flame is longer, often luminous (yellow), and the combustion rate is controlled by **mixing** (e.g., candle, pool fire).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for distinction with example)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the $t^2$ fire growth model and list the four NFPA growth coefficients.**

**Model Answer:**

The $t^2$ fire growth model is an empirical relationship for the heat release rate during the growth stage of a free-burning fire:

$$\dot{Q}(t) = \alpha t^2$$

where $\dot{Q}$ is in kW, $t$ is in seconds, and $\alpha$ is the fire growth coefficient.

| Growth Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Time to 1 MW |
|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| Slow | 0.00293 | > 600 s |
| Medium | 0.01172 | 300 s |
| Fast | 0.0469 | 150 s |
| Ultra-Fast | 0.1876 | 75 s |

*(3 marks — 1 for formula, 2 for table)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **four heat stages** of a compartment fire with the help of a labeled temperature-vs-time curve. Mention the temperature range and dominant heat transfer mode at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the $t^2$ fire growth model from first principles starting from the assumption of a circular fire spread. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Four Heat Stages:**

1. **Incipient Stage (0 – 5 min):** Temperature remains near ambient (~20–100 °C). The fuel undergoes pyrolysis and smoulders. Dominant mode: **conduction** into the solid. HRR is negligible. Smoke detectors may activate.

2. **Growth Stage (5 – 10 min):** Flames spread across the fuel surface. Temperature rises to 100–600 °C. Dominant modes: **convection** in the plume and **radiation** from the flame. HRR grows approximately as $\alpha t^2$.

3. **Fully Developed Stage (10 – 25 min):** All combustible contents are involved. Upper gas layer temperature reaches 600–1100 °C. **Flashover** occurs at ~600 °C. Dominant mode: **radiation** (60–80% of total heat transfer). Peak HRR is ventilation-limited.

4. **Decay Stage (> 25 min):** Fuel is consumed. Temperature and HRR fall. Dominant mode: declining mixed heat transfer. Smouldering may persist.
