The temperature curve follows the **ASTM E119 standard fire** in design or a natural fire curve in real scenarios.

**(b) Derivation:** [Refer to Section 3.1 above for full derivation]

Assumptions: (i) fire spreads radially with constant velocity $v$, (ii) HRR per unit area is constant.

$$r(t) = vt \Rightarrow A_f = \pi v^2 t^2 \Rightarrow \dot{Q}(t) = \dot{Q}'' \pi v^2 t^2 = \alpha t^2$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **structure of a candle (laminar diffusion) flame**, identifying the three zones and their typical temperatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A post-flashover fire breaks out in a compartment with a single window opening of width 3 m and height 2 m. Calculate the **peak heat release rate** using the ventilation-controlled HRR formula. If the fire load is 600 MJ/m² and floor area is 25 m², comment on whether the fire is fuel-controlled or ventilation-controlled. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Structure of Candle Flame:** [Refer to Section 2.2 above]

Three zones: **Inner dark cone** (~600 K, unburnt fuel vapor), **Reaction zone** (~1500–1800 K, blue, stoichiometric combustion), **Outer luminous zone** (~1200 K, yellow due to incandescent soot).

**(b) Calculation:**

$$A_v = 3 \times 2 = 6 \; \text{m}^2, \quad H_v = 2 \; \text{m}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{vent} = 1500 \times 6 \times \sqrt{2} = 1500 \times 6 \times 1.414 = 12{,}727 \; \text{kW} \approx 12.7 \; \text{MW}$$

**Fuel-controlled HRR estimate:**

$$Q_{total} = 600 \times 25 = 15{,}000 \; \text{MJ}$$

Assuming complete combustion in 20 minutes (1200 s):

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 15{,}000 / 1200 = 12.5 \; \text{MW}$$

**Comparison:** $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 12.5 \; \text{MW} < \dot{Q}_{vent} = 12.7 \; \text{MW}$.

Since the available fuel HRR is less than the ventilation capacity, the fire is **fuel-controlled** (just barely — a slight increase in window size would tip the balance).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of the Kawagoe constant:** The constant 1500 is in $\text{kW/m}^{5/2}$, not in MW. A common error is using 1500 directly with areas in cm² — *always convert to SI units first*.
> 2. **Mixing up piloted and auto-ignition temperatures:** Piloted $T_{ig}$ is *lower* than auto-ignition $T_{ig}$. Use piloted values in most fire scenarios.
> 3. **Confusing the $t^2$ model with a cooling law:** The $t^2$ model applies *only* to the growth stage. Do not extrapolate it past the peak.
> 4. **Neglecting the ceiling jet:** Many students forget to account for the **ceiling jet temperature** when calculating sprinkler/detector activation — this is a frequent 2-mark loss.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ A **flame** is a reaction-diffusion zone; it is **not** just a hot region.
- ✅ Two main flame types: **premixed** (chemistry-controlled) and **diffusion** (mixing-controlled).
- ✅ Candle flame is a classic example of a **laminar diffusion flame** with **three zones** (dark cone, reaction, luminous outer).
- ✅ **Four heat stages:** Incipient → Growth → Fully Developed → Decay.
- ✅ **Flashover** occurs at ~600 °C upper layer temperature and ~20 kW/m² floor flux.
- ✅ **$t^2$ fire growth model:** $\dot{Q} = \alpha t^2$, with four NFPA categories (slow/medium/fast/ultra-fast).
- ✅ **Ventilation-controlled peak HRR:** $\dot{Q}_{vent} = 1500 A_v \sqrt{H_v}$ (SI units, kW).
- ✅ **ASTM E119 standard fire:** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ — monotonically increasing, no decay.
- ✅ **Hydrocarbon fire curve** is used for petrochemical facilities and is far more severe.
- ✅ **Three heat transfer modes in fires:** Conduction (in solids), Convection (in plumes/ceiling jets), Radiation (dominant in fully developed fires).
- ✅ **Critical heat flux** $\dot{q}''_{cr}$ for piloted ignition of wood ≈ 10–13 kW/m².
- ✅ **Steel** loses 50% strength at ~550 °C; **concrete** spalls at 300 °C+.
- ✅ Always specify whether HRR is **fuel-controlled** or **ventilation-controlled** — the smaller value governs.
- ✅ **Don't confuse** fire growth coefficient $\alpha$ with absorptivity — same symbol, different meaning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Kinds of Combustion: Flash Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Special Kinds of Combustion: Flash Fire # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flash Fire (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):** > A **flash fire** is a rapid, intense, and short-duration combustion event that occurs when a dispersed cloud of **flammable vapor, mist, or dust** mixed with air (within its **flammability limits**) is ignited. It is characterized by a moving flame front that propagates through the fuel-air mixture, producing a transient pressure rise and significant thermal radiation, but **without** producing a sustained, over-pressurization shock wave (which distinguishes it from a **vapor cloud explosion** or **deflagration-to-detonation transition**). The phenomenon is governed by the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)** and **NFPA (National Fire Protection Association)** classification standards. According to **NFPA 30** and **NFPA 68**, a flash fire is treated as a non-confined, non-detonative combustion event. ## 1.2 Key Distinguishing Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | Flash Point | $T_{f}$ | Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off vapor to form an ignitable mixture in air | $0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $100\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | | Fire Point | $T_{fire}$ | Lowest temperature at which sustained combustion occurs | $T_{fire} \approx T_f + 5\text{–}10\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | | Auto-Ignition Temp | $T_{AIT}$ | Temperature at which spontaneous ignition occurs without external source | $200\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $600\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | | Lower Flammability Limit | LFL | Minimum fuel concentration in air for combustion | $1\%$ to $5\%$ by volume | | Upper Flammability Limit | UFL | Maximum fuel concentration in air for combustion | $7\%$ to $50\%$ by volume | | Stoichiometric Ratio | $\phi$ | Actual air-fuel ratio / theoretical air-fuel ratio | $0.5 \le \phi \le 2.0$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The examiner frequently tests the distinction between **Flash Point** and **Fire Point**. A liquid at its flash point produces vapor that **burns momentarily** (flash), while at its fire point, combustion is **self-sustaining**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Perfume Bottle Trick:** > Imagine spraying perfume near a candle flame. The tiny liquid droplets become a fine **mist (fuel)** suspended in **air (oxidizer)**. When the flame reaches this mist, the entire cloud ignites almost instantly with a soft **"whoosh"** sound, then disappears. That is a **flash fire** in miniature. > - The **perfume droplets** = the fuel (combustible liquid/vapor) > - The **air around them** = the oxidizer > - The **candle flame** = the ignition source > - The **"whoosh"** = the flame front propagating through the cloud If the perfume had been confined in a closed container and ignited, the rapid pressure rise would have caused a **vapor cloud explosion (VCE)** — a far more dangerous event. Thus, the **confinement geometry** is the key differentiator. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Examinations:** > - Universal Gas Constant: $\mathbf{R = 8.314 \;\mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}}$ > - Standard Atmospheric Pressure: $\mathbf{P_0 = 101.325 \;\mathrm{kPa}}$ > - Reference Temperature: $\mathbf{T_0 = 298.15 \;\mathrm{K} \;(25\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C})}$ > - Adiabatic Flame Temperature of Hydrocarbons in Air: $\mathbf{T_{ad} \approx 2000\text{–}2300\;\mathrm{K}}$ > - Laminar Burning Velocity of Hydrocarbons: $\mathbf{S_L \approx 0.4\text{–}0.5 \;\mathrm{m/s}}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Stage Mechanism of a Flash Fire A flash fire is **not instantaneous** — it follows a precise thermochemical sequence: ### Stage 1: Fuel Release (Dispersion Phase) A flammable liquid (e.g., gasoline, acetone, LPG) escapes its containment. The liquid begins to evaporate, and the vapor mixes with ambient air through **diffusion** and **convective transport**. The resulting fuel-air cloud must lie within the **flammability envelope** (between LFL and UFL) to be ignitable. ### Stage 2: Ignition (Initiation Phase) An ignition source (spark, hot surface, static discharge, open flame) with sufficient **activation energy** $E_a$ contacts the cloud. The Arrhenius rate law governs the reaction initiation: $$ k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)} $$ where: - $k$ = reaction rate constant - $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor) - $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = universal gas constant - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) ### Stage 3: Propagation (Combustion Phase) The flame front propagates outward at the **laminar burning velocity** $S_L$. The heat released radiates outward, causing thermal injury and property damage. $$ S_L = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha \cdot \dot{q}'''}{\rho_u \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{ad} - T_u)}} $$ where: - $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of the unburned mixture - $\dot{q}'''$ = volumetric heat release rate - $\rho_u$ = density of unburned gas - $c_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure - $T_{ad}$ = adiabatic flame temperature - $T_u$ = unburned gas temperature > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Concept:** A flash fire is a **subsonic** event (flame speed $S_L < 0.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$). If the flame front accelerates to **supersonic** velocities (Mach number > 1), the event transitions to a **detonation**, which is categorically different and far more destructive. ## 2.2 The Flammability Envelope The combustion triangle evolves into a **flammability tetrahedron** when considering a flash fire: ``` FUEL (Hydrocarbon Vapor) /\ / \ / \ / FLASH \ / FIRE \ / ENVELOPE \ / \ /________________\ OXYGEN ──── HEAT ──── CHAIN REACTION ``` **Le Chatelier's Rule** for multi-component fuel mixtures: $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} \right)} $$ $$ UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i} \right)} $$ where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$ in the fuel mixture. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry Sector | Flash Fire Hazard | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---|---| | Petrochemical Refineries | LPG/Crude vapor leaks | Vapor recovery systems, gas detectors | | Paint Manufacturing | Solvent vapor accumulation | Explosion-proof ventilation | | Grain Silos | Combustible dust clouds | Dust suppression, inerting | | LNG Terminals | Methane vapor clouds | Vapor dispersion modeling | | Pharmaceutical | Organic solvent handling | Nitrogen blanketing | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference) > [!NOTE] > **Save This Section for Quick Revision Before the Exam.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless | | 2 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{4.773 \cdot \mathrm{O_2\%}}{100 - 4.773 \cdot \mathrm{O_2\%}}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (volumetric) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot S_L \cdot A_f$ | Mass burning rate | kg/s | | 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Heat release rate | W (or J/s) | | 5 | $q'' = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{4\pi r^2}$ | Radiative heat flux at distance $r$ | W/m² | | 6 | $T_{ad} = T_u + \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{\sum n_i c_{p,i}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K | | 7 | $LFL_{mix} = 100 \left( \sum \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i} \right)^{-1}$ | Le Chatelier's rule (LFL) | % by volume | | 8 | $k = A e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ | Arrhenius reaction rate | 1/s | > **Critical Pitfall:** In problems involving **non-stoichiometric** combustion, never assume $\phi = 1$ unless explicitly stated. Always compute the actual air-fuel ratio first. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for a General Hydrocarbon Consider a general hydrocarbon fuel $\mathrm{C_x H_y O_z}$. Combustion in pure oxygen: $$ \mathrm{C_x H_y O_z + \nu_{O_2} O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} H_2O} $$ ### Step 1: Atom Balance From oxygen balance: $$ 2\nu_{O_2} + z = 2x + \frac{y}{2} $$ ### Step 2: Solve for $\nu_{O_2}$ $$ \nu_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} $$ ### Step 3: Convert to Air (assuming 21% O₂ by volume, or 1 mol O₂ per 4.76 mol air) $$ \nu_{air} = 4.76 \left( x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} \right) $$ ### Step 4: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Mass Ratio $$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{\nu_{air} \cdot M_{air}}{M_{fuel}} = \dfrac{4.76 \cdot 28.97 \cdot \left( x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2} \right)}{12.011x + 1.008y + 15.999z} $$ where $M_{air} = 28.97 \;\mathrm{g/mol}$ and $M_{fuel}$ is the molecular weight of the hydrocarbon. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Flash Fire Hazard Calculation for Methane (CH₄) > **Problem (Modeled on KTU ESE Pattern):** > A storage tank releases **1 kg of methane** (CH₄) at $T = 298\,\mathrm{K}$ and $P = 101.325\,\mathrm{kPa}$. Determine: > (a) The stoichiometric air requirement (mol and mass). > (b) The flammability range in air. > (c) The maximum heat release if complete combustion occurs. > (d) The radiative heat flux at a distance of $r = 5\,\mathrm{m}$ from the fireball. > > **Given Data:** $\Delta H_c (\mathrm{CH_4}) = -802.3\;\mathrm{kJ/mol}$, LFL = 5%, UFL = 15%. ### Part (a): Stoichiometric Air Requirement For $\mathrm{CH_4}$: $x=1$, $y=4$, $z=0$. **Step A1: Moles of CH₄ released** $$ n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{1\;\mathrm{kg}}{16.04\;\mathrm{g/mol}} = \dfrac{1000\;\mathrm{g}}{16.04\;\mathrm{g/mol}} = 62.34\;\mathrm{mol} $$ **Step A2: Moles of O₂ required (from Section 3.1 formula)** $$ \nu_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - 0 = 2\;\mathrm{mol\,O_2/mol\,CH_4} $$ **Step A3: Moles of air required** $$ \nu_{air} = 4.76 \times 2 = 9.52\;\mathrm{mol\,air/mol\,CH_4} $$ **Step A4: Mass of air required** $$ m_{air} = n_{CH_4} \times \nu_{air} \times M_{air} = 62.34 \times 9.52 \times 28.97\;\mathrm{g} $$ $$ m_{air} = 17{,}195\;\mathrm{g} = 17.20\;\mathrm{kg} $$ **Final Answer (a):** The stoichiometric air requirement is **9.52 mol air / mol CH₄** or **17.20 kg air per kg of CH₄**. ### Part (b): Flammability Range Verification For methane: LFL = 5%, UFL = 15% (given). **Mass concentration of CH₄ in stoichiometric mixture:** In stoichiometric combustion of CH₄ with air, products are 1 mol CO₂ and 2 mol H₂O. Total moles (fuel + air) = 1 + 9.52 = 10.52. $$ \text{Fuel vol\%} = \frac{1}{10.52} \times 100\% = 9.5\% $$ Since $5\% < 9.5\% < 15\%$, the stoichiometric mixture is **flammable** — a critical condition for flash fire. ### Part (c): Maximum Heat Release $$ \dot{Q}_{max} = n_{CH_4} \times \vert \Delta H_c \vert = 62.34\;\mathrm{mol} \times 802.3\;\mathrm{kJ/mol} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{max} = 50{,}015\;\mathrm{kJ} = 50.02\;\mathrm{MJ} $$ ### Part (d): Radiative Heat Flux at $r = 5\,\mathrm{m}$ Using the point-source radiation model (engineering approximation): $$ q'' = \frac{\dot{Q} \cdot \chi_r}{4\pi r^2} $$ where $\chi_r$ is the radiative fraction. For methane flash fires, $\chi_r \approx 0.2$ (per NFPA empirical data). $$ q'' = \frac{50.02 \times 10^6 \;\mathrm{W} \cdot 0.2}{4\pi \times 5^2} $$ $$ q'' = \frac{10.004 \times 10^6}{314.16} = 31{,}846\;\mathrm{W/m^2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A heat flux of $31.8\,\mathrm{kW/m^2}$ exceeds the threshold for **instantaneous skin ignition** ($\approx 12\,\mathrm{kW/m^2}$). At 5 m, a person would suffer **third-degree burns within 10 seconds** — confirming the lethal nature of unmitigated flash fires. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Flash Fire vs Other Combustion Events | Parameter | Flash Fire | Pool Fire | Vapor Cloud Explosion | Detonation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Flame Speed | $0.4\text{–}0.5\;\mathrm{m/s}$ | Stationary | $100\text{–}500\;\mathrm{m/s}$ | $>1000\;\mathrm{m/s}$ (Mach 3+) | | Overpressure | Negligible ($< 5\;\mathrm{kPa}$) | None | $5\text{–}100\;\mathrm{kPa}$ | $>100\;\mathrm{kPa}$ | | Duration | $< 1$ second | Minutes to hours | Milliseconds | Microseconds | | Confinement | Open (atmospheric) | Open | Confined / partially confined | Confined | | Damage Mode | Thermal radiation | Thermal | Pressure + thermal | Pressure-dominant | | KTU Tag | **Core Topic** | Module 2 | Module 2 / Module 3 | Module 3 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flash Fire Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Source
Leaking Tank] --> B[Liquid Release
Spill or Spray] B --> C[Evaporation
Vaporization] C --> D{Vapor-Air
Concentration} D -->|Below LFL| E[Too Lean
No Ignition] D -->|Within LFL to UFL| F[FLAMMABLE
CLOUD FORMED] D -->|Above UFL| G[Too Rich
No Ignition] F --> H[Ignition Source
Spark / Hot Surface] H --> I[Flash Fire Event
Subsonic Flame Front] I --> J[Thermal Radiation
and Burn Injury] I --> K[Vapor Disperses
Event Terminates] J --> L[Mitigation
Response Activated] K --> L L --> M[System Reset
and Reassessment] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Flash Fire Detection System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DetectionLayer["Layer 1: Detection Sensors"] S1[Flammable Gas
Sensor LEL] S2[IR / UV
Flame Detector] S3[Smoke / Aerosol
Detector] S4[Heat / Temp
Sensor] end subgraph ProcessingLayer["Layer 2: Signal Processing Unit"] P1[Analog to Digital
Converter] P2[Microprocessor
with Threshold Logic] P3[Alarm Decision
Algorithm] end subgraph ResponseLayer["Layer 3: Active Response"] R1[Audible / Visual
Alarm System] R2[Emergency Ventilation
Activation] R3[Fire Suppression
System Trigger] R4[Emergency Shutdown
ESD Valve Closure] end S1 --> P1 S2 --> P1 S3 --> P1 S4 --> P1 P1 --> P2 --> P3 P3 --> R1 P3 --> R2 P3 --> R3 P3 --> R4 ``` ## 4.3 The Combustion Tetrahedron (Mermaid Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD FUEL[Fuel
Hydrocarbon Vapor] --> RXN[Combustion
Reaction] OX[Oxygen
Atmospheric Air] --> RXN HT[Heat
Activation Energy] --> RXN CHN[Chain Reaction
Free Radicals] --> RXN RXN --> PROD[Products
CO2 H2O Heat Light] CHN -.Free Radicals Sustain.-> RXN ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note to the KTU Examiner Pattern:** The four-sided **combustion tetrahedron** (Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Chain Reaction) is the modern replacement for the classical **fire triangle**. Removal of **any one** element extinguishes the flash fire. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define flash fire and distinguish it from a pool fire. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A **flash fire** is a rapid, short-duration combustion of a dispersed flammable vapor-air cloud, propagating as a moving flame front at subsonic velocity without significant overpressure. **Distinction from a Pool Fire:** | Feature | Flash Fire | Pool Fire | |---|---|---| | Fuel State | Dispersed vapor cloud | Liquid pool on surface | | Geometry | 3-dimensional cloud | 2-dimensional surface | | Duration | < 1 second | Minutes to hours | | Flame Location | Translating flame front | Stationary over pool | ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **State Le Chatelier's rule. A fuel mixture contains 60% methane (LFL = 5%) and 40% ethane (LFL = 3%) by volume. Find the LFL of the mixture. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Le Chatelier's Rule (Statement):** The lower flammability limit of a mixture of combustible gases is given by the reciprocal of the sum of the mole-fraction-weighted reciprocals of the individual LFLs. **Calculation:** $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{y_{CH_4}}{LFL_{CH_4}} + \dfrac{y_{C_2H_6}}{LFL_{C_2H_6}}} $$ $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{60}{5} + \dfrac{40}{3}} = \frac{100}{12 + 13.33} = \frac{100}{25.33} $$ $$ LFL_{mix} = 3.95\% \approx 4\% $$ **Final Answer:** LFL of the mixture = **3.95% by volume**. --- ## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** Define the following terms with units: (i) Flash Point, (ii) Fire Point, (iii) Flammability Limits. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A process vessel contains a mixture of 70% propane (LFL = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5%) and 30% n-butane (LFL = 1.8%, UFL = 8.4%) by volume. Calculate: (i) LFL and UFL of the mixture using Le Chatelier's rule. (ii) The equivalence ratio $\phi$ if the actual air supply is 80% of stoichiometric. (iii) Comment on the fire hazard. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** Definitions with units. **(i) Flash Point ($T_f$):** The minimum temperature at which a liquid produces enough vapor to form an **ignitable mixture with air** near its surface. The vapor burns momentarily when an ignition source is applied, but combustion is **not** self-sustaining. **Unit: °C or K**. **(ii) Fire Point ($T_{fire}$):** The minimum temperature at which the liquid produces vapor that supports **continuous combustion** for at least **5 seconds** after removal of the ignition source. **Unit: °C or K**. Generally, $T_{fire} \approx T_f + 5\text{–}10\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. **(iii) Flammability Limits (LFL and UFL):** The **range of fuel concentration in air** within which combustion can propagate. - **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit):** Minimum fuel concentration for combustion. - **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit):** Maximum fuel concentration for combustion. - **Unit:** % by volume. **Part (b):** Calculations. **(i) LFL of mixture:** $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{70}{2.1} + \dfrac{30}{1.8}} = \frac{100}{33.33 + 16.67} = \frac{100}{50.00} = 2.00\% $$ **UFL of mixture:** $$ UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\dfrac{70}{9.5} + \dfrac{30}{8.4}} = \frac{100}{7.37 + 3.57} = \frac{100}{10.94} = 9.14\% $$ **(ii) Equivalence ratio $\phi$:** For propane ($\mathrm{C_3H_8}$), stoichiometric $\nu_{O_2} = 3 + \frac{8}{4} = 5\;\mathrm{mol}$. Air required: $5 \times 4.76 = 23.8\;\mathrm{mol}$. For n-butane ($\mathrm{C_4H_{10}}$), stoichiometric $\nu_{O_2} = 4 + \frac{10}{4} = 6.5\;\mathrm{mol}$. Air required: $6.5 \times 4.76 = 30.94\;\mathrm{mol}$. Weighted stoichiometric air per mol of mixture: $$ (A/F)_{stoich} = 0.7 \times 23.8 + 0.3 \times 30.94 = 16.66 + 9.28 = 25.94\;\mathrm{mol\;air} $$ Actual air supply (80% of stoichiometric): $$ (A/F)_{actual} = 0.80 \times 25.94 = 20.75\;\mathrm{mol\;air} $$ Equivalence ratio: $$ \phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}} = \frac{25.94}{20.75} = 1.25 $$ **(iii) Comment on fire hazard:** Since $\phi = 1.25 > 1$, the mixture is **fuel-rich**. The actual fuel concentration lies between LFL (2.00%) and UFL (9.14%), confirming **high flammability**. The fuel-rich nature makes combustion **incomplete**, producing **excess soot and unburnt hydrocarbons** (toxic CO), and increases the risk of a **flash fire** upon any leak. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]** **(a)** Explain the combustion tetrahedron and the mechanism of a flash fire with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A tank containing **2 kg of acetone** ($\mathrm{C_3H_6O}$, $M = 58.08\;\mathrm{g/mol}$, $\Delta H_c = -1788\;\mathrm{kJ/mol}$, LFL = 2.6%, UFL = 12.8%) develops a leak in a ventilated workshop. (i) Calculate the stoichiometric air requirement in kg. (ii) Determine the adiabatic flame temperature. Assume products have $c_p = 40\;\mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$. (iii) If only 50% of the acetone vaporizes before ignition, what mass of air is in the flammable range? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** Combustion Tetrahedron. The combustion tetrahedron is the modern model of fire, consisting of **four** essential elements: 1. **Fuel** (combustible vapor/liquid) 2. **Oxygen** (atmospheric air) 3. **Heat** (activation energy / ignition source) 4. **Chain Reaction** (sustaining free-radical mechanism) **Mechanism of Flash Fire:** 1. Fuel escapes containment → evaporates 2. Vapor mixes with air → forms a cloud within flammability limits 3. Ignition source provides activation energy 4. Free-radical chain reaction propagates the flame front 5. Flame passes through the cloud, releasing heat and light 6. Cloud is consumed → event terminates (no residual fuel) **Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes. **Part (b):** Calculations. **(i) Stoichiometric air for acetone:** Combustion equation: $\mathrm{C_3H_6O + \nu_{O_2} O_2 \rightarrow 3\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O}$ Oxygen balance: $\nu_{O_2} = 3 + \frac{6}{4} - \frac{1}{2} = 4\;\mathrm{mol\;O_2/mol\;acetone}$. Moles of acetone: $n = \frac{2000\;\mathrm{g}}{58.08\;\mathrm{g/mol}} = 34.44\;\mathrm{mol}$. Moles of air: $\nu_{air} = 4 \times 4.76 = 19.04\;\mathrm{mol\;air/mol\;acetone}$. Mass of air: $m_{air} = 34.44 \times 19.04 \times 28.97\;\mathrm{g} = 19{,}000\;\mathrm{g} = 19.0\;\mathrm{kg}$. **(ii) Adiabatic flame temperature:** Total heat released: $Q = 34.44 \times 1788 = 61{,}580\;\mathrm{kJ}$. Total product moles: $34.44 \times 6 = 206.6\;\mathrm{mol}$. $$ T_{ad} = T_u + \frac{Q}{n \cdot c_p} = 298 + \frac{61{,}580{,}000}{206.6 \times 40} $$ $$ T_{ad} = 298 + \frac{61{,}580{,}000}{8264} = 298 + 7452 \approx 7750\;\mathrm{K} $$ > **Theoretical note:** The very high $T_{ad}$ is due to simplified constant $c_p$ assumption. Real-world $T_{ad}$ for acetone ≈ 2200 K with variable $c_p$. This simplified calculation is acceptable for KTU exam. **(iii) Mass of air in flammable range (50% vaporized):** Vaporized acetone: $n_{vap} = 0.5 \times 34.44 = 17.22\;\mathrm{mol}$. Volume of acetone vapor at STP: $V_{vap} = 17.22 \times 22.4 = 385.7\;\mathrm{L}$. For the mixture to be **flammable**, vapor concentration must lie between 2.6% and 12.8% by volume. Volume of mixture at LFL: $V_{LFL} = \frac{385.7}{0.026} = 14{,}835\;\mathrm{L}$. Volume of mixture at UFL: $V_{UFL} = \frac{385.7}{0.128} = 3{,}013\;\mathrm{L}$. Volume of air in flammable range (LFL to UFL): $V_{air,LFL} = 14{,}835 - 385.7 = 14{,}449\;\mathrm{L}$. $V_{air,UFL} = 3{,}013 - 385.7 = 2{,}628\;\mathrm{L}$. Mass of air: $m_{air} = V_{air} \times \rho_{air} = V_{air} \times 1.225\;\mathrm{kg/m^3}$. $m_{air,LFL} = 14{,}449 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.225 = 17.7\;\mathrm{kg}$. $m_{air,UFL} = 2{,}628 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.225 = 3.22\;\mathrm{kg}$. **Final Answer:** For the cloud to be flammable, the air mass must be between **3.22 kg and 17.7 kg** (assuming 50% vaporization). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Loss Areas):** > 1. **Units Confusion:** Always specify LFL/UFL in **% by volume**, not by mass. The KTU examiner deducts 0.5 marks for missing unit annotation. > 2. **Equivalence Ratio Inversion:** Some students write $\phi = (A/F)_{actual} / (A/F)_{stoich}$. The **correct** definition is $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} / (F/A)_{stoich} = (A/F)_{stoich} / (A/F)_{actual}$. > 3. **Confusing Flash Point and Fire Point:** A liquid at flash point gives a **momentary flame**; at fire point, combustion is **sustained**. Examiners frequently test this. > 4. **Le Chatelier's Mistake:** The formula has a **100 in the numerator** (it converts to percentage). Forgetting this leads to incorrect units. > 5. **Missing Adiabatic Flame Temperature Correction:** If the question gives a real $c_p$ vs. temperature, you must integrate. KTU sometimes uses a constant $c_p$ simplification — read the question carefully. --- ## Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Revision (60-Second Scan):** - [x] A **flash fire** is a short-duration, subsonic combustion of a flammable vapor-air cloud. - [x] It is **NOT** a detonation (no shock wave) and **NOT** a pool fire (no liquid surface). - [x] **Flash point** → momentary burn; **Fire point** → sustained burn. - [x] Combustion **triangle** has 3 elements; **tetrahedron** adds **chain reaction** (free radicals). - [x] **Flammability envelope** lies between LFL and UFL; outside = no propagation. - [x] **Le Chatelier's Rule:** $LFL_{mix} = 100 / \Sigma (y_i / LFL_i)$. - [x] **Equivalence ratio** $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} / (F/A)_{stoich}$. $\phi < 1$ = lean, $\phi > 1$ = rich. - [x] **Stoichiometric air** for $\mathrm{C_xH_yO_z}$: $\nu_{air} = 4.76(x + y/4 - z/2)$. - [x] **Adiabatic flame temperature** for hydrocarbons ≈ 2000–2300 K (real value). - [x] **Laminar burning velocity** $S_L \approx 0.4\text{–}0.5\;\mathrm{m/s}$ for hydrocarbons in air. - [x] **Arrhenius rate law** $k = A e^{-E_a/(RT)}$ governs reaction initiation. - [x] **Radiative heat flux** (point source): $q'' = \chi_r \dot{Q} / (4\pi r^2)$. - [x] **NFPA standards** governing flash fire: NFPA 30 (Flammable Liquids), NFPA 68 (Deflagration). - [x] **Lethal heat flux** for skin: $12.5\;\mathrm{kW/m^2}$ (3rd-degree burns in 10 s). - [x] Key mitigation: **inerting**, **ventilation**, **gas detection**, **ESD systems**, **vapor recovery**. - [x] Four layers of detection system: **Sensors → Processing → Decision → Response**.

topics.title: Pool Fire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pool Fire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Pool Fires

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pool Fire (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *pool fire* is a diffusion flame established above a horizontal pool of liquid fuel (or solid/liquid fuel with a melting point below its decomposition temperature) where combustion is sustained by the vaporization of the fuel from the liquid surface into the surrounding oxidizer stream. The flame is *fuel-controlled* (liquid-phase controlled) at steady state, meaning the rate of vaporization dictates the rate of combustion.

Mathematically, the global equivalence ratio of a pool fire is always **fuel-rich** inside the luminous flame zone, because the liquid surface provides fuel vapor at a rate determined primarily by **conductive and radiative heat feedback** from the flame itself to the fuel surface (a phenomenon called *re-radiation* or *re-radiative feedback*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pool fires are central to KTU Fire Engineering:** Approximately **65%–70%** of all industrial hydrocarbon fires in process plants (refineries, petrochemical storage yards, transformer bunds) are classified as pool fires. As a result, KTU 2024 OECHT835 devotes a major portion of Module 1 to pool fire analysis — covering mass burning rate, flame geometry, radiative heat flux, and smoke yield.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Campfire-Coffee Model

Imagine you are boiling water in a small **open vessel** on a gas stove. The water surface is your "fuel pool." The bubbles of steam rising are analogous to the fuel vapor (though steam is non-combustible, the **physical mechanism of mass transfer from a free liquid surface is identical**). The rate at which steam leaves the pot is governed by how much heat the burner transfers *back* to the water.

In a pool fire:
- The **flame** is the "burner" — but instead of an external energy source, it gets its energy from its own combustion products.
- Heat is convected and radiated from the flame down to the liquid fuel surface.
- The fuel re-vaporizes and rises as a buoyant jet of vapor.
- This vapor mixes with entrained air and burns.
- A *self-sustaining loop* is established.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Beginners:** A pool fire is fundamentally a **self-feedback system**. The flame's own heat vaporizes more fuel, and that fuel sustains the flame. Break the loop (e.g., drain the pool) and the fire extinguishes.

## 1.3 Classification of Pool Fires

| Pool Fire Type | Description | Typical KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Spill Pool Fire** | Fuel released on open ground/grade; pool spreads until reaching a barrier or equilibrium thickness | Oil tanker rollover accident |
| **Bund Pool Fire** | Fuel contained within a bund (dike) wall; rectangular geometry | Refinery tank dike fire |
| **Circular Pool Fire** | Idealized round pool used in lab correlations and engineering models | Circular pan burner tests |
| **Sub-surface Pool Fire** | Burning above a fuel that vaporizes through a porous medium (e.g., LNG on water, soil-soaked fuel) | LNG spill on water |
| **Cryogenic Pool Fire** | Fuel boils at cryogenic temperatures (e.g., LNG, LPG) | LNG terminal incident |

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities in a Pool Fire

- **Mass Burning Rate (ṁ):** Mass of fuel consumed per unit time (kg/s).
- **Mass Flux / Burning Rate per Unit Area (ṁ″):** Mass consumed per unit pool area per unit time (kg/m²·s).
- **Heat Release Rate (Q̇ or HRR):** Total chemical energy released per unit time (kW or MW).
- **Flame Height (L_f):** Visible height of the luminous flame (m).
- **Radiative Fraction (χ_r):** Fraction of total HRR emitted as thermal radiation (typically 0.20–0.45 for hydrocarbon pool fires).
- **Soot Yield (y_s):** Mass of soot produced per mass of fuel burned (kg/kg).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** A typical **1 m diameter gasoline pool fire** releases approximately **2.5 MW** of total heat, with a burning rate of about **0.04 kg/m²·s** and a flame height of roughly **2.0 m** under still-air conditions. (These values are *order-of-magnitude* for KTU problems; exact values depend on fuel type, diameter, and wind.)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of a Pool Fire — Step-by-Step Logic

The mass burning rate of a pool fire is governed by a **quasi-steady heat balance** on the fuel surface. Following the classical analysis (Burgess, Hertzberg, and later summarized by Drysdale):

### Step 1: Energy Balance on the Liquid Pool Surface
At steady state, the heat required to vaporize the fuel equals the heat arriving at the pool surface from the flame:

$$q_{rad}'' + q_{conv}'' = \dot{m}'' \cdot \left[ L_v + c_{p,l} (T_{boil} - T_{amb}) \right]$$

Where:
- $q_{rad}''$ = Radiative heat flux from flame to surface (kW/m²)
- $q_{conv}''$ = Convective heat flux from flame to surface (kW/m²)
- $\dot{m}''$ = Mass burning rate per unit area (kg/m²·s)
- $L_v$ = Latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg)
- $c_{p,l}$ = Specific heat of liquid (kJ/kg·K)
- $T_{boil}$ = Boiling point of fuel (K)
- $T_{amb}$ = Ambient temperature (K)

### Step 2: Recognition of the Limiting Behavior
For **small pools**, edge effects dominate — heat losses to the pan/walls are large, and the burning rate is reduced. For **large pools**, the central region behaves as an infinite pool, and burning rate saturates. This leads to the **diameter-dependent burning rate** correlation.

### Step 3: The Classical Burning Rate Correlation (Hottel / Blinov–Khudiakov)

$$\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left( 1 - e^{-k \beta D} \right)$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ = Asymptotic (infinite pool) mass flux (kg/m²·s) — typically **0.035–0.08 kg/m²·s** for liquid hydrocarbons
- $k\beta$ = Optical extinction coefficient × mean beam length correction (m⁻¹) — typically **1.1–2.3 m⁻¹** depending on fuel
- $D$ = Pool diameter (m)

### Step 4: Total Mass Burning Rate

$$\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p = \dot{m}'' \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 5: Heat Release Rate

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_p \cdot \Delta H_c$$

Where $\Delta H_c$ is the **lower (or higher) heat of combustion** in kJ/kg.

### Step 6: Flame Height Correlations
Two correlations are universally required for KTU problems:

**Thomas Correlation (most common in KTU problems):**

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_{\infty} \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$$

**Heskestad Correlation (alternative, for large fires):**

$$L_f = 0.235 \, \dot{Q}^{2/5} - 1.02 \, D$$

### Step 7: Radiative Heat Flux to a Remote Target

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot \frac{\cos\theta}{4\pi r^2}$$

For a *point source model*. For a more accurate *solid flame model*:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}_{rad} \cdot F_{12} \cdot \tau$$

Where $F_{12}$ is the **view factor** and $\tau$ is the **atmospheric transmissivity** (often computed via the Lilley or Wayne models).

### Step 8: Froude Number and the Wind Effect

$$Fr = \frac{u_w^2}{gD}$$

Where $u_w$ is the wind speed at flame height. For $Fr < 0.1$, wind effects are negligible; for $Fr > 1$, flame tilt becomes severe and the fire becomes elongated.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU 2024 Pool Fire problems. Memorize all of these — they appear in nearly every board exam paper.

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ | $\dot{m}''$ in kg/m²·s; $D$ in m | Burning rate vs. pool diameter |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s | Total fuel consumption |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_c$ | $\dot{Q}$ in kW; $\Delta H_c$ in kJ/kg | Total Heat Release Rate |
| 4 | $L_f/D = 42(\dot{m}''/\rho_{\infty}\sqrt{gD})^{0.61}$ | $L_f, D$ in m; $\rho_{\infty}$ in kg/m³ | Thomas flame height |
| 5 | $L_f = 0.235 \dot{Q}^{2/5} - 1.02 D$ | $L_f, D$ in m; $\dot{Q}$ in kW | Heskestad flame height (large fires) |
| 6 | $Fr = u_w^2 / (gD)$ | $u_w$ in m/s; $D$ in m; $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Wind effect / flame tilt |
| 7 | $\dot{q}''_r = \chi_r \dot{Q} \cos\theta / (4\pi r^2)$ | $\dot{q}''_r$ in kW/m²; $r$ in m | Point-source radiation |
| 8 | $\tau = \exp(-\kappa r)$ or via Lilley/Wayne | $\kappa$ = extinction coeff. (1/m) | Atmospheric transmissivity |
| 9 | $y_{smoke} = y_s \cdot \dot{m} \cdot (1 + \text{air ratio})$ | $y_s$ in kg/kg | Smoke production rate |
| 10 | $Re_f = \dot{m}'' D / \mu_{\infty}$ | dimensionless | Flame Reynolds number |

## 2.3 Physical Insight — The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why is $\dot{m}''$ diameter-dependent?** Because the flame's radiative feedback to the surface is finite. The flame cannot re-radiate back to the entire pool equally — small pools lose heat to edges, so the effective $\dot{m}''$ is lower.
- **Why does flame height first rise then fall with diameter?** At small $D$, buoyancy (large $\dot{m}''/D$) drives the flame high. At large $D$, the increased diameter spreads combustion laterally, lowering the aspect ratio $L_f/D$.
- **Why does wind tilt the flame?** Cross-wind momentum competes with buoyant vertical rise. The Froude number captures this balance.
- **Why is the radiative fraction ~ 0.3?** Soot particles in the luminous flame zone are the primary radiators. More sooty fuels (e.g., diesel, kerosene) radiate more, but also produce darker smoke.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Process Plant Safety** | Determine thermal radiation on adjacent equipment (e.g., API 521 / API 752) |
| **Fire Protection Engineering** | Size water spray and foam systems (NFPA 11) |
| **Structural Fire Engineering** | Compute thermal exposure on steel/tank shells |
| **Building Fire Safety** | Design compartment fire scenarios for egress analysis |
| **Forensic Fire Investigation** | Reconstruct fire size from observed flame height and burn patterns |
| **Offshore / Marine Safety** | Evaluate helideck and process module exposure |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Blinov–Khudiakov (Hottel) Mass Burning Rate Correlation

**Starting Premise:** Consider a circular pool of diameter $D$ burning steadily. We postulate that the heat transferred from the flame to the pool surface depends on the optical thickness of the flame gases. The governing equation is:

$$\dot{q}_{surface}'' = \dot{m}'' \cdot h_{eff}$$

Where $h_{eff} = L_v + c_{p,l}(T_{boil} - T_{amb})$ is the effective heat of gasification (J/kg). For a *luminous*, *optically thick* flame, the radiative contribution to $\dot{q}_{surface}''$ follows Beer's law of attenuation through a flame of path length $\beta D$:

$$\dot{q}_{rad,surface}'' = \dot{q}_{rad,flame}'' \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-k\beta D} \right)$$

Combining convective (assumed constant at $C$) and radiative contributions:

$$\dot{m}'' \cdot h_{eff} = C + \dot{q}_{rad,flame}'' \left( 1 - e^{-k\beta D} \right)$$

For the limiting case $D \to \infty$ (infinite pool), the term $e^{-k\beta D} \to 0$:

$$\dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot h_{eff} = C + \dot{q}_{rad,flame}''$$

Therefore $C = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot h_{eff} - \dot{q}_{rad,flame}''$. Substituting back and noting that $\dot{q}_{rad,flame}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \cdot h_{eff}$ (i.e., the limiting feedback equals the total gasification enthalpy), we obtain the **Blinov–Khudiakov equation**:

$$\boxed{\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_{\infty} \left( 1 - e^{-k\beta D} \right)}$$

**Interpretation:**
- As $D \to 0$: $\dot{m}'' \to 0$ (small pool, dominated by edge losses).
- As $D \to \infty$: $\dot{m}'' \to \dot{m}''_{\infty}$ (saturated, infinite pool).
- The transition occurs around $D \approx 2/(k\beta)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thomas Flame Height Correlation (from Dimensional Analysis)

**Step 1 — List governing variables:**
The flame height $L_f$ of a pool fire depends on:
- $D$ = pool diameter (m)
- $\dot{m}''$ = mass burning flux (kg/m²·s)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²)
- $\rho_{\infty}$ = ambient air density (kg/m³)
- $\Delta T$ / $T_{\infty}$ = temperature ratio (dimensionless, often absorbed into the Froude number)

**Step 2 — Form dimensionless groups (Buckingham π):**
Choose repeating variables $D$, $\dot{m}''$, $\rho_{\infty}$, $g$. The dimension of $L_f$ is [L]. The relevant non-dimensional group is:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_f}{D}, \quad \Pi_2 = \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_{\infty} \sqrt{gD}}$$

**Step 3 — Functional form:**

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = f\left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_{\infty} \sqrt{gD}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Empirical fit (Thomas, 1963) from experiments on circular pool fires:**

$$\frac{L_f}{D} = 42 \left( \frac{\dot{m}''}{\rho_{\infty} \sqrt{gD}} \right)^{0.61}$$

**Step 5 — Validity Range:**
- Valid for $0.3 \text{ m} \le D \le 3 \text{ m}$ in still air.
- For larger fires, use Heskestad.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Release Rate to a Remote Target (Point-Source Approximation)

Consider a pool fire treated as a **point source** of total radiative power $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_r \cdot \dot{Q}$ located at the geometric center of the flame. The radiative flux to a target located at distance $r$ from the source, oriented at angle $\theta$ from the flame's vertical axis, is obtained from the inverse-square law for radiation:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4\pi r^2} \cos\theta$$

(assuming a Lambertian surface and a small solid angle; this is the standard engineering approximation for KTU problems).

**For a horizontal target on the ground (so $\theta = 90°$ from vertical source):**
$\cos\theta = h/r$ where $h$ is the elevation of the flame center above the target. Thus:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q} \cdot h}{4\pi r^3} = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q} \cdot h}{4\pi (x^2 + h^2)^{3/2}}$$

where $x$ is the horizontal distance from the pool center to the target.

**With atmospheric attenuation** (Lilley's formula for water vapor + CO₂ extinction):

$$\tau = 1.013 - 0.0046 \cdot (CO_2\%) - 0.0225 \cdot (H_2O\%) \quad \text{(Wayne, for short paths)}$$

For longer paths, $\tau = e^{-\kappa r}$ with $\kappa$ from the Lilley model. The final flux is:

$$\dot{q}_r'' = \frac{\chi_r \dot{Q} \cdot h}{4\pi r^3} \cdot \tau$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A circular pool of gasoline with diameter $D = 3$ m burns in still air. Given:
- $\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 0.055$ kg/m²·s
- $k\beta = 1.1$ m⁻¹
- $\Delta H_c = 43.7$ MJ/kg
- $\chi_r = 0.30$
- $\rho_{\infty} = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Calculate:** (a) Mass burning rate ṁ, (b) Heat release rate Q̇, (c) Flame height L_f (Thomas), (d) Radiation flux at 10 m horizontal distance, target at ground level, flame center 1.5 m high.

**Step (a):** Mass burning flux:
$\dot{m}'' = 0.055 \cdot (1 - e^{-1.1 \times 3}) = 0.055 \cdot (1 - e^{-3.3}) = 0.055 \cdot (1 - 0.0369) = 0.055 \cdot 0.9631$
$\dot{m}'' = 0.05297$ kg/m²·s

Pool area: $A_p = \pi (3)^2/4 = 7.069$ m²

$\dot{m} = 0.05297 \times 7.069 = 0.3745$ kg/s

**Step (b):** Heat release rate:
$\dot{Q} = 0.3745 \times 43700 = 16365$ kW ≈ **16.37 MW**

**Step (c):** Flame height (Thomas):
$\dot{m}''/(\rho_{\infty}\sqrt{gD}) = 0.05297/(1.2 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 3}) = 0.05297/(1.2 \times 5.424) = 0.05297/6.509 = 0.00814$

$L_f/D = 42 \times (0.00814)^{0.61}$

Compute $(0.00814)^{0.61}$: take $\ln(0.00814) = -4.811$, multiply by 0.61: $-2.935$, exponentiate: $e^{-2.935} = 0.0531$

$L_f/D = 42 \times 0.0531 = 2.230$

$L_f = 2.230 \times 3 =$ **6.69 m**

**Step (d):** Radiation flux:
$r = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{102.25} = 10.11$ m

$\dot{q}_r'' = (\chi_r \dot{Q} \cdot h)/(4\pi r^3) = (0.30 \times 16365 \times 1.5)/(4\pi \times 10.11^3)$
$= 7364.25/(4\pi \times 1033.6) = 7364.25/12988.4$
$= 0.567$ kW/m² = **567 W/m²**

This is sufficient to cause **second-degree skin burns in ~10 seconds** and to damage unprotected structural steel over a short exposure. (Recall: 1 kW/m² for 30 s ⇒ pain; 5 kW/m² ⇒ wood ignites spontaneously; 25 kW/m² ⇒ steel temperature rises rapidly.)

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** Always end your solutions with a *physical interpretation* sentence — examiners award marks for it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pool Fire

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Fuel Pool at T_amb] --> B[Heat Feedback from Flame]
    B --> C[Convective Heat Transfer]
    B --> D[Radiative Heat Transfer]
    C --> E[Fuel Vaporization at Surface]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Buoyant Fuel Vapor Plume]
    F --> G[Entrainment of Ambient Air]
    G --> H[Mixing Zone in Flame]
    H --> I[Combustion Reaction Zone]
    I --> J[Hot Combustion Products Plume]
    J --> K[Convective Heat Release to Atmosphere]
    J --> L[Radiative Heat Release to Surroundings]
    L --> M[Remote Target Receives q_r]
    K --> N[Smoke Plume Rise]
    N --> O[Soot and Particulate Dispersal]
    B -.->|Self-Sustaining Loop| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrow at the bottom represents the **self-sustaining feedback loop** that makes a pool fire a self-driven, fuel-controlled phenomenon. The flame's own heat vaporizes the fuel; that fuel sustains the flame. This is the central conceptual point of Module 1.

## 4.2 Flow of Mass and Energy in a Pool Fire System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1[Fuel Reservoir]
        P1[Liquid Pool]
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Energy Transfer]
        P2[Flame Radiative + Convective Feedback]
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Combustion]
        P3[Diffusion Flame in Mixing Zone]
    end
    subgraph Phase4[Outputs]
        P4[Sensible Heat to Air]
        P5[Radiative Heat to Targets]
        P6[Soot and Smoke]
        P7[Combustion Products CO2, H2O]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P3 --> P6
    P3 --> P7
    P2 -.->|Loop| P1
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Key Variables and Their Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Pool Diameter D] --> mflux[Mass Flux m_dot_double_prime]
    kbeta[k times beta Optical Coeff] --> mflux
    minf[m_dot_infty Asymptotic Flux] --> mflux
    mflux --> Q[Heat Release Rate Q_dot]
    Area[Pool Area A_p] --> Q
    DHC[Heat of Combustion Delta_H_c] --> Q
    Q --> Lf[Flame Height L_f]
    D --> Lf
    Q --> ChiR[Radiative Fraction chi_r]
    ChiR --> Qrad[Radiative Power]
    Qrad --> qr[Radiative Flux to Target]
    Dist[r distance] --> qr
    ViewF[View Factor F_12] --> qr
    Trans[Atmospheric Transmissivity tau] --> qr
    Wind[u_w wind speed] --> Froude[Froude Number]
    D --> Froude
    Froude --> Tilt[Flame Tilt Angle]
    Tilt --> Lf
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Flame Height Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Pool Fire Problem] --> A{Pool Diameter}
    A -->|D less than 3 m| B[Use Thomas Correlation]
    A -->|D greater than 3 m| C[Use Heskestad Correlation]
    A -->|Strong Wind, u_w greater than 2 m/s| D[Apply Wind Tilt Correction]
    B --> E{L_f result vs 1.5D}
    E -->|L_f greater than 1.5D| F[Flame elongated, use care]
    E -->|L_f less than 1.5D| G[Flame compact, typical]
    C --> H{Compare with API 521 limit}
    H -->|Within 10 percent| I[Use result]
    H -->|Outside bound| J[Re-evaluate parameters]
    D --> K[Compute Tilt angle theta_t]
    K --> L[Adjust flame height by cos theta_t]
```
