# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> **Define a fire detector. List the four classical signatures of a fire and name the corresponding detector family for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fire detector is a device that senses one or more physical/chemical phenomena produced by a fire and converts them into an electrical signal to trigger the alarm. The four signatures and families are:
1. **Smoke / aerosol** → Ionization, Photoelectric, Beam, Aspirating (VESDA) detectors
2. **Heat** → Fixed-temperature, Rate-of-rise, Rate-compensated, Linear heat cable detectors
3. **Flame / EM radiation** → UV, IR, UV/IR, IR3, Video image detectors
4. **Combustion gases (CO, CO₂)** → Electrochemical, Semiconductor, NDIR, Catalytic-bead detectors

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Differentiate between ionization and photoelectric smoke detectors. State one best-suited fire type for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Ionization Detector | Photoelectric Detector |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing principle | Reduced ionization current by smoke aerosols (using Am-241) | Light scattering by smoke particles onto a photodiode |
| Optimal particle size | 0.01–0.3 µm (small, invisible) | 0.3–10 µm (larger, visible) |
| Best for | **Fast-flaming fires** (paper, wood, gasoline) | **Smoldering fires** (PVC, upholstery, electrical) |
| Typical alarm threshold | ~1.5 % obscuration/m | ~2–4 % obscuration/m |

---

## 📕 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**
> **a)** Explain the working principle of an **ionization chamber smoke detector** with a neat labelled sketch. Mention the role of Americium-241, the ionization current behaviour, and the alarm threshold.
> 
> **b)** A smooth-ceiling warehouse is 25 m × 16 m with a ceiling height of 5 m. As a fire engineer, determine **(i)** the maximum detector spacing using NFPA 72 rules, **(ii)** the number of point heat detectors required, and **(iii)** the placement of detectors relative to walls.

#### ✅ Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle of Ionization Chamber Smoke Detector (7 marks):**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** The detector has two chambers — a measuring chamber (open to ambient air) and a reference chamber (sealed). Both contain a tiny amount of **Americium-241 (³²⁴₁ Am)**, an α-emitter of activity ≈ **0.9 µCi (33 kBq)**. The two chambers are connected electrically in series across a small DC supply.

2. **Normal operation (2 marks):** α-particles emitted by Am-241 ionize the air molecules in the chambers, producing positive ions and free electrons. These charged particles move toward oppositely-charged electrodes, creating a **steady ionization current (typically 1–10 pA)** through the chambers.

3. **Smoke entry and alarm (2 marks):** When smoke particles (0.01–0.3 µm) enter the open measuring chamber, they **attach to the ions**, making them heavier and less mobile. This **reduces the ionization current**. The control circuit compares the current with a reference value. When the drop exceeds a pre-set threshold (≈ 1.5 % obscuration/m), the comparator triggers the alarm signal.

4. **Safety (1 mark):** The Am-241 quantity is so small (kBq range) that it is harmless externally. The detector is also **radiation-tested** to comply with **AERB (India)** and **NRC (USA)** safety norms.

#### ✅ Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** Smooth ceiling, $L = 25$ m, $W = 16$ m, $H = 5$ m.

**(i) Maximum spacing (2 marks):**
Since $H = 5$ m < 10 ft ≈ 3.05 m… but we are using SI. NFPA 72 metric equivalent uses:
$$ S = 0.7 H = 0.7 \times 5 = 3.5 \text{ m} $$

**(ii) Number of detectors (3 marks):**
Along 25 m: $N_L = \lceil 25 / 3.5 \rceil = 8$ (with 8 internal + 2 wall half-spacings → 9 columns effective).
Along 16 m: $N_W = \lceil 16 / 3.5 \rceil = 5$ (with 5 + 2 → 6 rows effective).
Total detectors: $N = 8 \times 5 = 40$ detectors (excluding wall half-spacing rows).
Including wall-aligned detectors: $9 \times 6 = 54$ detectors (as derived in Section 3.5).

**(iii) Wall placement (2 marks):**
First row of detectors must be placed at a distance of $0.5 S = 1.75$ m from any wall. The grid origin is shifted so that detectors are symmetrically placed 1.75 m from both end-walls.

---

### **Question B — [Internal Choice – Alternative, 14 Marks]**
> **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of an aspirating smoke detection (ASD / VESDA / HSSD) system**. State its typical applications and sensitivity range.
> 
> **b)** Discuss the **classification of heat detectors** with sketches, and explain the working of a **rate-of-rise (ROR) heat detector** using the **pneumatic diaphragm principle**. Mention the typical activation threshold.

#### ✅ Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Working of Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD / VESDA / HSSD) (7 marks):**

1. **Block diagram & principle (3 marks):** A high-suction pump draws air continuously through a network of **PVC or ABS sampling pipes** fitted with multiple **sampling holes (capillary inlets)**. The sampled air is filtered, then passed through a **laser-based optical chamber** (or nephelometer) where a diode laser and photodiode detect light scattered by even sub-micron aerosols. A return pipe exhausts the air to the protected space. The control unit processes the signal and triggers alarms at four progressively escalating levels: **Alert → Action → Fire-1 → Fire-2**.

2. **Sensitivity (2 marks):** Sensitivity of HSSD systems ranges from **0.001 % obs/m to 0.1 % obs/m**, i.e., **1000× to 10000× more sensitive** than a standard point detector (which is ~5 % obs/m). This is the principle of *Very Early Warning* — fires are detected when only a tiny fraction of the material has begun pyrolysing.

3. **Applications (2 marks):** Used in **data centres, semiconductor cleanrooms, telecom exchanges, museums, archives, cold stores (frozen food warehouses), heritage buildings, and hospital MRI rooms**. Typical pipe length 50–200 m, sampling holes 10–80 per pipe.

#### ✅ Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Classification of Heat Detectors (3 marks):**
1. **Fixed-Temperature** — bimetallic, eutectic alloy, glass-bulb
2. **Rate-of-Rise (ROR)** — pneumatic-diaphragm, thermistor bridge
3. **Rate-Compensated** — combined ROR + fixed
4. **Linear Heat Cable (LHDC)** — digital or analogue, fibre-optic
5. **Continuously-acting line type** — thermocouple/thermistor cable

**Working of Pneumatic ROR Detector (4 marks):**
The detector consists of a **sealed air chamber** fitted with a flexible **diaphragm** and a calibrated **venting orifice**. The chamber is connected to the ambient through a small hole. In normal conditions, air enters and exits the chamber slowly, keeping the diaphragm in a neutral position. When a fire suddenly heats the air, the air inside the chamber **expands faster than it can vent out**, increasing the internal pressure. This pressure pushes the diaphragm, which in turn **closes an electrical contact** and triggers the alarm. The **activation threshold is ≈ 8.3 °C/min (15 °F/min) rise in temperature**. Because it responds to the *rate* of rise, it is **faster than a fixed-temperature detector** for rapidly-growing fires, but **insensitive to slow smoldering** scenarios — which is why both principles are often combined.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in This Topic**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing detection with suppression** — a fire detector does *not* extinguish a fire. Don't write "the detector sprays water." (–2 marks)
> 2. ❌ **Wrong units in ceiling-jet formula** — $Q$ must be in **kW** and $H$ in **metres** for the constant 5.38 to be valid. Using $Q$ in watts or $H$ in feet gives a wrong answer. (–2 marks)
> 3. ❌ **Forgetting wall half-spacing rule** — first detector is at $0.5 S$ from the wall, not $S$. (–1 mark)
> 4. ❌ **Calling Americium "dangerous"** — the quantity is so small (microcurie) that it is harmless. The examiner expects you to mention **AERB compliance**. (–1 mark)
> 5. ❌ **Mixing up photoelectric and ionization** — saying photoelectric detects "tiny invisible aerosols" is incorrect; it detects *larger* scattering particles. (–2 marks)
> 6. ❌ **Not drawing a labelled sketch** in part (a) questions — KTU examiners *expect* a diagram for full 7 marks. (–1 to 2 marks)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔥 **Fire detection** is the sensing of *smoke, heat, flame, or gas* — followed by alarm announcement and emergency control.
- ⏰ **Time-temperature curve** of a real fire is exponential; automatic detection buys the critical *first 1–4 minutes* when evacuation is still safe.
- 🏷️ **Detector families:** Heat | Smoke (point, beam, ASD) | Flame (UV, IR, IR3, VIFD) | Gas (CO, multi-gas) | Multi-criteria.
- 📐 **NFPA 72 spacing:** $S = 0.7 H$ for $H \le 10$ ft; $S = 0.4(H-10)+7$ for $H > 10$ ft; **$S_{max} \le 30$ ft**; wall rule: $0.5 S$.
- 🌡️ **Ceiling jet temperature:** $\Delta T_{cj} = 5.38 Q^{2/3} / H^{5/3}$ — **THE** design equation.
- ⚛️ **Ionization** uses Am-241 (≈ 0.9 µCi); best for **flaming** fires.
- 💡 **Photoelectric** uses LED + photodiode (Mie scattering); best for **smoldering** fires.
- 🌀 **VESDA / HSSD** has sensitivity **0.001 % obs/m** (1000× point detector) — used in **data centres**.
- 🔥 **Flame detectors** use solar-blind **UV 185–260 nm** or flicker-discriminating **IR3 (4.3, 4.45, 4.6 µm)**.
- 💨 **ROR threshold = 8.3 °C/min (15 °F/min)**.
- 🧮 **RTI** (Heskestad): $\tau = \text{RTI}/\sqrt{u}$ — lower RTI = faster detector.
- ⚖️ **Code references:** NFPA 72, IS 2189, IS 11360, IS 14604, NBC 2016 (Part 4 – Fire & Life Safety), BS 5839.
- 🛠️ **Standards body:** BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards), AERB (for radioactive source in ionization detectors).
- 🎯 **KTU mantra:** "**Multi-signature detection for multi-hazard buildings**" — never rely on a single detector type for life safety.
- 🧠 **Memory trick — "SHRP":** **S**moke (nasal) → **H**eat (skin) → **R**adiation-flame (eyes) → **P**oison-gas (tongue).
- 🚫 **What detectors DO NOT do:** extinguish, suppress, contain, or rescue — they only **announce and trigger** other systems.
- ✅ **What detectors DO:** reduce fatality risk by **40–60 %** (WHO), enable pre-flashover evacuation, satisfy **NBC + IS codes**, reduce insurance premium, integrate with BMS / SCADA / IoT smart-building platforms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Detectors, Smoke Detectors, Flame Detectors, Flammable Gas Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Detectors, Smoke Detectors, Flame Detectors, Flammable Gas Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Automatic Fire Detectors?

An **automatic fire detector** is a sensor-based device that senses one or more characteristic signatures of a developing fire (heat, smoke, flame, or gas) and converts the same into an audible/visual alarm signal, typically through a Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** Fire detectors are classified by the **fire signature** they respond to — namely, **thermal (heat), aerosol (smoke), electromagnetic (flame/light), and chemical (combustion gas)** signatures. Each detector type has a distinct operating principle, sensitivity profile, and application suitability.

---

## 1.2 The Four Detector Families at a Glance

| Detector Type | What It Senses | Typical Response Time | Best Suited For |
|---------------|----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Heat Detector** | Rise in temperature / fixed temperature | Slow (seconds–minutes) | Garages, kitchens, dusty areas |
| **Smoke Detector** | Airborne particulates (aerosols) | Fast (seconds) | Offices, bedrooms, corridors |
| **Flame Detector** | Optical radiation (UV/IR) | Very fast (milliseconds) | Petrochemical, hangars, paint shops |
| **Gas Detector** | Combustion gases (CO, H₂, CH₄, LPG) | Medium–Fast | Battery rooms, gas plants, refineries |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fire Detective Squad"

Imagine a building is a city, and fire is a sneaky criminal. The building employs four specialized detectives:

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> - **Heat Detector** → The **thermometer detective**. He only shouts "Fire!" when the room gets *really* hot. He's slow but reliable and never gets fooled by a cigarette.
> - **Smoke Detector** → The **nose detective**. He sniffs the air for the faintest whiff of smoke. Highly sensitive, but can be tricked by burnt toast or steam.
> - **Flame Detector** → The **eye detective**. He stares 24/7 looking for the unmistakable flicker of flames. Lightning-fast but needs a clear line of sight.
> - **Gas Detector** → The **chemist detective**. He runs chemical tests on the air looking for invisible toxic gases like CO before fire even breaks out — a true early-warning specialist.

---

## 1.4 Why Four Detectors and Not One?

No single detector works for **all fire scenarios**. The classic ISO 7240 / NFPA 72 matrix considers four fire types:

$$T_{test} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$$

Where:
- **T1** = Smouldering cellulosic fire (e.g., wood) → *smoke detector wins*
- **T2** = Flammable liquid fire (e.g., heptane) → *flame/heat detector wins*
- **T3** = Flammable gas fire (e.g., propane) → *gas/flame detector wins*
- **T4** = Deep-seated polymer fire → *heat detector wins*

This is why **multi-criteria detectors** combining smoke + heat + CO have become popular in modern KTU-aligned designs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Detectors — Operational Principles

Heat detectors are the **oldest and most rugged** type of fire detector. They convert thermal energy from a fire into a measurable signal.

### 2.1.1 Fixed-Temperature Heat Detector (FT)
- Operates on the principle of **phase change** or **thermal expansion**.
- **Eutectic solder type:** A low-melting alloy (e.g., Wood's metal, mp ≈ **70 °C**) holds a spring-loaded plunger. When the alloy melts, the plunger releases and triggers the alarm.
- **Glass-bulb (frangible bulb) type:** A liquid-filled glass bulb expands with heat; above its rated temperature the bulb shatters, releasing a switch contact.

The fundamental thermal response equation:

$$T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)\, e^{-t/\tau}$$

Where:
- $T(t)$ = instantaneous detector temperature (°C)
- $T_\infty$ = ambient/gas temperature (°C)
- $T_0$ = initial detector temperature (°C)
- $\tau$ = thermal time constant (s)

### 2.1.2 Rate-of-Rise (ROR) Heat Detector
- Triggers when the **rate of temperature rise** exceeds a threshold (typically **8.3 °C/min** as per EN 54-5).
- Uses a differential air chamber: a vented diaphragm compares expansion of an inner air pocket with the surrounding air.

Mathematically:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} \geq \left(\frac{dT}{dt}\right)_{threshold} = 8.3\ ^\circ C/min$$

### 2.1.3 Rate Compensated Detector
- Combines FT and ROR logic. Uses two metallic strips with different expansion coefficients → triggers only when both *rate* and *absolute temperature* conditions are met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Recall — Heat Detector Classes (EN 54-5):**
> - Class A1: Trigger @ **60 °C** (ROR + FT)
> - Class A2: Trigger @ **70 °C**
> - Class B: Trigger @ **80 °C** (fixed only)
> - Class C: Trigger @ **90 °C**

---

## 2.2 Smoke Detectors — Operational Principles

Smoke detectors respond to **aerosol particulates** generated during combustion. Two dominant physical principles are used.

### 2.2.1 Ionization Chamber Smoke Detector (IISD)
- Uses a tiny radioactive source (**Americium-241**, ~**0.9 µCi**) to ionize air between two electrodes.
- A small steady current flows. When smoke enters, ions attach to smoke particles → current drops → alarm triggers.

Steady-state ionization current:

$$I_{ion} = \frac{n \cdot e \cdot V \cdot \mu}{d}$$

Where:
- $n$ = ion density (ions/m³)
- $e$ = electron charge ($1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C)
- $V$ = applied voltage (V)
- $\mu$ = ion mobility (m²/V·s)
- $d$ = electrode gap (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Am-241?** Alpha particles (~5 MeV) have low penetration — they ionize air efficiently but cannot escape the chamber, making IISD safe when intact.

### 2.2.2 Photoelectric (Optical) Smoke Detector
- Uses a **light scattering** principle: an LED emits light into a sensing chamber; under normal conditions, the photodiode receives minimal light. When smoke enters, light scatters onto the photodiode → alarm triggers.

Light scattering intensity (Mie regime approximation):

$$I_{scat} = I_0 \cdot \frac{N \cdot \sigma_s}{A} \cdot e^{-\mu_{ext} \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $N$ = particle number density
- $\sigma_s$ = scattering cross-section
- $\mu_{ext}$ = extinction coefficient
- $L$ = optical path length

### 2.2.3 Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD / HSSD)
- Actively draws air through a pipe network into a highly sensitive laser chamber.
- Sensitivity can reach **0.005 % obscuration/m** (vs. ~5 %/m for point detectors).
- Used in **clean rooms, data centres, heritage buildings**.

---

## 2.3 Flame Detectors — Optical Fire Signatures

Flame detectors sense **electromagnetic radiation** emitted by combustion — primarily in the **Ultraviolet (UV)** and **Infrared (IR)** bands.

### 2.3.1 Spectral Bands of Combustion

| Band | Wavelength | Source |
|------|-----------|--------|
| UV | **180 – 260 nm** | Hydrocarbon flames, H₂ flames |
| Near-IR (NIR) | **0.7 – 1.1 µm** | Hot CO₂ (4.3 µm fundamental) |
| Mid-IR (MIR) | **4.3 – 4.4 µm** | Strong CO₂ emission peak |
| Visual | 0.4 – 0.7 µm | Glowing soot |

### 2.3.2 UV Flame Detector
- Uses a **phototube** (e.g., filled with CaF₂ or MgF₂ window) sensitive to 180–260 nm.
- Senses solar-blind UV radiation (UV-C blocked by ozone → no false alarms from sunlight).
- Detector output is a series of pulses; alarm fires when pulse rate exceeds a threshold (typically **1–10 Hz**).

### 2.3.3 IR Flame Detector
- Single-frequency IR: responds to **4.3 µm CO₂ peak** — narrow-band optical filter.
- Dual-frequency (IR²): compares **4.3 µm** vs **reference band (e.g., 3.8 µm)**. The ratio:

$$R_{IR^2} = \frac{I_{4.3\,\mu m}}{I_{3.8\,\mu m}}$$

A flame produces $R_{IR^2} > R_{threshold}$ (typically **≥ 1.3**), rejecting sunlight, hot bodies, and arc lamps.

### 2.3.4 UV/IR & IR³ Multi-Spectral
- Combines UV + IR; or triple IR (3.8 / 4.3 / 5.0 µm) → virtually eliminates false alarms.
- Field of View (FoV) typically **90° conical**; detection range up to **65 m** for a 0.1 m² n-heptane fire.

---

## 2.4 Flammable Gas Detectors — Combustion Precursor Sensing

Gas detectors identify **combustible or toxic gases** *before* ignition — they are the *earliest* of all four detectors.

### 2.4.1 Catalytic Bead (Pellistor) Sensor
- Two matched beads: one **active** (coated with Pd/Pt catalyst), one **reference** (passive).
- Combustible gas oxidizes on the active bead → temperature rises → resistance changes.
- Forms a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_a - R_r}{R_a + R_r}$$

Where $R_a$ = active bead resistance, $R_r$ = reference bead resistance.

### 2.4.2 Infrared (NDIR) Gas Sensor
- Uses **Non-Dispersive Infrared** absorption. Beer-Lambert Law:

$$I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-\alpha(\lambda) \cdot C \cdot L}$$

Where:
- $I(\lambda)$ = transmitted intensity at wavelength $\lambda$
- $I_0(\lambda)$ = source intensity
- $\alpha(\lambda)$ = absorption coefficient of target gas
- $C$ = gas concentration (ppm or %LEL)
- $L$ = optical path length (m)

### 2.4.3 Semiconductor (Metal Oxide) Sensor
- Typically **SnO₂** doped with Pd. In clean air, O₂ traps electrons → high resistance. Combustible gas reduces the surface → electrons released → resistance drops.

### 2.4.4 Electrochemical Sensor (for toxic gases like CO, H₂S)
- Three-electrode cell (working, counter, reference). Target gas undergoes redox reaction → current proportional to concentration:

$$I_{cell} = n \cdot F \cdot \Phi_g$$

Where $n$ = electrons per molecule, $F$ = Faraday constant (**96,485 C/mol**), $\Phi_g$ = molar flux of gas.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|--------------------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)e^{-t/\tau}$ | Detector thermal response | °C vs s |
| 2 | $dT/dt \geq 8.3$ °C/min | ROR threshold (EN 54-5) | °C/min |
| 3 | $I_{ion} = n e V \mu / d$ | Ionization chamber current | Amperes |
| 4 | $I_{scat} = I_0 \cdot (N\sigma_s/A) e^{-\mu_{ext} L}$ | Mie scattering intensity | W/m² |
| 5 | $\% obs/m = \frac{100}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Smoke obscuration | % per m |
| 6 | $R_{IR^2} = I_{4.3}/I_{3.8}$ | Flame spectral ratio | dimensionless ≥ 1.3 |
| 7 | $I(\lambda) = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ | Beer-Lambert gas absorption | — |
| 8 | $I_{cell} = nF\Phi_g$ | Electrochemical cell current | A |
| 9 | $\%LEL = (C_{gas}/C_{LEL}) \times 100$ | Fraction of Lower Explosive Limit | % |
| 10 | $LFL/LEL$ | Lowest concentration ignitable | e.g., Methane = **5 % v/v** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Threshold Values to Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Alarm setpoint (CO sensor):** 30 ppm (8 h), 70 ppm (1 h)
> - **LEL of LPG:** ~**2.1 %** in air
> - **LEL of Methane:** **5.0 %** in air
> - **LEL of Hydrogen:** **4.0 %** in air
> - **Alarm trip:** typically **10–25 % LEL**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Thermal Time Constant of a Heat Detector

A heat detector is modelled as a **lumped thermal mass** exchanging heat with surrounding gas by convection and losing by radiation.

### Energy balance on the detector element:

$$\rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T_\infty - T) + \varepsilon \sigma A (T_\infty^4 - T^4)$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of detector (kg/m³)
- $V$ = volume (m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $h$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area (m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant (**5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**)

### Step 1: Neglect radiation (valid for $T \leq 200$ °C)

At typical detector operating ranges, the radiation term is $\leq 5\%$ of convection → we drop it for a first-order model:

$$\rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A (T_\infty - T)$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to standard first-order form

$$\frac{dT}{dt} + \frac{hA}{\rho V c_p} T = \frac{hA}{\rho V c_p} T_\infty$$

### Step 3: Identify the time constant

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}}$$

### Step 4: Solve with initial condition $T(0) = T_0$

Multiply integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[T\, e^{t/\tau}\right] = \frac{T_\infty}{\tau}\, e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$T(t)\, e^{t/\tau} - T_0 = T_\infty\!\left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

### Step 5: Final closed-form solution

$$T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)\, e^{-t/\tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A brass heat-detector element ($\rho$ = 8500 kg/m³, $c_p$ = 380 J/kg·K, $V = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m³, $A = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m²) is in air with $h$ = 25 W/m²·K.
>
> $$\tau = \frac{8500 \times (1\times10^{-6}) \times 380}{25 \times (6\times10^{-4})} = \frac{3.23}{0.015} \approx 215\, s$$
>
> A small $\tau$ → fast response → suitable for spot detectors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Smoke Obscuration (% Obscuration/m)

The photometric definition of smoke obscuration is derived from the **Beer-Lambert law**:

### Step 1: Beer-Lambert transmittance

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu_{ext} L}$$

### Step 2: Take natural log

$$\mu_{ext} L = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 3: Define percent obscuration

Obscuration is the fraction of light *blocked*, expressed per metre of path:

$$\%\, obs/m = \frac{100}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 4: Conversion to optical density (OD)

$$OD = -\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \%\, obs/m = 230.26 \times OD$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU values:**
> - Photoelectric alarm setpoint ≈ **4.6 % obs/m**
> - Aspirating (HSSD) alarm setpoint ≈ **0.1 % obs/m** (100× more sensitive)

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Catalytic Bead Bridge Output

A Wheatstone bridge with the active bead $R_a$ and reference $R_r$:

### Step 1: Bridge balance condition (zero gas)

$$R_a = R_r \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = 0$$

### Step 2: Active bead resistance after gas exposure

Catalyst oxidizes gas → bead heats by $\Delta T$ → resistance rises:

$$R_a = R_r (1 + \beta\, \Delta T)$$

Where $\beta$ = Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR, typically **3500 ppm/°C** for Pt wire).

### Step 3: Bridge output

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_a - R_r}{R_a + R_r} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\beta \Delta T}{2 + \beta \Delta T}$$

For small $\beta \Delta T$:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}\, \beta\, \Delta T}{2}$$

### Step 4: Convert $\Delta T$ to gas concentration

Heat released by oxidation $\propto$ gas concentration:

$$\Delta T \propto C_{gas} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} \approx k \cdot C_{gas}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Detector Selection Decision Tree

| Fire Scenario | Best Detector | Reason |
|---------------|---------------|--------|
| Smouldering wood furniture | Smoke (photoelectric) | Sub-micron particles, low heat |
| Flammable liquid pool (heptane) | Flame (IR²) | High radiant output, fast optical signature |
| Kitchen / pantry | Heat (FT @ 90 °C) | Avoid false alarms from steam/cooking aerosols |
| Battery charging room | Gas (H₂ sensor) | Detect off-gassing pre-ignition |
| Data centre ceiling void | ASD (HSSD) | Earliest incipient detection, clean environment |
| Outdoor petrochemical tank farm | Flame (IR³ / UV/IR) | Long range, sun-immune |
| Underground coal conveyor | Heat (ROR) | Dusty environment masks smoke |
| Car park (closed) | Heat or CO sensor | Engine exhaust causes nuisance |
| LNG storage dome | Gas (IR-CH₄) + flame | Multi-criteria redundancy |

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Flame Detector Spectral Ratio Threshold

For an **IR²** flame detector, we want the ratio $R_{IR^2} = I_{4.3}/I_{3.8}$ to exceed 1.3 only for flames.

### Step 1: Solar/sun radiation
Sun radiates as a **blackbody at 5778 K**. By Planck's law, the radiance ratio:

$$\frac{L_{\lambda_1}}{L_{\lambda_2}} = \frac{\lambda_2^5}{\lambda_1^5} \cdot \frac{e^{hc/\lambda_2 k_B T} - 1}{e^{hc/\lambda_1 k_B T} - 1}$$

For $T = 5778$ K, $\lambda_1 = 3.8$ µm, $\lambda_2 = 4.3$ µm:

$$\frac{L_{3.8}}{L_{4.3}} \approx 1.10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{IR^2}^{sun} \approx 0.91$$

### Step 2: Hydrocarbon flame at ~1200 K
The 4.3 µm band has a strong CO₂ emission line (selective emission, not Planckian). The ratio:

$$R_{IR^2}^{flame} = \frac{I_{4.3}^{flame}}{I_{3.8}^{flame}} \geq 1.3$$

This is why **1.3 is a robust threshold** — it sits well above the maximum solar ratio.

---

## 3.6 Master Comparison Table for KTU Exam

| Property | Heat (FT) | Heat (ROR) | Smoke (IISD) | Smoke (Photo) | Flame (UV) | Flame (IR²) | Gas (Pellistor) | Gas (NDIR) |
|----------|-----------|------------|--------------|---------------|------------|-------------|------------------|------------|
| Detects | Temp | dT/dt | Small ions | Scattered light | UV photons | IR photons | Combustible gas | Gas absorption |
| Sensitivity | Low | Med | High (small particles) | High (large particles) | High | High | ppm–%LEL | ppm |
| False-alarm risk | Very low | Low | High (steam) | Med (cooking) | Medium (arc) | Low | Med (silicon) | Very low |
| Speed | Slow | Med | Fast | Fast | Very fast | Fast | Med | Med |
| Cost | $ | $ | $ | $$ | $$$ | $$$ | $$ | $$$ |
| Maintenance | Low | Low | Med (radioactive) | Low | High (window) | Med | High (poisoning) | Low |
| Best for | Kitchens | Garages | Bedrooms | Corridors | Outdoor tanks | Refineries | Refineries | Clean rooms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Master Taxonomy of Fire Detectors

```mermaid

    A["AUTOMATIC FIRE DETECTORS"] --> B["HEAT DETECTORS"]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Detector Decision Logic (ASCII Fallback)

```
+------------------- DETECTOR CLASSIFICATION MATRIX -------------------+
|  INPUT SIGNATURE   |   PHYSICAL TRANSDUCER    |  OUTPUT ACTION      |
+--------------------+---------------------------+---------------------+
|  Temperature (T)   |  Thermistor / Bimetal     |  Contact closure    |
|  dT/dt             |  Differential chamber     |  Contact closure    |
|  Smoke particles   |  Ionization (Am-241)      |  Current drop       |
|  Smoke particles   |  Photo-diode + LED        |  Scattered light    |
|  UV photons        |  Phototube (Geiger mode)  |  Pulse train        |
|  IR photons        |  Pyro-electric + filter   |  Ratio logic        |
|  Combustible gas   |  Pellistor + bridge       |  mV signal          |
|  Toxic gas (CO)    |  Electrochemical cell     |  µA current         |
+--------------------+---------------------------+---------------------+
                       |
                       v
                +--------------+
                |  FACP LOGIC  |
                +--------------+
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+--------------+
        v              v              v              v
    SOUNDER         BEACON       BMS RELAY      FIRE PUMP
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a fixed-temperature heat detector and a rate-of-rise heat detector. State any two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fixed-Temperature (FT) | Rate-of-Rise (ROR) |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Operating principle** | Activates when surrounding air reaches a preset threshold (e.g., **57 °C** or **90 °C**). | Activates when the *rate* of temperature rise exceeds **8.3 °C/min** regardless of absolute temperature. |
| **Trigger element** | Eutectic alloy / frangible bulb / bimetal strip. | Pneumatic diaphragm / thermistor differential circuit. |
| **Applications (any 2)** | Kitchens, boiler rooms, garages — where slow buildup is expected. | Warehouses, car parks, corridors — where flashover risk demands early warning. |

*(1 mark — definition of each; 1 mark — key difference; 1 mark — applications.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the working of an ionization smoke detector. Why is Americium-241 used as the source?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- An **ionization chamber** contains two electrodes with a small **Americium-241** source that emits α-particles (~**5 MeV**), ionizing air and producing a steady current (~pA) between the electrodes. **(1 mark)**
- When combustion smoke enters, ions attach to smoke particles, reducing ion mobility → chamber current drops below a threshold → comparator triggers alarm. **(1 mark)**
- **Am-241 is used because:** (i) α-particles have *very low penetration* (<10 cm in air, stopped by skin/paper) → safe; (ii) long half-life (**432 years**); (iii) α-particles produce dense ionization at low energy, ideal for chamber design. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Q3A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Describe with neat sketches the construction and working of a **dual-infrared (IR²) flame detector**. Explain how it discriminates flames from non-flame IR sources. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the relative merits and limitations of UV, single-IR, dual-IR, and triple-IR (IR³) flame detectors for an offshore oil platform. Recommend the most suitable type with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q3A

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working of IR² Detector (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Optical window** — typically sapphire or quartz, transparent to IR.
2. **Beam splitter** — divides incoming IR into two paths.
3. **Narrow-band optical filter at 4.3 µm** — passes the strong CO₂ emission line of hydrocarbon flames; rejects everything else.
4. **Reference narrow-band filter at 3.8 µm** — non-flame region; used as baseline.
5. **Two pyro-electric sensors** — convert IR radiation to electrical signals (sensor A at 4.3 µm, sensor B at 3.8 µm).
6. **Signal-processing electronics** — amplifiers, rectifiers, ratio computer, flicker analyser.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Flame radiates strongly at 4.3 µm due to vibrational-rotational transitions of hot CO₂ (flame temp ~**1200 K**).
2. Both sensors receive flux; outputs $V_A$ and $V_B$ are computed.
3. The **ratio** $R = V_A/V_B$ is computed continuously. For a true flame $R \geq 1.3$; for sun, lamps, hot surfaces $R < 1.0$.
4. A **flicker analyser** further checks for pulsations at **1–20 Hz** characteristic of turbulent diffusion flames.
5. When both ratio *and* flicker criteria are satisfied → alarm.

**Discrimination from non-flame sources (1 mark):**
- Sun radiates as a blackbody at **5778 K** — spectral ratio at 4.3 / 3.8 ≈ **0.91**, well below 1.3.
- Tungsten lamps have similar blackbody profile → ratio < 1.0.
- Hot machinery surfaces peak at 3–5 µm but ratio drops sharply.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis for Offshore Platform (7 marks)

| Detector Type | Advantages | Limitations |
|---------------|------------|-------------|
| **UV (180–260 nm)** | Solar-blind (no sun false alarms); instant response. | Range limited (~15 m); window fouling by salt spray/oil mist; arc welding false alarms. |
| **Single IR (4.3 µm)** | Long range (up to 65 m); robust. | Cannot reject sunlight modulated by rotating machinery or flickering heaters. |
| **Dual IR (IR²)** | Excellent solar/arc rejection via ratio + flicker. | Slightly higher cost; periodic window cleaning needed. |
| **Triple IR (IR³)** | Best false-alarm rejection (3 bands); longest range; suitable for harsh environments. | Highest cost; more complex electronics. |

**Recommendation (2 marks):**
For an **offshore oil platform**, the recommended detector is the **Triple-IR (IR³)** or **UV/IR combined** detector, because:
1. Platform has hot equipment, welding activity, sunlight reflections — IR²/IR³ handles these via spectral ratio logic.
2. Salt-laden marine atmosphere fouls optical windows; IR³'s automatic window-health monitoring is critical.
3. Detecting a **0.1 m² hydrocarbon pool fire** at >**30 m** is essential to give operators time to activate deluge systems.

---

### Q3B. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the operation of an **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD/HSSD)** system. List four advantages over conventional point smoke detectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A photoelectric smoke detector operates on the principle of light scattering by smoke particles. Derive an expression for **percent obscuration per metre** from the Beer-Lambert law. An ASD chamber reports $I/I_0 = 0.85$ over an optical path $L = 1.5$ m. Calculate the **% obscuration/m**. Comment on whether this triggers a typical fire alarm. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Q3B

#### Part (a) — ASD/HSSD System (7 marks)

**Block diagram description (4 marks):**

```
   [Protected Space]
         |
   ┌─────▼─────┐
   │ Sampling  │
   │ Pipe Net  │  ← typically 25–100 m, with sampling holes every 5–10 m
   └─────┬─────┘
         │
   ┌─────▼─────┐
   │ Aspirator │  ← high-efficiency blower (24 V DC)
   │  Fan      │
   └─────┬─────┘
         │
   ┌─────▼─────┐    ┌────────────┐
   │  Filter   │───▶│ Laser Opti-│
   │ Cartridge │    │ cal Chamber│
   └───────────┘    └─────┬──────┘
                          │
                    ┌─────▼──────┐
                    │ Detector & │
                    │  Display   │── Alarm relays
                    └────────────┘
```

**Working (2 marks):**
1. Aspirator continuously draws air from protected area through pipe network.
2. Air passes through a coarse/dust filter and into the laser detection chamber.
3. A laser diode (typically **650 nm**) illuminates the air; photodetector measures forward-scattered light.
4. Microprocessor computes obscuration; multi-level alarms (Alert, Pre-Alarm, Fire-1, Fire-2) generated.
5. Outputs: relay contacts, 4–20 mA, Modbus, BACnet to BMS/FACP.

**Four advantages over point detectors (1 mark):**
1. **Up to 1000× higher sensitivity** (0.005 % obs/m) → detects incipient fires hours before point detectors.
2. **Concealed installation** — pipes hidden above ceilings; aesthetic + tamper-proof.
3. **Multi-hole sampling** — single detector covers many zones; cost-effective for large areas.
4. **Active sampling** — works in harsh environments (cold, dusty) inaccessible to point detectors.

---

#### Part (b) — % Obscuration Derivation & Calculation (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from Beer-Lambert transmittance:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu_{ext} L}$$

Take natural logarithm:

$$\mu_{ext} L = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

Define percent obscuration as the *percentage of light blocked per metre*:

$$\%\, \text{obs/m} = \frac{100}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

**Given data (1 mark):**
$I/I_0 = 0.85$, $L = 1.5$ m

**Step 1: Compute the log term**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.85}\right) = \ln(1.1765)$$

$$= 0.1625$$

**Step 2: Compute % obs/m**

$$\%\, \text{obs/m} = \frac{100}{1.5} \times 0.1625 = 66.67 \times 0.1625$$

$$= 10.83\ \%\text{obs/m}$$

**Comment on alarm triggering (2 marks):**

| Detector type | Typical alarm setpoint | Does this reading trigger alarm? |
|---------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Point photoelectric | ~**4.6 % obs/m** | YES (alarm at much lower smoke) |
| Standard ionization | ~**8–12 % obs/m** | YES (likely alarm) |
| Aspirating HSSD | **Alert** at 0.1 %; **Fire-2** at ~**2 %** | YES (already past Fire-2; *severe smoke*) |

> **Conclusion:** A reading of **10.83 % obs/m** indicates a **well-developed fire condition** — well above the alarm threshold for *all* smoke detector types. Immediate evacuation and suppression system activation are warranted.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing % obscuration with light transmission %** — these are *different*. Transmission = $I/I_0 \times 100$; obscuration = $(1 - I/I_0) \times 100$ for *unobstructed path* — but the *standard* is % obs/m via the logarithmic formula above. Many students write the wrong formula and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — ROR threshold is **°C/min**, not °C/s. Class A1 = 60 °C, NOT 57 °C in EN 54-5.
> 3. **Writing "smoke detector senses heat"** — this is a *category error*; smoke detectors respond to particulates, not temperature.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation in Q3B(b)** — the 4-mark derivation must be shown step-by-step; jumping straight to the calculation loses almost all marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3):**

### 🔥 Heat Detectors
- ✅ Two main types: **Fixed Temperature (FT)** and **Rate-of-Rise (ROR)**
- ✅ FT triggers at preset °C; ROR triggers at **dT/dt ≥ 8.3 °C/min**
- ✅ Thermal time constant: $\tau = \rho V c_p / (hA)$ — *smaller $\tau$ = faster response*
- ✅ EN 54-5 classes: A1 (60 °C), A2 (70 °C), B (80 °C), C (90 °C)
- ✅ Slowest of all four detector types; **least prone to false alarms**

### 💨 Smoke Detectors
- ✅ **Ionization** — Am-241 α-source; better for *fast-flaming* fires
- ✅ **Photoelectric** — Light scattering; better for *smouldering* fires
- ✅ **ASD/HSSD** — Aspirating; sensitivity **1000× higher** than point detectors
- ✅ **% obs/m formula** = $(100/L) \ln(I_0/I)$ — used in every KTU exam
- ✅ Typical alarm setpoint ≈ **4.6 % obs/m**

### 🔥 Flame Detectors
- ✅ **UV** — 180–260 nm; solar-blind; short range (~15 m)
- ✅ **Single IR** — 4.3 µm CO₂ peak; long range but sun-vulnerable
- ✅ **IR²** — ratio of 4.3 / 3.8 µm **≥ 1.3**; rejects false alarms
- ✅ **IR³ / UV+IR** — best for **offshore, petrochemical, hangars**
- ✅ Field of view typically **90°**; range up to **65 m**

### ⚗️ Flammable Gas Detectors
- ✅ **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor)** — for combustible gases up to **100 % LEL**
- ✅ **NDIR** — Beer-Lambert; selective, poison-resistant
- ✅ **Semiconductor (SnO₂)** — low cost, used in domestic LPG detectors
- ✅ **Electrochemical** — for toxic gases (CO, H₂S) at ppm level
- ✅ Typical alarm trip: **10–25 % LEL** (safety margin before reaching LEL)
- ✅ LEL of methane = **5.0 % v/v**, LPG ≈ **2.1 % v/v**, H₂ = **4.0 % v/v**

### 🧠 Cross-Cutting Concepts (Always Asked)
- ✅ **T1–T4 fire test scenarios** (ISO 7240) — which detector for which fire
- ✅ **Multi-criteria detectors** = smoke + heat + CO → modern best practice
- ✅ **Detector zoning** — max **2000 m²** per zone (NFPA 72)
- ✅ **Spacing rules** — point smoke detectors ≤ **9.1 m** apart; point heat ≤ **5.3 m**
- ✅ Always end with: *"Selection of detector type depends on **fire signature expected**, **ambient conditions**, and **false-alarm environment**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Alarm System: Classification of Alarm System, Automatic Alarm System, Various Components and Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Alarm System: Classification of Alarm System, Automatic Alarm System, Various Components and Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fire Alarm System?

A **Fire Alarm System (FAS)** is an integrated network of electromechanical and electronic devices, sensors, control units, notification appliances, and interconnecting wiring/cabling, designed to **detect the unwanted presence of fire** (by monitoring environmental changes such as smoke, heat, radiation, or combustion gases), **notify the building occupants** through audio-visual signals, **transmit the emergency signal** to the fire brigade or remote monitoring station, and **activate auxiliary fire safety functions** (such as HVAC shutdown, smoke vent operation, door release, and sprinkler system activation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fire alarm system does NOT extinguish fire. Its sole purpose is **DETECT → ALERT → COMMUNICATE → INITIATE SAFETY RESPONSE**. Fire suppression is handled by separate systems (sprinklers, gas flooding, etc.).

### Formal Standards References
- **IS 2189:2008** — Indian Standard for "Selection, Installation and Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm System" (Code of Practice).
- **BS 5839-1:2017** — British Standard for "Fire detection and fire alarm systems for buildings."
- **NFPA 72:2022** — National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code (USA).
- **NBC 2016 (India), Part 4** — Fire and Life Safety, mandates fire alarm systems for specific building occupancies.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sensory Nervous System" of a Building 💡

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Think of a building as a human body, and the fire alarm system as its **sensory nervous system**.
> - **Detectors** = Sensory organs (eyes, nose, skin) → sense danger.
> - **Manual Call Points (MCP)** = The ability to scream when you see a problem.
> - **Wiring / Loop** = Nerves → transmit signals to the brain.
> - **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** = The brain → interprets the signal and decides action.
> - **Hooters, Strobes, Speakers** = The mouth & face → announce danger to everyone.
> - **Relays / Output Modules** = Reflexes → automatically trigger doors opening, fans stopping, elevators returning to ground floor.
> - **Repeater / Central Monitoring Station** = The doctor/ambulance service you call for external help.

Just as a healthy nervous system keeps a body alive, a properly designed fire alarm system keeps a building's occupants alive by giving them **early warning** — typically 1 to 5 minutes before untenable conditions develop.

---

## 1.3 Objectives of a Fire Alarm System (KTU 2024 Board Expectation)

1. **Life Safety** — Provide adequate warning for safe egress.
2. **Property Protection** — Enable early fire-fighting intervention, reducing structural damage.
3. **Environmental Monitoring** — Continuous surveillance of defined zones.
4. **Process Continuity** — In industrial plants, protect critical equipment/processes.
5. **Code Compliance** — Satisfy statutory requirements of NBC 2016, IS 2189, local fire service rules.
6. **Integration** — Interface with BMS, sprinklers, gas flooding, elevators, public address.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Fire Alarm Systems — Overview

A fire alarm system can be classified in **multiple orthogonal ways**. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to know all major classification axes:

### A. Classification by **Degree of Automation**

| Type | Trigger | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Fire Alarm System** | Human activation via MCP (Break-glass unit) | Small offices, low-risk areas, watchman booths |
| **Automatic Fire Alarm System** | Self-acting detectors (smoke, heat, flame) | All high-risk, multi-storey, public buildings |
| **Semi-Automatic (Manual + Automatic)** | Both MCP and detectors | Most modern commercial installations |
| **Pre-action / Verifying System** | Two detector confirmations or detector + MCP | Data centres, museums, libraries (to prevent false alarms) |

### B. Classification by **Detection Technology (Automatic systems only)**

| Type | Sensor Principle | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionization Smoke Detector** | Radioactive source ionizes air; smoke disrupts current | Fast-flaming fires |
| **Photoelectric (Optical) Smoke Detector** | Light scattering by smoke particles | Smoldering fires, residential |
| **Heat Detector (Fixed Temperature)** | Bi-metallic strip or eutectic alloy melts at rated °C | Kitchens, garages, dusty areas |
| **Heat Detector (Rate-of-Rise, ROR)** | Pneumatic or thermistor detects temperature rise rate | General offices, corridors |
| **Combination Heat Detector** | Both fixed + ROR | Industrial / warehouses |
| **Flame Detector (IR / UV / IR3 / UV-IR)** | Optical detection of flame radiation | Petrochemical, transformer yards |
| **Gas / CO Detector** | Electrochemical / semiconductor | Vehicle parks, boiler rooms |
| **Aspirating Smoke Detector (ASD / VESDA)** | Active air sampling with high-sensitivity laser | Data centres, clean rooms, heritage buildings |
| **Beam Smoke Detector** | Light beam obscuration | Atriums, large halls, warehouses |
| **Video Image Smoke Detection (VISD)** | CCTV-based algorithm | Tunnels, large industrial sites |

### C. Classification by **Wiring Architecture (Signal Transmission)**

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Conventional (Hard-wired / Zone-based)** | Each zone has its own pair of wires; panel only knows *which zone*, not *which device* |
| **Addressable (Analogue / Intelligent)** | All devices on a single loop; each device has a unique address; panel knows exact device location |
| **Wireless (Radio)** | Battery-powered devices communicating by RF to a translator; used where cabling is impractical |
| **Hybrid** | Combines addressable loop with conventional sub-loops |
| **Networked / Peer-to-Peer** | Multiple FACPs communicate on a token-ring or peer-to-peer network (e.g., Notifier, EST-3) |

### D. Classification by **Alarm Signalling Pathway (BS 5839-1:2017)**

- **C Classification (Call Points only)** — Manual break-glass systems.
- **L Classification (Life Safety — Automatic Detection):**
  - **L1** — Detection in **all** areas.
  - **L2** — Detection in **high-risk** + escape-route areas.
  - **L3** — Detection only in **escape routes + rooms opening onto escape routes**.
  - **L4** — Detection only in **escape routes** (corridors, staircases).
  - **L5** — Local detection for **specific fire-engineering objective**.
- **M Category (Manual)** — Manual call points only.
- **P Category (Property Protection)** — Detector coverage to protect property.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** *L = Life* (auto), *M = Manual* (call points only), *P = Property*. L1 = highest, L5 = lowest life-safety coverage.

### E. Classification by **Monitoring Arrangement**

- **Local Alarm** — Sounds only at site (e.g., hooters, bells).
- **Remote Alarm** — Communicates to a remote manned centre.
- **Central Station** — Third-party 24×7 monitoring with dispatch.
- **Proprietary / Direct brigade call** — Direct line to fire service.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Modern Automatic Fire Alarm System

A complete fire alarm system executes a closed-loop sequence:

$$
\text{Detect} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Annunciate} \rightarrow \text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Communicate}
$$

Let us dissect each step with engineering rigour.

### Step 1: DETECT (Initiation Phase)
Automatic **initiating devices** continuously monitor an environmental parameter:
- Smoke aerosol density (obscurement in $\%/\text{m}$)
- Temperature ($^\circ$C) or rate of change ($^\circ$C/min)
- Flame radiation intensity (in specific IR/UV bands, W/m²)
- Combustion gas concentration (CO in ppm, gas in % LEL)

The detector converts the physical phenomenon into an **analogue electrical signal** (current or voltage) and compares it to a preset or dynamically-adjusted **alarm threshold**.

### Step 2: PROCESS (Decision Phase)
The **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)** receives the signal, runs algorithms (drift compensation, pre-alarm thresholds, voting logic), confirms whether the event is **GENUINE** or **FALSE**, and decides:
- **Pre-alarm** (warning)
- **Fire alarm** (full evacuation)
- **Supervisory** (fault, e.g., device removed, line broken)
- **Trouble** (system integrity issue)

### Step 3: ANNUNCIATE (Notification Phase)
- Activates **Audio Notification Appliances** — Electronic hooters, bells, sirens (typically $\ge$ 65 dB at bed-head, 75 dB at occupant location).
- Activates **Visual Notification Appliances** — Xenon strobes (1 Hz flash, 15/30/75/110 candela), beacons.
- Triggers **Voice Evacuation Messages** through speakers.

### Step 4: CONTROL (Output Phase)
**Control relays / output modules** operate:
- Door hold-open release
- HVAC fan stop
- Smoke vent / exhaust fan start
- Elevator recall
- Fire-pump start signal
- Sprinkler flow-switch monitoring
- Access control override

### Step 5: COMMUNICATE (Reporting Phase)
- **Local indication** on FACP LCD/LED.
- **Remote transmission** via:
  - GSM/GPRS dialer
  - IP communicator
  - Dedicated telephone line
  - Wireless radio
  - Fibre-optic / cloud
- **Graphical User Interface (GUI)** at control room.
- **Cloud-based event logging**.

---

## 2.2 Why Classify? — Engineering Reasoning

Classification is **not academic taxonomy** — it drives:
1. **Code compliance** (NBC 2016 mandates L1 in high-rise hospitals).
2. **Device selection** (ionization vs photoelectric — IS 2189 Table 1).
3. **Cable sizing** (conventional vs addressable — affects current budget).
4. **Cost optimization** (L4 is 30–40% cheaper than L1).
5. **Reliability and maintenance** (addressable systems reduce troubleshooting time by ~70%).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEP THIS TABLE FOR REVISION** — Examiners love picking detector spacing and battery calculations.

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression / Value | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Detector Spacing (smoke/heat) | $S \le 0.7 \times H$ for point detectors; or use **smoke $S_{\max}=10$ m**, **heat $S_{\max}=5$ m** | metres (m), **IS 2189 / BS 5839-1** |
| 2 | Detector Coverage Area | $A_{\text{cov}} = S^2$ (point), beam = $S \times W$ | m² |
| 3 | Minimum Sound Level | $L_{\text{PA}} \ge 65$ dB(A) (sleeping) or $L_{\text{PA}} \ge 75$ dB(A) general | dB(A) — **IS 2189 Cl. 6.3** |
| 4 | Sound Level at Distance | $L(d) = L_0 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ (point source, free field) | dB |
| 5 | Sound Level Combined (multiple devices) | $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | dB |
| 6 | Battery Sizing (Standby + Alarm) | $C_{\text{bat}} = 1.25 \times \left[(I_{\text{sb}} \times T_{\text{sb}}) + (I_{\text{al}} \times T_{\text{al}})\right]$ | Ah |
| 7 | Standard Standby Time | $T_{\text{sb}} = 24$ h (general), $48$ h (some high-risk) | hours |
| 8 | Standard Alarm Time | $T_{\text{al}} = 0.5$ h to $1$ h (per local rule) | hours |
| 9 | Loop Voltage Drop | $V_{\text{drop}} = 2 \times I \times R \times L$ (2-wire loop) | Volts |
| 10 | Max Devices per Loop | Addressable $\le 159$ (legacy) or $\le 250$ (modern CLIP/advanced) | devices |
| 11 | Detector Threshold — Heat Fixed | $57\,^\circ\text{C}$ (ordinary), $90\,^\circ\text{C}$ (high) | °C |
| 12 | ROR Threshold | $\Delta T / \Delta t \ge 8.3\,^\circ\text{C/min}$ | °C/min |
| 13 | Smoke Obscuration | $m = \dfrac{10}{d} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)$ | $\%/\text{m}$ |
| 14 | UV Flame Detector Range | 0.18 – 0.26 µm wavelength | micrometres |
| 15 | IR Flame Detector Range | 4.35 µm CO₂ band (single), 1.0/2.5/4.35 µm (IR3 triple) | micrometres |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In the formulas above, the absolute value bars and case distinctions were deliberately converted to $\log_{10}$, $\sum$, $\dfrac$, $\Delta$ to avoid the `\|` character which would break markdown table syntax. Always use $\log_{10}$ (not $\log$) and $\vert$ / $\mid$ (not `|`) inside any markdown table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Hospitals** → L1 with **voice evacuation** to maintain order and assist patients.
- **Shopping Malls** → L1 with **beam detectors** in atriums.
- **IT/Data Centres** → **VESDA** for ultra-early warning, integrated with **FM-200 / Novec 1230** gas flooding.
- **Petrochemical Plants** → **IR3 / UV-IR flame detectors** with hazardous-area certification (ATEX/IECEx).
- **High-rise Apartments** → **L2/L3** with **addressable** loop and **central monitoring**.
- **Hotels** → **L1 with voice + room-annunciator module** to alert specific room.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Battery Sizing — Complete IS 2189 / NFPA 72 Method

This is the **single most important calculation** for KTU board exam. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Problem Context
A building has an addressable FACP with the following load:
- Standby current: $I_{\text{sb}} = 0.30$ A
- Alarm current (full load): $I_{\text{al}} = 4.50$ A
- Required standby time: $T_{\text{sb}} = 24$ h
- Required alarm time: $T_{\text{al}} = 0.5$ h
- Battery derating factor (aging, temperature, efficiency): $K = 1.25$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute standby energy demand.**
The panel draws $I_{\text{sb}}$ continuously for $T_{\text{sb}}$ hours. Energy required is:

$$
Q_{\text{standby}} = I_{\text{sb}} \times T_{\text{sb}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{\text{standby}} = 0.30 \times 24 = 7.20 \text{ Ah}
$$

> **Logic:** At 0.30 A draw, in 24 hours the panel consumes 7.20 Ampere-hours of charge. This is the charge needed to *just survive* the standby period.

**Step 2: Compute alarm energy demand.**
After standby, the panel operates at $I_{\text{al}}$ for $T_{\text{al}}$ hours during fire condition:

$$
Q_{\text{alarm}} = I_{\text{al}} \times T_{\text{al}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{\text{alarm}} = 4.50 \times 0.5 = 2.25 \text{ Ah}
$$

> **Logic:** In full alarm (all sounders, strobes, relays active) for 30 minutes (0.5 h), 2.25 Ah is consumed.

**Step 3: Sum the total energy demand.**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{standby}} + Q_{\text{alarm}} = 7.20 + 2.25 = 9.45 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the derating / safety factor.**

$$
C_{\text{bat}} = K \times Q_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{bat}} = 1.25 \times 9.45 = 11.8125 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 5: Select next standard size.**

Available standard battery sizes are **7, 12, 17, 26, 38, 65, 100 Ah** etc. The next size **greater than or equal to** 11.8125 Ah is:

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{bat}} = 12 \text{ Ah (2 × 12V, 12Ah sealed lead-acid)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why derate 25%?** Real lead-acid batteries lose capacity due to (a) aging, (b) cold temperature, (c) incomplete recharge, (d) inefficiency. NFPA 72 mandates 1.25 multiplier. IS 2189 also accepts a similar margin.

---

## 3.2 Detector Coverage Calculation

### Given:
- Ceiling height $H = 4$ m
- Use point-type smoke detector with manufacturer spacing $S_0 = 8$ m

### Step 1: Compute maximum spacing on a 4 m ceiling.

$$
S_{\max} = 0.7 \times H = 0.7 \times 4 = 2.8 \text{ m} \quad \text{(height-based limit)}
$$

But this would *over-restrict* if the manufacturer is rated for $S_0$. The actual rule is:

$$
S_{\text{actual}} = \min(S_0, 0.7H) = \min(8, 2.8) = 2.8 \text{ m}
$$

So spacing must be **reduced** on low ceilings? Actually, this is wrong intuition — let us apply **IS 2189 Cl. 5.2** correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 2189 Rule:** For ceilings $\le$ 4 m, the spacing may be the **manufacturer's rated spacing** (up to 10 m for smoke). For ceilings **above 4 m**, spacing must be **reduced** using $S \le 0.7H$ as upper limit. So 4 m ceiling → use full 10 m spacing. Ceiling 6 m → $S \le 4.2$ m.

### Step 2: Coverage area per detector.

$$
A_{\text{cov}} = S^2 = 10^2 = 100 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3: Number of detectors in $A_{\text{room}} = 500$ m².

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{A_{\text{room}}}{A_{\text{cov}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{500}{100} \right\rceil = 5 \text{ detectors}
$$

---

## 3.3 Sound Pressure Level (SPL) at Distance

A hooter has rated SPL $L_0 = 105$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ m. Find SPL at $d = 8$ m.

$$
L(d) = L_0 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
L(8) = 105 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{8}{1}\right) = 105 - 20 \times 0.903 = 105 - 18.06 = 86.94 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

This satisfies IS 2189 minimum of **75 dB(A)**.

---

## 3.4 Two Hooters Combined SPL

Two hooters at 90 dB(A) and 87 dB(A):

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{90/10} + 10^{87/10}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{9} + 10^{8.7}\right)
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.0 \times 10^{9} + 5.01 \times 10^{8}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.501 \times 10^{9}\right)
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 9.176 = 91.76 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

> **Logic:** Two hooters only add ~2 dB (because decibels are logarithmic).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🧭 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** of a modern addressable fire alarm system, showing the interactions between initiation, processing, notification, control, and communication sub-systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Field Initiating Devices Layer]:::init
    B[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]:::brain
    C[Notification Appliances Layer]:::notify
    D[Control and Output Layer]:::control
    E[Power Supply System]:::power
    F[Monitoring and Communication Layer]:::monitor

    subgraph S1 [Detection Field]
      D1[Smoke Detectors]
      D2[Heat Detectors]
      D3[Flame IR UV]
      D4[Manual Call Points]
      D5[VESDA Aspirating]
      D6[Beam Detector]
    end

    subgraph S2 [Central Processing Unit]
      P1[Main FACP CPU]
      P2[Loop Driver Card]
      P3[Network Interface]
    end

    subgraph S3 [Notification Field]
      N1[Hooters and Bells]
      N2[Xenon Strobes]
      N3[Voice Speakers]
    end

    subgraph S4 [Output Control]
      O1[Door Hold Open Relays]
      O2[HVAC Shutdown]
      O3[Elevator Recall]
      O4[Fire Pump Start]
    end

    subgraph S5 [Communication]
      M1[Remote Monitoring]
      M2[GSM IP Dialer]
      M3[Cloud BMS GUI]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    E -.powers.- B
    B --> F
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3

    D1 --> P2
    D2 --> P2
    D3 --> P2
    D4 --> P2
    D5 --> P2
    D6 --> P2

    P1 --> N1
    P1 --> N2
    P1 --> N3

    P1 --> O1
    P1 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P1 --> O4

    P3 --> M1
    P3 --> M2
    P3 --> M3

    classDef init fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff6b35,color:#000
    classDef brain fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef notify fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0288d1,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef monitor fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The top-level flow (A → B → C, D, F) shows the canonical **DETECT → PROCESS → ANNOUNCE → CONTROL → COMMUNICATE** sequence. The five sub-graphs enumerate the actual field devices and modules. The dashed line from E indicates that the Power Supply System is a *parallel enabler* — not part of the signal flow itself.

---

## 4.2 Conventional vs Addressable — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CV [Conventional Zone Wiring]
      Z1[Zone 1 Devices]
      Z2[Zone 2 Devices]
      Z3[Zone 3 Devices]
      Z4[Zone 4 Devices]
      CVPanel[Conventional FACP]
      Z1 --- CVPanel
      Z2 --- CVPanel
      Z3 --- CVPanel
      Z4 --- CVPanel
    end

    subgraph AD [Addressable Loop Wiring]
      L1[Detector 01]
      L2[Detector 02]
      L3[MCP 03]
      L4[Module 04]
      L5[Sounder 05]
      ADPanel[Addressable FACP]
      L1 --- L2 --- L3 --- L4 --- L5 --- ADPanel
    end

    classDef convBox fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef addrBox fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    class CV,CVPanel convBox
    class AD,ADPanel addrBox
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Insight:** In **conventional** systems, every zone needs a *dedicated pair* of wires back to the panel. In **addressable** systems, **all devices share a single loop** (a 2-core cable daisy-chained), with each device having a unique serial address (0–159 or 0–250). This saves copper, simplifies installation, and allows pinpoint fault location.

---

## 4.3 Detector Decision Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Fire alarm panel starts polling loop] --> S1{Device OK?}
    S1 -- No --> S2[Display Trouble / Device missing]
    S1 -- Yes --> S3{Analogue value above pre-alarm threshold?}
    S3 -- No --> S1
    S3 -- Yes --> S4{Analogue value above fire threshold?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Pre-alarm logged, optional buzzer]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6{Coincidence voting enabled?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Fire condition: full alarm]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8{Second detector confirms within 60 sec?}
    S8 -- No --> S9[Pre-alarm only, no evacuation]
    S8 -- Yes --> S7
    S7 --> S10[Activate sounders, strobes, voice]
    S7 --> S11[Activate output relays]
    S7 --> S12[Notify remote station]
    S10 --> S13[Log event with timestamp]
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S13
```

---
