# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Set-B]`
**Q:** Differentiate between **conventional** and **addressable** fire alarm systems. Mention **two advantages** of addressable systems over conventional.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conventional | Addressable |
|---|---|---|
| Identification | Only identifies the **zone** | Identifies the **exact device** |
| Wiring | Multiple radial circuits | **Single closed loop** |
| Cost per device | Lower | Higher |
| Information per device | ON/OFF | Analogue value (smoke density, temperature) |
| Cable cost | Higher (many pairs) | Lower (1 loop) |
| False alarm management | Manual verification | **Drift compensation, voting** |

**Two advantages of addressable:**
1. **Pin-point device identification** → faster troubleshooting, faster response.
2. **Drift compensation & pre-alarm** → reduces false alarms by dynamically adjusting thresholds to detector contamination.
3. (Bonus) Less cabling → cheaper installation in large buildings.

**Marking Pattern (3M):** 1.5 M for comparison table, 1 M for any two advantages, 0.5 M for neatness.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Q:** What is a **Manual Call Point (MCP)**? Why are MCPs typically installed at **1.4 m height** above floor level?

**Model Answer:**

A **Manual Call Point (MCP)**, also called a *break-glass unit* or *pull station*, is a manually-operated initiation device that allows a person discovering a fire to **trigger the fire alarm system** by breaking a frangible glass element (or pressing a resettable plastic flap) which closes an electrical contact and sends an alarm signal to the FACP.

**Reasons for 1.4 m mounting height:**
1. **Ergonomic reach** — average adult hand reaches comfortably without bending or stretching.
2. **Wheelchair accessibility** — disabled persons in wheelchairs can easily operate it.
3. **Visibility** — not obstructed by furniture; visible against wall.
4. **Standard compliance** — IS 2189, BS 5839-1, and NBC 2016 all mandate 1.2 – 1.4 m.

**Marking Pattern (3M):** 1 M definition, 1 M working, 1 M for height justification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Internal choice** allowed. Solve **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (not both).

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**(a)** Classify **automatic fire alarm systems** in detail based on (i) detection technology, (ii) wiring architecture, and (iii) alarm signalling pathway (BS 5839-1). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional components** of a modern addressable fire alarm system. Explain the role of the **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Classification by Detection Technology (3 marks):**

1. **Smoke Detectors** — sense aerosol particles; sub-types:
   - *Ionization chamber* — uses Americium-241; better for flaming fires.
   - *Photoelectric (optical)* — uses light scattering; better for smoldering fires.
   - *Beam* — for large atriums.
   - *Aspirating (VESDA)* — active air sampling, ultra-sensitive.

2. **Heat Detectors** — sense temperature:
   - *Fixed temperature* (57°C / 90°C eutectic alloy).
   - *Rate-of-Rise (ROR)* — triggers on 8.3°C/min rise.
   - *Combination* — both.

3. **Flame Detectors** — sense electromagnetic radiation:
   - *IR single-frequency* (4.35 µm CO₂ band).
   - *IR3* (multi-frequency triple IR — more reliable).
   - *UV* (0.18–0.26 µm).

4. **Gas / CO Detectors** — sense specific gases (CO, LPG, methane).

**Classification by Wiring Architecture (2 marks):**

- **Conventional (Zone-based)** — radial circuits, panel identifies zone only.
- **Addressable (Analogue Addressable)** — single loop, each device has unique ID, panel pin-points device.
- **Wireless** — RF-based, no cables.
- **Hybrid / Networked** — combinations.

**Classification by BS 5839-1 (2 marks):**

| Category | Coverage | Use |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | All areas | Highest life-safety (hospitals) |
| L2 | High-risk + escape routes | High-rise residential |
| L3 | Escape routes + rooms opening onto them | Offices |
| L4 | Escape routes only | Small buildings |
| L5 | Specific objective | Engineered solution |
| M | Manual call points only | Watchman posts |

#### Model Solution (b)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes (the Mermaid diagram covers all the components).

**Functional Components (2 marks):**
- *Initiating devices* (smoke, heat, flame, MCP).
- *Loop* (2-core cable, closed loop, max 159–250 devices).
- *FACP* (main controller, CPU, loop driver, power supply, display).
- *Notification appliances* (hooters, strobes, speakers).
- *Output modules* (door holders, fan control, elevator recall).
- *Power supply + standby battery*.
- *Remote communication* (GSM/IP).

**Role of FACP (2 marks):**
1. **Continuously polls** every device on the loop and receives analogue values.
2. **Compares** values to alarm thresholds and pre-alarm thresholds.
3. **Annunciates** alarms visually on LCD and activates notification circuits.
4. **Implements logic** — voting, delay, coincidence, time-based.
5. **Logs events** with date/time for compliance audit.
6. **Diagnoses** device health (drift, contamination, removal).
7. **Interfaces** with BMS, fire pumps, sprinklers, voice evacuation.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **components and modules** of a fire alarm control panel, with a focus on initiating, notification, and control modules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire alarm panel has standby current 0.4 A and alarm current 5.0 A. If the required standby time is **24 h** and alarm time is **0.5 h**, calculate the **battery capacity** (use derating factor **1.25**). What is the next standard size you would select? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Initiating Modules (3 marks):**
- **Initiating Circuit Module (Conventional zones)** — supervised 2-wire line with EOL resistor; reads short, open, normal.
- **Loop Driver Module (Addressable)** — drives the SLC loop, powers devices, polls addresses.
- **Isolator Modules** — short-circuit isolators prevent total loop failure if cable is damaged.
- **Sounder Strobe Driver** — supervised notification circuit.

**Notification Modules (2 marks):**
- **NAC (Notification Appliance Circuit)** — Class B (radial) or Class A (loop).
- **Voice Evacuation Module** — synthesizes pre-recorded messages.
- **Strobe / Visual Module** — synchronizes flash rate.

**Control Modules (2 marks):**
- **Relay Modules** — 24 V coil DPDT contacts for fans, doors, pumps.
- **Input Modules** — monitor sprinkler flow switches, tampers, pressure switches.
- **Output Modules** — supervised 24 V to start fire pumps, gas flooding.
- **Network Communication Module** — connects multiple panels.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1:** Compute standby energy.

$$
Q_{\text{sb}} = I_{\text{sb}} \times T_{\text{sb}} = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute alarm energy.

$$
Q_{\text{al}} = I_{\text{al}} \times T_{\text{al}} = 5.0 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 3:** Total.

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 9.6 + 2.5 = 12.1 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply derating.

$$
C_{\text{bat}} = 1.25 \times 12.1 = 15.125 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 5:** Select next standard size.

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{bat}} = 17 \text{ Ah (commonly used standard size)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never forget the 1.25 derating factor** — students commonly compute 12.1 Ah and write 12 Ah, losing 1 mark.
> - **Battery is 2 × 12 V series** — fire panels use 24 V DC. Always specify 2 numbers.
> - **Show all 4 steps** (standby → alarm → sum → derate → select) — each carries a sub-mark.
> - **Unit Ah, not A or mAh** — common slip.
> - **Round UP** to the *next* higher standard size, never round down.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### 🔹 Definitions to Memorize
- **FACP** — Fire Alarm Control Panel.
- **MCP** — Manual Call Point.
- **NAC** — Notification Appliance Circuit.
- **SLC** — Signaling Line Circuit (the addressable loop).
- **VESDA** — Very Early Smoke Detection Apparatus.
- **CO₂ band** — 4.35 µm IR flame detection principle.
- **Pre-alarm vs Fire** — pre-alarm is *warning*; fire is *evacuation*.
- **Drift compensation** — automatic adjustment of detector threshold to compensate for dust/contamination.

### 🔹 Classification Mnemonics
- **Detection tech** — **SHFG** = **S**moke, **H**eat, **F**lame, **G**as.
- **Wiring** — **CAWN** = **C**onventional, **A**ddressable, **W**ireless, **N**etworked.
- **BS 5839 L1–L5** — Higher number = Lower coverage.
- **Smoke spacing 10 m, Heat spacing 5 m** — easy to remember.

### 🔹 Numbers to Memorize
- **MCP height:** 1.2–1.4 m.
- **Min SPL:** 65 dB(A) sleeping, 75 dB(A) general.
- **Battery standby:** 24 h.
- **Alarm time:** 0.5 h.
- **Derating factor:** 1.25.
- **Loop voltage drop formula:** $V_{\text{drop}} = 2IRL$.
- **ROR threshold:** 8.3 °C/min.
- **Fixed heat:** 57 °C, 90 °C.

### 🔹 Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting 1.25 derating → ❌
- ❌ Writing conventional instead of addressable when asked for a hospital → ❌
- ❌ Confusing ROR (8.3°C/min) with Fixed (57°C) → ❌
- ❌ Specifying L4 when the question says "hospital" — hospital needs L1 → ❌
- ❌ Confusing ionization (fast flame) with photoelectric (smoldering) → ❌
- ❌ Hooter SPL is measured at 1 m, not at the device → ❌
- ❌ Addressable loop is **closed (loop)**, not radial → ❌

### 🔹 Standards Recap
- **IS 2189:2008** — India, automatic fire detection.
- **BS 5839-1:2017** — UK, comprehensive.
- **NFPA 72:2022** — USA.
- **NBC 2016 Part 4** — India, building code.

### 🔹 Real-World Insight
> A well-designed addressable fire alarm system with **drift compensation**, **coincidence voting**, and **pre-alarm warnings** can reduce false alarm rates by **up to 95%** — a major cost-saving and occupant-confidence factor in modern buildings.

---

> 🏁 **End of Module 3 Notes — Fire Alarm System: Classification, Automatic Systems, Components & Modules.**
> **All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE examinations!** 🎓🔥

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressable and Non-Addressable System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Addressable and Non-Addressable System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Addressable Fire Detection and Alarm System

An **Addressable Fire Alarm System** (also known as an *Intelligent Fire Alarm System*) is an advanced fire detection configuration in which every initiating device — including smoke detectors, heat detectors, manual call points (MCPs), and monitor modules — is assigned a **unique binary or hexadecimal address** by the manufacturer. These devices are connected to the Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP) via a single, closed-loop **Signaling Line Circuit (SLC)** that allows bi-directional communication. The control panel can individually poll (interrogate) each device, identify its precise physical location, monitor its real-time analog sensitivity (smoke obscuration percentage, temperature in °C), and issue per-device commands such as isolate, test, or reset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** An addressable system is one in which each detector or call point is identified by a unique address on the loop, enabling the FACP to pinpoint the *exact* location of the triggered device and to evaluate its analog environmental readings.

#### Real-World Analogy

Think of a large apartment complex with **100 flats**. In a *non-addressable* setup, the building's electrical wiring only tells the security desk: *"There is a fire somewhere on the 5th floor."* The watchman must run to the 5th floor to find out which flat is burning. In an *addressable* setup, every flat has its own digital ID number (e.g., Flat 5B reports `Smoke Obscuration: 12%` at 14:32 hrs). The security desk instantly knows **exactly** which flat and even what kind of smoke density is present — without leaving the chair.

### 1.2 Non-Addressable (Conventional) Fire Alarm System

A **Non-Addressable System** — also termed a **Conventional**, **Hard-Zoned**, or *Zone-Type* Fire Alarm System — groups multiple initiating devices electrically in **parallel** within a defined geographical zone. All devices in a single zone share **one common pair of wires** that terminates at a designated zone input on the FACP. When any device in the zone is activated, the panel registers a *zone-level* alarm but **cannot identify the specific device** that triggered it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** In conventional systems, the panel resolution is **zone-level**, not device-level. For a typical KTU question, always specify the *maximum number of detectors per zone* as per IS 2189 (latest revision) and NFPA 72.

#### Real-World Analogy

Continuing the apartment analogy: in a conventional system, the building is divided into zones — say, Zone A covers flats 5A–5E on the 5th floor. If a fire starts in Flat 5C, the alarm panel shows a red LED labeled *"Zone 5"* but the watchman still has to walk all 5 flats to find the source. There is no per-flat identification.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Terminology

| Term | Expansion | Significance in KTU Exams |
|------|-----------|---------------------------|
| FACP | Fire Alarm Control Panel | The "brain" of the system |
| SLC | Signaling Line Circuit | The addressable loop carrying bi-directional data |
| MCP | Manual Call Point | "Break glass" unit for manual activation |
| IDC | Initiating Device Circuit | The radial circuit in conventional systems |
| NAC | Notification Appliance Circuit | Drives sounders, strobes, bells |
| OBS | Obscuration (\%/m) | Smoke density metric for optical sensors |
| RTI | Response Time Index | Thermal detector response characteristic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Emphasis:** Understanding the **differences in cabling topology**, **fault tolerance**, **device count limits**, and **information granularity** between these two systems is a high-weightage topic and frequently appears as a 7-mark or 14-mark Part B question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of a Conventional (Non-Addressable) System

**Topology:** Radial / Star configuration.
**Communication:** One-way — from device to panel (no polling).
**Wiring per Zone:** 2-core (or 4-core if line integrity monitored).
**Logic:** "OR" logic within a zone — *any* device activation triggers the zone alarm.

**Operational Steps:**
1. The FACP continuously applies a small **monitoring voltage** (typically 24 V DC) across each Initiating Device Circuit (IDC).
2. Each detector contains a **normally open** switching element (e.g., a reed switch, a thermistor-triggered relay, or a thyristor in ionization chambers).
3. When a fire condition is sensed, the device's switch closes, causing a sharp **step increase in current** drawn from the IDC.
4. The FACP detects this current step, identifies the *zone* (via the zone card that has tripped), and activates:
   - The corresponding **zone LED** on the FACP fascia
   - The common **fire output relay**
   - All **Notification Appliance Circuits (NACs)** for sounders and strobes
5. The panel *cannot* tell which specific detector triggered; only the zone.

### 2.2 Architectural Decomposition of an Addressable System

**Topology:** Closed loop (Class A) or open loop (Class B) — *Class A is mandatory for life-safety per NFPA 72*.
**Communication Protocol:** Proprietary digital protocols (e.g., Hochiki ESP, Apollo Discovery, Notifier CLIP, Advanced MX).
**Wiring per Loop:** 2-core (same physical cable as conventional, but now carrying digital packets).
**Logic:** Per-device binary + analog values transmitted serially (typically using Manchester encoding).

**Operational Steps:**
1. The FACP sends a **sequential polling command** down the SLC at intervals of typically **1 to 4 seconds** per device.
2. Each device responds with its **unique address** and its **real-time analog value** (e.g., smoke obscuration 4\%, temperature 38°C).
3. The panel maintains a real-time **device status map** in its memory.
4. When a device's analog reading crosses its pre-programmed alarm threshold, the panel:
   - Logs the alarm with **device ID, zone, location text (e.g., "5th Floor, Room 502, Optical Smoke Detector"), and timestamp**.
   - Issues **selective NAC activation** (e.g., sounders only on 5th floor and 1 floor above/below for phased evacuation).
   - Initiates cause-and-effect programming: e.g., close fire dampers, recall elevators, start smoke extract fans.
5. **Class A loops** provide redundancy — if the cable is severed at any point, devices on both sides of the break continue to communicate via the return path.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Standard / Source | Units |
|---------------------|------------|-------------------|-------|
| Maximum devices per SLC loop (addressable) | $N_{max} = 159 \text{ to } 318$ | NFPA 72 / Manufacturer | devices |
| Maximum detectors per conventional zone (light load) | $N \le 20$ | IS 2189:2008 (Table 1) | detectors |
| Maximum detectors per conventional zone (heavy load / high risk) | $N \le 10$ | IS 2189:2008 (Table 1) | detectors |
| Maximum search distance for locating a fire | $D_{search} \le 30$ | IS 2189:2008 | metres |
| Alarm verification cycle time | $t_{verify} = 5$ to $60$ | NFPA 72 | seconds |
| Detector coverage area (smoke, smooth ceiling) | $A = 80$ | IS 2189:2008 | m² |
| Detector coverage area (heat, smooth ceiling) | $A = 50$ | IS 2189:2008 | m² |
| Detector spacing (smoke, smooth ceiling) | $S \le 9$ | IS 2189:2008 | metres |
| Detector spacing (heat, smooth ceiling) | $S \le 7$ | IS 2189:2008 | metres |
| Battery standby duration (general) | $T_{standby} = 24$ | IS 2189 / NBC 2016 | hours |
| Battery alarm duration | $T_{alarm} = 15$ | IS 2189 / NBC 2016 | minutes |
| Battery capacity formula | $C_{Ah} = 1.25 \times (I_{standby} \cdot T_{standby} + I_{alarm} \cdot T_{alarm} / 60)$ | BS 5839 / IS 2189 | Ampere-hours |
| Loop resistance (typical addressable) | $R_{loop} \le 40$ | Manufacturer spec | ohms |
| Loop capacitance (typical addressable) | $C_{loop} \le 0.5$ | Manufacturer spec | µF |
| End-of-line resistor (conventional IDC) | $R_{EOL} = 4.7$ typical | Manufacturer spec | kΩ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The factor **1.25** in the battery capacity formula is a **derating factor** for battery aging. Many students forget this and lose 1–2 marks. Always include it.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

* **High-rise buildings, IT parks, hospitals, airports, malls, and data centres** universally employ **addressable systems** because:
  * They provide device-level identification, critical when lives and high-value assets are at stake.
  * They enable **phased evacuation sequences** (e.g., the "X+Y" evacuation strategy where only the fire floor and adjacent floors evacuate initially).
  * They generate **point-precise event logs** for forensic post-incident analysis.
* **Conventional systems** are still economically justified in:
  * Small offices, warehouses with low device count (< 30), budget-constrained residential buildings.
  * Legacy installations where re-cabling is impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Maximum Devices on an Addressable Loop

The SLC loop has two electrical constraints — **resistance** and **capacitance** — and one **protocol constraint** (the addressing scheme).

Let us derive the **maximum allowable cable length** based on cable resistance and capacitance.

**Step 1 — Resistance Constraint**

The voltage drop along a copper loop of length $L$ (in metres) and cross-section $A$ (in mm²) with resistivity $\rho = 0.0172 \,\Omega \cdot \text{mm}^2/\text{m}$ for copper must be less than the panel's tolerated drop $V_{drop}$:

$$V_{drop} = \frac{2 \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot I_{max}}{A}$$

Here, the factor **2** accounts for the *outgoing* and *return* conductors forming the closed loop. Solving for $L$:

$$L_{max}^{(R)} = \frac{V_{drop} \cdot A}{2 \cdot \rho \cdot I_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance Constraint**

The capacitance per unit length $c$ (in pF/m) and the maximum permissible loop capacitance $C_{max}$ give:

$$L_{max}^{(C)} = \frac{C_{max}}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Protocol Constraint**

The number of addressable points per loop is hard-limited by the protocol's bit-depth. For example, Hochiki ESP supports 127 addresses per loop; Apollo Discovery supports 126; Notifier CLIP supports 159; Advanced MxPro supports up to 1,016.

$$N_{max} \le 2^{n} - \text{reserved addresses}$$

where $n$ is the number of address bits. For a 7-bit scheme: $2^{7} - 1 = 127$.

**Step 4 — Combined Permissible Length**

The actual maximum loop length is the **smallest** of all three constraints:

$$L_{allowed} = \min(L_{max}^{(R)},\, L_{max}^{(C)},\, L_{protocol})$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A 7-storey office building requires 240 addressable devices (smoke + heat + MCPs). Each device draws $200\,\mu\text{A}$ in standby. The FACP loop voltage is $32\,\text{V DC}$ with a permitted drop of $5\,\text{V}$. The 2-core cable is $1.5\,\text{mm}^2$ copper with capacitance $120\,\text{pF/m}$. The protocol allows $127$ devices per loop. Find:
> (a) the maximum cable length from the resistance constraint,
> (b) the maximum cable length from the capacitance constraint,
> (c) the number of SLC loops required,
> (d) the total standby current at $24\,\text{V}$ across all loops.

**Solution:**

**(a) Resistance-constrained maximum length:**

Total standby current on one loop (worst case):

$$I_{max} = 240 \times 200\,\mu\text{A} = 48{,}000\,\mu\text{A} = 0.048\,\text{A}$$

Substituting into the resistance formula:

$$L_{max}^{(R)} = \frac{5 \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.0172 \times 0.048}$$

Computing the denominator step-by-step:

$$2 \times 0.0172 \times 0.048 = 0.0016512$$

Therefore:

$$L_{max}^{(R)} = \frac{7.5}{0.0016512} \approx 4542\,\text{metres}$$

This is the *theoretical* maximum if the entire loop were a single cable — but practical limits (capacitance, device count, voltage sag along spur legs) typically restrict loop length to **1500–2000 m** in real installations.

**(b) Capacitance-constrained maximum length:**

Given $C_{max} = 0.5\,\mu\text{F}$ (typical panel limit):

$$L_{max}^{(C)} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{120 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2 \times 10^{-10}} = 4166\,\text{metres}$$

**(c) Number of SLC loops required:**

Since the protocol allows $127$ devices per loop:

$$N_{loops} = \left\lceil \frac{240}{127} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.89 \right\rceil = 2 \text{ loops}$$

**(d) Total standby current:**

At panel voltage of $24\,\text{V DC}$ (rectifier output), the total standby current is simply the per-device current times the total device count:

$$I_{total} = 240 \times 200\,\mu\text{A} = 0.048\,\text{A} = 48\,\text{mA}$$

If we include FACP quiescent current of typically $150\,\text{mA}$:

$$I_{standby,total} = 0.048 + 0.150 = 0.198\,\text{A}$$

### 3.3 Battery Sizing Derivation (Addressable FACP)

To size the standby battery bank, we use the standard BS 5839 / IS 2189 formula:

$$C_{Ah} = 1.25 \times \left( I_{standby} \cdot T_{standby} + I_{alarm} \cdot \frac{T_{alarm}}{60} \right)$$

Let $I_{standby} = 0.198\,\text{A}$, $T_{standby} = 24\,\text{h}$, $I_{alarm} = 1.5\,\text{A}$ (during full alarm condition with all sounders), and $T_{alarm} = 15\,\text{min} = 0.25\,\text{h}$:

$$C_{Ah} = 1.25 \times \left( 0.198 \times 24 + 1.5 \times 0.25 \right)$$

Computing each term:

$$0.198 \times 24 = 4.752\,\text{Ah}$$

$$1.5 \times 0.25 = 0.375\,\text{Ah}$$

Adding:

$$4.752 + 0.375 = 5.127\,\text{Ah}$$

Applying derating:

$$C_{Ah} = 1.25 \times 5.127 = 6.41\,\text{Ah}$$

Therefore, a **12 V, 7 Ah** sealed lead-acid battery would be selected (next standard size above 6.41 Ah).

### 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Conventional (Non-Addressable) | Addressable (Intelligent) |
|---------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Information granularity** | Zone-level only | Device-level (exact detector ID + location text) |
| **Cable topology** | Radial (one pair per zone) | Closed loop (single 2-core SLC) |
| **Cabling cost** | High for large buildings | Lower per device, even for very large buildings |
| **Device count limit per circuit** | 20 detectors per zone (IS 2189) | 127 to 1016 per loop (protocol-dependent) |
| **Fault tolerance** | None — single break disables that zone | Class A loop isolates break, both sides communicate |
| **Maintenance diagnostics** | Walk-the-zone inspection required | Remote diagnostics: drift compensation, sensitivity read-out |
| **Cost per device** | Low (e.g., ₹ 800–1500) | Higher (e.g., ₹ 2500–5000) but less cabling |
| **Total project cost (small building)** | Lower | Higher |
| **Total project cost (large/high-rise)** | Higher (cabling dominates) | Lower despite expensive devices |
| **Analogue value transmission** | Not possible | Yes (obscuration %, temperature °C) |
| **Alarm verification & drift comp.** | No | Yes — reduces false alarms |
| **Cause-and-effect programming** | Basic (zone → sounder) | Sophisticated (device → specific sounder, damper, etc.) |
| **Phased evacuation support** | Difficult | Native (designed-in) |
| **Repeat indication panel (RIP)** | Required for identification | Not required — panel shows exact location |
| **False alarm risk** | Higher | Lower (with verification algorithms) |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Design Logic for Zone Selection (Conventional)

1. Read architectural floor plans.
2. Mark each enclosed compartment / room as a candidate zone.
3. Apply the **30 m search distance rule**: no part of the zone should be more than **30 m** walking distance from the zone indicator.
4. Apply the **20-detector rule** per zone (or **10** for high-risk areas).
5. Ensure **zone area** does not exceed **2000 m²** for a single zone.
6. Verify that the IDC cable resistance is below the panel threshold (typically $50\,\Omega$).
7. Cross-check with IS 2189:2008 (latest) Table 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why is an addressable system preferred for hospitals?", the answer must reference *phased evacuation*, *false-alarm reduction via drift compensation*, and *device-level identification* — not just "it's modern". Always tie features to the *application*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Conventional (Non-Addressable) System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V] --> B[Rectifier and Battery Charger]
    B --> C[Battery Bank 12V or 24V DC]
    B --> D[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    D --> E[Zone Card 1 Logic]
    D --> F[Zone Card 2 Logic]
    D --> G[Zone Card N Logic]
    E --> E1[Smoke Detector IDC Zone 1]
    E --> E2[Heat Detector IDC Zone 1]
    E --> E3[MCP IDC Zone 1]
    E --> E4[EOL Resistor 4.7 kΩ]
    F --> F1[Smoke Detector IDC Zone 2]
    F --> F2[Heat Detector IDC Zone 2]
    F --> F3[EOL Resistor 4.7 kΩ]
    D --> H[NAC Output 1 Sounders]
    D --> I[NAC Output 2 Strobes]
    D --> J[Repeat Indicator Panel RIP]
    D --> K[FACP Fascia LEDs and LCD]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Addressable System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[AC Mains 230V] --> B[PSU and Charger]
    B --> C[Sealed Lead Acid Battery Bank]
    B --> D[Addressable FACP Main CPU]
    D --> D1[Loop Card 1 SLC Driver]
    D --> D2[Loop Card 2 SLC Driver]
    D --> D3[Loop Card N SLC Driver]
    D1 --> L1a[SLC Loop 1 Outbound]
    L1a --> L1b[Addressable Optical Smoke Detector 001]
    L1b --> L1c[Addressable Heat Detector 002]
    L1c --> L1d[Addressable MCP 003]
    L1d --> L1e[Addressable Monitor Module 004]
    L1e --> L1f[SLC Loop 1 Return Class A]
    L1f --> D1
    D2 --> L2a[SLC Loop 2 Class B Outbound Only]
    L2a --> L2b[Addressable Beam Detector 101]
    L2b --> L2c[Addressable Aspirating Detector 102]
    L2c --> L2d[EOL Resistor]
    D --> E1[Cause and Effect Matrix Engine]
    E1 --> N1[Sounder Base 1]
    E1 --> N2[Addressable Sounder Strobe 2]
    E1 --> N3[Control Module for Fire Damper]
    E1 --> N4[Control Module for AHU Shutdown]
    D --> F1[Event Log Printer or Network Node]
    D --> F2[LCD Display and Operator Panel]
    D --> F3[Remote Programming PC via RS485 or Ethernet]
```

### 4.3 Decision-Flow Diagram for System Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[New Project Fire Alarm Requirement] --> Q1{Building height and occupant load?}
    Q1 -->|Small low rise under 15m| Q2{Number of devices under 30?}
    Q1 -->|High rise or large complex| Q3{Does phased evacuation apply?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Add[Select Addressable System]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Budget critical?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Conv[Select Conventional System with RIP]
    Q4 -->|No| Add
    Q2 -->|Yes| Conv
    Q2 -->|No| Q5{Is per device identification required?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Add
    Q5 -->|No| Conv
    Conv --> End1[Deliver Conventional Design per IS 2189]
    Add --> End2[Deliver Addressable Design per IS 2189 plus NFPA 72]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**  
*Define an addressable fire alarm system. List any two advantages it offers over a conventional system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
An addressable fire alarm system is one in which every initiating device (smoke detector, heat detector, MCP) is assigned a unique digital address and connected to the FACP via a common Signaling Line Circuit (SLC) loop. The FACP polls each device individually and identifies the exact location and nature of the alarm.  
*Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):*
1. Device-level identification instead of zone-level — enables rapid pinpointing of fire location.
2. Analog value transmission (e.g., obscuration %, temperature °C) — enables alarm verification and reduces false alarms.
3. Class A loop redundancy — single cable break does not disable detection.
4. Remote diagnostics and maintenance — drift compensation, sensitivity adjustment.

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**  
*State the maximum number of detectors permitted per zone in a conventional fire alarm system as per IS 2189, and the maximum walking search distance within a zone.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Maximum detectors per zone** — *20 detectors* in normal/light hazard occupancies; *10 detectors* in high-risk areas (1 mark).
2. **Maximum search distance** — *30 metres* walking distance from any point in the zone to the zone indicator (1 mark).
3. Both these limits are derived from the assumption that a single trained person should be able to physically locate the source of fire within the zone in a reasonable time (approx. 1 minute per the IS 2189 philosophy). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

#### ➤ QUESTION A (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working principle of an **addressable fire alarm system** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a conventional system on at least **six technical parameters**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A multi-storey office building has **240 addressable devices** distributed over **6 floors**. The FACP supports **127 devices per SLC loop**. The standby current per device is **200 µA at 24 V DC** and the FACP quiescent current is **150 mA**. Calculate:  
   (i) The number of SLC loops required.  
   (ii) The total standby current.  
   (iii) The required battery capacity in Ah for **24 hours** standby plus **15 minutes** alarm, with a **25% derating factor**. Take alarm load as **1.5 A**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

*Architecture and Working (3.5 marks):*  
The addressable fire alarm system consists of (i) the FACP with one or more SLC loop drivers, (ii) the SLC loop (2-core fire-survival cable), (iii) addressable initiating devices (smoke, heat, MCP, monitor modules), and (iv) addressable notification and control modules. Each device has a factory-set or field-set unique address. The FACP continuously polls the SLC in a sequential manner, typically completing one full loop scan in 1–4 seconds. Each device responds with its address and real-time analog value. When a fire signature exceeds the programmed threshold, the FACP identifies the specific device, displays its location text on the LCD, logs the event with timestamp, and activates the appropriate cause-and-effect outputs.

*Comparison Table (3.5 marks — award 0.5 per correctly explained parameter):*

| Parameter | Conventional | Addressable |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Identification | Zone level only | Per-device exact ID |
| Cabling | Multi-core radial | Single 2-core loop |
| Fault tolerance | Single break loses that zone | Class A loop survives break |
| Cost (large install) | Higher | Lower |
| False alarm reduction | Manual verification | Analog alarm verification |
| Maintenance | Walk-and-check | Remote diagnostics |

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Number of SLC loops:**

$$N_{loops} = \left\lceil \frac{240}{127} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.89 \rceil = 2 \text{ loops}$$

**(ii) Total standby current:**

$$I_{device} = 240 \times 200\,\mu\text{A} = 48\,\text{mA} = 0.048\,\text{A}$$

$$I_{standby,total} = 0.048 + 0.150 = 0.198\,\text{A} \approx 200\,\text{mA}$$

**(iii) Battery capacity:**

$$C_{Ah} = 1.25 \times \left( 0.198 \times 24 + 1.5 \times \frac{15}{60} \right)$$

$$C_{Ah} = 1.25 \times (4.752 + 0.375) = 1.25 \times 5.127 = 6.41\,\text{Ah}$$

Selected standard size: **12 V, 7 Ah SLA battery** (2 nos. for 24 V system).

---

#### ➤ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **architecture and operation of a conventional (non-addressable) fire alarm system**. Discuss the role of the **End-of-Line (EOL) resistor** in monitoring line integrity. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An industrial warehouse is to be protected by a conventional fire detection system. The single floor has a total area of **1800 m²**, divided into **three zones** of equal area. Heat detectors with **50 m² coverage each** are to be used. Calculate:  
   (i) The number of heat detectors required per zone.  
   (ii) The total number of detectors for the entire warehouse.  
   (iii) Verify whether the design complies with the **30 m search distance rule** if the warehouse is rectangular with dimensions **60 m × 30 m**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

*Architecture (3 marks):* A conventional system consists of an FACP, multiple Initiating Device Circuits (IDCs) each representing one geographical zone, an End-of-Line resistor at the end of each IDC, Notification Appliance Circuits (NACs) for sounders/strobes, and an FACP fascia with zone LEDs. Each IDC is a radial 2-core cable. Devices are wired in parallel.

*Operation (2 marks):* The FACP maintains a small monitoring voltage. An alarm short-circuits the IDC, causing a current step that the FACP detects and identifies as a zone alarm. NACs are activated.

*EOL Resistor (2 marks):* The EOL resistor (typically $4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$) at the far end of each IDC ensures that the FACP can detect a **break** (open circuit) or **short** (EOL bypassed) condition by sensing the DC voltage at the IDC terminals. Without an EOL, the FACP could not distinguish "healthy line" from "broken line" — both would appear open. The EOL establishes a known current draw during normal supervision.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Detectors per zone:**

Each zone area:

$$A_{zone} = \frac{1800}{3} = 600\,\text{m}^2$$

Detectors per zone:

$$N_{zone} = \left\lceil \frac{600}{50} \right\rceil = \lceil 12 \rceil = 12 \text{ detectors}$$

Since 12 ≤ 20 (IS 2189 limit), the design is acceptable.

**(ii) Total detectors:**

$$N_{total} = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ detectors}$$

**(iii) Search distance verification:**

The longest walking distance from any point to the zone indicator in a 60 m × 30 m rectangular zone would be along the diagonal. But search distance is measured along actual walking paths, not straight lines. For a 60 m × 30 m rectangular zone, the *farthest point from the centre* (where the indicator typically is) is along the long-axis corner:

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{60}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{30}{2}\right)^2}$$

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{900 + 225} = \sqrt{1125} \approx 33.5\,\text{m}$$

Since $33.5\,\text{m} > 30\,\text{m}$, the design **violates** the 30 m search distance rule.

**Remediation:** Divide each of the three zones into two sub-zones (6 zones × 300 m² each) so that the maximum diagonal halves to:

$$d_{max,new} = \sqrt{15^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{450} \approx 21.2\,\text{m} \le 30\,\text{m} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "30 detectors per zone" — the correct IS 2189:2008 limit is **20 detectors in light/normal hazard** and **10 in high hazard**. Markers deduct 1 mark for this error. Also, students often forget the **25% derating factor (1.25)** in battery sizing — a guaranteed 2-mark loss if missed. Always state units and show intermediate steps; partial marking rewards displayed working.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Conventional (Non-Addressable) System** = zone-level identification, radial wiring, simple, low-cost, suitable for small buildings.
- **Addressable (Intelligent) System** = device-level identification, single-loop SLC, supports phased evacuation, lower false alarms, mandatory for high-rise.
- **Maximum detectors per zone (conventional)** = **20** light hazard, **10** high hazard (IS 2189:2008).
- **Maximum search distance** in any zone = **30 m**.
- **Maximum devices per SLC loop** = typically **127** (varies by manufacturer; up to 1016 in some).
- **Class A loop** topology provides cable-break redundancy; mandatory for life-safety per NFPA 72.
- **End-of-Line Resistor (EOL)** is essential in conventional systems for line-integrity supervision.
- **Battery sizing formula** must include the **1.25 derating factor** for aging.
- **Standby duration** = **24 hours**; **Alarm duration** = **15 minutes** (per IS 2189 / NBC 2016).
- **Detector coverage**: smoke = **80 m²**, heat = **50 m²** (smooth ceiling, IS 2189).
- **Detector spacing**: smoke = **9 m**, heat = **7 m** (smooth ceiling, IS 2189).
- **Key analog parameters** transmitted in addressable systems: obscuration in **%/m**, temperature in **°C**.
- **Drift compensation** in addressable detectors automatically adjusts the alarm threshold to account for gradual sensor contamination — significantly reducing false alarms in dusty environments.
- **Cause-and-effect matrix** in addressable systems enables *device-level* outputs (specific fire damper, specific sounder, specific elevator recall).
- **KTU exam keywords to use**: "SLC loop", "polling", "analog value", "Class A topology", "EOL resistor", "IS 2189", "NFPA 72", "phased evacuation", "drift compensation".
- **Comparison matrix** (granularity, cabling, fault tolerance, cost, false alarms, maintenance) is the **most asked** question structure in KTU Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Fire Extinguishments: Basic Concept of Fire Fighting with Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Fire Extinguishments: Basic Concept of Fire Fighting with Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Fire Extinguishment** is the controlled process of suppressing a combustion reaction by disrupting one or more elements of the **Fire Tetrahedron** (Fuel, Oxygen, Heat, and Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction). When any one of these four components is removed or sufficiently reduced below its critical threshold, the combustion process ceases.

**Water as a Fire Fighting Agent** is the most universally employed extinguishing medium due to its exceptional thermophysical properties — notably its high specific heat capacity, exceptionally high latent heat of vaporization, and ability to convert into steam that displaces oxygen. Water primarily extinguishes fire through the **cooling (sensible + latent heat absorption)** mechanism, supplemented by a secondary **smothering (oxygen dilution)** effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus**: This topic directly addresses **Course Outcome CO3** — *Apply the principles of fire extinguishment and select appropriate extinguishing media for different classes of fires.* Understanding water's role is foundational because water is the default medium for **Class A fires** (ordinary combustibles: wood, paper, cloth, rubber).

---

## 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — Foundation Concept

Modern fire science models combustion as a **tetrahedron** (four-sided pyramid) rather than the older "fire triangle." All four faces must be intact for sustained combustion.

$$
\text{Fire Tetrahedron} = \{F, O, H, CCR\}
$$

Where:
- **F** = Fuel (vaporizable combustible material)
- **O** = Oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen, $\geq 15\%$ concentration required for most fuels)
- **H** = Heat (energy above the **ignition temperature** / **activation energy threshold**)
- **CCR** = Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction (self-propagating radical reaction)

**Removal Strategies (the 4 Principles of Extinguishment):**

| Principle | Mechanism | Agent Example |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Cooling** | Absorb heat below ignition temperature | Water, Foam |
| **Smothering** | Exclude / displace oxygen | CO₂, Dry Chemical, Steam |
| **Starvation** | Remove fuel supply | Foam, Closing valves |
| **Chemical Inhibition** | Break chain reaction | Dry Chemical Powder, Halons |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Stove & Saucepan"**
>
> Imagine cooking on a gas stove. Three things keep the flame alive: **(1)** the gas flowing from the nozzle **(2)** the air around it, and **(3)** the spark that started it plus the steady heat it produces. Now imagine pouring a cup of cold water on the flame. What happens?
>
> - The water absorbs the heat **instantly** (cooling — the dominant effect).
> - The water turns to steam, which **pushes the air away** from the flame (smothering).
> - With no heat and no oxygen, the gas has nothing to react with — the flame dies.
>
> This is precisely how water extinguishes a Class A fire. The magic numbers are: each kilogram of water can absorb roughly **2,257 kJ just to turn into steam**, before counting the cooling of the water itself and the heating of the steam.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants of Water (Critical for KTU Numericals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Properties of Water (at 1 atm, 20°C unless noted)**:
> - **Specific heat capacity of water:** $c_w = \mathbf{4.186\ kJ/(kg \cdot K)} = 1.0\ cal/(g\cdot° C)$
> - **Latent heat of vaporization (at 100°C):** $L_v = \mathbf{2{,}257\ kJ/kg} = 540\ cal/g$
> - **Latent heat of fusion (at 0°C):** $L_f = \mathbf{334\ kJ/kg} = 80\ cal/g$
> - **Density:** $\rho = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$
> - **Boiling point:** $\mathbf{100\ °C}$ at 1 atm
> - **Steam specific heat:** $c_s = \mathbf{2.01\ kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$
> - **Expansion ratio (water → steam):** $\mathbf{1 : 1{,}700}$ (1 L of water → 1,700 L of steam at 100°C)
> - **Surface tension:** $\mathbf{0.0728\ N/m}$ at 20°C

These constants are **non-negotiable** in KTU board exam numerical problems. Memorize them precisely.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Water Is the Most Effective Fire Fighting Agent

Water is uniquely suited to fire suppression because of a rare combination of properties:

1. **Highest latent heat of vaporization among common liquids** — when water turns to steam, it absorbs ~2,257 kJ per kg, which is more than 5× the energy needed to heat water from 20°C to 100°C.
2. **High specific heat capacity** — stores large amounts of sensible heat per degree of temperature rise.
3. **Expansion upon vaporization** — 1 m³ of water produces ~1,700 m³ of steam at 100°C, displacing oxygen.
4. **Non-toxic, non-corrosive (in most cases), abundant, and cheap.**
5. **Stable storage** — does not degrade, expire, or require pressurization unlike other agents.

---

## 2.2 Mechanisms of Fire Extinguishment by Water

### Mechanism A — Cooling Effect (Primary, ~80% contribution)

Water absorbs heat in **three sequential stages**:

**Stage 1: Sensible heating of liquid water (from ambient $T_a$ to boiling point 100°C)**

$$
Q_1 = m \cdot c_w \cdot (100 - T_a)
$$

**Stage 2: Phase change — water to steam at 100°C (latent heat absorption)**

$$
Q_2 = m \cdot L_v
$$

**Stage 3: Sensible heating of steam (from 100°C to fire temperature $T_f$)**

$$
Q_3 = m \cdot c_s \cdot (T_f - 100)
$$

**Total heat absorbed per kg of water:**

$$
Q_{total} = m \left[ c_w (100 - T_a) + L_v + c_s (T_f - 100) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Stage 2 dominates**: $L_v = 2{,}257\ kJ/kg$ is roughly **5.4× larger** than the sensible heating term $c_w \cdot (100 - T_a)$ for typical ambient conditions. This is why vaporization is the heart of water's extinguishing power.

### Mechanism B — Smothering Effect (Secondary)

When water vaporizes to steam, it expands **~1,700 times** in volume. This steam:
- Displaces oxygen at the fire's reaction zone.
- Reduces oxygen concentration below the **limiting oxygen concentration (LOC)**, typically **<15%** for most fuels.

### Mechanism C — Emulsification (for Class B oil fires with special additives)

Ordinary water **cannot** extinguish oil fires because oil floats on water. However, with surfactants, water can form an emulsion that cools and seals the oil surface.

### Mechanism D — Dilution Effect (for water-miscible liquids)

For solvents like alcohols, acetone, and glycols, water dilutes the fuel below its **flash point**, making it non-flammable.

---

## 2.3 Classes of Fire and Suitability of Water

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Water Suitable? | Reasoning |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Class A** | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics | ✅ **Excellent** | Cooling + smoldering penetration |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids (oil, petrol, solvents) | ❌ **Dangerous** (plain water) | Water sinks; oil floats and spreads fire |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (LPG, methane) | ❌ **Generally not used** | Risk of explosion |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K) | ❌ **Forbidden** | Reacts violently, may produce H₂ |
| **Class E (K)** | Electrical / Kitchen oils | ❌ **Forbidden** | Electrocution; violent steam explosion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Class D fires + Water = Catastrophe**: Sodium and potassium react with water producing hydrogen gas and intense heat, often causing explosions. Magnesium reacts with water producing flammable hydrogen. **Never** apply water to metal fires.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU numerical is solved using one of these formulas.**

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_1 = m \cdot c_w \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by liquid water | kJ |
| 2 | $Q_2 = m \cdot L_v$ | Latent heat during vaporization | kJ |
| 3 | $Q_3 = m \cdot c_s \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by steam | kJ |
| 4 | $Q_{total} = m[c_w(100-T_a) + L_v + c_s(T_f-100)]$ | **Total heat extracted per kg** | kJ |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{Q_{fire}}{Q_{total}/m}$ | Mass of water required | kg |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{m}{\rho_w}$ | Volume of water required | m³ or L |
| 7 | $Q_{discharge} = K\sqrt{P}$ | Nozzle discharge rate (Hydraulic) | L/min |
| 8 | $Q_{discharge} = C_d \cdot A\sqrt{2gH}$ | Theoretical discharge from orifice | m³/s |
| 9 | $\text{Expansion ratio} = \dfrac{V_{steam}}{V_{water}} \approx 1700$ | Steam volume produced | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Fire load} = \dfrac{Q_{combustion}}{A_{floor}}$ | Heat per unit floor area | kJ/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LaTeX in tables**: All absolute-value or "such-that" bars in the formulas above use `\vert` or `\mid` (or simple parentheses) — never the raw `\|` pipe character — to keep the markdown table parser intact.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The principles above are applied in designing:
- **Automatic sprinkler systems** (NFPA 13) — typically designed at 4–5 L/min·m² density.
- **Fire hydrant networks** — IS 13039 / NFPA 24 compliant.
- **Water mist systems** — high-pressure (40–200 bar) fine droplets for ship engine rooms and tunnels.
- **Fire water storage tanks** — sized for 2–3 hours of fire flow at peak demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Heat Absorbed by 1 kg of Water in a Fire Scenario

**Problem Setup:** A fire compartment has an average flame temperature of **800°C**. Water is applied at an ambient temperature of **20°C**. Calculate the total heat absorbed per kg of water as it heats from 20°C liquid → steam at 100°C → steam at 800°C.

**Step 1 — Sensible heating of water from 20°C to 100°C:**

$$
Q_1 = m \cdot c_w \cdot (T_{boil} - T_{amb})
$$

Substituting $m = 1\ kg$, $c_w = 4.186\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$, $(100 - 20) = 80\ K$:

$$
Q_1 = 1 \times 4.186 \times 80 = 334.88\ kJ
$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of vaporization at 100°C:**

$$
Q_2 = m \cdot L_v = 1 \times 2257 = 2257\ kJ
$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heating of steam from 100°C to 800°C:**

$$
Q_3 = m \cdot c_s \cdot (T_{fire} - T_{boil})
$$

Substituting $c_s = 2.01\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$, $(800 - 100) = 700\ K$:

$$
Q_3 = 1 \times 2.01 \times 700 = 1407\ kJ
$$

**Step 4 — Total heat absorbed per kg:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 334.88 + 2257 + 1407 = 3998.88\ kJ/kg
$$

**Interpretation:** Each kg of water absorbs approximately **3,999 kJ ≈ 4.0 MJ** of heat. Notice that latent heat ($Q_2$) alone accounts for:

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_{total}} = \frac{2257}{3998.88} \times 100\% \approx 56.4\%
$$

This proves that **vaporization is the dominant extinguishing mechanism**, contributing more than half of the total cooling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of Water Required to Extinguish a Fire of Given Heat Release

**Problem Setup:** A wooden pallet stack releases **5,000 kJ** of heat during combustion. If water at 25°C is applied and the fire temperature is 700°C, find the mass of water needed for complete extinguishment (assuming 100% efficiency).

**Step 1 — Compute total heat absorbed per kg of water at fire temperature 700°C:**

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = c_w(100 - T_a) + L_v + c_s(T_f - 100)
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 4.186 \times (100 - 25) + 2257 + 2.01 \times (700 - 100)
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 4.186 \times 75 + 2257 + 2.01 \times 600
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 313.95 + 2257 + 1206 = 3776.95\ kJ/kg
$$

**Step 2 — Required mass of water:**

$$
m_{water} = \frac{Q_{fire}}{Q_{per\ kg}} = \frac{5000}{3776.95}
$$

$$
m_{water} \approx 1.324\ kg
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volume (litres):**

$$
V_{water} = \frac{m}{\rho_w} = \frac{1.324}{1000}\ m^3 = 1.324 \times 10^{-3}\ m^3 \approx 1.324\ L
$$

**Result:** Approximately **1.32 litres** of water absorbs 5,000 kJ of heat (assuming perfect efficiency). Real designs apply a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0**, so design quantity ≈ **2–2.6 L**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Volume Produced and Oxygen Displacement

**Step 1 — Volume of 1 L of water:**

$$
V_{water} = 1\ L = 0.001\ m^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of steam produced at 100°C and 1 atm (using ideal gas approximation):**

One mole of water (18 g) occupies 22.4 L at STP. At 100°C (373 K):

$$
V_{steam} = \frac{m}{\rho_w} \times 1700 \approx 1\ L \times 1700 = 1700\ L
$$

**Step 3 — Oxygen concentration in displaced air:**

If 1 L of water becomes 1,700 L of steam in a sealed 10,000 L compartment:

$$
O_2\ reduction = \frac{1700}{10000} \times 21\% = 3.57\%
$$

New oxygen level = 21% − 3.57% ≈ **17.43%**, still above the LOC for most fuels (~15%). This shows that **cooling is the primary mechanism**; smothering by steam alone is rarely sufficient except in confined spaces.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Water vs Other Agents

| Parameter | **Water** | **Foam** | **CO₂** | **Dry Chemical (ABC)** |
|-----------|-----------|----------|---------|------------------------|
| Primary mechanism | Cooling | Smothering + Cooling | Smothering | Chemical inhibition |
| Heat absorbed per kg | ~3,999 kJ | ~3,000 kJ | Negligible | Negligible |
| Electrical fire safe | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Class B suitability | ❌ No (oil) | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good | ✅ Excellent |
| Storage cost | Very Low | Moderate | High | High |
| Toxicity | None | Low | Asphyxiant | Low |
| Environmental impact | None | Detergent | GHG | Particulate |
| Typical application | Class A | Class A & B | Electrical, Class B | All classes |

---

## 3.5 Discharge Through a Fire Hose Nozzle

**Discharge rate formula** (empirical, widely used in hydraulics):

$$
Q = K\sqrt{P}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = discharge in L/min
- $K$ = nozzle coefficient (typical: $K \approx 30$ for standard 12 mm nozzle)
- $P$ = nozzle pressure in kPa (typically 700 kPa = 7 bar)

**Example:** At nozzle pressure $P = 700\ kPa$ with $K = 30$:

$$
Q = 30 \times \sqrt{700} = 30 \times 26.46 \approx 793.7\ L/min
$$

Theoretical orifice equation for cross-validation:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A \sqrt{2gH}
$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (0.95–0.98)
- $A$ = nozzle area
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$
- $H$ = pressure head in metres
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the four principles of fire extinguishment. Explain how water primarily extinguishes a Class A fire.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four principles of fire extinguishment correspond to removing one element of the **Fire Tetrahedron**:

1. **Cooling** — removing heat below the ignition temperature
2. **Smothering** — excluding oxygen
3. **Starvation** — removing fuel
4. **Chemical inhibition** — interrupting the chain reaction

For a **Class A fire** (wood, paper, textiles), water extinguishes primarily by **cooling**: as water absorbs heat and vaporizes (latent heat = 2,257 kJ/kg), it cools the fuel below its ignition temperature. The secondary smothering effect of the generated steam (~1,700× volume expansion) displaces oxygen around the fire, reinforcing extinguishment.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three properties of water that make it an effective fire fighting agent. Why is water not used on Class D (metal) fires?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three properties making water effective:**

1. **High latent heat of vaporization** ($L_v = 2,257\ kJ/kg$) — absorbs enormous heat during phase change.
2. **High specific heat capacity** ($c_w = 4.186\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$) — absorbs sensible heat efficiently.
3. **Steam expansion** (~1,700× volume) — displaces oxygen, providing a secondary smothering effect. It is also non-toxic, abundant, and inexpensive.

**Why water fails on Class D (metal) fires:** Reactive metals like **sodium, potassium, and magnesium** react violently with water, producing **hydrogen gas** and intense heat, often resulting in explosions:

$$
2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2 \uparrow + \text{Heat}
$$

For Class D fires, special **dry powder agents** (e.g., sodium chloride, graphite, or copper-based powders) are used instead.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, students must answer **one full question** from the choice offered. Each question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) and (b), typically **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanisms of fire extinguishment by water**, with a neat sketch of the heat absorption process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire involving a stack of wooden pallets in a warehouse generates **12,000 kJ** of heat. Water at **25°C** is applied and the flame temperature is **750°C**. Calculate:
1. The **total heat absorbed per kg** of water.
2. The **mass of water** required (assuming 100% efficiency).
3. The **design quantity** of water using a safety factor of **1.5**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Mechanisms [7 marks]

Water extinguishes fire through four mechanisms, ranked by contribution:

**1. Cooling Effect (Primary — ~80%) [3 marks]**

Water undergoes three heat-absorption stages:
- **Sensible heating** from ambient $T_a$ to 100°C: $Q_1 = m c_w (100 - T_a)$
- **Latent heat of vaporization** at 100°C: $Q_2 = m L_v = 2257\ kJ/kg$
- **Sensible heating of steam** from 100°C to fire temperature $T_f$: $Q_3 = m c_s (T_f - 100)$

**2. Smothering Effect (Secondary — ~15%) [1.5 marks]**

Steam produced expands ~1,700×, displacing oxygen and reducing its concentration below the LOC (~15%).

**3. Emulsification & Dilution [1.5 marks]**

For Class B water-miscible fuels (alcohols, glycols), water dilutes the fuel below its flash point. For oil fires, surfactants in foam-water mixes can form emulsions.

**Sketch:** (See Section 4.2 of these notes for the full block flow diagram.)

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Total heat absorbed per kg:**

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = c_w(100 - T_a) + L_v + c_s(T_f - 100)
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 4.186(100 - 25) + 2257 + 2.01(750 - 100)
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 4.186 \times 75 + 2257 + 2.01 \times 650
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 313.95 + 2257 + 1306.5 = 3877.45\ kJ/kg
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water required:**

$$
m = \frac{Q_{fire}}{Q_{per\ kg}} = \frac{12000}{3877.45} \approx 3.095\ kg
$$

**Step 3 — Design quantity with safety factor 1.5:**

$$
m_{design} = 1.5 \times 3.095 \approx 4.64\ kg \approx 4.64\ L
$$

**Final Answers:**
1. Heat absorbed per kg ≈ **3,877.45 kJ/kg**
2. Mass of water required ≈ **3.10 kg**
3. Design quantity ≈ **4.64 L**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **fire tetrahedron** with a labelled diagram. Describe how each of its four elements can be removed using water, foam, CO₂, and dry chemical powder respectively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sprinkler head discharges water at a nozzle pressure of **5 bar** through a nozzle with coefficient **K = 28**. Calculate:
1. The **discharge rate** in L/min.
2. If this rate must absorb **6,500 kJ/min** of heat from a Class A fire at 700°C (water at 30°C), determine whether the discharge is **sufficient** (assume 100% heat transfer). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Fire Tetrahedron [7 marks]

**Fire Tetrahedron Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid diagram.)

**Four Elements and Their Removal [4 marks, 1 each]:**

| Element | Agent | Mechanism |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| **Heat** | Water | Cooling via latent heat absorption |
| **Oxygen** | CO₂, Foam | Displacement / barrier |
| **Fuel** | Foam, water spray | Cooling below flash point, blanketing |
| **Chain Reaction** | Dry Chemical Powder (ABC) | Interrupts free-radical propagation |

**Identification of Class A suitability of water [3 marks]:**

Water's high $L_v$ makes it ideal for Class A fires (wood, paper, cloth). The 1,700× steam expansion contributes to smothering, while the cooling penetrates smoldering embers.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to kPa:**

$$
P = 5\ bar = 5 \times 100 = 500\ kPa
$$

**Step 2 — Discharge rate using $Q = K\sqrt{P}$:**

$$
Q = 28 \times \sqrt{500} = 28 \times 22.36 \approx 626.1\ L/min
$$

**Step 3 — Heat absorbed per kg of water at $T_f = 700°C$, $T_a = 30°C$:**

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 4.186(100 - 30) + 2257 + 2.01(700 - 100)
$$

$$
Q_{per\ kg} = 293.02 + 2257 + 1206 = 3756.02\ kJ/kg
$$

**Step 4 — Heat absorbed per minute:**

Since 1 L of water ≈ 1 kg:

$$
Q_{absorbed} = 626.1\ L/min \times 3756.02\ kJ/L = 2{,}351{,}756\ kJ/min
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to required 6,500 kJ/min:**

$$
Q_{absorbed} = 2.35 \times 10^6\ kJ/min \gg 6.5 \times 10^3\ kJ/min
$$

**Result:** The discharge is **vastly sufficient** — by a factor of ~362×. The sprinkler easily absorbs the required 6,500 kJ/min.

**Final Answers:**
1. Discharge rate ≈ **626.1 L/min**
2. Yes, the discharge is **more than sufficient** to absorb 6,500 kJ/min.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the steam sensible heat term ($Q_3$)** — many students compute only $Q_1 + Q_2$, losing 1–2 marks. Even if $Q_3$ is numerically smaller than $Q_2$, KTU examiners award marks for **complete derivation**.
> 2. **Confusing units** — $L_v$ is sometimes given in kJ/kg, sometimes in cal/g. KTU expects **kJ/kg**. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert the safety factor** — if the problem asks for **design quantity**, students often forget to multiply by the safety factor (1.5–2.0). This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Wrong fire-class application** — saying "water is good for all fires" is a critical conceptual error. Always specify **Class A** as the primary applicability and explicitly state water's incompatibility with **Class B (oil), Class D (metal), and Class E (electrical)** fires.
> 5. **Missing the 1,700× expansion** — for steam displacement questions, students often skip the volume-expansion fact, losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing fire triangle instead of tetrahedron** — KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly teaches the tetrahedron model; using the older triangle loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron** = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Chain Reaction. Remove any one to extinguish.
- ✅ **Four Principles of Extinguishment**: Cooling, Smothering, Starvation, Chemical Inhibition.
- ✅ **Water is best for Class A fires only** — never use on Class B (oil), Class D (metal), or Class E (electrical).
- ✅ **Latent heat of vaporization** $L_v = 2,257\ kJ/kg$ — the **single most important constant** for water fire fighting.
- ✅ **Specific heat of water** $c_w = 4.186\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$; **steam** $c_s = 2.01\ kJ/(kg\cdot K)$.
- ✅ **Total heat absorbed per kg** = $c_w(100 - T_a) + 2257 + 2.01(T_f - 100)$ — memorize this exact formula.
- ✅ **Steam expands 1,700×** — this gives water its secondary smothering effect.
- ✅ **Nozzle discharge formula** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ — used for sprinkler/hydrant calculations.
- ✅ **Latent heat dominates**: vaporization accounts for **>50%** of total cooling — always highlight this in descriptive answers.
- ✅ **Always apply a safety factor of 1.5–2.0** to design quantities in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Sodium + Water = Explosion**: $2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2\uparrow$ — never apply water to alkali metal fires.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 mapping**: This topic maps to **CO3** of OECHT835 — *Apply principles of fire extinguishment and select appropriate extinguishing media*.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Principles of Fire Extinguishments: Basic Concept of Fire Fighting with Water**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Carbon Dioxide

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** is a colorless, odorless, electrically non-conductive, and chemically inert (under normal conditions) gaseous fire-extinguishing agent, with the chemical formula **CO₂** and a molecular weight of **44.01 g/mol**. In fire engineering, CO₂ is classified as a **gaseous clean agent** and is one of the oldest and most widely deployed **fire suppressants** in the world. It extinguishes fire primarily through the mechanism of **oxygen dilution (asphyxiation)** and secondary **cooling** via the Joule–Thomson effect during rapid adiabatic expansion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Fire Suppression Systems):**
> Carbon Dioxide is studied as a **gaseous fire extinguishing agent** under the classification of *clean agents* and *chemical/physical action extinguishants*. Students must master its physical/chemical properties, extinguishing mechanism, design concentrations, total flooding and local application systems, and **NFPA 12 / IS 6385** code compliance for installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clean Agent Definition (KTU):** A fire-extinguishing agent that is *electrically non-conductive*, *volatile*, *non-toxic* (within design limits), and leaves **no residue** upon discharge — preventing damage to sensitive electronics, archival documents, and precision machinery.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Blanket" Principle

Imagine a **transparent, invisible blanket** that you can throw over a burning candle. The blanket doesn't soak up the wax, doesn't put water on the flame, and most importantly — **you can remove it later without any trace**. Instead, it simply **pushes the oxygen away** so the candle cannot find the air it needs to keep burning. The flame suffocates and dies.

This is exactly how **Carbon Dioxide** works in a fire protection system:

| Real-World Analogy | Fire-Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The invisible blanket | High-pressure CO₂ gas discharged from a cylinder |
| Oxygen pushed away | Reduction of ambient O₂ concentration below 14% (fuel's LOC) |
| Candle suffocates | Combustion triangle is broken at the **Oxidizer leg** |
| Blanket leaves no trace | CO₂ disperses naturally — **zero residue damage** |
| Pulling blanket away = reopening space | Automatic ventilation post-discharge |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** *"CO₂ — Cuts Oxygen, Zero damage."* This single line covers both the mechanism and the principal engineering advantage.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical & Chemical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following values are **KTU-high-yield constants**. Memorize them for Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | CO₂ | — |
| Molecular Weight (M) | **44.01** | g/mol |
| Boiling Point (at 1 atm) | **−78.5** (194.7 K) | °C |
| Critical Temperature (Tc) | **31.0** | °C |
| Critical Pressure (Pc) | **73.8** (7.38 MPa) | bar |
| Density (gas at 21 °C, 1 atm) | **1.836** | kg/m³ |
| Density (liquid at 15 °C, ~50 bar) | ≈ **770** | kg/m³ |
| Specific Heat Ratio (γ = Cp/Cv) | **1.30** | — |
| Vapor Pressure at 21 °C | **57.23** | bar |
| ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) | **0** | — |
| GWP (Global Warming Potential) | **1** | — |
| Auto-ignition Temperature | Non-flammable | — |
| Lethal Concentration (LC₅₀, 30 min) | > 20% by volume | — |

---

## 1.4 Classification of CO₂ in Fire Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Question Favourite:** *"Classify fire extinguishing agents. Where does CO₂ fit in?"*

1. **By Physical State Upon Discharge:**
   - **Gaseous agent** (stored as high-pressure liquid, discharged as gas + snow)
2. **By Extinguishing Action:**
   - **Physical (Smothering) action** — *primarily* smothers fire
   - **Chemical action** — *negligible*; not a chain-breaker like Halon
3. **By Application Method:**
   - **Total Flooding System** (closed rooms — server farms, archives)
   - **Local Application System** (open or semi-open hazards — paint booths, generator pits)
4. **By Cleanliness:**
   - **Clean agent** — residue-free, electronics-safe
5. **By Toxicity:**
   - **Low toxicity** at design concentration (< 5%), but **high asphyxiation risk** above 10%

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Combustion Tetrahedron & Where CO₂ Attacks

Modern fire science replaces the classic **fire triangle** (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen) with the **fire tetrahedron** (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen + Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction). CO₂ does **not** attack the chemical chain reaction leg — that is the domain of **Halons** and **dry chemical powders**. Instead, CO₂ strikes the **Oxidizer (O₂)** leg with overwhelming dominance.

$$\text{Fire} = \big[\text{Fuel}\big] + \big[\text{Heat}\big] + \big[\text{O}_2\big] + \big[\text{Chain Reaction}\big]$$

When CO₂ is discharged, the **partial pressure of oxygen** in the protected enclosure drops below the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** of the fuel:

$$P_{O_2}^{final} < P_{O_2}^{LOC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Combustion cannot be sustained}$$

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Extinguishment — Three Sequential Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Likeliness:** *"Explain the extinguishing mechanism of CO₂ with all three modes of action."*

### Mode 1 — Primary: **Oxygen Displacement (Smothering)**
- Flooding the hazard zone with **34% to 75% CO₂ by volume** reduces O₂ from 21% to **≤ 14%**.
- Most flammable liquids and solids cannot sustain combustion below **14% O₂** (typical fuel LOC).

### Mode 2 — Secondary: **Cooling via Joule–Thomson Effect**
- Liquid CO₂ stored at ≈ **57 bar** and ≈ **−18 °C** undergoes **rapid adiabatic expansion** when released through the discharge nozzle.
- The **Joule–Thomson coefficient** (μJT) for CO₂ at room temperature is approximately **+1.10 K/bar** — meaning it *cools upon expansion*.
- A fraction of the liquid also flashes to **dry-ice snow** at −78.5 °C, providing localized cryogenic cooling.

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{V}{C_p}(T\alpha - 1) \approx +1.10 \text{ K/bar (for CO}_2\text{)}$$

### Mode 3 — Tertiary: **Thermal Radiation Absorption**
- CO₂ is a strong absorber of infrared radiation in the **2.7 µm, 4.3 µm, and 15 µm bands**.
- It re-radiates this heat more uniformly back to the surroundings, lowering peak flame temperatures.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Carbon Dioxide Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essential.** Pay special attention to units — they are the most common mark-loss point in KTU valuation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of CO₂ required | $m = V \cdot c \cdot \rho$ | kg | V = volume (m³), c = concentration (vol %), ρ = CO₂ density |
| 2 | Actual design mass (with safety factor) | $m_{design} = m \cdot SF$ | kg | SF = 1.20 to 1.40 per NFPA 12 |
| 3 | CO₂ cylinder gas volume (at NTP) | $V_g = m / \rho_g$ | m³ | ρg = 1.836 kg/m³ at 21 °C |
| 4 | Liquid CO₂ fill density (cylinder) | $\rho_{fill} = m_{liq}/V_{cyl}$ | kg/L | NFPA limit: **≤ 0.68 kg/L** at 15 °C |
| 5 | Discharge time (NFPA 12 total flooding) | $t_d \leq 60$ s | s | For Class A & B hazards |
| 6 | Local application discharge time | $t_d \leq 30$ s | s | Deep-seated fires: extended up to 3 min |
| 7 | Minimum design concentration (Class A) | $c \geq 50\%$ | vol % | Heptane: 65%; Ethanol: 60% |
| 8 | Minimum design concentration (Class B) | $c \geq 34\%$ | vol % | Depends on fuel LOC |
| 9 | Oxygen concentration post-flooding | $O_2^{final} = 0.21(1 - c)$ | vol fraction | Assuming complete mixing |
| 10 | Number of cylinders | $N = m_{design}/m_{cyl}$ | integer | Round up to next integer |
| 11 | Nozzle area (for orifice flow) | $A_n = \dot{m}/(C_d \sqrt{2\rho \Delta P})$ | m² | C_d ≈ 0.85 to 0.95 |
| 12 | Pipe flow (Darcy–Weisbach) | $\Delta P = f \frac{L}{D}\frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | f = friction factor |
| 13 | Joule–Thomson cooling | $\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P$ | K | μJT = +1.10 K/bar |
| 14 | Specific heat ratio (isentropic flow) | $\gamma = C_p/C_v = 1.30$ | — | For CO₂ gas |
| 15 | Critical flow (choked orifice) | $\dot{m}_{max} = A P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{RT_0}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | kg/s | For choked discharge |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall:** When writing |ΔP| or |T| in markdown tables, always use **$\vert$** or **$\mid$** — never the literal pipe symbol. The vertical bar `|` inside a table row **breaks the markdown table parser** and you lose formatting.

## 2.4 Design Concentration by Fuel Class — KTU Reference Table

| Fuel / Hazard | Minimum CO₂ Design Conc. (vol %) | Resulting O₂ (vol %) |
|---|---|---|
| Class A — Surface (wood, paper) | **50** | 10.5 |
| Class A — Deep-seated | **65** | 7.35 |
| Class B — Heptane | **65** | 7.35 |
| Class B — Ethanol | **60** | 8.4 |
| Class B — Gasoline | **66** | 7.14 |
| Class B — JP-4 (jet fuel) | **64** | 7.56 |
| Class C — Energized electrical | **50** | 10.5 |
| Paint booth (Class I/II liquids) | **65** | 7.35 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These values are not arbitrary — they are derived experimentally as **(Limiting Oxygen Concentration + 5 to 10% safety margin)**. For a typical hydrocarbon fuel with LOC ≈ 12%, the design concentration is set at ≈ **50–65%**.

## 2.5 Total Flooding vs. Local Application — Conceptual Distinction

| Feature | Total Flooding | Local Application |
|---|---|---|
| Hazard enclosure | **Sealed/closed** room | **Open or partial** hazard |
| CO₂ distribution | Fills entire enclosure uniformly | Concentrated on the burning surface |
| Typical concentration | 50–75 vol % | High local density |
| Discharge time | ≤ 60 s | ≤ 30 s (surface), up to 180 s (deep-seated) |
| Required detection | Automatic detection mandatory | Manual or automatic |
| Hazard type | Server rooms, archives, switchgear | Paint dip tanks, quench tanks, oil pits |
| NFPA 12 reference | Chapter 4 | Chapter 5 |
