# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Mass of CO₂ for Total Flooding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked derivation — KTU 14-mark standard.** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. Read each transition row.

### Problem Setup
You are tasked to design a **CO₂ total flooding system** for a **server farm** of dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 4 m (height)**. The room is reasonably tight (tight-seal class II). Design concentration per NFPA 12 for Class C (electrical) hazard is **c = 50%**. Compute the **minimum mass of CO₂** required.

### Step 1 — Volume of Protected Enclosure

$$V_{room} = L \times B \times H$$

$$V_{room} = 10 \times 8 \times 4$$

$$\boxed{V_{room} = 320 \text{ m}^3}$$

### Step 2 — Express Required CO₂ Volume as a Function of Room Volume

For a *total flooding* system, the required CO₂ **volume at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm)** is:

$$V_{CO_2} = V_{room} \times \frac{c}{1 - c}$$

**Justification:** When CO₂ occupies a volume fraction c of a sealed enclosure, the remaining (1 − c) fraction is air (and other inerts). The CO₂ occupies a *real physical volume* equal to:

$$V_{CO_2} = V_{room} \cdot c \quad \text{(apparent)}$$

But because some residual air remains, the equivalent *free gas volume* at NTP that we must release is the ratio:

$$V_{CO_2}^{NTP} = V_{room} \times \frac{c}{1 - c}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{CO_2}^{NTP} = 320 \times \frac{0.50}{1 - 0.50} = 320 \times \frac{0.50}{0.50}$$

$$V_{CO_2}^{NTP} = 320 \times 1 = 320 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (at NTP)}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Gas Volume to Mass Using CO₂ Density at NTP

$$\rho_{CO_2}^{NTP} = 1.836 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \text{(at 21 °C, 1 atm — close to NTP)}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = V_{CO_2}^{NTP} \times \rho_{CO_2}^{NTP}$$

$$m_{CO_2} = 320 \times 1.836$$

$$\boxed{m_{CO_2} = 587.52 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Safety Factor per NFPA 12

For a *Class II enclosure* (small leakage openings), NFPA 12 recommends a safety factor:

$$SF = 1.20 \quad \text{(tight enclosure with no compensation)}$$

$$m_{design} = m_{CO_2} \times SF = 587.52 \times 1.20$$

$$\boxed{m_{design} = 705.02 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 5 — Determine Number of Cylinders

Assume each cylinder contains **45 kg of liquid CO₂** (standard commercial size).

$$N = \frac{m_{design}}{m_{cyl}} = \frac{705.02}{45} = 15.67$$

Round **up** to the next whole integer:

$$\boxed{N = 16 \text{ cylinders}}$$

### Step 6 — Verify Fill Density Compliance (NFPA 12)

Each cylinder has an internal water volume of **≈ 68 L**.

$$\rho_{fill} = \frac{45 \text{ kg}}{68 \text{ L}} = 0.662 \text{ kg/L}$$

Check against NFPA 12 maximum:

$$\rho_{fill} = 0.662 \text{ kg/L} \leq 0.68 \text{ kg/L} \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 7 — Final Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Room volume | Vroom | 320 | m³ |
| Design concentration | c | 50 | vol % |
| Theoretical CO₂ mass | mCO₂ | 587.52 | kg |
| Safety factor | SF | 1.20 | — |
| Design mass | mdesign | 705.02 | kg |
| Cylinders required | N | 16 | nos |
| Fill density | ρfill | 0.662 | kg/L |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Time and Orifice Sizing for CO₂ Nozzle

### Step 1 — Define Required Discharge Flow Rate

NFPA 12 mandates discharge in ≤ 60 s for total flooding.

$$\dot{m} = \frac{m_{design}}{t_d} = \frac{705.02}{60} = 11.75 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Choked-Orifice (Critical) Flow Equation

For a gas discharging from a high-pressure reservoir (P₀ ≈ 57 bar, T₀ ≈ 255 K) through a converging nozzle into a much lower back-pressure (≈ 1 bar), flow is **choked**. The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_0} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$

For CO₂: γ = 1.30, R = 188.92 J/(kg·K), P₀ = 57 × 10⁵ Pa, T₀ = 255 K.

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Isentropic Exponent Term

$$\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}} = \left(\frac{2}{2.30}\right)^{\frac{2.30}{0.30}} = (0.8696)^{7.667}$$

$$\boxed{(0.8696)^{7.667} \approx 0.3458}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Coefficient Term

$$\frac{\gamma}{R T_0} = \frac{1.30}{188.92 \times 255} = \frac{1.30}{48{,}174.6} = 2.698 \times 10^{-5} \text{ (Pa·K)}^{-1}$$

$$\sqrt{2.698 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3458} = \sqrt{9.33 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.055 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Required Nozzle Area

Rearranging for Aₙ (assume Cd = 0.90):

$$A_n = \frac{\dot{m}}{C_d \cdot P_0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{RT_0}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}}$$

$$A_n = \frac{11.75}{0.90 \times 57 \times 10^{5} \times 3.055 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$A_n = \frac{11.75}{15{,}672.6} = 7.498 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_n \approx 750 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d_n \approx 30.9 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical design note:** The actual nozzle size is selected from the manufacturer's catalog (typically 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 mm). Choose the nearest **standard equal-to-or-greater** size — here, a **32 mm** orifice would be acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for CO₂ System Selection

| Step | Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the hazard enclosed? | Consider **Total Flooding** | Go to Step 2 |
| 2 | Is the hazard localized (e.g., dip tank)? | Consider **Local Application** | Re-evaluate hazard classification |
| 3 | Are occupants present normally? | **Avoid CO₂** — use FM-200 / Novec 1230 | CO₂ acceptable with safety lock-out |
| 4 | Are sensitive electronics involved? | **CO₂ is safe** (non-conductive, residue-free) | CO₂ still applicable |
| 5 | Is the fire deep-seated (Class A)? | Use **65% concentration** + extended discharge | **50% concentration** is sufficient |
| 6 | Is the room adequately sealed? | Add leakage compensation per NFPA 12 | Improve sealing first |

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Standby --> DetectionTriggered: Smoke or Heat Detected
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*List the three mechanisms by which Carbon Dioxide extinguishes a fire. Which is the dominant mode?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carbon dioxide extinguishes fire through **three sequential mechanisms**:

1. **Oxygen Displacement (Smothering)** — Primary mechanism. Flooding the enclosure with 50–75% CO₂ reduces ambient oxygen below the fuel's Limiting Oxygen Concentration (≈ 14%), starving the combustion reaction.
2. **Cooling via Joule–Thomson Effect** — When liquid CO₂ at ≈ 57 bar expands through the discharge nozzle to atmospheric pressure, it cools at μJT ≈ +1.10 K/bar, partially forming dry-ice snow at −78.5 °C.
3. **Thermal Radiation Absorption** — CO₂ absorbs infrared radiation strongly at 2.7 µm, 4.3 µm, and 15 µm bands, lowering flame temperature.

The **dominant mechanism is oxygen displacement**, which alone is sufficient to extinguish most surface fires within the design concentration envelope.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is Carbon Dioxide classified as a "clean agent"? Mention two applications where this property is critical.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CO₂ is termed a **clean agent** because it is:
- **Electrically non-conductive**
- **Leaves zero residue** upon discharge (gas disperses harmlessly)
- **Non-corrosive** to metals
- **Non-damaging** to delicate surfaces

Two critical applications:
1. **Server farms and data centers** — residue-free discharge prevents damage to PCB boards, hard drives, and optical storage.
2. **Library archives and heritage galleries** — protects irreplaceable paper, manuscripts, and paintings from water damage (unlike sprinkler systems).

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A — Part (a)

**Given:**
- L = 6 m, B = 5 m, H = 4 m
- c = 50% (Class C)
- ρCO₂ = 1.836 kg/m³ (NTP)
- SF = 1.20
- Cylinder = 45 kg, Vcyl = 68 L
- ρfill, max = 0.68 kg/L

**Step 1 — Room Volume:**

$$V = 6 \times 5 \times 4 = 120 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ Volume at NTP:**

$$V_{CO_2} = V \times \frac{c}{1-c} = 120 \times \frac{0.50}{0.50} = 120 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Mass:**

$$m_{CO_2} = V_{CO_2} \times \rho_{CO_2} = 120 \times 1.836 = 220.32 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Design Mass (with SF):**

$$m_{design} = 220.32 \times 1.20 = 264.38 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Number of Cylinders:**

$$N = \frac{264.38}{45} = 5.88 \rightarrow \boxed{N = 6 \text{ cylinders}}$$

**Step 6 — Fill Density Check:**

$$\rho_{fill} = \frac{45}{68} = 0.662 \text{ kg/L} \leq 0.68 \text{ kg/L} \quad \checkmark$$

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Room volume | 120 m³ |
| CO₂ mass (theoretical) | 220.32 kg |
| Design mass | 264.38 kg |
| Cylinders | 6 nos |
| Fill density | 0.662 kg/L (compliant) |

---

### Solution to Question A — Part (b)

**Working of CO₂ Total Flooding System:**

The system remains in **standby** mode with all cylinders pressurized. Upon detection of fire by **smoke/heat detectors**, the sequence is:

1. **Detection:** Smoke or heat detector senses fire → sends signal to **control panel**.
2. **Pre-discharge alarm:** Alarm bells/strobes activate → gives **30-second evacuation delay** (for occupied spaces).
3. **Discharge command:** Control panel energizes the **solenoid valve** on the CO₂ cylinder bank.
4. **Discharge:** Liquid CO₂ flows through the **manifold → pilot-operated valves → distribution pipework → nozzles**. Discharge completes in **≤ 60 s**.
5. **Soak period:** CO₂ is held in the sealed enclosure for **10–20 minutes** to prevent re-ignition.
6. **Reset:** Manual reset, ventilation, and re-arming of the system.

**Difference between Total Flooding and Local Application:**

| Parameter | Total Flooding | Local Application |
|---|---|---|
| Hazard enclosure | Sealed room | Open/semi-open hazard |
| Discharge pattern | Distributed uniformly | Concentrated at fire source |
| Concentration basis | By volume of room | By surface area of hazard |
| Detector requirement | Mandatory | Mandatory |
| Discharge time | ≤ 60 s | ≤ 30 s (surface); ≤ 180 s (deep-seated) |
| Examples | Server rooms, archives | Paint tanks, oil pits |

---

### Solution to Question B — Part (a)

**Physical Properties of CO₂:**

- **Physical state:** Colorless, odorless gas (heavier than air, ρrel ≈ 1.5)
- **Molecular weight:** 44.01 g/mol
- **Boiling point:** −78.5 °C at 1 atm (sublimes from solid)
- **Storage:** Pressurized liquid at 57.23 bar (≈ 850 psi) at 21 °C
- **Density:** 1.836 kg/m³ (gas, NTP); 770 kg/m³ (liquid, 15 °C)
- **Specific heat ratio γ:** 1.30
- **Electrical conductivity:** Non-conductive
- **Odor:** None (cannot be detected by human senses — a hazard!)
- **ODP:** 0; **GWP:** 1

**Chemical Properties:**

- **Inert under normal conditions** (does not react with most fuels)
- **Reacts with strong reducing agents** at high temperatures (e.g., Mg burns in CO₂)
- **Dissolves in water** to form carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) — minor corrosion concern
- **No chain-breaking action** on combustion chemistry (unlike Halons)
- **Non-flammable** — will not itself support combustion

**Why CO₂ is Preferred over Water for Electrical Fires:**

1. **Non-conductive** — water contains dissolved ions and conducts electricity, posing severe **electrocution risk** to firefighters and short-circuit hazards to equipment. CO₂, being a non-polar molecule, is **electrically non-conductive**.
2. **No residue / no damage** — water damages PCBs, transformers, and insulation; CO₂ evaporates completely.
3. **No short-circuit risk** — water on energized circuits causes arcing, flashover, and explosion.
4. **Rapid discharge** — CO₂ discharges in ≤ 60 s with quick extinguishment.
5. **No corrosion** — water initiates oxidation of copper/aluminum busbars; CO₂ does not.

---

### Solution to Question B — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Dip tank: 3 m × 2 m × 1.5 m
- Fuel: Heptane (Class IB)
- Application: Local
- Discharge time: 60 s

**Step 1 — Application Rate for Heptane (NFPA 12 Table):**

For **Class IB liquids (heptane, acetone)**, the local application rate is:

$$R = 13.4 \text{ kg/m}^2/\text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Hazard Surface Area:**

For a dip tank, the effective area is the **top opening** (where vapors ignite):

$$A_{eff} = L \times B = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total Mass for 60 s Discharge:**

$$m_{total} = R \times A_{eff} \times \frac{t_d}{60} = 13.4 \times 6 \times 1 = 80.4 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Number of Nozzles:**

Each nozzle discharges **6 kg/min**.

$$N_{nozzles} = \frac{80.4}{6} = 13.4 \rightarrow \boxed{N = 14 \text{ nozzles}}$$

**Step 5 — Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Application rate (heptane) | 13.4 kg/m²/min |
| Effective area | 6 m² |
| Total CO₂ mass | 80.4 kg |
| Discharge time | 60 s |
| Nozzle flow rate | 6 kg/min |
| Number of nozzles | 14 |

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on CO₂ Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the (1 − c) denominator** — students write $V_{CO_2} = V \times c$ instead of $V \times c/(1-c)$. This single error costs **3–4 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Omitting the safety factor** — NFPA 12 mandates SF = 1.20 to 1.40. A "perfectly sealed room" assumption is unrealistic.
> 3. **Wrong unit on density** — ρCO₂ = **1.836 kg/m³** is for NTP gas, not liquid (770 kg/m³). Mixing these is a **disqualifying error**.
> 4. **Missing fill-density check** — NFPA 12 caps liquid CO₂ fill at **0.68 kg/L** at 15 °C. Exceeding this risks cylinder rupture.
> 5. **Ignoring discharge time** — NFPA 12 mandates ≤ 60 s for total flooding. Nozzle sizing must satisfy this.
> 6. **Not stating the discharge state** — explicitly mention that CO₂ exits the nozzle as a **gas + dry-ice snow mixture**, not pure liquid.
> 7. **Confusing γ for CO₂ with air** — γCO₂ = 1.30, **not** 1.40 (which is air).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your final-night revision sheet for the CO₂ portion of Module 4.**

### ⚡ Core Concept Anchors
- [x] CO₂ extinguishes via **oxygen displacement** (primary), **Joule–Thomson cooling** (secondary), and **radiation absorption** (tertiary)
- [x] CO₂ is a **clean agent**: non-conductive, residue-free, electronics-safe
- [x] CO₂ is **not** a chain-breaker (Halon territory)
- [x] CO₂ is **heavier than air** — therefore effective for low-lying hazards

### ⚡ Critical Numbers to Memorize
- [x] **γ = 1.30** (specific heat ratio)
- [x] **ρNTP = 1.836 kg/m³**
- [x] **Boiling point = −78.5 °C**
- [x] **Vapor pressure @ 21 °C = 57.23 bar**
- [x] **Fill density limit = 0.68 kg/L**
- [x] **Discharge time ≤ 60 s** (total flooding); ≤ 30 s (local surface)
- [x] **Class B minimum = 34%; Class A minimum = 50%; Deep-seated = 65%**

### ⚡ Mandatory Formula Recall
- [x] $V_{CO_2}^{NTP} = V_{room} \times \dfrac{c}{1-c}$
- [x] $m_{design} = m_{CO_2} \times SF$
- [x] $N_{cyl} = \lceil m_{design} / m_{cyl} \rceil$
- [x] $\rho_{fill} \leq 0.68 \text{ kg/L}$
- [x] $O_2^{final} = 0.21 \times (1 - c)$

### ⚡ Code & Standard References
- [x] **NFPA 12** — Standard on Carbon Dioxide Extinguishing Systems
- [x] **IS 6385** — Indian Standard equivalent for CO₂ systems
- [x] **NFPA 12 Chapter 4** — Total Flooding
- [x] **NFPA 12 Chapter 5** — Local Application
- [x] **NFPA 12 Chapter 6** — Hand-Hose Line Systems

### ⚡ Key Distinctions to Remember
- [x] **Total flooding** = sealed room + uniform concentration
- [x] **Local application** = open hazard + surface-directed discharge
- [x] **Class II enclosure** vs **Class III enclosure** — affects SF
- [x] **Surface fire** (≤ 60 s discharge) vs **deep-seated** (≤ 180 s discharge)
- [x] **Heptane** → 65% concentration; **ethanol** → 60%; **Class C** → 50%

### ⚡ Safety & Hazard Reminders
- [x] CO₂ is an **asphyxiant** — **5% by volume** is the IDLH threshold (Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health)
- [x] Always provide **pre-discharge alarm** with **30-second evacuation delay** for occupied spaces
- [x] **Manual release** stations must be installed as backup
- [x] **Door hold magnetic locks** must release on discharge signal
- [x] **HVAC must shut down** to prevent agent loss
- [x] **Personnel must NOT enter** during/after discharge without SCBA

---

*End of Notes — Carbon Dioxide | Module 4 | OECHT835 — Fire Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Powders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Fire Extinguishing Powders

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Fire Engineering** and **Fire Protection Engineering**, the term **"Powders"** refers to a broad family of finely divided, dry, solid particulate chemical agents — collectively known as **Dry Chemical Powders (DCP)** — that are pneumatically discharged to interrupt, suppress, or extinguish the **fire tetrahedron** (Fuel, Heat, Oxygen, and the Uninhibited Chain Reaction). 

A Dry Chemical Powder is officially defined by **IS 13849 : 1993 (Bureau of Indian Standards)** and **NFPA 17 (Standard for Dry Chemical Extinguishing Systems)** as:

> *"A finely divided, free-flowing solid chemical compound, normally with particle sizes in the range of **10 to 75 micrometers (µm)**, which is capable of interrupting the combustion chain reaction when discharged at a controlled rate through a propellant gas (typically Nitrogen, N₂, at pressures of **1.0 to 1.5 MPa**)."*

Dry powders are classified as **Class A, B, C, D, K, and E (Electrical)** fire extinguishing media depending upon the **base chemical salt** used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are cooking and a small grease fire erupts in your pan. You cannot use water (it will explode and spread the fire), and a foam blanket may be too messy. What if you could throw a **super-fine cloud of chemically treated sand** that instantly smothers the fire, neutralizes the chemical flames at a molecular level, and leaves almost no residue?

That is essentially what a dry chemical powder does. Think of it as:

- **"Molecular sand"** — the powder particles act like millions of tiny "firefighters" that float through the air.
- **"Chain-breaker"** — each particle physically and chemically **intercepts the free-radical chain reactions** in the flame zone, similar to how a vaccine interrupts a viral chain reaction in biology.
- **"Heat sponge"** — the particles absorb thermal energy as they decompose, simultaneously cooling the fire surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between **"Dry Chemical Powders (DCP)"** and **"Dry Powders for Class D Fires"** (graphite-based, sodium chloride-based, etc.). These are NOT the same, even though the colloquial term "Powders" is used for both.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Particle Size** | **10 µm to 75 µm** (typically 70% between 10–45 µm) |
| **Bulk Density** | **0.85 to 1.05 g/cm³** for ABC powder |
| **Operating Pressure** | **1.0 to 1.5 MPa** (10 to 15 bar) |
| **Propellant Gas** | **N₂ (Nitrogen)** or **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** |
| **Discharge Time** | **8 to 60 seconds** depending on hazard class |
| **Effective Throw** | **3 to 5 meters** for handheld extinguishers |
| **Storage Temperature** | **−20 °C to +60 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition to Memorise (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** The four-fold fire extinguishing mechanism of dry powders is **(1) Chemical Chain Breaking, (2) Heat Absorption (Endothermic Decomposition), (3) Smothering/Oxygen Displacement, and (4) Radiation Shielding** by the powder cloud.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Dry Chemical Powders (DCP)

Dry chemical powders are systematically classified based on the **base chemical salt** used. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following categories:

### 2.1.1 ABC Powder (Multi-Purpose Powder)
- **Composition:** **Monoammonium Phosphate (NH₄H₂PO₄)** — typically **40 to 60%** by mass
- **Additives:** Barium sulfate, silica, mica, talc, and waterproofing agents (silicone-coated)
- **Color Code:** **Cream / Off-White** (as per IS 13849)
- **Fire Classes:** Effective on **Class A, B, and C** fires.
- **Mechanism:** Forms a **glassy sticky residue** (metaphosphoric acid) on hot surfaces, sealing and preventing re-ignition.

### 2.1.2 BC Powder (Standard Dry Powder)
- **Composition:** **Sodium Bicarbonate (NaHCO₃)** — typically **85 to 92%** by mass.
- **Color Code:** **Blue** body.
- **Fire Classes:** **Class B (flammable liquids) and Class C (flammable gases)**.
- **Mechanism:** Decomposes endothermically to release **CO₂** and water, while producing a **sodium carbonate crust** that smothers the surface.

### 2.1.3 Purple-K Powder (PKP)
- **Composition:** **Potassium Bicarbonate (KHCO₃)** — **90%+ purity**.
- **Color Code:** **Violet / Purple**.
- **Fire Classes:** High-performance **Class B and Class C** fire suppression.
- **Special Property:** Most effective against **hydrocarbon pool fires**; reaction rate is **~2× faster** than sodium bicarbonate.

### 2.1.4 Class D Powders (Special Application)
- **Sodium Chloride (NaCl) Powder** — for alkali metal fires (Na, K, Mg alloys).
- **Copper Powder** — for **lithium and lithium-alloy** fires.
- **Graphite-based powders** — for **high-temperature metal fires**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"ABC" = Ammonium Phosphate (A=B+C effect). "BC" = Bicarbonate (B+C). "PKP" = Potassium Purple Powder (B+C, high-performance). "D" = Dry Metal Powders.**

## 2.2 The Four-Fold Mechanism of Fire Extinguishment

The "Why" and "How" of dry powder fire suppression is summarized as a four-step phenomenon:

1. **Chemical Chain Breaking (Primary Mechanism):** Powder particles dissociate in the flame and release **free radicals (K⁺, Na⁺, PO₄³⁻)** that intercept the **H•, OH•, and O• radicals** sustaining combustion.
2. **Endothermic Heat Absorption:** Decomposition reactions (e.g., $2\text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$) absorb significant heat, cooling the fuel surface.
3. **Smothering Effect:** Decomposed powder forms a **solid crust (ash layer)** that physically blocks oxygen diffusion to the fuel.
4. **Cloud Shielding:** The discharged powder cloud **attenuates radiative heat transfer**, reducing flame spread.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_p = A_f \times R_a \times t_d$ | Total powder mass required ($M_p$ in **kg**), where $A_f$ = floor/fire area in **m²**, $R_a$ = application rate in **kg/m²·s**, $t_d$ = discharge time in **s** |
| 2 | $Q_N_2 = \dfrac{V_c \times P_w}{P_a}$ | Nitrogen propellant volume required ($Q_{N_2}$ in **m³**), where $V_c$ = extinguisher container volume, $P_w$ = working pressure (Pa), $P_a$ = atmospheric pressure (~101,325 Pa) |
| 3 | $A_{cov} = \pi r_{thr}^2$ | Effective coverage area in **m²**, where $r_{thr}$ = effective throw radius (typically **3 to 5 m**) |
| 4 | $\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_p}{V_{bulk}}$ | Bulk density of powder in **g/cm³** or **kg/m³**; critical for storage and discharge flow calculations |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho_{bulk} \times A_{or} \times v_{gas}$ | Mass flow rate of powder ($\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**), where $A_{or}$ = orifice cross-sectional area, $v_{gas}$ = propellant gas velocity |
| 6 | $t_d = \dfrac{M_p}{\dot{m}}$ | Discharge time in **seconds** |
| 7 | $D_{mean} = \dfrac{\sum n_i D_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Mean particle diameter by sieve analysis in **µm** |
| 8 | $\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{P_{rad,flame} - P_{rad,with\,powder}}{P_{rad,flame}}$ | Radiative extinction efficiency (dimensionless) |
| 9 | $R_a = k \times \sqrt{\rho_{bulk}}$ | Empirical application rate correlation for Class B fires |
| 10 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_{gas} v_{gas} D_{mean}}{\mu_{gas}}$ | Reynolds number for powder pneumatic conveyance (must be > 2000 for fluidization) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In a 14-mark problem, students are often asked to compute the **mass of dry chemical powder required** for a given hazard. The most common mark-earning step is correctly identifying $M_p = A_f \times R_a \times t_d$ and selecting the **correct application rate from IS 13849 / NFPA 17 tables** (e.g., **5.2 kg/m² for high-hazard Class B**, **4.1 kg/m² for medium-hazard**).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dry chemical powders are the **workhorse of industrial fire protection**. They are deployed in:

- **Offshore Oil & Gas platforms** — for hydrocarbon pool and jet fires.
- **Petrol refuelling stations** — handheld BC extinguishers.
- **Aircraft hangars** — large fixed DCP systems (NFPA 17 compliant).
- **LPG bottling plants** — for jet fires on pressurized gas leaks.
- **Battery storage (Li-ion)** — **Purple-K and ABC powders** are the primary first-line defense.
- **Pharmaceutical and chemical industries** — for Class A and electrical fires.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Mass of Dry Chemical Powder Required

**Problem:** A Class B hydrocarbon storage area measures **12 m × 8 m**. Per IS 13849 / NFPA 17 standards, the application rate for high-hazard Class B fires is **$R_a = 5.2 \, \text{kg/m}^2$** with a minimum discharge time of **$t_d = 30 \, \text{s}$**. Calculate the total mass of ABC powder required and verify against standard extinguisher ratings.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation** (from KTU Formula Sheet, Row 1):

$$
M_p = A_f \times R_a \times t_d
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the fire area $A_f$:**

$$
A_f = L \times W = 12 \times 8 = 96 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values** (note: application rate $R_a$ in the **NFPA / IS convention** is given in **kg/m²** for the *entire discharge time*, not per second. The correct form is $M_p = A_f \times R_a$ directly):

$$
M_p = A_f \times R_a = 96 \times 5.2 = 499.2 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the rigorous formulation** with $R_a$ in $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ and discharge time $t_d$:

$$
R_{a,\text{per sec}} = \dfrac{5.2}{30} = 0.1733 \, \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

$$
M_p = A_f \times R_{a,\text{per sec}} \times t_d = 96 \times 0.1733 \times 30 = 499.2 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to standard extinguisher capacity:**
The nearest standard capacity (in India) is **500 kg** mobile trolley-mounted extinguisher or a fixed **500 kg DCP skid unit**.

$$
M_p \approx 500 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification via discharge flow rate:**
Assuming a discharge flow rate of $\dot{m} = 16.6 \, \text{kg/s}$ (typical for large mobile units):

$$
t_d = \dfrac{M_p}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{500}{16.6} = 30.12 \, \text{s} \, \approx 30 \, \text{s} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Nitrogen Propellant Volume Calculation

**Problem:** A handheld ABC powder extinguisher has a cylinder internal volume **$V_c = 6.5 \, \text{L}$** and is pressurized to **$P_w = 1.4 \, \text{MPa}$** at **$T = 27 \, \text{°C} = 300 \, \text{K}$**. The extinguisher must discharge **4 kg of powder** at a rate of **$\dot{m} = 0.5 \, \text{kg/s}$**. Calculate the volume of nitrogen required and verify discharge time.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the ideal gas volume conversion formula** (from KTU Formula Sheet, Row 2):

$$
Q_{N_2} = \dfrac{V_c \times P_w}{P_a}
$$

where $P_a = 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$ (atmospheric).

**Step 2 — Substitute values** (convert all units to SI):

$$
V_c = 6.5 \, \text{L} = 6.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3
$$

$$
P_w = 1.4 \, \text{MPa} = 1.4 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
Q_{N_2} = \dfrac{(6.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.4 \times 10^6)}{101325}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate numerator:**

$$
(6.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.4 \times 10^6) = 6.5 \times 1.4 \times 10^3 = 9.1 \times 10^3 = 9100 \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by atmospheric pressure:**

$$
Q_{N_2} = \dfrac{9100}{101325} = 0.0898 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres:**

$$
Q_{N_2} = 89.8 \, \text{L of N₂ at atmospheric pressure}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate discharge time** (from KTU Formula Sheet, Row 6):

$$
t_d = \dfrac{M_p}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{4}{0.5} = 8 \, \text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification of propellant sufficiency:**
A $6.5 \, \text{L}$ cylinder at $1.4 \, \text{MPa}$ provides $89.8 \, \text{L}$ of propellant gas at atmospheric pressure, which is more than sufficient to discharge 4 kg of powder in 8 s for a standard handheld extinguisher.

$$
t_d = 8 \, \text{s} \, \approx \text{NFPA 10 standard (8 to 12 s for handheld)} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Bulk Density and Sieve Analysis

**Problem:** A sample of ABC powder is subjected to sieve analysis. The retained masses on standard sieves are given below. Calculate the **mean particle diameter** and **bulk density**.

| Sieve Mesh (Tyler) | Sieve Opening $D_i$ (µm) | Mass Retained $m_i$ (g) |
|---|---|---|
| 65 | 210 | 5 |
| 100 | 149 | 12 |
| 150 | 105 | 25 |
| 200 | 74 | 38 |
| 325 | 44 | 15 |
| Pan | <44 | 5 |
| **Total** | — | **100** |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply the mean diameter formula** (KTU Formula Sheet, Row 7):

$$
D_{mean} = \dfrac{\sum n_i D_i}{\sum n_i}
$$

where $n_i$ is the *number* of particles in each fraction. For a mass-based sieve analysis, since $n_i = m_i / (\text{particle volume} \times \rho_p)$, and assuming $\rho_p$ is constant, the **mass-weighted mean diameter** is approximated as:

$$
D_{mean} \approx \dfrac{\sum m_i D_i}{\sum m_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $m_i \times D_i$ for each fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5)(210) &= 1050 \\
(12)(149) &= 1788 \\
(25)(105) &= 2625 \\
(38)(74)  &= 2812 \\
(15)(44)  &= 660 \\
(5)(44)   &= 220 \quad (\text{assume pan midpoint})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the products:**

$$
\sum m_i D_i = 1050 + 1788 + 2625 + 2812 + 660 + 220 = 9155
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the masses:**

$$
\sum m_i = 100 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute mean diameter:**

$$
D_{mean} = \dfrac{9155}{100} = 91.55 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute bulk density** (KTU Formula Sheet, Row 4):
Suppose the powder fills a **100 mL graduated cylinder** with a mass of **94 g** (empty cylinder mass = 50 g, gross = 144 g):

$$
\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{M_p}{V_{bulk}} = \dfrac{94 - 50}{100} = \dfrac{44}{100} = 0.44 \, \text{g/cm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A bulk density of **0.44 g/cm³** is on the **lower end of the acceptable range** for ABC powder (typical: **0.85 – 1.05 g/cm³**). This indicates possible **excess additive content** or **improper compaction**, which would impair pneumatic discharge performance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Powder Types

| Property | ABC (Ammonium Phosphate) | BC (Sodium Bicarbonate) | Purple-K (KHCO₃) | Class D (NaCl/Cu) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Base Salt** | $\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4$ | $\text{NaHCO}_3$ | $\text{KHCO}_3$ | NaCl / Cu / Graphite |
| **Fire Classes** | A, B, C, E | B, C, E | B, C, E | D only |
| **Min. Extinguishing Concentration (Class B)** | ~28% | ~32% | ~22% | N/A |
| **Reaction Speed (relative)** | 1× | 0.7× | 2.0× | Variable |
| **Residue & Cleanup** | Sticky glassy film | Light powder | Light powder | Heavy crust |
| **Color Code** | Cream | Blue | Violet | Grey/Black |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | 1.5× | 1.0× | 2.0× | 4.0× |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DCP Discharge System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a typical fixed Dry Chemical Powder suppression system, from detection to suppression, with all alphanumeric-safe node IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Fire Detection Sensor] --> A1[Control Panel Signal]
    A1 --> A2[Actuation Trigger]
    A2 --> A3[N2 Cylinder Activation]
    A3 --> A4[High Pressure Propellant Line]
    A4 --> A5[Powder Storage Cylinder]
    A5 --> A6[Powder Fluidization in N2 Stream]
    A6 --> A7[Distribution Piping Network]
    A7 --> A8[Nozzle Discharge to Protected Zone]
    A8 --> A9[Pneumatic Cloud Formation]
    A9 --> A10[Flame Zone Contact]
    A10 --> A11[Chain Reaction Interruption]
    A11 --> A12[Endothermic Decomposition]
    A12 --> A13[Crust Formation on Fuel]
    A13 --> A14[Fire Suppression Achieved]
    A14 --> A15[System Reset and Recharge]
    A1 --> B1[Audible Alarm]
    A1 --> B2[Visual Strobe]
    A1 --> B3[Emergency Shutdown Interlock]
    A3 --> B4[Pressure Gauge Monitoring]
    A5 --> B5[Low Pressure Switch]
    A7 --> B6[Pipeline Blockage Sensor]
```

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Dry Powders

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Dry Powders] --> P1[Class A B C E - Multi Purpose]
    P0 --> P2[Class B C E - Standard]
    P0 --> P3[Class B C E - High Performance]
    P0 --> P4[Class D - Metal Fires]
    P1 --> P1A[Ammonium Phosphate Base]
    P1A --> P1A1[ABC Powder 40 to 60 percent Phosphate]
    P2 --> P2A[Sodium Bicarbonate Base]
    P2A --> P2A1[Standard BC Powder 85 to 92 percent NaHCO3]
    P3 --> P3A[Potassium Bicarbonate Base]
    P3A --> P3A1[Purple K Powder 90 percent plus KHCO3]
    P3 --> P3B[Potassium Chloride Base]
    P3B --> P3B1[Super K Powder 90 percent plus KCl]
    P4 --> P4A[Sodium Chloride Base]
    P4A --> P4A1[Alkali Metal Fires Na K Mg]
    P4 --> P4B[Copper Powder Base]
    P4B --> P4B1[Lithium and Lithium Alloy Fires]
    P4 --> P4C[Graphite Based Powder]
    P4C --> P4C1[High Temperature Metal Fires]
```

---

## 4.3 Mechanism of Extinguishment Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Powder Cloud Reaches Flame] --> M2[Endothermic Decomposition Begins]
    M2 --> M3[Heat Absorption from Flame]
    M3 --> M4[Free Radicals Released K Na PO4]
    M4 --> M5[Radical Capture of H and OH Species]
    M5 --> M6[Chain Reaction Terminated]
    M1 --> N1[Particles Settle on Fuel Surface]
    N1 --> N2[Glassy Crust or Ash Layer Forms]
    N2 --> N3[Oxygen Diffusion Blocked]
    N3 --> M6
    M2 --> O1[Water and CO2 Released]
    O1 --> O2[Local Oxygen Dilution]
    O2 --> M6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the four-fold mechanism of fire extinguishment by dry chemical powders.**

**Model Answer:**

Dry chemical powders extinguish fire by four complementary mechanisms operating simultaneously:

1. **Chemical Chain Breaking:** The decomposition of salts like **monoammonium phosphate** or **potassium bicarbonate** releases free radicals (K⁺, Na⁺, PO₄³⁻) that combine with the combustion-sustaining free radicals (H•, OH•) in the flame, terminating the chain reaction.

2. **Endothermic Heat Absorption:** Decomposition reactions such as $2\text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$ absorb heat, cooling the fuel below its ignition temperature.

3. **Smothering Effect:** The decomposed salts form a **solid crust** (e.g., sodium carbonate ash, metaphosphoric acid glass) that physically blocks oxygen diffusion to the burning fuel.

4. **Radiation Shielding:** The dense powder cloud in the flame zone **attenuates radiative heat transfer**, preventing flame spread to adjacent fuel.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between ABC powder and Purple-K powder. State at least three points of distinction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ABC Powder | Purple-K Powder |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Salt** | Monoammonium Phosphate ($\text{NH}_4\text{H}_2\text{PO}_4$) | Potassium Bicarbonate ($\text{KHCO}_3$) |
| **Fire Classes** | Class A, B, C, E | Class B, C, E (high-performance) |
| **Color Code** | Cream / Off-White | Violet / Purple |
| **Extinguishing Efficiency (Class B)** | Moderate (~28% concentration) | High (~22% concentration) |
| **Residue** | Sticky glassy film (hard to clean) | Light, easy to clean |
| **Typical Application** | General-purpose industrial and domestic | Hydrocarbon pool fires, refineries, aircraft hangars |

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice: **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** *With the help of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **stored-pressure dry chemical powder extinguisher**. List the components and explain the sequence of operation during discharge. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *An industrial storage shed for Class B hydrocarbons measures **20 m × 15 m**. As per **IS 13849**, the application rate for high-hazard Class B fires is **5.2 kg/m²** and the minimum discharge time is **30 seconds**. Calculate: (i) the total mass of ABC powder required, (ii) the discharge flow rate in kg/s, and (iii) the volume of nitrogen propellant required at 1.4 MPa for a cylinder with internal volume of 80 L. Take atmospheric pressure as 101.325 kPa. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction of a Stored-Pressure DCP Extinguisher:**

A stored-pressure dry chemical powder extinguisher consists of the following components, all housed inside a single cylindrical pressure vessel:

1. **Cylindrical Shell (1)** — Fabricated from **mild steel / stainless steel** hydrostatically tested to **2.5 MPa** (≈ 2.5× working pressure as safety factor).
2. **Powder Storage Section (2)** — The main body filled with **ABC or BC powder** (typically 1 to 9 kg in handheld units).
3. **Propellant Gas Compartment (3)** — A small upper plenum above the powder bed, pre-charged with **dry nitrogen (N₂)** at **1.0 to 1.4 MPa**.
4. **Dip Tube (4)** — A perforated/slotted plastic tube reaching the bottom of the powder bed, which allows N₂ to fluidize the powder.
5. **Siphon Tube (5)** — A second tube that carries the fluidized powder upward during discharge.
6. **Head Assembly (6)** — Contains the **carrying handle, discharge lever, and safety pin (7)**.
7. **Pressure Gauge (8)** — A Bourdon-type gauge indicating cylinder pressure.
8. **Discharge Hose and Nozzle (9)** — A high-pressure hose (rated ≥ 2.0 MPa) ending in a **discharge nozzle (10)** for directing the cloud.
9. **Safety Pin (7)** — Prevents accidental discharge.

**Working Sequence:**

- The user **removes the safety pin**, **squeezes the discharge lever**, which opens the internal valve.
- **N₂ from the upper plenum** rushes down the dip tube and **fluidizes the powder bed**.
- The fluidized powder is **carried up the siphon tube** by the propellant gas.
- The mixture **discharges through the hose and nozzle** as a high-velocity, dense cloud into the flame zone.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Floor area: $L \times W = 20 \times 15 = 300 \, \text{m}^2$
- Application rate: $R_a = 5.2 \, \text{kg/m}^2$
- Discharge time: $t_d = 30 \, \text{s}$
- Working pressure: $P_w = 1.4 \, \text{MPa} = 1.4 \times 10^6 \, \text{Pa}$
- Cylinder volume: $V_c = 80 \, \text{L} = 0.08 \, \text{m}^3$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_a = 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$

**(i) Total mass of ABC powder required:**

$$
M_p = A_f \times R_a = 300 \times 5.2 = 1560 \, \text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Discharge flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{M_p}{t_d} = \dfrac{1560}{30} = 52 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**(iii) Volume of N₂ propellant required (at 1.4 MPa):**

First, compute the equivalent N₂ volume at atmospheric pressure using the gas law conversion:

$$
Q_{N_2} = \dfrac{V_c \times P_w}{P_a} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 1.4 \times 10^6}{101325}
$$

$$
Q_{N_2} = \dfrac{112000}{101325} = 1.105 \, \text{m}^3 = 1105 \, \text{L}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Mistakes That Cost Marks):**
> - **Unit conversion error:** Failing to convert MPa → Pa (× 10⁶) and L → m³ (× 10⁻³). This single mistake can cost **3 of 7 marks** instantly.
> - **Confusing $R_a$ conventions:** Some texts quote $R_a$ in **kg/m²·s** (per-second) while IS 13849 quotes it in **kg/m²** (over total discharge). Always re-read the problem statement carefully.
> - **Forgetting the factor of 2 safety on cylinder pressure:** In real design, the cylinder must be tested at **2.5× the working pressure** (≈ 3.5 MPa hydrostatic test). Students writing the construction in (a) often miss this.
> - **Mixing up ABC and BC classes:** ABC powder is **NOT recommended for high-hazard Class B hydrocarbon fires** at large scale; Purple-K is preferred. The problem states Class B but specifies ABC — note this in design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain the **IS / BIS classification of fires** in India. For each class, name the most suitable dry powder agent and justify your choice. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A handheld ABC extinguisher with capacity **6 kg of powder** is pressurized to **0.9 MPa** with N₂ at **$27 \, \text{°C}$**. The cylinder volume is **9.5 L**. Calculate: (i) the mass of N₂ in the cylinder (molecular weight of N₂ = 28 g/mol, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$), (ii) the time for which the extinguisher will discharge at a flow rate of **0.6 kg/s**, and (iii) the effective coverage area assuming a throw radius of **3.5 m**. (7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**IS / BIS Fire Classification (As per IS 2190:2010):**

| Class | Fuel Type | Most Suitable Powder | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Solid combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) | **ABC Powder (Monoammonium Phosphate)** | Only ABC forms a **glassy residue** that seals porous Class A fuels, preventing re-ignition. |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids (petrol, oil, solvents) | **Purple-K (KHCO₃)** | Highest **chain-breaking efficiency** (~2× faster than BC), best for hydrocarbon pool fires. |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (LPG, CNG, methane) | **ABC or BC Powder** | Rapid chain interruption cuts off the gas-phase combustion chain before flashback. |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Na, K, Mg, Al, Li) | **Specialised D-powders (NaCl, Cu, Graphite)** | ABC and BC are **reactive** with alkali metals (produce H₂ gas — risk of explosion). Only D-powders form a **stable crust** to isolate the metal from O₂. |
| **Class E / Electrical** | Live electrical equipment | **ABC or BC (non-conductive)** | Dry powders are **electrically non-conductive** when applied as a cloud. CO₂ is also suitable. |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Powder capacity: $M_p = 6 \, \text{kg}$
- Pressure: $P_w = 0.9 \, \text{MPa} = 9 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- Temperature: $T = 27 \, \text{°C} = 300 \, \text{K}$
- Cylinder volume: $V_c = 9.5 \, \text{L} = 9.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$
- Molecular weight of N₂: $M_{N_2} = 28 \, \text{g/mol} = 0.028 \, \text{kg/mol}$
- Gas constant: $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$
- Discharge rate: $\dot{m} = 0.6 \, \text{kg/s}$
- Throw radius: $r_{thr} = 3.5 \, \text{m}$

**(i) Mass of N₂ in the cylinder:**

Using ideal gas law: $P_w V_c = n R T$, where $n = \dfrac{m_{N_2}}{M_{N_2}}$

$$
n = \dfrac{P_w V_c}{R T} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^5 \times 9.5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 300}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{8550}{2494.2} = 3.428 \, \text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{N_2} = n \times M_{N_2} = 3.428 \times 0.028 = 0.096 \, \text{kg} = 96 \, \text{g}
$$

**(ii) Discharge time:**

$$
t_d = \dfrac{M_p}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{6}{0.6} = 10 \, \text{s}
$$

**(iii) Effective coverage area:**

$$
A_{cov} = \pi r_{thr}^2 = \pi \times (3.5)^2 = \pi \times 12.25 = 38.48 \, \text{m}^2
$$

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **"Powders"** in Fire Engineering = **Dry Chemical Powders (DCP)** used to interrupt the **fire tetrahedron**.
- ✅ **Four-fold mechanism:** Chain breaking + Endothermic decomposition + Smothering + Radiation shielding.
- ✅ **ABC powder** = Ammonium Phosphate; **BC powder** = Sodium Bicarbonate; **Purple-K** = Potassium Bicarbonate; **Class D** = NaCl / Cu / Graphite-based.
- ✅ **Particle size:** **10 to 75 µm**; **Bulk density:** **0.85 to 1.05 g/cm³** (ABC).
- ✅ **Working pressure:** **1.0 to 1.5 MPa**; propellant = **N₂ or CO₂**.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $M_p = A_f \times R_a \times t_d$ (most asked in KTU exams).
- ✅ **Propellant volume:** $Q_{N_2} = \dfrac{V_c \times P_w}{P_a}$ (always use SI units — MPa → Pa, L → m³).
- ✅ **Discharge time for handheld:** **8 to 12 s**; for large mobile: **30 to 60 s**.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** **IS 13849 (Indian)**, **IS 2190 (fire classification)**, **NFPA 17 (fixed DCP systems)**, **NFPA 10 (portable extinguishers)**.
- ✅ **Color codes:** Cream = ABC, Blue = BC, Violet = Purple-K, Grey/Black = D-powders.
- ✅ **Class D fires** (alkali metals) — **NEVER** use ABC or BC (produces H₂ → explosion risk).
- ✅ **Effective throw radius** of handheld extinguisher: **3 to 5 m**.
- ✅ **Minimum application rate** for high-hazard Class B (IS 13849): **5.2 kg/m²**.
- ✅ **Cylinder hydrostatic test pressure:** **2.5× the working pressure** (safety factor).
- ✅ **Only ABC** powder can be used on **Class A** fires (forms glassy metaphosphoric acid seal).
- ✅ **Purple-K has 2× faster chain-breaking efficiency** than BC powder — preferred for refineries and aircraft hangars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Advice:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **(1) Definition → (2) Diagram / Schematic → (3) Step-by-step Calculation with units → (4) Standards and Justification → (5) Conclusion / Limitations.** Examiners award step-marks generously when each sub-part is clearly numbered and shown with units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Fire Engineering**, **foam** is defined as an aggregate of gas-filled bubbles (typically air) formed by the mechanical agitation of a **foam concentrate** mixed with water (forming the *foam solution*) in the presence of an expanding agent. The resulting three-phase system (water, foam concentrate, and air) is characterized by a density significantly lower than water, enabling it to float on the surface of flammable liquids and suppress fire through a combination of **smothering (oxygen exclusion)**, **cooling**, and **fuel-vapor separation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Foam is a homogenous mass of air-filled bubbles, formed by mixing a foam concentrate with water and air in mechanically agitated proportioning equipment. It possesses a specific gravity lower than the lightest flammable liquid, allowing it to float on, seal, and cool the fuel surface.*

### 1.2 Standard Classification (As per NFPA 11 / IS 636 Standards)

Foams used in fire protection are classified based on their **Expansion Ratio (E)**:

| Classification | Expansion Ratio | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low Expansion Foam** | $\text{E} \lt 20$ | Hydrocarbon fires, storage tank bunds |
| **Medium Expansion Foam** | $20 \le \text{E} \le 200$ | Spill fires, dike protection, dust suppression |
| **High Expansion Foam** | $\text{E} \gt 200$ | Warehouses, aircraft hangars, basements, mines |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Bubble Bath" Principle**
> Imagine you are bathing. The layer of bubbles on the surface of the water acts as an **insulating blanket**. A match dropped onto the bubbles will not ignite the water beneath. Fire-fighting foam operates on the *exact same physical principle*. Instead of a flame reaching the fuel (the water/liquid), the millions of tiny bubbles form a **stable, cohesive blanket** that does three jobs:
> 1. **Smothers** the fire by cutting off the oxygen supply.
> 2. **Cools** the burning fuel as the water content in the bubbles evaporates.
> 3. **Separates** the flame from the fuel vapors being released.

### 1.4 Key Physical Properties of Foam

- **Foam Specific Gravity:** Always **less than 1.0** (typically 0.001 to 0.1), ensuring buoyancy on hydrocarbon fuels.
- **Drainage Time (25%):** The time required for 25% of the foam's water content to separate out. Higher is better (greater stability).
- **Burnback Resistance:** The ability of the foam blanket to withstand direct flame impingement without breaking down.
- **Standard Application Density:** **4.1 L/min/m²** (0.10 gpm/ft²) for hydrocarbon fires; **6.5 L/min/m²** (0.16 gpm/ft²) for polar solvent fires.
- **Proportioning Ratios (Induction Rates):** **1%, 3%, 6%** (volume of concentrate to volume of water).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Components of the Foam Fire Suppression System

A foam fire suppression system has three fundamental hardware components that work in tandem:

1. **Foam Concentrate Storage & Proportioning Equipment**
   - The concentrate is stored in a dedicated tank.
   - It is injected (proportioned) into the water supply line at a precise ratio (1%, 3%, or 6%) using:
     - **In-line eductor (venturi proportioner)** — used for small to moderate flows.
     - **Balanced Pressure Proportioner (BPP)** — used for large industrial flows; maintains accuracy despite fluctuating water pressure.
     - **Around-the-pump proportioner** — foam is drawn from a tank and injected into the pump suction.

2. **Foam-Generating Hardware**
   - The mixed **foam solution** (water + concentrate) is then aerated to form finished foam.
   - **Aspirating Nozzles:** Atmospheric air is inducted by venturi effect; produces low/medium expansion foam.
   - **Water Spray Nozzles:** Used for non-aspirated (compressed air) foam.
   - **High-Expansion Generators:** High-capacity fans pull air through a screen wet with foam solution; produces $\text{E} > 200$ foam.

3. **Delivery & Application Devices**
   - **Handline Nozzles:** For portable monitor use (e.g., branchpipes, foam chambers).
   - **Fixed Foam Pourers / Foam Makers:** Mounted on storage tank shell above the liquid level; foam is gently poured over the fuel surface.
   - **Subsurface Injection:** Foam is injected directly into the fuel storage tank through a pipe below the liquid level; the bubbles rise and fill the tank.

### 2.2 Foam Concentrate Chemistry — Structural Breakdown

| Type | Base Material | Application | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Protein Foam (P)** | Hydrolyzed protein (horn, hoof, feathers) | Hydrocarbon fires | Slow flow, robust blanket, resistant to heat |
| **Fluoroprotein Foam (FP)** | Protein + fluorosurfactants | Hydrocarbon fires | Improved fuel-shedding (alcohol-resistant flow over fuel) |
| **AFFF (Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)** | Synthetic fluorosurfactants + hydrocarbon surfactants | Hydrocarbon fires | Spreads an aqueous film on fuel surface |
| **AR-AFFF (Alcohol-Resistant AFFF)** | AFFF + polymer gelling agent | Polar solvent + hydrocarbon | Polymer film prevents alcohol from destroying foam |
| **FFFP (Film-Forming Fluoroprotein)** | Protein + fluorosurfactants | Hydrocarbon fires | Combines protein stability with AFFF film |
| **Class A Foam** | Synthetic surfactants | Wood, paper, vegetation | Reduces water surface tension; deep penetration |
| **High-Expansion Foam** | Synthetic surfactants | 3D fires (warehouses) | Floods volume with foam to displace oxygen |

### 2.3 Mechanism of Fire Suppression — The "Three E's" Principle

The fire extinguishing action of foam is explained by three fundamental mechanisms:

1. **Exclusion of Oxygen (Smothering):**
   The foam blanket physically isolates the fuel surface from atmospheric oxygen, breaking the fire tetrahedron (fuel, oxygen, heat, chain reaction).

2. **Evaporative Cooling (Heat Removal):**
   The water content in the foam bubbles absorbs latent heat of vaporization and rises as steam, cooling the fuel surface. The latent heat of water is **2260 kJ/kg**.

3. **Emulsification / Vapor Suppression (Fuel Isolation):**
   AFFF and FFFP form an aqueous film on the fuel surface that suppresses flammable vapor release. AR-AFFF uses a polymeric membrane for polar solvents.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Expansion Ratio (E)** | $\text{E} = \dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{solution}}}$ | Dimensionless | Volume of foam produced per unit volume of foam solution |
| 2 | **25% Drainage Time** | $t_{25\%}$ | minutes (min) | Quality indicator; higher is better |
| 3 | **Application Rate (Density)** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{solution}}}{A}$ | $\text{L/min/m}^2$ | NFPA 11 standard: 4.1 (HC), 6.5 (polar) |
| 4 | **Concentrate Volume Required** | $V_c = V_w \times \dfrac{P}{100}$ | Liters (L) | $P$ = proportioning ratio (%) |
| 5 | **Total Foam Solution Volume** | $V_s = V_w + V_c$ | Liters (L) | Water + concentrate |
| 6 | **Theoretical Foam Volume** | $V_f = V_s \times \text{E}$ | Liters (L) | Expanded foam output |
| 7 | **Nozzle Flow Rate (manning)** | $Q_n = K \sqrt{P_n}$ | L/min | $K$ = nozzle coefficient, $P_n$ = nozzle pressure |
| 8 | **Sub-surface Application Velocity** | $V_{\text{sub}} \lt 3 \text{ m/s}$ | m/s | To avoid fuel agitation/electrostatics |
| 9 | **Critical Shear Stress** | $\tau_c$ | N/m² | Stress above which foam collapses |
| 10 | **Burnback Resistance** | $t_{\text{BR}}$ | minutes | Time foam resists direct flame impingement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are vital in the design of fire protection systems for petroleum refineries, oil storage depots (terminals), aircraft hangars, chemical plants, and marine firefighting (SOLAS Convention compliance). The expansion ratio dictates nozzle selection; the application rate dictates hazard area coverage calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Determination of Total Foam Solution Required for a Given Fire Area

**Problem Statement:**
A hydrocarbon storage tank farm has a bund (dike) area of $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$. The design must be based on NFPA 11 specifications. Compute the required water volume, foam concentrate volume, and total foam solution, using a 3% AFFF concentrate. Assume the minimum application density is $4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$ and a fire-fighting operation duration of $T = 65 \text{ minutes}$.

---

**Step 1: Identify the Design Application Rate**

According to NFPA 11, for hydrocarbon (non-polar) fires:
$$\text{AR}_{\text{HC}} = 4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$$

> **Logic:** This is the *minimum* foam solution application rate that ensures sufficient heat absorption and oxygen exclusion. Polar solvents require a higher rate (6.5 L/min/m²) because they aggressively destroy foam.

---

**Step 2: Compute the Total Foam Solution Flow Rate**

The total foam solution flow rate $Q_s$ is the product of the application rate and the area of the fire hazard.

$$Q_s = \text{AR}_{\text{HC}} \times A$$

Substituting the known values:

$$Q_s = 4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2 \times 200 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{Q_s = 820 \text{ L/min}}$$

> **Logic:** This means the system must continuously deliver 820 liters of foam-water solution every minute to be effective.

---

**Step 3: Compute the Total Volume of Foam Solution over the Operation Duration**

Multiply the flow rate by the operation time $T$ to get the total volume of foam solution required:

$$V_s = Q_s \times T$$

$$V_s = 820 \text{ L/min} \times 65 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 53{,}300 \text{ L} = 53.3 \text{ m}^3}$$

> **Logic:** This is the cumulative quantity of foam-water solution (water + concentrate combined) the storage tanks must hold.

---

**Step 4: Compute the Foam Concentrate Volume (3% Proportioning)**

For a 3% proportioning ratio, 3 parts of concentrate are mixed with 97 parts of water (i.e., 3 parts concentrate to 100 parts total solution).

$$V_c = V_s \times \dfrac{P}{100}$$

$$V_c = 53{,}300 \text{ L} \times \dfrac{3}{100}$$

$$\boxed{V_c = 1599 \text{ L} \approx 1.6 \text{ m}^3}$$

> **Logic:** The 3% ratio means 3 L of concentrate per 100 L of finished solution. This is a critical calculation for storage tank sizing during plant commissioning.

---

**Step 5: Compute the Water Volume**

The remainder of the foam solution is water:

$$V_w = V_s - V_c$$

$$V_w = 53{,}300 \text{ L} - 1599 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_w = 51{,}701 \text{ L} \approx 51.7 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

**Step 6: Compute the Theoretical Foam Volume (Expanded Foam)**

If the AFFF nozzle produces an expansion ratio of $\text{E} = 8$ (low expansion), the actual foam volume delivered would be:

$$V_f = V_s \times \text{E}$$

$$V_f = 53{,}300 \text{ L} \times 8$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 426{,}400 \text{ L} = 426.4 \text{ m}^3}$$

> **Logic:** The expansion ratio of 8 means each liter of foam solution creates 8 liters of finished foam — a 700% volumetric gain. This is why foam is a highly efficient suppression agent for large surface fires.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Foam Concentrate Selection Guide

| Fuel Type | Polar/Non-Polar | Recommended Foam | Expansion | Min. App. Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gasoline | Non-polar | AFFF, FFFP, FP | Low (E = 6-8) | 4.1 L/min/m² |
| Crude Oil | Non-polar | Fluoroprotein | Low (E = 6-8) | 4.1 L/min/m² |
| Diesel | Non-polar | Protein | Low (E = 6-8) | 4.1 L/min/m² |
| Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Polar | AR-AFFF | Low (E = 6-8) | 6.5 L/min/m² |
| Methanol (CH₃OH) | Polar | AR-AFFF | Low (E = 6-8) | 6.5 L/min/m² |
| Acetone | Polar | AR-AFFF | Low (E = 6-8) | 6.5 L/min/m² |
| Warehouse / 3D | N/A | High-Expansion Synthetic | High (E = 200-1000) | Volume-flooding |
| Class A (Wood) | N/A | Class A Foam | Low/Medium | 2.5 L/min/m² |

### 3.3 Verification Using Dimensional Analysis

To confirm unit consistency in Step 2:

$$\left[\text{L/min/m}^2\right] \times \left[\text{m}^2\right] = \left[\text{L/min}\right] \quad \checkmark$$

For Step 3:

$$\left[\text{L/min}\right] \times \left[\text{min}\right] = \left[\text{L}\right] \quad \checkmark$$

For Step 4:

$$\left[\text{L}\right] \times \dfrac{\left[\text{dimensionless}\right]}{100} = \left[\text{L}\right] \quad \checkmark$$

All units are dimensionally consistent, validating the calculations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Foam Generation & Application Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WTR["Water Supply"]:::source
    FOC["Foam Concentrate Tank"]:::source
    PRP["Proportioner 1%, 3% or 6%"]:::proc
    SOL["Foam Solution Water plus Concentrate"]:::proc
    ASP["Aspirating Nozzle OR High Expansion Generator"]:::proc
    AIR["Atmospheric Air Induced"]:::source
    FOA["Finished Foam E greater than 1"]:::prod
    FUE["Fuel Surface"]:::target
    BLN["Foam Blanket Forms"]:::target
    RES["Results Smothering, Cooling, Separation"]:::result

    WTR --> PRP
    FOC --> PRP
    PRP --> SOL
    SOL --> ASP
    ASP -->|draws in| AIR
    ASP --> FOA
    FOA -->|applied to| FUE
    FUE --> BLN
    BLN --> RES

    classDef source fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef prod fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef target fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

```

### 4.2 Storage Tank Foam Pourer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    VAL["Control Valve"]:::proc
    VAP["Vapor Seal Chamber"]:::proc
    POU["Foam Pourer on Tank Shell"]:::prod

    FLM["Flame Zone Above Tank"]:::result

    SUB --> VAL
    VAL --> VAP
    VAP --> POU
    POU -->|pours gently| FL1
    FL1 --> BLA
    BLA -->|excludes| FLM

```
