Each transition represents a critical engineering decision point (proportioner selection, nozzle type, application technique).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Expansion Ratio" of a foam. State the standard classification of fire-fighting foams based on expansion ratio as per NFPA 11.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **expansion ratio (E)** of a foam is the ratio of the volume of finished foam produced to the volume of foam solution (water + concentrate) from which it is generated:

$$\text{E} = \dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{solution}}}$$

As per NFPA 11, foams are classified as:
1. **Low Expansion Foam:** $\text{E} < 20$ — used for hydrocarbon spill and tank fires.
2. **Medium Expansion Foam:** $20 \le \text{E} \le 200$ — used for dike and bund protection.
3. **High Expansion Foam:** $\text{E} > 200$ — used for 3D volume-flooding fires in warehouses, mines, and hangars. **(1 + 2 = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any THREE mechanisms by which foam extinguishes a fire. Give one example of a foam concentrate used for polar solvent fires.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Foam extinguishes fire by:
1. **Smothering** — the foam blanket excludes atmospheric oxygen from the fuel surface.
2. **Cooling** — water content evaporates, absorbing latent heat ($2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$).
3. **Fuel-Vapor Separation** — the foam blanket physically blocks fuel vapors from mixing with air; AFFF additionally forms an aqueous film.

**Example for polar solvent fires:** **AR-AFFF (Alcohol-Resistant Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)**, which uses a polymeric gelling agent to form a protective membrane that prevents polar solvents (e.g., methanol, ethanol) from breaking down the foam. **(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. (Question A) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **components and working of a low-expansion foam fire suppression system** for a hydrocarbon storage tank. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydrocarbon bund area of $350 \text{ m}^2$ is to be protected by an AFFF system at a 3% proportioning ratio. Using NFPA 11 design standards, calculate the required:
  - (i) Foam solution flow rate
  - (ii) Total foam solution volume for a 60-minute operation
  - (iii) Volume of foam concentrate required
  - (iv) Volume of water required
  - (v) Total finished foam volume produced (assume $\text{E} = 7$)

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A low-expansion foam system for hydrocarbon storage tanks typically includes the following components (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):

1. **Water Supply System:** A dedicated fire water main, hydrant network, or storage reservoir providing water at sufficient pressure (typically $7\text{-}12$ bar) and flow rate. **(1 mark)**

2. **Foam Concentrate Storage Tank:** Contains AFFF or fluoroprotein concentrate. Sized for the design discharge duration. **(1 mark)**

3. **Proportioning Equipment:** Typically a **Balanced Pressure Proportioner (BPP)** for large tank farms. The BPP uses a diaphragm-regulated ratio controller to maintain the concentrate ratio (1%, 3%, or 6%) despite variations in water flow or pressure. **(1 mark)**

4. **Foam Solution Manifold / Piping Network:** Distributes the foam solution to the foam pourers or monitors. **(0.5 mark)**

5. **Foam Pourers / Foam Makers:** Mounted on the tank shell above the maximum liquid level. The foam solution is forced through a screen with air-aspirating openings; the resulting foam is gently poured onto the fuel surface to avoid splashing and fuel agitation. **(1.5 marks)**

6. **Vapor Seal Chamber:** Prevents flammable vapors from passing back through the foam line into the manifold. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **Control Valves & Actuation System:** Manual or automatic (deluge) valves that initiate foam discharge on fire detection. **(0.5 mark)**

8. **Discharge & Suppression:** Finished foam forms a coherent, cohesive blanket over the hydrocarbon surface, achieving **smothering, cooling, and vapor suppression** simultaneously. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Area $A = 350 \text{ m}^2$
- Proportioning ratio $P = 3\%$
- Operation time $T = 60 \text{ min}$
- Application density (NFPA 11 for hydrocarbon) $\text{AR} = 4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- Expansion ratio $\text{E} = 7$

---

**(i) Foam Solution Flow Rate:**

$$Q_s = \text{AR} \times A$$

$$Q_s = 4.1 \text{ L/min/m}^2 \times 350 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{Q_s = 1435 \text{ L/min}}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(ii) Total Foam Solution Volume for 60 min Operation:**

$$V_s = Q_s \times T$$

$$V_s = 1435 \text{ L/min} \times 60 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 86{,}100 \text{ L} = 86.1 \text{ m}^3}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(iii) Volume of Foam Concentrate (3%):**

$$V_c = V_s \times \dfrac{P}{100}$$

$$V_c = 86{,}100 \times 0.03$$

$$\boxed{V_c = 2583 \text{ L} \approx 2.58 \text{ m}^3}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(iv) Volume of Water:**

$$V_w = V_s - V_c$$

$$V_w = 86{,}100 - 2583$$

$$\boxed{V_w = 83{,}517 \text{ L} \approx 83.52 \text{ m}^3}$$ — **(1 mark)**

---

**(v) Total Finished Foam Volume (E = 7):**

$$V_f = V_s \times \text{E}$$

$$V_f = 86{,}100 \times 7$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 602{,}700 \text{ L} = 602.7 \text{ m}^3}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**OR**

---

**Q4. (Question B) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the properties, application areas, and limitations of **Protein, AFFF, and AR-AFFF** foam concentrates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A medium-expansion foam generator produces foam at a rate of $2500 \text{ L/min}$ of foam solution with an expansion ratio of 50. The system uses a 6% concentrate to protect a chemical warehouse. Calculate:
  - (i) Total foam solution flow rate
  - (ii) Finished foam output flow rate
  - (iii) Concentrate consumption per hour
  - (iv) Water consumption per hour
  - (v) Comment on the suitability of this foam for a methanol storage fire.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Property | **Protein Foam (P)** | **AFFF** | **AR-AFFF** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Base Material** | Hydrolyzed animal protein | Synthetic fluorosurfactants + hydrocarbon surfactants | AFFF + polymer gelling agent |
| **Application** | Hydrocarbon fires (slow flow) | Hydrocarbon fires (fast flow) | Polar + Hydrocarbon fires |
| **Flow Speed** | Slow (heater-resistant) | Fast (fast knockdown) | Fast |
| **Vapor Suppression** | Good (rigid blanket) | Excellent (aqueous film) | Excellent (polymer + film) |
| **Fuel Compatibility** | Hydrocarbons only | Hydrocarbons only | All fuels including alcohols |
| **Limitations** | Cannot be used on polar solvents; slow application | Cannot be used on polar solvents (film destroyed) | More expensive; slightly lower shelf life |
| **Shelf Life** | 15-25 years | 10-15 years | 10-15 years |

**(7 rows × 1 mark = 7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Foam solution flow rate $Q_s = 2500 \text{ L/min}$
- Expansion ratio $\text{E} = 50$
- Proportioning ratio $P = 6\%$

---

**(i) Total Foam Solution Flow Rate:**

Given directly:
$$\boxed{Q_s = 2500 \text{ L/min}}$$ — **(1 mark)**

---

**(ii) Finished Foam Output Flow Rate:**

$$Q_f = Q_s \times \text{E}$$

$$Q_f = 2500 \text{ L/min} \times 50$$

$$\boxed{Q_f = 125{,}000 \text{ L/min} = 125 \text{ m}^3\text{/min}}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(iii) Concentrate Consumption per Hour:**

Concentrate flow rate:
$$Q_c = Q_s \times \dfrac{P}{100} = 2500 \times 0.06 = 150 \text{ L/min}$$

Per hour:
$$Q_{c,1h} = 150 \times 60 = \boxed{9000 \text{ L/hr}}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(iv) Water Consumption per Hour:**

Water flow rate:
$$Q_w = Q_s - Q_c = 2500 - 150 = 2350 \text{ L/min}$$

Per hour:
$$Q_{w,1h} = 2350 \times 60 = \boxed{141{,}000 \text{ L/hr}}$$ — **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(v) Suitability for Methanol Storage Fire:**

**Methanol** is a **polar solvent**. A 6% AFFF (without the "AR" alcohol-resistant designation) is **NOT suitable** because:
- Polar solvents aggressively attack and break down standard AFFF bubbles.
- The fluorosurfactant film is destroyed by the alcohol's solvency.
- **Recommended action:** Substitute with **AR-AFFF (Alcohol-Resistant AFFF)** or **AR-FFFP** at an increased application rate of **6.5 L/min/m²**.
- Additionally, medium-expansion foam ($\text{E} = 50$) is better suited for *spill* fires in chemical warehouses rather than for tank-side polar solvent protection, where low-expansion AR-AFFF is preferred. **(1.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Many students confuse the *application rate* (4.1 L/min/m²) with the *expansion ratio* — they are different. Application rate refers to foam solution; expansion ratio refers to the *finished foam volume*.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **subtract** the concentrate volume from the foam solution to get the water volume. Remember: $V_w = V_s - V_c$, not $V_s \times 0.97$ (the 97% approximation is a common error trap).
> - **Mistake 3:** Using **6.5 L/min/m²** for non-polar hydrocarbons. Always cross-check the fuel type. NFPA 11 specifies 4.1 for hydrocarbons, 6.5 for polar solvents.
> - **Mistake 4:** Failing to state the **mechanism of action** (smothering, cooling, separation) in theoretical questions. Examiners allocate 1-2 marks specifically for this.
> - **Mistake 5:** Not mentioning the **latent heat of vaporization of water (2260 kJ/kg)** when explaining the cooling mechanism. This is a high-value one-liner in the marking scheme.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ☐ **Foam** = air-filled bubbles formed by mechanical agitation of foam solution (water + concentrate + air).
- ☐ **Expansion Ratio (E)** = $V_{\text{foam}} / V_{\text{solution}}$; classifies foam as Low (<20), Medium (20-200), High (>200).
- ☐ **Standard Proportioning Ratios:** **1%, 3%, 6%** of concentrate to total solution volume.
- ☐ **Standard Application Density (NFPA 11):** **4.1 L/min/m²** (hydrocarbons) | **6.5 L/min/m²** (polar solvents).
- ☐ **Three E's Mechanism:** **Exclusion** (smothering), **Evaporative cooling**, **Emulsification/vapor suppression**.
- ☐ **Latent heat of water vaporization:** **2260 kJ/kg** (use this number in cooling-related answers).
- ☐ **AFFF** — hydrocarbon fuels only; forms aqueous film. **AR-AFFF** — for polar solvents; polymeric membrane.
- ☐ **Foam Pourer** — mounted on tank shell; gentle application to avoid fuel splashing.
- ☐ **Subsurface Injection** — foam injected below fuel level; velocity must be **< 3 m/s** to avoid static charge.
- ☐ **High-Expansion Foam** — used in enclosed 3D spaces (mines, hangars, warehouses) to flood the volume.
- ☐ **Proportioner types:** In-line eductor, Balanced Pressure Proportioner (BPP), Around-the-pump.
- ☐ **Key Derived Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $V_s = \text{AR} \times A \times T$
  - $V_c = V_s \times (P/100)$
  - $V_w = V_s - V_c$
  - $V_f = V_s \times \text{E}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Halons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Halons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Halons

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Halon** is a halogenated hydrocarbon extinguishing agent belonging to the chemical family of haloalkanes, in which one or more hydrogen atoms of a hydrocarbon molecule have been replaced by atoms drawn from the halogen group of the periodic table (primarily **fluorine, chlorine, and bromine**). The term "Halon" itself is a registered trademark of the American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE), originally adopted in the 1950s to describe a class of saturated aliphatic organic compounds containing bromine, chlorine, or fluorine. Halons were historically designated by a numerical naming convention developed by the U.S. Army (and later adopted by NFPA), which precisely identifies the atomic composition: the first digit represents the number of carbon atoms, the second the number of fluorine atoms, the third the number of chlorine atoms, and the fourth the number of bromine atoms (hydrogen atoms complete the valency).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Halons are classified as **Total Flooding Clean Agent Fire Suppression Systems**. They extinguish Class A, B, and C fires primarily through *chemical flame inhibition* (interrupting the combustion chain reaction) rather than cooling or oxygen displacement.

## 1.2 The Three Historical Halons of Engineering Importance

The three halons that achieved widespread commercial and military adoption in fire protection engineering are:

| Halon Number | IUPAC Chemical Name | Chemical Formula | Primary Application |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Halon 1301** | Bromotrifluoromethane | **CF₃Br** | Total flooding of occupied spaces (low toxicity) |
| **Halon 1211** | Bromochlorodifluoromethane | **CF₂ClBr** | Portable hand-held extinguishers, streaming applications |
| **Halon 2402** | 1,2-Dibromotetrafluoroethane | **C₂F₄Br₂** | Specialized military and marine applications (USSR/China) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Halon Number 1301:**  
> Digit 1 = **1** carbon atom  
> Digit 2 = **3** fluorine atoms  
> Digit 3 = **0** chlorine atoms  
> Digit 4 = **1** bromine atom  
> Remaining valency (4 for carbon) is filled by hydrogen = 0 hydrogen.  
> Result: **C F₃ Br₁** → CF₃Br ✓

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: Halons as "Chemical Bomb Squads"

Imagine a campfire that keeps burning because three mischievous "free radical" troublemakers — Hydrogen (H•), Oxygen (O•), and Hydroxyl (OH•) — are constantly stealing and passing energy particles around in a never-ending relay race. The fire cannot die because this trio keeps recycling the chemical energy.

Now imagine a Halon molecule as a **highly trained "sniper" molecule** waiting quietly in the air. The moment the fire starts, the sniper releases its **bromine atom** — a precision interceptor. This bromine atom captures each of the three troublemakers one by one, halting the relay race instantly. The fire is extinguished not by removing heat or oxygen, but by **arresting the chemistry of combustion itself**.

This is the genius — and the danger — of Halons. They are extraordinarily efficient at suppressing fires because they attack the **fire's chain reaction** directly, requiring only a few percent concentration in air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Pearl:** Halons extinguish fire through **chemical interference (flame inhibition)**, not through cooling, oxygen depletion, or fuel removal. This is why only **3–5% concentration** of Halon 1301 in air is enough to suppress a fire, compared with 30–40% required for CO₂.

## 1.4 Physical State at Standard Conditions

At standard temperature and pressure (**25 °C, 101.325 kPa**):
- **Halon 1301** is a colourless, odourless **gas** (boiling point −57.8 °C), so it can be stored as a pressurised liquefied gas.
- **Halon 1211** is a colourless, faintly sweet-smelling **liquid** (boiling point −3.9 °C), making it suitable for discharge in liquid form as a streaming agent.
- **Halon 2402** is a colourless **liquid** (boiling point 47.3 °C).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse Halon 1211 with Halon 1301 in storage state. Halon 1211 is a liquid at room temperature and is discharged as a **streaming** liquid-vapour mixture. Halon 1301 is a gas and is used for **total flooding** of enclosures.

## 1.5 Why Halons Were Considered "Near-Perfect" Extinguishing Agents

The combination of physical and chemical properties made Halons the gold standard for specialised fire protection from the 1960s to the 1990s:

1. **Extremely Effective:** 3–5 vol% concentration extinguishes most fires in seconds.
2. **Clean:** Leaves **zero residue**, no water damage, no corrosion, no cleanup required.
3. **Electrically Non-Conductive:** Safe for energised electrical equipment, server rooms, control panels.
4. **Non-Damaging to Equipment:** Used to protect irreplaceable electronics, aircraft, telecommunications, military assets.
5. **Low Toxicity (1301):** Cardiac sensitisation threshold (LOAEL) = 7.5%, No Observable Adverse Effect Level (NOAEL) = 5%, design concentration typically 5%, allowing human occupancy.
6. **Long Storage Life:** Pressurised cylinders can remain on standby for decades.
7. **Wide Fire Class Coverage:** Effective against Class A, B, and C fires.

> [!NOTE]
> The discovery of the **ozone-depleting potential** of brominated compounds by Molina and Rowland (1974) and the subsequent **Montreal Protocol (1987)** ended the era of Halon production. The Montreal Protocol placed Halons in **Annex A, Group II**, mandating global phase-out.

## 1.6 The Historical Legacy — Why KTU Asks About Halons

Although Halons are no longer manufactured, KTU's 2024 scheme includes them because:

- Many **existing fire protection systems** (civil aviation, military, maritime, banking) still contain stockpiled Halon.
- The design principles, toxicity benchmarks (NOAEL/LOAEL), and clean-agent concepts originate from Halon technology.
- Halons serve as the **reference benchmark** against which all modern clean agents (FM-200, Novec 1230, Inergen) are measured.
- India's National Halon Bank at BPCL Mumbai recycles and supplies reclaimed Halon for critical applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Halon Numbering Convention — Decoded

The standard ASHRAE / U.S. Army halon designation follows the rule:

$$\text{Halon } ABCD$$

where each letter represents the count of a specific atom, reading from left to right:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Number of carbon atoms} \\
B &= \text{Number of fluorine atoms} \\
C &= \text{Number of chlorine atoms} \\
D &= \text{Number of bromine atoms}
\end{aligned}$$

The hydrogen count is **implicit** and completes the carbon valency of 4:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 2A + 2 - 2B - C - D \quad \text{(general for saturated alkanes, ignoring cyclic structures)}
\end{aligned}$$

For **Halon 1301** (A=1, B=3, C=0, D=1):
$$H = 2(1) + 2 - 2(3) - 0 - 1 = 4 - 6 - 1 = -3$$

A negative value indicates that the structure cannot be a saturated alkane — instead, the carbon valency is satisfied by halogens only. The general balance becomes:

$$\text{Carbon valency} = 4 = 2B + C + D \quad \text{(when } H = 0\text{)}$$

For Halon 1301: $2(3) + 0 + 1 = 7 \neq 4$ — but with **one double bond character** in the structure (or considering that the formula is empirical, not structural), the halon system works because the atoms are tabulated rather than mathematically derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Mnemonic for KTU:** **"Caught By (the) Fire's Cooled Blaze"** → Carbon, Bromine, Fluorine, Chlorine, Bromine.  
> Or simply remember: **C-F-Cl-Br** reading left to right = **1-3-0-1** for Halon 1301.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Fire Extinguishment — Detailed

Halons extinguish fire through **three simultaneous mechanisms**, but one dominates:

### 2.2.1 Primary Mechanism: Chemical Flame Inhibition (~70–80% of effectiveness)

The combustion of hydrocarbons in air proceeds via the **radical chain reaction**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Chain initiation:} \quad & \text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R•} + \text{HO}_2\text{•} \\
\text{Chain branching:} \quad & \text{H•} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH•} + \text{O•} \\
\text{Chain propagation:} \quad & \text{OH•} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H•} \\
& \text{H•} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH•} + \text{O•}
\end{aligned}$$

The reaction is self-sustaining because **H•, O•, and OH•** radicals catalyse the chain. When **bromine atoms** are released from the halon (by thermal decomposition in the flame zone), they act as **radical scavengers**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{H•} + \text{Br•} &\rightarrow \text{HBr} \\
\text{OH•} + \text{HBr} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br•} \\
\text{H•} + \text{HBr} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{Br•} \\
\text{O•} + \text{HBr} &\rightarrow \text{OH•} + \text{Br•}
\end{aligned}$$

The critical insight is that **Br• is regenerated** in the second and fourth reactions — meaning **one bromine atom can neutralise thousands of chain-carrying radicals** before being permanently consumed. This is called **catalytic radical recombination**.

### 2.2.2 Secondary Mechanism: Heat Absorption (Latent Heat of Vaporisation)

When Halon 1211 (liquid) is discharged, vaporisation absorbs heat:

$$Q = m \cdot L_v$$

For Halon 1211, $L_v \approx 132 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 25 °C, contributing modest cooling. For Halon 1301 (gas), this mechanism is negligible.

### 2.2.3 Tertiary Mechanism: Physical Oxygen Displacement (Minor)

Halons do not significantly displace oxygen because their extinguishing concentration is only 3–5%, leaving atmospheric oxygen at ~20%.

## 2.3 Critical Toxicity Metrics — KTU High-Yield

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most examination-relevant numerical framework for Halons is the **toxicity hierarchy**.

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Halon 1301 | Halon 1211 |
|--------|--------|------------|------------|------------|
| **No Observable Adverse Effect Level** | NOAEL | Highest concentration with no detectable physiological effect over exposure period | **5.0%** | 0.5% |
| **Lowest Observable Adverse Effect Level** | LOAEL | Lowest concentration showing measurable cardiac sensitisation (epinephrine arrhythmia) in dogs | **7.5%** | 1.0% |
| **Extinguishing Concentration (Heptane)** | EC | Minimum vol% in air to extinguish fire | **3.5%** | 4.2% |
| **Design Concentration** | DC | Safety factor (typically 1.2–1.3 × EC) for system design | **5.0%** | 5.5% |
| **Ozone Depletion Potential** | ODP | Relative to CFC-11 = 1.0 | **10.0** | 3.0 |
| **Global Warming Potential (100-yr)** | GWP | Relative to CO₂ = 1 | **6,900** | 1,300 |
| **Atmospheric Lifetime** | $\tau$ | Years | **65 years** | 16 years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** For **Halon 1301**, the design concentration (5%) **equals** the NOAEL. This is the engineering reason why Halon 1301 was uniquely suited for occupied spaces — designers could use the maximum safe concentration and still achieve reliable fire suppression. For **Halon 1211**, the design concentration (5.5%) **exceeds** the LOAEL (1.0%), so it is NOT safe for occupied spaces — it is restricted to unoccupied or manually-applied scenarios.

## 2.4 Environmental Impact Framework

### 2.4.1 Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)

The **ODP** quantifies a compound's ability to destroy stratospheric ozone, normalised to CFC-11 (= 1.0):

$$\text{ODP}_X = \frac{\text{Ozone destroyed per unit mass of } X}{\text{Ozone destroyed per unit mass of CFC-11}}$$

The ODP of a halon scales with:
- **Number of bromine atoms** (each Br ~ 50× more destructive than Cl)
- **Stability** (longer atmospheric lifetime → more time to reach stratosphere)

For Halon 1301 (CF₃Br): ODP = 10.0 — the **highest of any commercially used chemical**, because it has **one Br atom** and is extremely stable (65-year lifetime allows it to reach the stratosphere intact).

### 2.4.2 Global Warming Potential (GWP)

$$\text{GWP}_X = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_X \cdot [X](t)\, dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot [CO_2](t)\, dt}$$

where $a_X$ is the instantaneous radiative forcing per unit mass and $TH$ is the time horizon (typically 100 years).

Halon 1301's GWP of **6,900** means 1 kg of Halon 1301 traps 6,900× the heat of 1 kg of CO₂ over 100 years.

### 2.4.3 Atmospheric Lifetime $\tau$

The lifetime is governed by first-order photolysis in the stratosphere:

$$[X](t) = [X]_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

For Halon 1301, $\tau = 65$ years — meaning a molecule released today will continue destroying ozone for the **next seven decades**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Halon numbering | Halon ABCD → A=Carbon, B=Fluorine, C=Chlorine, D=Bromine | H implicit, satisfies valency |
| 2 | Extinguishing efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\text{EC}_{\text{CO}_2}}{\text{EC}_{\text{Halon}}} = \frac{30\%}{3.5\%} \approx 8.6$ | Halons ~8–10× more efficient than CO₂ |
| 3 | Minimum design mass (1301) | $m = \frac{V \cdot C}{S \cdot (1 - C) / v_d}$ | where $V$=room volume, $C$=design concentration, $S$=specific volume, $v_d$=density factor |
| 4 | Specific volume at 21 °C, 1 atm | $S_{1301} = 0.148 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ | NFPA 12A reference |
| 5 | NOAEL (1301) | $5.0\%$ by volume | Cardiac safety threshold |
| 6 | LOAEL (1301) | $7.5\%$ by volume | Cardiac sensitisation onset |
| 7 | Ozone Depletion Potential | $\text{ODP}_{1301} = 10.0$ | Reference: CFC-11 = 1 |
| 8 | Global Warming Potential | $\text{GWP}_{1301} = 6900$ | 100-year horizon, CO₂ = 1 |
| 9 | Atmospheric lifetime | $\tau_{1301} = 65$ years | Stratospheric sink |
| 10 | Radical scavenging efficiency | 1 Br atom $\approx 10^3$–$10^4$ radicals neutralised | Catalytic cycle |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The halon framework still underpins:
- **Aircraft fire suppression:** Boeing 737, Airbus A320 cargo holds still contain Halon 1301.
- **Military ground vehicles:** M1 Abrams, BMP-2.
- **IT Server rooms** built before 1994: legacy Halon 1301 systems still operational under grandfathering.
- **India's Halon Management Rules (2000, amended 2007)** under the Montreal Protocol.
- **BPCL Mumbai Halon Bank:** the sole authorised national facility for Halon recovery, reclamation, and recycling.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Mass of Halon 1301 Required for Total Flooding

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A server room measures **8 m × 6 m × 3.5 m**. Using Halon 1301, design the system to suppress a heptane fire. Required design concentration: **5.0%**. Calculate the **mass of Halon 1301** required, the **number of standard cylinders** (each containing 68 kg), and verify that the design does not exceed the LOAEL safety threshold.

### 3.1.2 Given Data

- Room volume: $V = 8 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 168 \text{ m}^3$
- Design concentration: $C = 5.0\% = 0.05$ (by volume)
- Specific volume of Halon 1301 at 21 °C, 1 atm: $S = 0.148 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- Standard cylinder capacity: 68 kg
- NOAEL = 5.0%, LOAEL = 7.5%

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the volume of Halon 1301 vapour required at room conditions.**

The room volume that must contain 5% halon vapour:

$$V_{\text{halon}} = C \cdot V = 0.05 \times 168 \text{ m}^3 = 8.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert vapour volume to mass using specific volume.**

By definition, $S$ = volume per unit mass at given conditions, so:

$$m = \frac{V_{\text{halon}}}{S} = \frac{8.4 \text{ m}^3}{0.148 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{8.4}{0.148} \text{ kg} \\
  &= 56.76 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply engineering safety factor (typically 10–15% over theoretical minimum).**

$$m_{\text{design}} = m \times 1.10 = 56.76 \times 1.10 = 62.44 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the number of cylinders required.**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{m_{\text{design}}}{m_{\text{cylinder}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{62.44}{68} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.918 \rceil = 1 \text{ cylinder}$$

(rounded up — fractional cylinders are non-existent)

**Step 5 — Verify safety: confirm design concentration ≤ NOAEL.**

Design concentration = 5.0%, NOAEL = 5.0%, LOAEL = 7.5%.

Since $C = 5.0\% \leq 5.0\% = \text{NOAEL}$, the system is **safe for occupied spaces**, with zero margin. A more conservative designer might reduce $C$ to **4.5%** (extinguishing efficiency retained, safety margin of 0.5% below NOAEL).

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{m_{\text{design}} \approx 62.4 \text{ kg of Halon 1301, supplied by one 68 kg cylinder.}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Comparative Atmospheric Impact of Halons vs CO₂

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Quantify the **ozone destruction potential** of releasing **1 kg of Halon 1301** versus **1 kg of Halon 1211** versus **1 kg of CO₂**. Compare with the ozone-destruction equivalent of CFC-11.

### 3.2.2 Solution

$$\text{Ozone destroyed}_{\text{Halon}} \propto \text{ODP} \times \text{Mass} \times \text{Lifetime factor}$$

For equal mass (1 kg):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Halon 1301:} \quad & \text{ODP} \times \text{mass} = 10.0 \times 1 = 10.0 \text{ ODP-kg} \\
\text{Halon 1211:} \quad & 3.0 \times 1 = 3.0 \text{ ODP-kg} \\
\text{CFC-11:} \quad & 1.0 \times 1 = 1.0 \text{ ODP-kg} \\
\text{CO}_2: \quad & 0 \times 1 = 0 \text{ ODP-kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** One kilogram of Halon 1301 destroys **ten times** the ozone of CFC-11 and **zero** is destroyed by CO₂ (hence CO₂ has no ODP — its environmental harm is purely radiative forcing, not ozone damage).

## 3.3 Derivation: Bromine Atom Regeneration Cycle — Quantitative

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A single bromine atom is released in a flame. Estimate the number of chain-carrying radicals it can neutralise before permanent consumption, given that the chain-branching reaction $\text{H•} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH•} + \text{O•}$ occurs with a probability of 0.85 per encounter, and that bromine is permanently consumed when it forms Br₂ via reaction $\text{Br•} + \text{Br•} + M \rightarrow \text{Br}_2 + M$.

### 3.3.2 Solution

Each Br• atom can, on average, complete a catalytic cycle ~1,000–10,000 times before combining with another Br• to form stable Br₂ (a termination reaction). The catalytic cycle proceeds through:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Br•} + \text{RH} &\rightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R•} \\
\text{HBr} + \text{H•} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{Br•} \quad \text{(Br regenerated)}
\end{aligned}$$

Net effect per cycle: **one H• radical removed, one Br• regenerated**.

The terminal loss occurs by:

$$\text{Br•} + \text{Br•} + M \rightarrow \text{Br}_2$$

This is a **three-body collision**, with rate constant ~$10^{-32}$ cm⁶/molecule²·s, making it statistically rare in atmospheric-pressure flames. Hence the high turnover number.

$$\boxed{N_{\text{radicals}} \approx 10^3 \text{–} 10^4 \text{ radicals neutralised per Br atom}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Halons vs Modern Clean Agents

| Property | Halon 1301 | FM-200 (HFC-227ea) | Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12) | Inergen (IG-541) | CO₂ |
|----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|------|
| Extinguishing mechanism | Chemical inhibition | Chemical inhibition | Chemical inhibition | Oxygen displacement | Oxygen displacement |
| Design concentration | 5.0% | 7.0% | 4.5% | 38–43% | 30–40% |
| NOAEL | 5.0% | 9.0% | 10.0% | 43% | ~5% (asphyxiation) |
| Storage state | Pressurised liquid | Pressurised liquid | Liquid (low pressure) | High-pressure gas | High-pressure liquid |
| ODP | **10.0** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | **6,900** | 2,050 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| Atmospheric lifetime | 65 yr | 33 yr | 5 days | N/A (N₂, Ar, CO₂) | N/A |
| Suitable for occupied | Yes (at 5%) | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** (asphyxiant) |
| Cost (relative) | High (banned, stockpile) | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Very low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the trajectory of fire-suppression innovation. **Halon 1301 → FM-200 → Novec 1230 → Inergen** represents a progressive movement from ozone-depleting → high-GWP → ultra-low GWP → zero-GWP solutions. The *mechanism* also shifted from pure chemical inhibition to oxygen dilution in the case of Inergen. KTU expects students to understand *why* each transition occurred (regulatory pressure, environmental harm, safety).

## 3.5 Worked Example: Toxicity Safety Check

A Halon 1301 system is designed for a 200 m³ control room. The calculated discharge mass is 70 kg. Verify whether the resulting concentration is safe for occupied operation.

**Step 1 — Vapour volume generated.**

$$V_{\text{halon}} = m \cdot S = 70 \times 0.148 = 10.36 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Concentration in room.**

$$C = \frac{V_{\text{halon}}}{V_{\text{room}}} = \frac{10.36}{200} = 0.0518 = 5.18\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare with NOAEL and LOAEL.**

$$C = 5.18\% > \text{NOAEL} = 5.0\%$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

The design concentration **exceeds NOAEL** by 0.18 percentage points. The system is **unsafe for occupied spaces** in its current configuration. Either:
- Reduce the discharge mass to ≤ 67.6 kg, or
- Increase room volume by 4 m³ (or equivalent), or
- Restrict access until ventilation restores concentration below NOAEL (typically 5–10 minutes with adequate HVAC).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the halon numbering convention with reference to Halon 1301 and Halon 1211."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The halon numbering convention, adopted from the U.S. Army designation system, identifies the atomic composition of a halogenated hydrocarbon using a four-digit code: **Halon ABCD**, where **A = number of carbon atoms**, **B = number of fluorine atoms**, **C = number of chlorine atoms**, and **D = number of bromine atoms**; hydrogen atoms are implicit and satisfy the remaining carbon valency.

- **Halon 1301:** A=1, B=3, C=0, D=1 → 1 carbon, 3 fluorines, 0 chlorines, 1 bromine → chemical formula **CF₃Br** (bromotrifluoromethane).
- **Halon 1211:** A=1, B=2, C=1, D=1 → 1 carbon, 2 fluorines, 1 chlorine, 1 bromine → chemical formula **CF₂ClBr** (bromochlorodifluoromethane).

**(3 marks: 1 for convention + 1 each for the two halons.)**

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**"Why are halons considered effective fire extinguishing agents despite their low concentration requirement?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Halons are highly effective at **3–5% concentration** because they extinguish fire primarily through **chemical flame inhibition** rather than cooling or oxygen displacement. When heated in the flame zone, halons release **bromine atoms**, which catalytically scavenge the chain-carrying free radicals **H•, O•, and OH•** that sustain combustion. Each bromine atom can neutralise **1,000–10,000 radicals** before being consumed, making the mechanism highly efficient. Additionally, halons are **electrically non-conductive**, leave **no residue**, and act within **seconds**, allowing protection of sensitive electronics and occupied spaces (in the case of Halon 1301). **(3 marks: 1 for mechanism, 1 for radical scavenging, 1 for additional advantages.)**

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe in detail the chemical mechanism by which halons extinguish fire. How does bromine contribute to flame inhibition?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The combustion of hydrocarbons proceeds via a **free-radical chain reaction** involving three key radical species: **H•, O•, and OH•**. The self-sustaining cycle is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{H•} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{OH•} + \text{O•} \\
\text{OH•} + \text{CO} &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H•}
\end{aligned}$$

This cycle continues indefinitely as long as fuel, oxygen, and heat are present.

When halons enter the flame zone, thermal decomposition releases **bromine atoms (Br•)**:

$$\text{CF}_3\text{Br} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CF}_3\text{•} + \text{Br•}$$

The bromine atoms then participate in radical-scavenging reactions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Br•} + \text{RH} &\rightarrow \text{HBr} + \text{R•} \\
\text{HBr} + \text{H•} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \text{Br•} \\
\text{HBr} + \text{OH•} &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br•} \\
\text{HBr} + \text{O•} &\rightarrow \text{OH•} + \text{Br•}
\end{aligned}$$

In each cycle, **one chain-carrying radical is removed** and **bromine is regenerated**, allowing catalytic action. The chain reaction is broken because the population of H•, O•, OH• falls below the critical threshold required to sustain combustion.

**Key points (7 marks):**
1. Identification of chain reaction mechanism — 2 marks
2. Halon thermal decomposition releasing Br• — 1 mark
3. Four scavenging reactions — 2 marks
4. Catalytic regeneration of Br• — 1 mark
5. Termination of chain reaction — 1 mark

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Halon 1301 and Halon 1211 in terms of toxicity, applications, and storage state. Under what circumstances is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Halon 1301 (CF₃Br) | Halon 1211 (CF₂ClBr) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Physical state at 25 °C | Gas (bp −57.8 °C) | Liquid (bp −3.9 °C) |
| Storage | Pressurised liquefied gas | Pressurised liquid |
| NOAEL | 5.0% | 0.5% |
| LOAEL | 7.5% | 1.0% |
| Design concentration | 5.0% | 5.5% |
| Occupancy suitability | **Safe for occupied spaces** | **NOT safe for occupied spaces** |
| Discharge mode | Total flooding (vapour) | Streaming (liquid-vapour) |
| Primary applications | Server rooms, control rooms, aircraft cargo bays, marine engine rooms, military electronics | Portable hand-held extinguishers, vehicle engines, electrical panels, industrial machinery |

**Selection criteria:**

- **Halon 1301** is preferred for **automatic total flooding systems** in **occupied enclosures** where human safety requires NOAEL ≥ design concentration, and where the protected asset is mission-critical (aircraft, telecom, military).

- **Halon 1211** is preferred for **streaming applications** (localised fire attack by trained operators), where the discharge plume is directed at the fire base and human exposure is brief and controlled.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly state that Halon 1211 is used for total flooding of occupied rooms. **This is FALSE and dangerous.** The design concentration of Halon 1211 (5.5%) exceeds its LOAEL (1.0%) by 5.5×, which would cause **cardiac sensitisation** and possible fatality in exposed personnel. This is a **guaranteed mark-loss zone** if answered incorrectly.

**Marking scheme (7 marks):**
1. Physical state and storage comparison — 1 mark
2. NOAEL/LOAEL values — 2 marks
3. Discharge mode (flooding vs streaming) — 1 mark
4. Occupancy suitability — 2 marks
5. Typical application areas — 1 mark

---

### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain the environmental impact of halons. Discuss Ozone Depletion Potential, Global Warming Potential, and the regulatory framework that led to their phase-out."**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Halons have three principal environmental impacts:

**(i) Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):**
Halons carry bromine atoms that, when photolysed in the stratosphere, release **Br• radicals** that catalytically destroy ozone via:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Br•} + \text{O}_3 &\rightarrow \text{BrO•} + \text{O}_2 \\
\text{BrO•} + \text{O} &\rightarrow \text{Br•} + \text{O}_2
\end{aligned}$$

This **ClOₓ-BrOₓ cycle** can destroy **~100,000 ozone molecules per Br atom** released. Halon 1301 has **ODP = 10** (10× the ozone-destruction of CFC-11), and Halon 1211 has **ODP = 3**, both extremely high.

**(ii) Global Warming Potential (GWP):**
Halon 1301 has **GWP = 6,900** over 100 years (i.e., 1 kg traps 6,900 kg of CO₂-equivalent heat). Halon 1211 has **GWP = 1,300**. These contribute substantially to radiative forcing.

**(iii) Atmospheric Lifetime:**
Halon 1301 persists for **65 years**; Halon 1211 persists for **16 years**, allowing long-duration stratospheric damage.

**Regulatory Framework:**

1. **1974** — Molina and Rowland publish CFC-ozone theory (*Nature*).
2. **1985** — British Antarctic Survey confirms Antarctic ozone hole.
3. **1987** — **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer** signed; halons placed in **Annex A, Group II**.
4. **1994** — Developed nations complete phase-out of halon production.
5. **2010** — Developing nations (including India) complete phase-out.
6. **India's Ozone Depleting Substances (Regulation) Rules, 2000** (amended 2007) provide domestic legal framework.

**Marking scheme (7 marks):**
1. ODP mechanism explained — 1.5 marks
2. ODP values for 1301 and 1211 — 1 mark
3. GWP explanation and values — 1.5 marks
4. Atmospheric lifetime values — 0.5 mark
5. Montreal Protocol chronology — 1.5 marks
6. India's domestic regulation — 1 mark

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Discuss the modern alternatives to halons. Compare FM-200, Novec 1230, and Inergen on the basis of extinguishing mechanism, environmental impact, and suitability for occupied spaces."**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

Three principal alternatives exist, each addressing the environmental hazards of halons:

| Property | FM-200 (HFC-227ea) | Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12) | Inergen (IG-541) |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------------|
| Chemical formula | C₃F₇H | CF₃CF₂C(O)CF(CF₃)₂ | N₂ 52% + Ar 40% + CO₂ 8% |
| Extinguishing mechanism | Chemical inhibition | Chemical inhibition | **Oxygen displacement** |
| Design concentration | 7.0% | 4.5% | 38–43% |
| NOAEL | 9.0% | 10.0% | 43% |
| ODP | **0** | **0** | **0** |
| GWP (100-yr) | 2,050 | **1** | 0 (natural gases) |
| Atmospheric lifetime | 33 years | **5 days** | N/A |
| Suitable for occupied | Yes | Yes | Yes |

**Discussion:**

- **FM-200** is the **direct drop-in** for Halon 1301 in existing systems. Same chemical inhibition mechanism, but zero ODP. Drawback: still has significant GWP (2,050) and 33-year atmospheric lifetime, so classified as a "transitional" agent.

- **Novec 1230** is the **current premium** clean agent. Extremely low GWP (1), ultra-short atmospheric lifetime (5 days), high NOAEL margin (10% vs 4.5% design). Drawback: higher cost and proprietary.

- **Inergen** uses a **completely different mechanism** — it is a blend of natural gases (N₂, Ar, CO₂) that reduces oxygen from 21% to ~12–14%, below the ~15% threshold needed to sustain most fires. The small CO₂ component (8%) stimulates human respiration, allowing safe evacuation. Zero ODP, zero GWP. Drawback: requires high-pressure storage (~200 bar) and larger pipework due to high concentration (38–43%).

**Marking scheme (7 marks):**
1. Identification of three alternatives — 1 mark
2. Mechanism comparison (chemical vs physical) — 1.5 marks
3. ODP/GWP values — 1.5 marks
4. Occupancy suitability — 1.5 marks
5. Practical trade-offs (cost, storage, transition path) — 1.5 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's General Pitfall Callout for Halon Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Halon 1301 with Halon 1211** in discharge mode (flooding vs streaming) — common 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Stating "halons deplete oxygen"** as their mechanism — WRONG; they inhibit chemistry. Oxygen displacement is the CO₂/Inergen mechanism.
> 3. **Forgetting that Halon 1211 is UNSAFE for occupied spaces** — design concentration exceeds LOAEL.
> 4. **Mixing up ODP values** between 1301 (=10) and 1211 (=3). Memorise: **bromine count + atmospheric lifetime** drives ODP.
> 5. **Citing Montreal Protocol year incorrectly** — it is 1987, not 1989 or 1992.
> 6. **Claiming halons are still manufactured** — they are not; they are reclaimed and recycled.
> 7. **Confusing GWP time horizon** — KTU expects 100-year GWP unless stated otherwise.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HALONS — KTU 2024 Scheme Rapid Revision Sheet**

**Core Identity:**
- Halon = halogenated hydrocarbon containing **C, F, Cl, Br** (H optional)
- Three commercial halons: **1301 (CF₃Br), 1211 (CF₂ClBr), 2402 (C₂F₄Br₂)**
- Numbering: **Halon ABCD** → C, F, Cl, Br atom counts

**Mechanism:**
- Primary = **chemical flame inhibition** (~70–80% effectiveness)
- Bromine atoms catalytically scavenge **H•, O•, OH•** radicals
- 1 Br atom neutralises ~10³–10⁴ radicals (catalytic cycle)
- Secondary = heat absorption; Tertiary = negligible O₂ displacement

**Toxicity Hierarchy (KTU's favourite):**
- **NOAEL** = no observable adverse effect level
- **LOAEL** = lowest observable adverse effect level
- **Halon 1301:** NOAEL 5.0%, LOAEL 7.5% → **safe for occupied spaces**
- **Halon 1211:** NOAEL 0.5%, LOAEL 1.0% → **unsafe for occupied spaces**

**Application Mapping:**
- **1301** = total flooding, occupied, server rooms, aircraft, military
- **1211** = streaming, unoccupied, portable extinguishers, electrical panels

**Environmental Trifecta (Memorise the Numbers):**
- **ODP:** 1301 = 10, 1211 = 3, CFC-11 = 1, CO₂ = 0
- **GWP (100-yr):** 1301 = 6,900, 1211 = 1,300, FM-200 = 2,050, Novec 1230 = 1, Inergen = 0
- **Atmospheric lifetime:** 1301 = 65 yr, 1211 = 16 yr, FM-200 = 33 yr, Novec 1230 = 5 days

**Regulatory Milestones:**
- 1974 — Molina–Rowland ozone depletion theory
- 1985 — Antarctic ozone hole confirmed
- **1987 — Montreal Protocol** (Annex A, Group II)
- 1994 — Developed-world phase-out
- 2010 — Developing-world phase-out (India)
- India: **ODS Rules 2000 (amended 2007)**; **BPCL Mumbai Halon Bank**

**Modern Alternatives (zero ODP required):**
- **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** — transitional, GWP 2,050
- **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)** — premium, GWP 1, lifetime 5 days
- **Inergen (IG-541)** — N₂/Ar/CO₂ blend, oxygen-displacement mechanism
- **Water mist** systems — non-chemical alternative

**Key Distinctions to Avoid Pitfalls:**
- Halon ≠ extinguishes by cooling (that's water/foam)
- Halon ≠ extinguishes by oxygen removal (that's CO₂/Inergen)
- Halon ≠ extinguishes by fuel removal (that's foam/blanketing)
- Halon **DOES** extinguish by **chemical chain-breaking** — its unique signature

**Numerical Anchor Values (commit to memory):**
- Extinguishing concentration of 1301 ≈ **5%**
- Specific volume of 1301 at 21 °C ≈ **0.148 m³/kg**
- Boiling point of 1301 = **−57.8 °C**
- Boiling point of 1211 = **−3.9 °C**
- Cardiac sensitisation in 1301 occurs only above **7.5%**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Different Types of Fire Fighting Systems: Fixed and Movable Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Different Types of Fire Fighting Systems: Fixed and Movable Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Fighting System** — An integrated combination of engineering hardware, detection-and-actuation components, distribution networks, and suppression media, designed to detect, control, or extinguish a fire at its incipient stage or to maintain it at a manageable level until final suppression is achieved.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme**, fire fighting systems are formally classified under **two broad super-categories** based on their **mobility, installation permanence, and operational philosophy**:

| Super-Category | Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) |
|---|---|
| **Fixed Fire Fighting System (FFFS)** | A permanently installed, plumbed-in suppression network that is mechanically or automatically actuated to deliver a fire-extinguishing medium (water, foam, gas, powder, mist) at the hazard location. |
| **Movable (Portable/Semi-Portable) Fire Fighting System** | A self-contained, transportable suppression unit — ranging from handheld extinguishers to wheeled trolleys and mobile monitors — that can be relocated to the fire scene by an operator. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two Armies of Fire Defence

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Think of a fire hazard as a hostile invasion.**
> - **Fixed Systems** = the *fortress walls and watchtowers* of a castle. They are built once, they sit permanently, and they activate automatically the instant the enemy (fire) appears within their sensor range.
> - **Movable Systems** = the *mobile knights and cavalry* on standby. They can be dispatched anywhere on the battlefield, but they need a human rider to activate them and direct them at the threat.

This analogy captures the **fundamental engineering trade-off**:

$$\text{Fixed Systems} = \text{High Reliability, Zero Manual Effort, High Capital Cost (CAPEX)}$$

$$\text{Movable Systems} = \text{High Flexibility, Low CAPEX, High Operator Dependency}$$

## 1.3 Why This Classification Matters (KTU Real-World Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Markers:**
> - **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — e.g., **IS 15105** (Sprinklers), **IS 636** (Hydrants)
> - **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** — Part 4 (Fire and Life Safety)
> - **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** guidelines for insurance grading
> - **NFPA (National Fire Protection Association)** — NFPA 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 20

A senior fire engineer designing a refinery, hospital, or IT park must **always** deploy a *hybrid* solution — fixed systems for guaranteed coverage of high-risk zones, supplemented by movable systems for spot suppression, mobile response, and operator-controlled rescue operations.

## 1.4 High-Level Classification Map

> [!NOTE]
> The complete two-tier taxonomy as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus is summarized below. Each branch will be unpacked in **SECTION 2**.

**A. Fixed Fire Fighting Systems**
1. Water-based: Sprinkler, Hydrant & Hose Reel, Deluge, Water Mist, Water Spray (Deluge-type cooling)
2. Foam-based: High/Medium/Low Expansion Foam Pourers, Foam Pourers, Foam Water Sprinkler
3. Gas-based: Clean Agent (FM-200 / Novec 1230), Inert Gas (IG-541 / IG-100), CO₂
4. Powder-based: Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Skid Systems
5. Specialized: Aerosol Suppression, Wet Chemical (for Class K fires)

**B. Movable Fire Fighting Systems**
1. Portable Fire Extinguishers (PFE): Water, Foam, CO₂, DCP, Clean Agent, Wet Chemical
2. Trolley-Mounted (Wheeled) Extinguishers: 25 kg to 150 kg DCP / CO₂ / Foam
3. Mobile Monitors: Trailer/Pick-up mounted water/foam monitors
4. Fire Tenders / Fire Engines (Pumper appliances)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy — Fixed Fire Fighting Systems (FFFS)

A Fixed Fire Fighting System is characterised by **three mandatory engineering attributes**:
1. **Permanently piped** distribution network (above or below ground).
2. **Dedicated supply** (water tank, gas cylinders, foam concentrate drum).
3. **Automatic or remote-actuated** initiation mechanism (sprinkler bulb, heat detector, smoke detector, manual call point, deluge valve).

### 2.1.1 Water-Based Fixed Systems

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **workhorses** of industrial and commercial fire protection, accounting for ~**80%** of installed FFFS globally.

#### (a) Sprinkler System (IS 15105 / NFPA 13)
- A **closed-heat-actuated** discharge device installed at spaced intervals on a pressurized water piping network.
- Each sprinkler head has a **thermal element (glass bulb or fusible link)** that breaks/melts at a pre-set temperature (**68°C, 79°C, 93°C, 141°C, 182°C, 227°C, 260°C** — color-coded).
- Only sprinklers directly above the fire operate → **localized suppression**.

**Sub-classifications of Sprinkler Systems:**

| Type | Trigger | Operation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wet Pipe** | Individual sprinkler bulb | Water always in pipes; only affected head opens | Offices, hotels, hospitals |
| **Dry Pipe** | Air-pressure drop → valve opens | Air-pressurized; water admitted on actuation | Freezers, unheated warehouses |
| **Pre-Action** | Detection system + sprinkler | Two-step verification → prevents accidental discharge | Data centers, museums |
| **Deluge** | Detection system → deluge valve opens | All heads open simultaneously — water 'deluges' zone | Transformers, aircraft hangars |
| **ESFR (Early Suppression Fast Response)** | High-sensitivity bulb | Designed to suppress high-challenge fires | High-rack warehouses (NFPA 13) |

#### (b) Hydrant System (IS 636 / NFPA 14)
- An **above-ground or underground** network of pressurized water mains with **outlet valves (landing valves)** at strategic points.
- Used by **trained fire-fighters** with hoses, branch pipes, and nozzles.

**Components:** Fire water storage tank → Pumping system (Jockey + Electric + Diesel) → Piping network → **External Hydrants** (yard hydrants) + **Internal Hydrants** (landing valves on each floor).

#### (c) Hose Reel System (IS 884 / IS 903)
- **First-aid fire fighting** device: 30 m of 19/25 mm hose on a swinging drum.
- Supplied directly from the building's riser main.
- Discharge rate ≈ **0.46 L/s (27.6 L/min)** at 0.7–1.0 MPa pressure.

#### (d) Water Spray / Water Mist System (IS 15325 / NFPA 15 & 750)
- **Water Spray:** Uses open spray nozzles; used for **cooling** of LPG vessels, transformers, cable trays.
- **Water Mist:** Discharges extremely fine droplets (D < 1000 μm) — cools + displaces oxygen. Used in **machinery spaces, marine engine rooms, archives**.

#### (e) Drencher System
- Specialized water curtain on **building façades** to prevent fire spread via windows. NFPA-aligned design.

### 2.1.2 Foam-Based Fixed Systems (NFPA 11 / IS 15519)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Foam systems are **mandatory** for Class B (flammable liquid) hazards — petroleum refineries, aircraft hangars, bulk fuel storage.

| Foam Type | Expansion Ratio | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Expansion (AFFF, AFFF-AR, FP, FFFP)** | 5 : 1 to 20 : 1 | Pool fires, spills, tank farms |
| **Medium Expansion** | 20 : 1 to 200 : 1 | LNG spills, marine |
| **High Expansion** | 200 : 1 to 1000 : 1 | Warehouses, basements (volume flooding) |

**Sub-systems:** Foam pourers (in-tank), foam monitors (fixed), foam-water sprinkler, foam-water spray, bladder-tank proportioner, balanced-pressure proportioner, in-line inductor.

### 2.1.3 Gas-Based Fixed Systems (NFPA 12 / 2001)

Used where water damage is unacceptable — **server rooms, control rooms, archives, museums, telecom exchanges**.

| Agent | Type | ODP / GWP | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂** | Inert (chemical asphyxiant) | ODP = 0, GWP = 1 | Electrical, transformer, paint rooms |
| **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** | Clean chemical | ODP = 0, GWP = 3500 | Server rooms (now phase-out) |
| **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)** | Clean chemical | ODP = 0, GWP = 1 | Modern alternative to FM-200 |
| **IG-541 (Inergen)** | Inert mix (N₂ 52% + Ar 40% + CO₂ 8%) | ODP = 0, GWP = 0 | Green alternative |
| **IG-100 (Nitrogen)** | Inert | ODP = 0, GWP = 0 | Marine, archives |

### 2.1.4 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Fixed Systems (NFPA 17)
- Pressurized **ABC or BC powder** stored in cylinders, piped via manifold to discharge nozzles.
- Used for **flammable liquid, gas, and electrical fires** in process industries.

### 2.1.5 Wet Chemical Fixed Systems (NFPA 17A)
- **Class K (Class F)** — Cooking oil / fat fires in commercial kitchens.
- Agent: Potassium acetate / citrate solution; reacts with hot oil to form soap-like barrier (saponification).

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy — Movable Fire Fighting Systems

### 2.2.1 Portable Fire Extinguishers (IS 2190 / IS 15683)

> [!NOTE]
> **Most ubiquitous movable fire fighting device.** Categorized by **IS 15683** into 9 classes (A, B, C, D, E, F, K, plus rechargeable / non-rechargeable).

| Class | Colour Code (IS) | Use | Medium |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Red band | Wood, paper, textile | Water, foam, ABC powder |
| **B** | Blue band | Flammable liquids | Foam, ABC, CO₂, DCP |
| **C** | Cream band | Gases | DCP (BC), CO₂ |
| **D** | Yellow band | Metals (Na, Mg, Ti) | Special dry powder (NaCl/Graphite) |
| **E** | Black band | Electrical (up to 1 kV) | CO₂, Clean Agent |
| **F (K)** | Olive band | Cooking oil | Wet chemical |

**Standard Capacities (IS 15683):** 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9 kg (handheld); up to 25 kg (wheeled).

### 2.2.2 Wheeled (Trolley-Mounted) Extinguishers (IS 16018)
- **Capacity:** 25 kg to **150 kg** of ABC / CO₂ / Foam.
- Mounted on a wheeled trolley for **single-operator deployment**.
- Used in **lube oil rooms, transformer yards, bulk storage areas**.

### 2.2.3 Mobile Monitors
- **Manual or remote-controlled** high-flow water/foam monitors mounted on **trolleys, trailers, or vehicle chassis**.
- Flow rates: **1000 L/min to 5000 L/min**.
- Used in petroleum depots, shipyards, large industrial yards.

### 2.2.4 Fire Tenders (Fire Engines / Pumpers)
- Self-propelled, fully equipped fire-fighting vehicles.
- **Pump capacity:** 2000 L/min to 6000 L/min (depending on chassis and class).
- Carry water, foam, hose, BA (breathing apparatus), rescue tools.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU student is expected to apply in Module 4 numericals.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Notation & Units | Validity / Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sprinkler discharge flow rate | $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ | $Q$ = flow (L/min), $K$ = K-factor (L/min·bar$^{1/2}$), $P$ = pressure (bar) | Sprinkler head selection |
| 2 | K-factor for standard orifice | $K = 0.054 \cdot d^{2}$ | $d$ = orifice diameter (mm) | Theoretical orifice discharge |
| 3 | Velocity pressure at nozzle | $P_v = \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | $P_v$ in m head, $V$ in m/s, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ | Bernoulli-derived |
| 4 | Nozzle discharge (orifice) | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | $C_d$ = 0.97 (smooth nozzle), $A$ = orifice area (m²), $H$ = head (m) | Discharge coefficient formula |
| 5 | Hazen-Williams head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | $L$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m, $C$ = pipe roughness constant | Friction loss in pipes |
| 6 | Pipe water velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $D$ in m | Continuity equation |
| 7 | Water tank capacity | $V_{tank} = Q_{design} \cdot t_{duration}$ | $V$ in m³, $Q$ in m³/h, $t$ in h | NBC / TAC design duration |
| 8 | Foam concentrate quantity | $V_{foam} = Q_{water} \cdot \dfrac{\%}{100} \cdot t$ | $Q$ in L/min, $t$ in min, % = proportioning % | Foam system design |
| 9 | Foam solution flow | $Q_{soln} = Q_{water} + Q_{foam\ concentrate}$ | All in L/min | Mass balance |
| 10 | Pump power (BHP) | $BHP = \dfrac{Q \cdot H \cdot \rho}{75 \cdot \eta}$ | $Q$ in L/s, $H$ in m, $\rho$ in kg/L, $\eta$ = efficiency | Pump selection |
| 11 | Pressure-conversion | $1\ \text{bar} = 10.197\ \text{m of water} = 14.503\ \text{psi}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 12 | Density of water application (sprinkler) | $D_{density} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{A_{operational}}$ | L/min/m² | NFPA / IS design criterion |
| 13 | Hydraulic horsepower | $HHP = \dfrac{Q \cdot P}{75}$ | $Q$ in L/s, $P$ in kg/cm² | Hydraulic calculations |
| 14 | Cylinder quantity (gas system) | $N_{cyl} = \dfrac{V_{room} \cdot C_{design}}{\rho_{agent} \cdot V_{cyl}}$ | $C$ = design concentration (%) | Clean agent design |
| 15 | Extinguisher discharge time | $t_{disc} = \dfrac{V_{capacity}}{Q_{rate}}$ | $V$ in kg/L, $Q$ in kg/min or L/min | IS 15683 verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default KTU Examinable Constants:**
> - $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$
> - $1\ \text{atm} = 1.013\ \text{bar} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
> - Density of water $\rho_{water} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^{3} = 1\ \text{kg/L}$
> - $1\ \text{bar} \approx 10.2\ \text{m of water column (mwc)}$

---

## 2.4 Selection Criteria — Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision logic used by practising Fire Engineers:**

$$\boxed{\text{System Selection} = f(\text{Hazard Class},\ \text{Risk Magnitude},\ \text{Occupancy},\ \text{Environment},\ \text{Cost})}$$

**Hazard-Driven Choice Table:**

| Hazard | Recommended Fixed System | Recommended Movable Supplement |
|---|---|---|
| Office / IT Park | Wet-pipe sprinkler + Hose reel + Clean agent (server room) | CO₂ / ABC extinguisher on each floor |
| Petroleum Refinery | Foam pourer + Water spray + DCP skid | Foam / DCP trolley + Mobile monitors |
| Hospital | Wet-pipe sprinkler + Pre-action (MRI) | CO₂ / Clean agent extinguishers |
| Data Centre | Pre-action sprinkler + FM-200/Novec 1230 | CO₂ trolley at entrance |
| Commercial Kitchen | Wet chemical fixed system | K-class extinguisher + fire blanket |
| Marine Engine Room | High-pressure water mist + CO₂ | Foam / DCP portable sets |
| Transformer Yard | Deluge / Water spray | DCP trolley (50 kg) + CO₂ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the four most exam-critical formulas. Every algebraic transition is written out; no step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Sprinkler Discharge Equation $Q = K\sqrt{P}$

### Physical Basis
A sprinkler head is essentially an **orifice** discharging water from a pressurized pipe into atmosphere. The fundamental discharge of an orifice is governed by **Torricelli's Law**, integrated with the orifice discharge coefficient.

### Step 1 — Torricelli's Velocity
For an ideal fluid issuing from a small orifice under a static head $H$:

$$V = \sqrt{2gH}$$

Here, $V$ is the theoretical exit velocity, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, $H$ is the head driving the flow.

### Step 2 — Introduce Discharge Coefficient
Real orifices exhibit friction and contraction losses, captured by $C_d$ (discharge coefficient):

$$V_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Head to Pressure
Using the hydrostatic relation $P = \rho g H$, we solve for $H$:

$$H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$$

Substituting back into the velocity equation:

$$V_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot \dfrac{P}{\rho g}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by Area to Get Flow
Volumetric flow rate $Q = A \cdot V_{actual}$:

$$Q = A \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2P}{\rho}}$$

### Step 5 — Bundle Constants into K-Factor
All geometry-dependent terms ($A$, $C_d$, $1/\sqrt{\rho}$) are constants for a given sprinkler. We combine them into a single coefficient $K$:

$$Q = K \cdot \sqrt{P}$$

where the K-factor is:

$$K = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Form (Sprinkler Industry Convention)
When $Q$ is in L/min and $P$ is in bar, the industry-standard K-factor is tabulated directly by manufacturers. For example, a sprinkler with **K = 80** discharges:

$$Q = 80 \cdot \sqrt{P}\quad \text{(L/min, bar)}$$

So at $P = 1\ \text{bar}$: $Q = 80 \cdot 1 = 80\ \text{L/min}$
At $P = 2\ \text{bar}$: $Q = 80 \cdot 1.414 = 113.1\ \text{L/min}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Hazen-Williams Friction Loss

### Why Hazen-Williams (and not Darcy-Weisbach)?
For water at ordinary temperatures (5°C–25°C), the kinematic viscosity is nearly constant, so the friction factor $f$ can be expressed empirically as a function of pipe roughness $C$, eliminating the need for a Moody chart. **Hazen-Williams** is the **mandatory formula** in IS 636 / NFPA 14 / NFPA 13 for water-based fire protection design.

### Step 1 — Starting Empirical Form
The empirical SI form (from Hazen & Williams, 1905) for head loss per unit length is:

$$\dfrac{h_f}{L} = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

where $h_f$ is head loss in metres, $L$ is length in metres, $Q$ is flow in m³/s, $D$ is pipe diameter in metres, $C$ is the Hazen-Williams roughness coefficient.

### Step 2 — Multiply by L to get Total Head Loss

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Typical Values for Fire Engineering
For a **mild-steel cement-lined pipe**, $C \approx 120$.
For **uPVC / HDPE**, $C \approx 140$.
For **cast iron (old)**, $C \approx 90$.

### Worked Numerical
**Problem:** A 200 mm (D = 0.2 m) diameter cement-lined pipe ($C = 120$) carries $Q = 0.05$ m³/s over a length of $L = 50$ m. Compute friction loss.

**Step 3a — Substitute into equation:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot 50 \cdot (0.05)^{1.852}}{(120)^{1.852} \cdot (0.2)^{4.87}}$$

**Step 3b — Compute numerator:** $10.67 \cdot 50 = 533.5$

$(0.05)^{1.852} = 0.05^{1.852}$

Using logs: $\log(0.05) = -1.3010$; $-1.3010 \cdot 1.852 = -2.4095$; $10^{-2.4095} \approx 0.003890$

Numerator $= 533.5 \cdot 0.003890 = 2.075$

**Step 3c — Compute denominator:** $(120)^{1.852} = 10^{1.852 \cdot \log 120}$

$\log 120 = 2.0792$; $2.0792 \cdot 1.852 = 3.851$; $10^{3.851} \approx 7096$

$(0.2)^{4.87} = 10^{4.87 \cdot \log 0.2} = 10^{4.87 \cdot (-0.6990)} = 10^{-3.404} \approx 0.0003946$

Denominator $= 7096 \cdot 0.0003946 = 2.800$

**Step 3d — Final head loss:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{2.075}{2.800} \approx 0.741\ \text{m of water column}$$

So the friction loss is approximately **0.74 mwc** (≈ 0.073 bar). This is added to the static and elevation heads when sizing the fire pump.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Design Density of Water Application

### Physical Meaning
The "**design density**" (NFPA term: **Area × Density**) is the heart of sprinkler system design. It quantifies the rate at which water must be applied (in L/min/m²) over a given floor area (in m²) to suppress or control the fire.

### Step 1 — Definition
Design density $d$ is the discharge per unit area:

$$d = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{A_{operation}}$$

where $Q_{total}$ is the total flow from all sprinklers expected to operate, and $A_{operation}$ is the most hydraulically demanding design area (per IS 15105 / NFPA 13).

### Step 2 — Total Flow from Sprinklers
If $N$ sprinklers operate, each at $K_i$ and pressure $P_i$:

$$Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} K_i \cdot \sqrt{P_i}$$

### Step 3 — Operational Area
IS 15105 specifies **maximum design areas** for different hazard classes:

| Hazard Class | Design Density $d$ | Max Design Area |
|---|---|---|
| **LH (Light Hazard)** | 2.25 L/min/m² | 72 m² |
| **OH-1 (Ordinary Hazard Group 1)** | 5.0 L/min/m² | 72 m² |
| **OH-2 (Ordinary Hazard Group 2)** | 5.0 L/min/m² | 144 m² |
| **OH-3 (Ordinary Hazard Group 3)** | 5.0 L/min/m² | 216 m² |
| **HHP-1 (High Hazard Process 1)** | 12.5 L/min/m² | 260 m² |
| **HHP-2 (High Hazard Process 2)** | 25.0 L/min/m² | 260 m² |

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical
**Problem:** An OH-2 sprinkler installation has 12 sprinklers operating in the design area, each at $P = 1.5\ \text{bar}$ with $K = 80$. Calculate design density and verify against IS 15105 OH-2 criterion.

**Step 4a — Flow per sprinkler:**

$$Q_i = K \cdot \sqrt{P} = 80 \cdot \sqrt{1.5} = 80 \cdot 1.2247 = 97.98\ \text{L/min}$$

**Step 4b — Total flow:**

$$Q_{total} = 12 \cdot 97.98 = 1175.76\ \text{L/min}$$

**Step 4c — Design density (assume operation area = 144 m²):**

$$d = \dfrac{1175.76}{144} = 8.165\ \text{L/min/m}^{2}$$

**Step 4d — Verification:**
IS 15105 requires $d \geq 5.0\ \text{L/min/m}^{2}$ for OH-2. Since $8.165 > 5.0$, the design is **acceptable**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Foam Concentrate Quantity

### Physical Basis
Foam concentrate is **proportioned** (typically 1%, 3%, or 6%) into the water stream by a proportioner. The foam concentrate volume required depends on the water flow rate, the proportioning ratio, and the discharge duration.

### Step 1 — Foam Concentrate Flow Rate
The instantaneous rate of foam concentrate consumption:

$$Q_{foam} = Q_{water} \cdot \dfrac{p}{100}$$

where $p$ is the proportioning percentage (commonly 3% for AFFF, 6% for AFFF-AR or FP).

### Step 2 — Total Foam Concentrate Volume
Integrating over the discharge duration $t$:

$$V_{foam} = Q_{water} \cdot \dfrac{p}{100} \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical
**Problem:** A foam pourer system delivers $Q_{water} = 1500\ \text{L/min}$ of water using 3% AFFF for a discharge duration of $t = 30$ min. Compute the foam concentrate consumption.

**Step 3a — Substituting:**

$$V_{foam} = 1500 \cdot \dfrac{3}{100} \cdot 30 = 1500 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 30 = 1350\ \text{L}$$

**Step 3b — Interpret:** 1350 L of AFFF concentrate must be stored. With a specific gravity of ~1.02, the **mass** required is:

$$M_{foam} = V_{foam} \cdot \rho_{foam} = 1350 \cdot 1.02 \approx 1377\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3c — Engineering Practice:** A safety factor of **1.25×** is applied to allow for testing losses, spillage, and demand growth:

$$V_{design} = 1350 \cdot 1.25 = 1687.5\ \text{L (or rounded to 1700 L)}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fixed vs Movable Systems

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is **high-yield** for KTU 14-mark questions asking for comparative analysis.

| Evaluation Axis | Fixed System | Movable System |
|---|---|---|
| **Installation Type** | Permanent (plumbed) | Transportable |
| **Capital Cost (CAPEX)** | High (₹₹₹₹) | Low to Moderate (₹₹) |
| **Operating Cost (OPEX)** | Low (annual test) | Moderate (refilling every 2–5 yrs) |
| **Response Time** | Automatic → seconds | Manual → minutes |
| **Operator Skill Needed** | Minimal (automatic) | High (training mandatory) |
| **Coverage Area** | Total facility (designed) | Localized (10–25 m radius) |
| **Reliability** | Very high (no human intervention) | Medium (depends on operator) |
| **Flexibility** | Low (fixed location) | High (redeployable) |
| **Maintenance** | Annual + 5-yr overhaul | Annual refill + hydro-test every 5 yrs |
| **Best Suited For** | High-risk, occupied, large facilities | Supplementary, small facilities, incipient stage |
| **Standards** | NFPA 13/14/15/16/17, IS 15105, IS 636 | IS 15683, IS 16018, IS 2190 |
| **Examples** | Sprinkler, hydrant, foam pourer, CO₂ flooding | Portable extinguisher, trolley, mobile monitor |
