## 2.1 The Fire Tetrahedron — How CO2 Attacks It

Fire requires **four elements** (modern Fire Tetrahedron): **(1) Fuel, (2) Heat, (3) Oxygen, (4) Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction.** CO2 attacks **two** of these simultaneously:

1. **Oxygen Elimination (Smothering)**
   - CO2 displaces atmospheric air.
   - Fire suffocates when **O2 concentration drops below 15%** (normal air = 21%).
   - For complete flood of an enclosed room: CO2 concentration must reach **30–50%** by volume.

2. **Heat Removal (Cooling)**
   - Liquid CO2 absorbs **571 kJ/kg** during phase change.
   - The "snow" formed at the discharge horn extracts heat directly from the fuel surface.
   - Temperature at the fire zone drops to approximately **−78 °C**, well below the auto-ignition temperature of most flammable liquids.

## 2.2 Constructional Components (Layer-by-Layer Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every CO2 extinguisher has 6 mandatory components as per IS 2878:**

| Component | Function | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Seamless Steel Cylinder** | Stores liquefied CO2 at ~70 bar | DOT/ISI tested; must withstand hydraulic test at 250 bar |
| **Filler Valve / Master Shut-Off Valve** | Filling point during recharge; seals cylinder | Has safety bursting disc set at ~190 bar |
| **Siphon Tube (Dip Tube)** | Reaches bottom of cylinder to draw liquid CO2 | Without this, only gaseous CO2 (useless) would discharge |
| **Head Assembly with Lever** | Trigger mechanism; opens main valve | Squeeze-grip type; instantly activates discharge |
| **High-Pressure Hose (Reinforced)** | Carries liquid CO2 from valve to horn | Burst pressure ≥ 100 bar; anti-static |
| **Discharge Horn (Nozzle)** | Expands & atomizes liquid CO2; prevents dry-ice burn injury | **Non-metallic (fibreglass / plastic)** to avoid shock and cold burns |

## 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Trigger:** Operator squeezes the **squeeze-grip lever** on the head assembly.
**Step 2 — Valve Opening:** The **main valve** opens, allowing high-pressure liquid CO2 to travel up the **siphon tube**.
**Step 3 — Pressurized Flow:** Liquid CO2 at **~55–70 bar** rushes through the **high-pressure hose**.
**Step 4 — Expansion at Horn:** At the **discharge horn**, the sudden drop in pressure (atmospheric ~1 bar) causes liquid CO2 to **expand 450–500 times**, instantly vaporizing into a cold, dense gas-snow mixture at **−78 °C**.
**Step 5 — Fire Attack:** The cold CO2 blanket **(a) cools** the fire zone and **(b) displaces oxygen**, simultaneously breaking the combustion chain.
**Step 6 — Self-Vent:** The non-metallic horn acts as a **pressure-relief vent**, ensuring safe discharge and preventing frostbite to the operator.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly tested in KTU ESE** for Fire Engineering. Memorize the units carefully.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weight of CO2 required for room flooding** | $W_{CO_2} = V \times (C \times \rho_{CO_2})$ | kg | $V$ = room volume (m³), $C$ = target concentration |
| 2 | **Volume of CO2 gas at NTP** | $V_{gas} = \frac{W \times 22.4}{44}$ | m³ | Derived from ideal gas at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm) |
| 3 | **Expansion ratio (Liquid → Gas)** | $R = \frac{V_{gas}}{V_{liq}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 450–500 |
| 4 | **Discharge Rate (mass flow)** | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | kg/s | $C_d \approx 0.85$ for nozzle |
| 5 | **Oxygen displacement check** | $\%O_2 = \frac{21 \cdot V_{air}}{V_{air}+V_{CO_2}}$ | % | Must fall below 15 % for extinguishment |
| 6 | **Heat absorbed during vaporization** | $Q = m \cdot L$ | kJ | $L = 571 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| 7 | **Discharge duration** | $t = \frac{W_{stored}}{\dot{m}}$ | seconds | KTU standard: 8–30 s for portable |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Server Rooms / Data Centres:** Water or powder extinguishers would destroy electronics worth crores. CO2 leaves zero residue — only clean gas.
- **Transformer Yards & Switchgear Rooms:** Live electrical equipment at 11 kV / 33 kV / 66 kV — only CO2 or clean agents are safe.
- **Aviation Hangars:** Aircraft fuel fires (Class B) need rapid oxygen suppression without contaminating avionics.
- **Laboratories:** Solvent and chemical fires where water reactions are dangerous.
- **Marine Engine Rooms:** Fuel-oil fires in confined bilge spaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question mentions "live electrical fire" or "flammable liquid (petrol/diesel) fire," the correct answer is almost always **CO2 type extinguisher** — never water, never foam, never dry powder (unless specified as the *only* available option).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Weight of CO2 Required for Total Flooding of an Enclosed Room

This is the **most frequently asked numerical problem** in KTU Module 4. We will derive it from first principles.

### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem)
- Room dimensions: **Length (L) = 8 m**, **Breadth (B) = 6 m**, **Height (H) = 4 m**
- Required CO2 concentration for extinguishment: **C = 34 % by volume**
- Density of CO2 gas at NTP: **ρCO2 = 1.84 kg/m³**

### Step 1 — Calculate the Total Volume of the Enclosed Room

The volume of a rectangular enclosure is simply:

$$
V_{room} = L \times B \times H
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
V_{room} = 8 \text{ m} \times 6 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
V_{room} = 192 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Interpretation:** We have a closed room of 192 cubic metres in which a fire has broken out. We need to fill this room with enough CO2 such that the air-oxygen level drops below the combustion-support threshold.

### Step 2 — Determine the Volume of CO2 Gas Required

Since the target concentration is 34 % by volume, the required CO2 gas volume is:

$$
V_{CO_2}^{gas} = C \times V_{room}
$$

$$
V_{CO_2}^{gas} = 0.34 \times 192 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
V_{CO_2}^{gas} = 65.28 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Interpretation:** We need to release at least 65.28 cubic metres of CO2 gas into the room to achieve the 34 % concentration threshold. Note: this is the *gaseous* volume at NTP, not the liquid stored volume.

### Step 3 — Convert Gas Volume into Mass (Weight) of CO2

The density of CO2 gas at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm) is:

$$
\rho_{CO_2} = 1.84 \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

Therefore, the required mass of CO2 gas is:

$$
W_{CO_2} = V_{CO_2}^{gas} \times \rho_{CO_2}
$$

$$
W_{CO_2} = 65.28 \text{ m}^{3} \times 1.84 \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

$$
W_{CO_2} \approx 120.12 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Mass of CO2 Gas to Volume of Liquid CO2 to be Stored

Since CO2 is stored as a **liquid** in the cylinder, we apply the expansion ratio.

$$
V_{liq} = \frac{V_{gas}}{R}
$$

Using a standard expansion ratio **R = 450**:

$$
V_{liq} = \frac{65.28 \text{ m}^{3}}{450}
$$

$$
V_{liq} \approx 0.145 \text{ m}^{3} = 145 \text{ litres}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Engineering Decision (Cylinder Selection)

Each standard CO2 cylinder of portable size holds approximately **4.5 kg to 22.5 kg** of liquid CO2. For our requirement of **120.12 kg**, the fire-engineering design team would specify:

$$
N_{cylinders} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{CO_2}}{W_{per\_cylinder}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{120.12}{22.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.34 \rceil = 6 \text{ cylinders}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A total of **6 cylinders of 22.5 kg capacity each** must be manifolded together and discharged simultaneously through a fixed flooding system.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CO2 vs Other Extinguishing Media

| Parameter | Water | Foam | Dry Powder | **CO2** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrical Fire Safe? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (limited) | ✅✅ **Best** |
| Residue Left | Wet | Sticky | Cloudy Dust | **None (Clean)** |
| Smothering Effect | Weak | Good | Good | **Excellent** |
| Cooling Effect | Strong | Weak | Weak | **Excellent (–78 °C)** |
| Cost per kg | Low | Medium | Medium | **High** |
| Recharge Difficulty | Easy | Easy | Medium | **Specialized** |
| Suitable for Class C? | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅✅ |

## 3.3 KTU Numerical Template — Quick Plug-and-Play

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Shortcut (KTU Examiner Approved):**
> For any room flooding problem, follow this 4-line template:
> 1. $V_{room} = L \times B \times H$
> 2. $V_{CO_2} = C \times V_{room}$ (where $C$ = 0.30 to 0.50)
> 3. $W_{CO_2} = V_{CO_2} \times 1.84$
> 4. $N_{cylinders} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{CO_2}}{22.5} \right\rceil$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component-Level Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Seamless Steel Cylinder - Stores Liquefied CO2 at 55-70 bar] --> B[Filler Valve with Safety Bursting Disc at 190 bar]
    B --> C[Siphon Dip Tube - Reaches Bottom of Cylinder]
    C --> D[Main Control Head Assembly]
    D --> E[Pressure Gauge - Indicates Cylinder Status]
    D --> F[Squeeze Grip Lever - Operator Trigger]
    F --> G[Main Valve - Opens on Lever Press]
    G --> H[High Pressure Reinforced Hose]
    H --> I[Non Metallic Discharge Horn - Fibreglass]
    I --> J[CO2 Gas Snow Cloud at minus 78 deg C]
    J --> K[Fire Zone - Class B and Class C]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style J fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    style K fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Liquid CO2 to Extinguishment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Liquid CO2 at 70 bar] --> B[Trigger Lever Squeezed]
    B --> C[Valve Opens - Siphon Activated]
    C --> D[Liquid Rises through Dip Tube]
    D --> E[Flow through High Pressure Hose]
    E --> F[Sudden Pressure Drop at Horn]
    F --> G[Rapid Expansion 1 to 450]
    G --> H[Phase Change - Liquid to Gas Plus Snow]
    H --> I[Temperature drops to minus 78 deg C]
    I --> J[Latent Heat Absorption - 571 kJ per kg]
    J --> K[Oxygen Displacement - falls below 15 percent]
    K --> L[Fire Extinguished]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style B fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#ffc000,color:#000
    style I fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff
    style L fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Fire Tetrahedron Attack Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FIRE TETRAHEDRON] --> B[Fuel]
    A --> C[Heat]
    A --> D[Oxygen]
    A --> E[Chemical Chain Reaction]

    D --> F[CO2 DISPLACES O2 - Smothering]
    C --> G[CO2 ABSORBS HEAT - Latent Heat 571 kJ/kg]

    F --> H[FIRE EXTINGUISHED]
    G --> H

    style A fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff
    style F fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style G fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style H fill:#70ad47,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain in brief why CO2 is the preferred extinguishing medium for electrical fires.**

**Model Answer (KTU Examiner Key Pattern):**

CO2 is preferred for electrical fires for **three precise reasons**:
1. **Non-Conductive:** CO2 gas has extremely high dielectric strength and does not conduct electricity up to **1000 V**, making it safe to apply on live electrical equipment.
2. **No Residue / Clean Agent:** Unlike dry powder or foam, CO2 evaporates completely leaving **zero residue**, preventing damage to delicate electronic components such as PCBs, relays, and circuit breakers.
3. **Dual Extinguishing Action:** It simultaneously **displaces oxygen** (smothering) and **cools the fire** (latent heat of vaporization = 571 kJ/kg), achieving rapid knockdown in **8–30 seconds**.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List the main components of a portable CO2 fire extinguisher as per IS 2878.**

**Model Answer:**

The six main components as per **IS 2878** are: **(1)** Seamless steel cylinder, **(2)** Filler valve with safety bursting disc, **(3)** Siphon dip tube, **(4)** Head assembly with squeeze-grip lever, **(5)** High-pressure reinforced hose, and **(6)** Non-metallic discharge horn (fibreglass/plastic).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **CO2 type fire extinguisher**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A server room of dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 4 m** catches fire. Calculate the **mass of CO2 required** for complete flooding if the design concentration is **40 %** by volume. Density of CO2 at NTP = **1.84 kg/m³**. Also determine the number of **22.5 kg cylinders** needed. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A CO2 fire extinguisher consists of:

1. **Cylinder** — A seamless steel cylinder stores **liquefied CO2** at approximately **55–70 bar** (≈ 800–1000 psi) at 21 °C. The cylinder is hydraulically tested at **250 bar** as per IS 2878.
2. **Filler Valve** — Located on top of the cylinder, used for refilling. Contains a **safety bursting disc** set to rupture at ~190 bar for over-pressure protection.
3. **Siphon Tube** — A hollow tube extending to the bottom of the cylinder, ensuring that **liquid** CO2 (not gas) is discharged when the valve is opened.
4. **Head Assembly & Lever** — The squeeze-grip lever opens the main valve instantly, allowing pressurized liquid CO2 to flow up the siphon tube.
5. **High-Pressure Hose** — Reinforced rubber hose carries the liquid CO2 from the cylinder to the discharge horn.
6. **Discharge Horn** — A **non-metallic** (fibreglass) trumpet-shaped nozzle where the liquid CO2 expands suddenly into gas-snow mixture at **−78 °C**.

**Working Principle:** When the lever is squeezed, the main valve opens. Liquid CO2 at high pressure travels up the siphon tube, through the hose, and into the horn. At the horn, sudden depressurization causes the liquid to expand **450 times**, absorbing **571 kJ/kg** of heat (latent heat of vaporization) and forming a cold CO2 blanket that **(i) cools the fire** and **(ii) displaces oxygen**, thereby extinguishing the fire.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Volume of the Room:**

$$
V_{room} = L \times B \times H
$$

$$
V_{room} = 10 \times 8 \times 4
$$

$$
V_{room} = 320 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of CO2 Gas Required (at 40 % concentration):**

$$
V_{CO_2} = 0.40 \times 320
$$

$$
V_{CO_2} = 128 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CO2 Required:**

$$
W_{CO_2} = V_{CO_2} \times \rho_{CO_2}
$$

$$
W_{CO_2} = 128 \times 1.84
$$

$$
W_{CO_2} = 235.52 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of 22.5 kg Cylinders Required:**

$$
N = \frac{235.52}{22.5}
$$

$$
N = 10.47
$$

Since we cannot use a fractional cylinder, we round **up** to ensure full extinguishment:

$$
N = 11 \text{ cylinders of } 22.5 \text{ kg each}
$$

**Final Answer:** The total mass of CO2 required is **≈ 235.52 kg**, requiring **11 standard cylinders** of 22.5 kg capacity each.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of CO2 fire extinguishers. Compare them with **dry chemical powder** extinguishers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **fire tetrahedron**, explain the **mechanism of extinguishment by CO2**. Calculate the **heat absorbed** if **5 kg** of liquid CO2 is fully vaporized. **(Latent heat = 571 kJ/kg)**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Advantages of CO2:**
1. **Clean Agent** — Leaves no residue; ideal for electronic, electrical, and precision equipment.
2. **Non-Conductive** — Safe on live electrical equipment up to 1000 V.
3. **Dual Action** — Smothering + cooling simultaneously.
4. **Non-Corrosive** — Does not damage machinery or stored materials.
5. **No Water Damage** — Safe for documents, server rooms, archives.

**Limitations of CO2:**
1. **Suffocation Hazard** — Displaces oxygen; cannot be used in occupied small rooms without evacuation.
2. **Limited Cooling for Class A** — Cannot sustain cooling for deep-seated smouldering fires (e.g., wood, paper).
3. **High Pressure Storage** — Cylinders require specialized refilling equipment.
4. **Short Discharge Range** — Effective range is only **1.5–2.5 metres**.
5. **Frostbite Risk** — The −78 °C discharge horn can cause cold burns if touched.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CO2 | Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) |
|---|---|---|
| Residue | None | Heavy white cloud / dust |
| Cooling | Excellent (latent heat) | Minimal |
| Smothering | Excellent | Good |
| Electrical Use | Excellent | Good |
| Recharge | Specialized | Easy |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Cleanliness | Clean Agent | Dirty Agent |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The modern **Fire Tetrahedron** consists of four elements that must all be present simultaneously for combustion:
**(1) Fuel, (2) Heat, (3) Oxygen, (4) Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction.**

CO2 attacks **two** of these elements:

**(i) Oxygen Removal (Smothering Effect):**
CO2 is **1.52 times heavier than air**, so it sinks and blankets the fire, displacing atmospheric oxygen. When O2 concentration falls below **15 %**, combustion cannot be sustained.

**(ii) Heat Absorption (Cooling Effect):**
The liquid-to-gas phase transition of CO2 absorbs enormous latent heat (571 kJ/kg), rapidly cooling the fuel surface below its **auto-ignition temperature**.

**Heat Absorbed Calculation:**

Given:
- Mass of liquid CO2, $m = 5$ kg
- Latent heat of vaporization, $L = 571$ kJ/kg

$$
Q = m \times L
$$

$$
Q = 5 \times 571
$$

$$
Q = 2855 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Final Answer:** The CO2 absorbs **2855 kJ** of heat during complete vaporization, which is sufficient to cool a sizeable Class B fire zone below the auto-ignition threshold.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting to ROUND UP** the number of cylinders in part (b) numericals. If you get 10.47 cylinders, write **11 cylinders** — examiners specifically look for the ceiling function.
> 2. **Mixing up CO2 density values.** Use **1.84 kg/m³ at NTP (0 °C)** unless the question specifies otherwise. Using 1.98 kg/m³ (at 20 °C) is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting the bursting disc** in the construction diagram. Mentioning only the main valve without the safety bursting disc costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Calling the discharge horn "metallic"** is a factual error. The horn must be non-metallic to prevent electrical conduction and operator frostbite.
> 5. **Not stating "as per IS 2878"** in construction answers. Standard reference tags give easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- **CO2 extinguishes fire by:** (i) Oxygen displacement (smothering) and (ii) Latent heat absorption (cooling).
- **Latent heat of vaporization of CO2 = 571 kJ/kg.**
- **Density of CO2 at NTP = 1.84 kg/m³.**
- **Specific gravity of CO2 gas = 1.522** (1.5× heavier than air → sinks).
- **Storage pressure = 55–70 bar at 21 °C.**
- **Expansion ratio = 1 : 450 to 1 : 500.**
- **Discharge temperature at horn = −78 °C** (dry ice temperature).
- **Bursting disc setting = ~190 bar.**
- **Hydraulic test pressure = ~250 bar** as per IS 2878.
- **Discharge time = 8–30 seconds** (portable extinguishers).
- **Effective range = 1.5–2.5 metres.**
- **Most preferred for: Class B + Class C fires.**
- **Governing IS Standard: IS 2878:2004.**
- **Critical temperature of CO2 = 31.04 °C** (above which it cannot exist as liquid regardless of pressure).
- **Siphon tube purpose = draws liquid CO2, not gas, from cylinder.**
- **Discharge horn material = non-metallic fibreglass/plastic** (avoids conduction + frostbite).
- **Room flooding concentration = 30–50 % CO2 by volume.**
- **Oxygen threshold for extinguishment = < 15 % O2.**
- **Suitable locations: server rooms, switchgear rooms, transformer yards, labs, aviation hangars, marine engine rooms.**
- **NOT suitable for: occupied small rooms, deep-seated Class A fires, reactive metals (Na, K, Mg — use dry powder instead).**
- **Calculation sequence for room flooding: Room Volume → CO2 Gas Volume → CO2 Mass → Liquid CO2 Volume → Number of Cylinders.**
- **CO2 has no residue — only clean-agent extinguishers recommended for electronics.**
- **For KTU numericals: always ROUND UP cylinder count (ceiling function).**
- **For KTU theory: always mention IS 2878 when discussing construction.**
- **CO2 is non-corrosive, non-conductive, but toxic in confined spaces (can cause asphyxiation).**
- **Vaporization absorbs heat; expansion blankets oxygen — both mechanisms are SIMULTANEOUS, not sequential.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Water Sprinkler System: Total Flooding System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT835 - FIRE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Water Sprinkler System: Total Flooding System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automatic Water Sprinkler System** is a fixed fire suppression installation comprising a pressurized water supply, a network of piping, and thermally sensitive sprinkler heads that automatically detect and suppress a fire at its point of origin by discharging water directly onto the heat source.

A **Total Flooding System** in the context of water-based suppression is specifically embodied by the **Deluge Sprinkler System**. It is defined under **NFPA 13** and **IS 15105** as a sprinkler system employing **open sprinkler heads** (no thermal sensing element), connected to a piping network that remains empty (or dry) until a separate **fire detection system** actuates a **deluge valve**, releasing water simultaneously from **every sprinkler head** in the designated hazard area. This instantaneous, simultaneous discharge over the entire protected space achieves the "total flooding" effect with water as the extinguishing medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The combination of *Automatic Detection* + *Deluge Valve* + *Open Nozzles* is what distinguishes the **Total Flooding Water Sprinkler System** from conventional automatic sprinkler systems (wet pipe / dry pipe / pre-action), where only the heat-affected heads operate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Synchronized Rainstorm"

Imagine a large shopping mall ceiling fitted with dozens of shower heads, but each shower head is **permanently open** (no tap to close it). A separate network of smoke and heat detectors acts as the **"brain"** of the system. The moment a fire starts, the detectors signal a master **deluge valve** — think of it as a giant single tap that controls water to *every* shower head. When the brain fires, the master tap opens, and water pours out of **all shower heads at once**, blanketing the entire hall like a synchronized rainstorm. The fire is not just hit at one point — it is drowned from above in a uniform, ceiling-down "flood" of water.

### 1.3 Core Components (Quick Reference)

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Water Supply** | Dedicated tank, municipal main, or pump-fed reservoir |
| **Deluge Valve** | Quick-opening master control valve (hydraulic, pneumatic, or electric release) |
| **Fire Detection System** | Heat detectors, smoke detectors, or flame detectors |
| **Control Panel** | Receives detector signal and triggers deluge valve |
| **Open Sprinkler Heads** | Always-open discharge nozzles (no thermal element) |
| **Piping Network** | Branch and main lines (dry until activation) |
| **Manual Release Station** | Backup manual trigger (per IS 15105) |
| **Drain & Test Connections** | For flow testing and system maintenance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Density:** The system is engineered for a **discharge density of 12.5 mm/min to 25 mm/min** (typical range for high-hazard occupancies) over the **Area of Operation (AoO)**, as per **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)** and **NFPA 13** guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Standby State:** The deluge valve is closed. The downstream piping is empty (dry) or held under pilot-line pressure. All sprinkler heads are open to atmosphere.
2. **Fire Inception:** A fire breaks out, generating heat, smoke, or flame signatures.
3. **Detection:** A **separate detection system** (smoke/heat/flame detector) senses the fire signature faster than the fire would activate a conventional sprinkler fusible link.
4. **Signal Transmission:** The detector sends an electrical signal to the **fire alarm control panel (FACP)**.
5. **Valve Actuation:** The FACP energizes a **solenoid valve** or releases pneumatic pressure, which causes the **deluge valve** to open rapidly.
6. **Simultaneous Discharge:** Water from the supply main rushes through the piping and discharges from **all open sprinkler heads** at once.
7. **Total Flooding Effect:** The protected area is uniformly blanketed with water, achieving rapid cooling, oxygen displacement (steam), and pre-wetting of unburnt combustibles.
8. **Manual Override:** Operators can manually trip the system via a **manual release station** if automatic detection fails.

### 2.2 Why Use Total Flooding (Deluge) Instead of Conventional Sprinklers?

| Criterion | Conventional (Wet/Dry Pipe) | Total Flooding (Deluge) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sprinkler Head** | Closed (thermal element) | **Open** |
| **Activation** | Heat at individual head | Detection system → all heads |
| **Response Time** | Slower (heat must reach each head) | **Fastest (simultaneous discharge)** |
| **Water Demand** | Lower (only affected heads) | **Highest (all heads operate)** |
| **Applications** | Offices, residential, hotels | Flammable liquids, aircraft hangars, transformer yards, chemical storage |
| **Pipe State (Standby)** | Wet pipe: pressurized with water | Dry (empty) |
| **Risk of Water Damage** | Localized | Widespread (by design) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS** — Memorize these for Part A & Part B derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \sqrt{P}$ | Sprinkler discharge equation | $Q$ in LPM, $P$ in bar |
| 2 | $K = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{P}}$ | K-factor (specific to sprinkler orifice) | — |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{A_{oO}}$ | Discharge density over Area of Operation | mm/min |
| 4 | $A_{oO} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{D}$ | Area of Operation | m² |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{4.52 \cdot Q^{1.85}}{C^{1.85} \cdot d^{4.87}} \cdot L$ | Hazen-Williams head loss (SI) | bar, m, m³/h |
| 6 | $h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ | Darcy-Weisbach equivalent (Hazen-Williams) | m of water |
| 7 | $P_{eq} = P_{static} - h_f - h_{elevation}$ | Available pressure at most remote head | bar |
| 8 | $Q_{total} = N \cdot K \cdot \sqrt{P_{avg}}$ | Total flow for N open heads | LPM |
| 9 | $V_{tank} = Q_{total} \cdot t$ | Required water storage volume | Litres |
| 10 | $P_{min} = \dfrac{(Q_{required})^2}{K^2}$ | Minimum required pressure at head | bar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard K-factors:** Residential (K = 4.2), Light Hazard (K = 5.6), Ordinary Hazard (K = 8.0), **Extra/High Hazard (K = 11.2 or K = 14.0 for deluge systems)**. The most common deluge sprinkler is **K = 11.2** (≈ 80 gpm/psi½).

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Design Constraints

- **Maximum Area of Operation (AoO):** 72 m² (light hazard) up to **260 m² (Extra High Hazard Group 2)** as per TAC.
- **Minimum Operating Pressure:** Typically **3.5 bar (50 psi)** at the most remote sprinkler head for deluge systems.
- **Duration of Supply:** Minimum **60 minutes** for high-hazard; **90 minutes** for extra high-hazard (per TAC/NFPA).
- **Detection System Reliability:** Must have **redundant detection** (e.g., 2 out of N detector confirmation) to prevent false discharge.
- **Water Supply Reliability:** Requires **Type X, Y, or Z** classified supply per IS 15105 (two independent sources, one of which must be electric/diesel pump-fed tank).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Total flooding sprinkler systems are the **gold standard** for protecting:
- **Aircraft hangars** (deluge + AFFF foam)
- **Petrochemical storage** and LNG terminals
- **Power transformer yards**
- **Paint spray booths**
- **Explosive magazines** and ordnance depots
- **Turbine enclosures** in power plants
- **Cable vaults** in substations

> [!NOTE]
> The **Deluge + Foam Concentrate** variant is widely used in the hydrocarbon industry, where the water acts as a carrier and the foam blanket suppresses flammable liquid fires by **vapor suppression**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sprinkler Discharge (From Bernoulli's Principle)

**Statement:** Derive the relationship $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ for a single open sprinkler head.

**Starting Point:** The discharge of water through a sharp-edged orifice is governed by the **Torricelli-Bernoulli equation**.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Inlet to Orifice):**
For a pressurized sprinkler head, the pressure energy at the inlet converts to kinetic energy at the orifice outlet.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 &= P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
At the inlet, $v_1 \approx 0$ (water is stationary in the branch line). At the outlet, $P_2 = P_{atm}$ (gauge) and $z_1 \approx z_2$ (no elevation change). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gauge} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Discharge Velocity $v_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_2 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_{gauge}}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Continuity Equation:**
The volumetric flow rate is the product of orifice area and discharge velocity, modified by the discharge coefficient $C_d$ (typically 0.7 to 0.9 for sprinkler orifices).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C_d \cdot A_o \cdot v_2 \\[4pt]
  &= C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Group the Constants into a Single Term K:**
All geometric and fluid properties ($C_d$, $A_o$, $\rho$) are constant for a given sprinkler. We define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= K \sqrt{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **fundamental sprinkler discharge equation** used universally in NFPA 13, IS 15105, and TAC hydraulic calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Water Demand for a Deluge System

**Statement:** A deluge system protects a transformer yard measuring **20 m × 15 m = 300 m²** with open sprinklers of **K = 11.2**. Design discharge density = **12.5 mm/min**. Calculate the total water demand.

**Step 1 — Determine the Area of Operation (AoO):**
Per TAC Table, for a transformer yard (Extra High Hazard), maximum AoO = **260 m²**. The protected area (300 m²) is larger, so we use the design AoO of **260 m²**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{oO} &= 260 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Required Total Discharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{A_{oO}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{total} = D \times A_{oO}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert density to LPM/m²:
$12.5 \text{ mm/min} = 12.5 \text{ L/min per m}^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= 12.5 \;\text{L/min/m}^2 \times 260 \;\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= 3{,}250 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to m³/h (since pipe sizing uses m³/h):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= \dfrac{3{,}250 \times 60}{1{,}000} = 195 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine Number of Open Heads Required:**
If sprinklers are spaced at **3 m × 3 m = 9 m²/head**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{A_{oO}}{\text{spacing}} = \dfrac{260}{9} \approx 29 \text{ heads}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Average Pressure per Head:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{head} &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{N} = \dfrac{3{,}250}{29} \approx 112.07 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $Q = K\sqrt{P}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{P} &= \dfrac{Q_{head}}{K} = \dfrac{112.07}{11.2} = 10.006
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= (10.006)^2 \approx 100.12 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This pressure is **impractically high**. Engineers must re-spec: either increase the K-factor (use K = 14.0 or K = 16.8) OR increase the spacing (use 4 m × 4 m = 16 m²/head, giving N ≈ 17 heads, and recalculate).

**Step 5 — Re-solve with K = 16.8 and 4 m × 4 m spacing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{260}{16} = 16.25 \rightarrow 17 \text{ heads}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{head} &= \dfrac{3{,}250}{17} \approx 191.18 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \left(\dfrac{191.18}{16.8}\right)^2 = (11.38)^2 \approx 129.5 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Still too high. This indicates the design density of 12.5 mm/min over 260 m² **exceeds the capacity of K = 16.8 sprinklers**. The designer must either **reduce AoO**, **reduce density**, or **split the hazard into multiple zones** with separate deluge valves.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hazen-Williams Pipe Friction Loss

**Statement:** The deluge valve supplies water to the most remote sprinkler through **120 m of 100 mm diameter** schedule 40 mild steel pipe. Flow rate is **30 m³/h**. Calculate friction head loss.

**Given:** $L = 120$ m, $d = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $Q = 30$ m³/h, $C = 120$ (MS pipe).

**Step 1 — Convert Flow to m³/s (for SI Hazen-Williams variant):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{30}{3{,}600} = 0.00833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hazen-Williams Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{10.67 \times 120 \times (0.00833)^{1.852}}{(120)^{1.852} \times (0.1)^{4.87}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute each term:
- $Q^{1.852} = (0.00833)^{1.852} \approx 1.06 \times 10^{-4}$
- $C^{1.852} = 120^{1.852} \approx 7{,}488$
- $d^{4.87} = 0.1^{4.87} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-5}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{10.67 \times 120 \times 1.06 \times 10^{-4}}{7{,}488 \times 1.35 \times 10^{-5}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{um} &= 10.67 \times 120 \times 1.06 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.1357
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{en} &= 7{,}488 \times 1.35 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.1011
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{0.1357}{0.1011} \approx 1.34 \text{ m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Bar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{friction} &= h_f \times 0.098 = 1.34 \times 0.098 \approx 0.131 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Total Flooding System Design

| Design Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Deluge) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge Density | $D$ | 10 – 25 mm/min | Based on fuel type & hazard |
| Area of Operation | $A_{oO}$ | 72 – 260 m² | Per TAC/NFPA 13 hazard class |
| Sprinkler K-factor | $K$ | 11.2 – 16.8 | High flow capacity needed |
| Operating Pressure | $P$ | 3.5 – 7.0 bar | Minimum for effective atomization |
| Detection System Type | — | Smoke + Heat (2ooN) | Redundancy to prevent false trip |
| Water Supply Duration | $t$ | 60 – 90 min | Per hazard classification |
| Deluge Valve Response | — | < 15 seconds | Quick-opening type required |
| Water Source | — | Tank + Pump + Municipal | Redundant (Type X/Y per IS 15105) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fire Initiation Source] --> B[Smoke and Heat Detector Network]
    B --> C[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    C --> D{Signal Validated 2ooN Confirmation}
    D -- No --> E[System Remains in Standby Mode]
    D -- Yes --> F[Solenoid or Pneumatic Actuator Triggers]
    F --> G[Deluge Valve Opens Rapidly]
    G --> H[Pressurized Water Releases from Main]
    H --> I[Water Travels Through Dry Piping Network]
    I --> J[Simultaneous Discharge from All Open Sprinkler Heads]
    J --> K[Total Flooding Effect Over Protected Area]
    K --> L[Fire Suppression by Cooling Steam Smothering and Pre Wetting]
    L --> M[Manual Override or Auto Shut Off After Event]
    M --> N[System Reset and Refill for Standby]
```

### 4.2 Deluge Valve Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Supply Main Inlet] --> B[Deluge Valve Body]
    B --> C[Pilot Line Pressure]
    C --> D{Detection Signal}
    D -- Absent --> E[Latched Closed Position]
    D -- Present --> F[Solenoid Releases Pilot Pressure]
    F --> G[Diaphragm Unseats]
    G --> H[Main Water Flow to Piping]
    H --> I[All Open Sprinkler Heads Discharge]
    I --> J[Drain and Alarm Activated]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between a Conventional Automatic Sprinkler System and a Total Flooding (Deluge) Sprinkler System. List any four applications of the latter.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Conventional Automatic Sprinkler System** uses **closed sprinkler heads** (with fusible link or glass bulb) that open individually when the localized heat reaches the activation temperature. Water is always present in the piping (wet pipe) or held under air pressure (dry pipe). Discharge is **localized** — only the affected heads operate.

A **Total Flooding (Deluge) Sprinkler System** uses **open sprinkler heads** (no thermal element). The piping remains dry in standby. A separate **fire detection system** (smoke/heat detectors) actuates a **deluge valve** when a fire is detected, causing **simultaneous discharge from all open heads** over the entire protected area.

| Aspect | Conventional | Total Flooding (Deluge) |
|---|---|---|
| Sprinkler heads | Closed (thermal release) | Open (no thermal element) |
| Activation | Heat at each head | Detection system signal |
| Discharge pattern | Localized | Simultaneous (all heads) |
| Piping state | Pressurized with water/air | Dry (empty) |
| Response speed | Slower | Fastest |

**Four Applications:**
1. Aircraft hangars
2. Petrochemical and flammable liquid storage
3. Power transformer yards
4. Paint spray booths and explosive magazines

**(3 Marks — Awarded for: 1 mark definition of each, 1 mark for tabulated differences, 1 mark for 4 applications.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define "K-factor" of a sprinkler head. A sprinkler has a K-factor of 11.2 and operates at 5 bar pressure. Calculate the discharge in LPM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **K-factor** of a sprinkler head is a constant that characterizes the discharge capacity of the orifice. It is defined as the ratio of flow rate $Q$ to the square root of pressure $P$. Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{P}}
\end{aligned}
$$

A higher K-factor indicates a **larger orifice** and therefore **greater water discharge** for a given pressure. Standard K-factors are K = 5.6 (residential), K = 8.0 (ordinary), K = 11.2 (high hazard/deluge), and K = 16.8 (extra high hazard).

**Calculation:**

Given: $K = 11.2$, $P = 5$ bar.

Using $Q = K\sqrt{P}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 11.2 \times \sqrt{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 11.2 \times 2.236
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 25.04 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ The sprinkler discharges approximately 25.04 LPM at 5 bar pressure.**

**(3 Marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for substitution and final answer.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Deluge Sprinkler System** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each major component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A total flooding sprinkler system is to be designed for a **chemical storage area of 240 m²**. The design discharge density is **15 mm/min**, and open sprinklers of **K = 11.2** are to be used, spaced at **3 m × 3 m**. Calculate:
- (i) The total water demand in LPM and m³/h.
- (ii) The number of sprinkler heads in operation.
- (iii) The required average pressure at the sprinkler head.
- (iv) The required water storage tank capacity for a 90-minute duration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Deluge Sprinkler System** is the most rapid-acting water-based fire suppression system. Its working principle is illustrated by the following block sequence:

1. **Detection Subsystem:** Smoke/heat detectors sense the fire and send a signal to the Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP).
2. **Confirmation Logic:** The FACP applies a **2-out-of-N voting logic** to prevent false activation. Only on valid confirmation is the next stage triggered.
3. **Actuation:** The FACP energizes a **solenoid valve** in the pilot line of the deluge valve. This releases the trapped pressure holding the deluge valve diaphragm in the closed position.
4. **Deluge Valve Opening:** Once the diaphragm unseats, the main water supply flows into the previously dry piping network.
5. **Simultaneous Discharge:** All **open sprinkler heads** in the protected area discharge water at once, creating the "total flooding" effect.
6. **Suppression Mechanism:** Water cools the fire, generates steam that displaces oxygen, and pre-wets surrounding combustibles to prevent fire spread.
7. **Reset:** After the fire is controlled, the system is manually shut off, drained, and reset to standby.

**Role of Major Components:**
- **Detection system:** Fast and accurate fire signature identification.
- **FACP:** Central intelligence and confirmation logic.
- **Deluge valve:** Master quick-opening water control device.
- **Open sprinkler heads:** Discharge points (no thermal element).
- **Piping network:** Distributes water from valve to heads.
- **Water supply:** Provides the required flow and pressure.
- **Manual release station:** Backup for operator-controlled activation.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Detector] --> B[FACP]
    B --> C[Deluge Valve]
    C --> D[All Open Heads]
    D --> E[Total Flooding]
```

**(7 Marks — Distributed: 4 marks for sequential explanation, 2 marks for component roles, 1 mark for diagram.)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Area of operation $A_{oO} = 240 \text{ m}^2$
- Discharge density $D = 15 \text{ mm/min} = 15 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- Sprinkler K-factor $K = 11.2$
- Spacing = 3 m × 3 m = 9 m²/head
- Duration $t = 90$ minutes

**(i) Total Water Demand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= D \times A_{oO} = 15 \times 240 = 3{,}600 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= \dfrac{3{,}600 \times 60}{1{,}000} = 216 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of Sprinkler Heads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{A_{oO}}{\text{spacing per head}} = \dfrac{240}{9} = 26.67 \approx 27 \text{ heads}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average Pressure at Head:**

Flow per head:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{head} &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{N} = \dfrac{3{,}600}{27} = 133.33 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $Q = K\sqrt{P}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{P} &= \dfrac{133.33}{11.2} = 11.905
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= (11.905)^2 = 141.73 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This pressure is **impractical for standard sprinkler installations** (typical operating range is 3.5 to 7 bar). The student must recognize this and recommend either a **higher K-factor sprinkler (K = 16.8 or K = 25.2)**, a **larger spacing**, or **zone-wise splitting** of the hazard.

**Re-evaluation with K = 16.8:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{P} &= \dfrac{133.33}{16.8} = 7.937
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= (7.937)^2 = 63.0 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Still excessive. Practical design: **Use K = 25.2 sprinklers**, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{P} &= \dfrac{133.33}{25.2} = 5.291
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= (5.291)^2 \approx 28.0 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Even K = 25.2 requires 28 bar — this signals the design **density is too high for 3 m × 3 m spacing**. Reduce density to 10 mm/min or use 4 m × 4 m spacing for a feasible system.

**(iv) Required Water Storage Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tank} &= Q_{total} \times t = 3{,}600 \text{ LPM} \times 90 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tank} &= 324{,}000 \text{ Litres} = 324 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 Marks — Distribution: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 1 mark, (iii) 3 marks, (iv) 1 mark.)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** What is a **"Total Flooding System"**? Compare **water-based total flooding (deluge)** with **gaseous total flooding systems** (e.g., CO₂, FM-200, Novec 1230) in terms of suitability, application, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Hazen-Williams equation**, calculate the friction head loss in a **150 m long, 150 mm diameter** MS pipe carrying a flow of **60 m³/h**. Take $C = 120$. Express the result in **meters of water column** and **bar**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **Total Flooding System** is a fire suppression system in which the extinguishing agent is **discharged into an enclosed or partially enclosed space** to achieve a uniform concentration throughout the volume, thereby suppressing the fire regardless of its exact location. The goal is to flood the entire protected volume with the suppressant.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Water Deluge (Total Flooding) | Gaseous Total Flooding (CO₂, FM-200, Novec) |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | Water (+ optional foam) | CO₂, Novec 1230, FM-200, Inergen |
| **Mechanism** | Cooling + steam smothering | Oxygen displacement + chemical interruption |
| **Suitable Fires** | Class A, B (with foam), some C | Class B, C, E (electrical) |
| **Suitability for Occupied Spaces** | **Yes** (people can be present) | **No** (CO₂ asphyxiating; Novec safer) |
| **Water Damage** | High (by design) | **None** |
| **Enclosure Integrity** | Not critical | **Critical** (must be sealed) |
| **Equipment Protection** | May damage electronics/valuables | **Excellent for data centers, museums** |
| **Storage Pressure** | Low (pumped water) | High (cylinders at 25–70 bar) |
| **Typical Application** | Aircraft hangars, flammable liquids | Server rooms, switchgear, archives |
| **Limitation** | High water demand, runoff, freeze risk | Asphyxiation risk, requires sealed room |
| **Cost** | Moderate (water is cheap) | High (clean agent is expensive) |

**Summary Insight:** Water deluge is the **preferred choice for industrial high-hazard outdoor or semi-enclosed areas** where water damage and runoff are not concerns. Gaseous systems are preferred for **enclosed electronic/data environments** where equipment preservation is paramount.

**(7 Marks — 2 marks for definition, 4 marks for comparison, 1 mark for concluding insight.)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Pipe length $L = 150$ m
- Pipe diameter $d = 150$ mm = 0.15 m
- Flow rate $Q = 60$ m³/h
- Hazen-Williams coefficient $C = 120$ (MS pipe)

**Step 1 — Convert Flow to m³/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{60}{3{,}600} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hazen-Williams Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Powers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{1.852} &= (0.01667)^{1.852}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using logarithms: $\log(0.01667) = -1.7782$
$\log(Q^{1.852}) = 1.852 \times (-1.7782) = -3.293$
$Q^{1.852} = 10^{-3.293} \approx 5.09 \times 10^{-4}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{1.852} &= 120^{1.852}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\log(120) = 2.0792$
$\log(C^{1.852}) = 1.852 \times 2.0792 = 3.851$
$C^{1.852} = 10^{3.851} \approx 7{,}096$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^{4.87} &= (0.15)^{4.87}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\log(0.15) = -0.8239$
$\log(d^{4.87}) = 4.87 \times (-0.8239) = -4.013$
$d^{4.87} = 10^{-4.013} \approx 9.68 \times 10^{-5}$

**Step 4 — Substitute All Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{10.67 \times 150 \times 5.09 \times 10^{-4}}{7{,}096 \times 9.68 \times 10^{-5}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{um} &= 10.67 \times 150 \times 5.09 \times 10^{-4} \\
       &= 1{,}600.5 \times 5.09 \times 10^{-4} \\
       &= 0.8147
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{en} &= 7{,}096 \times 9.68 \times 10^{-5} \\
       &= 0.6869
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Head Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= \dfrac{0.8147}{0.6869} = 1.186 \text{ m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to Bar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{friction} &= 1.186 \times 0.098 \approx 0.116 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Friction head loss = 1.186 m of water column ≈ 0.116 bar**

**(7 Marks — Distribution: 1 mark formula, 2 marks unit conversion, 3 marks calculation steps, 1 mark final answer with units.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Total Flooding / Deluge System Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Students frequently forget to convert LPM → m³/h when using Hazen-Williams, or mm/min → L/min/m² when computing density. **Always write the unit at every step.**
> 2. **Confusing wet-pipe with deluge:** A common KTU trap is asking *"Why is deluge preferred for flammable liquids?"* — students must mention the **simultaneous discharge + pre-wetting** mechanism, not just speed.
> 3. **Skipping the impractical pressure check:** In Part B design problems, if the calculated pressure exceeds 7 bar, the student **must flag it as infeasible** and propose a design correction. KTU examiners award marks for this critical engineering judgment.
> 4. **Missing detection redundancy:** A deluge system without **2ooN voting logic** is a poorly designed system. Always mention detector redundancy in long answers.
> 5. **Forgetting the manual release:** Per IS 15105, every deluge system **must have a manual release station** as backup. Students omit this in component lists.
> 6. **Misapplying K-factor units:** Some use K = 11.2 (gpm/psi½) directly in metric equations. Always confirm the K-factor is in **metric units (LPM/bar½)** before substituting.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total Flooding (Deluge) Sprinkler System** = Open heads + detection-actuated deluge valve + simultaneous discharge.
- **Key distinction:** Conventional systems = localized discharge; Deluge systems = total area discharge.
- **K-factor equation:** $Q = K\sqrt{P}$ — the **most frequently tested formula** in KTU Part A.
- **Discharge density $D$** for high-hazard deluge = 12.5 to 25 mm/min.
- **Area of Operation $A_{oO}$** is fixed by hazard classification (TAC Table), not by protected area size.
- **Water demand $Q_{total} = D \times A_{oO}$** in LPM.
- **Hazen-Williams** is the standard pipe friction loss method for sprinkler hydraulic calculations.
- **Operating pressure** at the most remote head should be **3.5 to 7 bar** for deluge systems; higher indicates design error.
- **Detection system** must use **2ooN voting logic** to prevent false discharge.
- **Manual release station** is mandatory backup per IS 15105.
- **Water supply** must be redundant (Type X or Y) — electric pump + diesel pump + tank.
- **Deluge + Foam** variant is used for hydrocarbon fires (e.g., aircraft hangars with AFFF).
- **Reset procedure** after discharge: drain piping, close deluge valve, restore pilot pressure, reset FACP.
- **Standards to remember:** **NFPA 13** (global), **IS 15105** (India), **TAC Guidelines** (insurance), **NBC Part 4** (India building code).
- **High K-factor sprinklers (K = 16.8, K = 25.2)** are preferred in deluge systems to keep operating pressure practical.
- **Quick-opening deluge valves** are essential for fast response — slow-closing valves defeat the purpose of a deluge system.
